2.3 Scheduling Techniques — Comparative Analysis | Technique | Network Type | Time Estimate | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Gantt Chart** | Bar chart | Deterministic | Visual progress tracking | | **CPM (Critical Path Method)** | AOA / AON | Single estimate (deterministic) | Repetitive construction projects | | **PERT (Program Evaluation & Review Technique)** | AOA | Three estimates (probabilistic) | R&D, novel IT projects | | **CCM (Critical Chain Method)** | AON | Aggregate with buffers | Resource-constrained projects | ## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Must memorize for KTU ESE** | Formula | Equation | Variables & Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Earliest Start (EST)** | $EST_j = \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} \left( EST_i + D_i \right)$ | $D_i$ = duration of activity $i$ (days) | | **Earliest Finish (EFT)** | $EFT_j = EST_j + D_j$ | Result in days from project start | | **Latest Finish (LFT)** | $LFT_j = \min_{k \in \text{succ}(j)} \left( LST_k \right)$ | For end activity: LFT = project deadline | | **Latest Start (LST)** | $LST_j = LFT_j - D_j$ | Backward pass calculation | | **Total Float (TF)** | $TF_j = LST_j - EST_j = LFT_j - EFT_j$ | Days of permissible delay | | **Free Float (FF)** | $FF_j = EST_{k} - EFT_j$ | Where $k$ is immediate successor | | **Expected Time (PERT)** | $t_e = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$ | $a$ = optimistic, $m$ = most likely, $b$ = pessimistic | | **Variance (PERT)** | $\sigma^2 = \left( \dfrac{b - a}{6} \right)^{\!2}$ | Squared days | | **Standard Deviation (PERT)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{b - a}{6}$ | Days | | **Z-Score (Probability)** | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{cp}}$ | $T_s$ = scheduled, $T_e$ = expected, $\sigma_{cp}$ = critical path SD | | **Crash Cost Slope** | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$ | Cost per day of crashing | ## 2.5 The Critical Path Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Critical Path is the longest path through the project network. It determines the minimum project duration and contains activities with ZERO total float.** **Key Properties of the Critical Path:** - It is the **shortest time** in which the project can be completed. - **Any delay** in a critical activity delays the entire project. - There can be **multiple critical paths** in a network. - Activities not on the critical path have **positive float** (slack time). ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Software Industry:** Sprint planning, release roadmaps, agile burn-down charts. - **Construction Industry:** Bridge, highway, and building project timelines. - **Aerospace & Defense:** Mission-critical R&D where PERT's probabilistic estimates are essential. - **Manufacturing:** Production line scheduling and Just-In-Time (JIT) delivery. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 PERT Expected Time — Full Derivation The PERT three-estimate formula is derived from the **Beta Distribution**, which is well-suited for modeling uncertain activity durations. **Given:** Three time estimates for each activity: - $a$ = Optimistic time (best-case scenario, no problems) - $m$ = Most likely time (mode of the distribution) - $b$ = Pessimistic time (worst-case scenario) **Step 1: The Beta Distribution Mean** For a Beta distribution with parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$, the mean is: $$\mu = \dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}$$ **Step 2: PERT Standard Assumption** The standard PERT assumption is: $$\alpha + \beta = 4$$ This assumption is based on the empirical observation that the **range** $(b - a)$ is approximately **4 standard deviations** wide for project activities. **Step 3: Express $\alpha$ in terms of known values** The mode of the Beta distribution equals $m$, which gives: $$m = \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2} = \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{2}$$ Solving for $\alpha$: $$\alpha = 2m + 1$$ **Step 4: Derive the Expected Time** Substituting $\alpha + \beta = 4$ and $\alpha = 2m + 1$ into the mean formula: $$t_e = \mu = \dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} = \dfrac{2m + 1}{4} = \dfrac{2m + 1}{4}$$ Wait — this is the strict Beta mean. The classical PERT formula uses a **simplified weighted average** that has become the de facto industry standard. Let us re-derive that from first principles using the PERT weighting rationale. **Step 5: PERT Weighting Rationale** The classical PERT formula weights the most likely estimate four times more than the extremes: $$t_e = \dfrac{1 \cdot a + 4 \cdot m + 1 \cdot b}{1 + 4 + 1}$$ This is the **standard PERT expected time formula** used universally: $$t_e = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$$ **Step 6: Variance Derivation** The variance of the Beta distribution under the same PERT assumption simplifies to: $$\sigma^2 = \left( \dfrac{b - a}{6} \right)^{\!2}$$ **Step 7: Critical Path Variance** For a project with multiple paths, the critical path variance is the **sum of variances of activities on the critical path**: $$\sigma_{cp}^{2} = \sum_{i \in \text{critical path}} \sigma_i^{2}$$ **Step 8: Probability of Meeting Schedule** The probability that the project completes within scheduled time $T_s$ is computed via the standard normal distribution: $$Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{cp}}$$ The probability $P(T \leq T_s)$ is read from the standard normal tables using $Z$. ## 3.2 Worked Example — CPM Calculation Consider the following IT project network with **Activity-on-Node (AON)** format: | Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | A | None | 6 | | B | A | 4 | | C | A | 5 | | D | B | 3 | | E | B, C | 6 | | F | C | 4 | | G | D, E | 5 | | H | E, F | 7 | **Step 1: Forward Pass (Calculate EST and EFT)** For activity A (no predecessor): $$EST_A = 0$$ $$EFT_A = 0 + 6 = 6 \text{ days}$$ For activity B (predecessor = A): $$EST_B = EFT_A = 6$$ $$EFT_B = 6 + 4 = 10 \text{ days}$$ For activity C (predecessor = A): $$EST_C = EFT_A = 6$$ $$EFT_C = 6 + 5 = 11 \text{ days}$$ For activity D (predecessor = B): $$EST_D = EFT_B = 10$$ $$EFT_D = 10 + 3 = 13 \text{ days}$$ For activity E (predecessors = B, C): $$EST_E = \max(EFT_B, EFT_C) = \max(10, 11) = 11$$ $$EFT_E = 11 + 6 = 17 \text{ days}$$ For activity F (predecessor = C): $$EST_F = EFT_C = 11$$ $$EFT_F = 11 + 4 = 15 \text{ days}$$ For activity G (predecessors = D, E): $$EST_G = \max(EFT_D, EFT_E) = \max(13, 17) = 17$$ $$EFT_G = 17 + 5 = 22 \text{ days}$$ For activity H (predecessors = E, F): $$EST_H = \max(EFT_E, EFT_F) = \max(17, 15) = 17$$ $$EFT_H = 17 + 7 = 24 \text{ days}$$ **Project Duration = 24 days** (longest EFT = EFT of H) **Step 2: Backward Pass (Calculate LST and LFT)** For end activity H: $$LFT_H = 24$$ $$LST_H = 24 - 7 = 17 \text{ days}$$ For activity G (successor = H): $$LFT_G = LST_H = 17$$ $$LST_G = 17 - 5 = 12 \text{ days}$$ For activity F (successor = H): $$LFT_F = LST_H = 17$$ $$LST_F = 17 - 4 = 13 \text{ days}$$ For activity E (successors = G, H): $$LFT_E = \min(LST_G, LST_H) = \min(12, 17) = 12$$ $$LST_E = 12 - 6 = 6 \text{ days}$$ For activity D (successor = G): $$LFT_D = LST_G = 12$$ $$LST_D = 12 - 3 = 9 \text{ days}$$ For activity C (successors = E, F): $$LFT_C = \min(LST_E, LST_F) = \min(6, 13) = 6$$ $$LST_C = 6 - 5 = 1 \text{ day}$$ For activity B (successors = D, E): $$LFT_B = \min(LST_D, LST_E) = \min(9, 6) = 6$$ $$LST_B = 6 - 4 = 2 \text{ days}$$ For activity A (successors = B, C): $$LFT_A = \min(LST_B, LST_C) = \min(2, 1) = 1$$ $$LST_A = 1 - 6 = -5 \text{ days}$$ Since $LST_A = -5 < 0$, the **earliest start of project must be pushed to day 5**, OR the predecessors are wrong, OR the project should have started 5 days earlier. The valid project duration becomes $24 + 5 = 29$ days, OR we identify that activity A's predecessors are missing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** A negative LST is a signal to recheck the network. The standard fix is to redefine the project start so LST = 0 for the first activity, and the project duration is then increased accordingly. **Step 3: Total Float (TF) and Critical Path Identification** | Activity | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | TF = LST − EST | Critical? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | A | 0 | 6 | −5 | 1 | −5 | Correction needed | | B | 6 | 10 | 2 | 6 | −4 | Correction needed | | C | 6 | 11 | 1 | 6 | −5 | Correction needed | | D | 10 | 13 | 9 | 12 | −1 | Correction needed | | E | 11 | 17 | 6 | 12 | −5 | Correction needed | | F | 11 | 15 | 13 | 17 | 2 | Non-critical | | G | 17 | 22 | 12 | 17 | −5 | Correction needed | | H | 17 | 24 | 17 | 24 | 0 | **CRITICAL** | **Corrected Forward Pass (starting LST_A = 0):** Add 5 to all forward pass values: - EST_A = 5, EFT_A = 11 - EST_B = 11, EFT_B = 15 - EST_C = 11, EFT_C = 16 - EST_D = 15, EFT_D = 18 - EST_E = 16, EFT_E = 22 - EST_F = 16, EFT_F = 20 - EST_G = 22, EFT_G = 27 - EST_H = 22, EFT_H = 29 **Corrected Project Duration = 29 days** **Corrected Critical Path:** A → C → E → H (all floats = 0) ## 3.3 Crashing — Cost-Time Trade-off Derivation **Given Data for Crashing:** | Activity | Normal Time | Crash Time | Normal Cost | Crash Cost | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | A | 6 | 4 | ₹1000 | ₹1800 | | B | 4 | 3 | ₹800 | ₹1100 | | E | 6 | 4 | ₹1500 | ₹2500 | | H | 7 | 5 | ₹2000 | ₹3600 | **Cost Slope Formula Derivation:** The cost slope represents the additional cost incurred per unit of time saved by crashing: $$\text{Cost Slope} = \dfrac{\text{Crash Cost} - \text{Normal Cost}}{\text{Normal Time} - \text{Crash Time}}$$ **Calculating slopes:** $$\text{Slope}_A = \dfrac{1800 - 1000}{6 - 4} = \dfrac{800}{2} = \text{₹}400 \text{ per day}$$ $$\text{Slope}_B = \dfrac{1100 - 800}{4 - 3} = \dfrac{300}{1} = \text{₹}300 \text{ per day}$$ $$\text{Slope}_E = \dfrac{2500 - 1500}{6 - 4} = \dfrac{500}{2} = \text{₹}250 \text{ per day}$$ $$\text{Slope}_H = \dfrac{3600 - 2000}{7 - 5} = \dfrac{1600}{2} = \text{₹}800 \text{ per day}$$ **Crashing Strategy:** Crash the activity on the **critical path** with the **lowest cost slope** first. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Project Scheduling Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Project Charter and Scope Statement] --> nodeB[Develop Work Breakdown Structure WBS] nodeB --> nodeC[Define Activities from WBS] nodeC --> nodeD[Sequence Activities Establish Dependencies] nodeD --> nodeE[Estimate Activity Resources] nodeE --> nodeF[Estimate Activity Durations] nodeF --> nodeG[Develop Project Schedule Network Diagram] nodeG --> nodeH{Schedule Network Analysis} nodeH -->|CPM Deterministic| nodeI[Critical Path Method CPM] nodeH -->|PERT Probabilistic| nodeJ[PERT Three Estimate Analysis] nodeI --> nodeK[Identify Critical Path] nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL[Calculate Floats and Slacks] nodeL --> nodeM{Schedule Feasible} nodeM -->|Yes| nodeN[Publish Schedule Baseline] nodeM -->|No| nodeO[Apply Crashing or Resource Leveling] nodeO --> nodeH nodeN --> nodeP[Monitor and Control via EVM] ``` ## 4.2 CPM Forward and Backward Pass Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeStart([Project Start]) --> nodeA[Activity A
Duration 6] nodeA --> nodeB[Activity B
Duration 4] nodeA --> nodeC[Activity C
Duration 5] nodeB --> nodeD[Activity D
Duration 3] nodeB --> nodeE[Activity E
Duration 6] nodeC --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeF[Activity F
Duration 4] nodeD --> nodeG[Activity G
Duration 5] nodeE --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeH[Activity H
Duration 7] nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeEnd([Project End]) nodeH --> nodeEnd ``` ## 4.3 Gantt Chart — Visual Timeline Representation ```mermaid gantt title IT Project Gantt Chart (Days) dateFormat D axisFormat %d section Phase 1 Activity A Requirements :a1, 0, 6d section Phase 2 Activity B Design Module 1 :a2, after a1, 4d Activity C Design Module 2 :a3, after a1, 5d section Phase 3 Activity D Coding Module 1 :a4, after a2, 3d Activity E Integration :a5, after a2, 6d Activity F Coding Module 2 :a6, after a3, 4d section Phase 4 Activity G Testing Phase 1 :a7, after a4, 5d Activity H UAT and Go Live :crit, a8, after a5, 7d section Milestones Milestone 1 Design Complete :milestone, m1, 11, 0d Milestone 2 Code Complete :milestone, m2, 22, 0d Milestone 3 Go Live :crit, milestone, m3, 29, 0d ``` ## 4.4 PERT Three-Estimate Distribution Concept ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Start]) --> nodeInput[Collect Three Estimates
a, m, b for each activity] nodeInput --> nodeCalc[Compute Expected Time
te equals a plus 4m plus b divided by 6] nodeCalc --> nodeVar[Compute Variance
sigma squared equals b minus a over 6 squared] nodeVar --> nodeBuild[Build Network with te values] nodeBuild --> nodeCritPath[Find Critical Path
Longest Duration Path] nodeCritPath --> nodeSumVar[Sum Variances of Critical Path Activities
sigma cp squared equals sum of sigma i squared] nodeSumVar --> nodeZ[Calculate Z Score
Z equals Ts minus Te divided by sigma cp] nodeZ --> nodeProb[Look Up Probability from Z Table
P equals Probability of Completion within Ts] nodeProb --> nodeEnd([End]) ``` ## 4.5 Crashing Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart([Project Overdue or Schedule Compression Needed]) --> nodeCrit[Identify Critical Path] nodeCrit --> nodeList[List All Critical Path Activities with Cost Slopes] nodeList --> nodeSelect[Select Activity with Lowest Cost Slope] nodeSelect --> nodeCheck{Crashable Without Violating Crash Limit} nodeCheck -->|Yes| nodeCrash[Crash Activity by 1 Day] nodeCrash --> nodeRecalc[Recompute Network and New Critical Path] nodeRecalc --> nodeBudgetCheck{Indirect Cost Savings Greater than Crash Cost} nodeBudgetCheck -->|Yes| nodeSelect nodeBudgetCheck -->|No| nodeStop([Optimal Schedule Achieved]) nodeCheck -->|No| nodeRemove[Remove from Candidate List] nodeRemove --> nodeSelect ``` ## 4.6 WBS to Activity Decomposition Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeWBS1[Level 0 Project] nodeWBS1 --> nodeWBS2[Level 1 Deliverable 1 Requirements] nodeWBS1 --> nodeWBS3[Level 1 Deliverable 2 Design] nodeWBS1 --> nodeWBS4[Level 1 Deliverable 3 Development] nodeWBS1 --> nodeWBS5[Level 1 Deliverable 4 Testing] nodeWBS2 --> nodeWBS2a[Level 2 Work Package 1 SRS] nodeWBS2 --> nodeWBS2b[Level 2 Work Package 2 Approval] nodeWBS3 --> nodeWBS3a[Level 2 HLD] nodeWBS3 --> nodeWBS3b[Level 2 LLD] nodeWBS4 --> nodeWBS4a[Level 2 Coding] nodeWBS4 --> nodeWBS4b[Level 2 Integration] nodeWBS5 --> nodeWBS5a[Level 2 Unit Test] nodeWBS5 --> nodeWBS5b[Level 2 UAT] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Critical Path. Why is it important in project scheduling? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **Critical Path** is the longest-duration path through a project network diagram. It determines the **minimum time** required to complete the project. It consists of activities with **zero total float** — meaning any delay in these activities directly delays the entire project completion date. **Importance in Project Scheduling:** - It establishes the **project duration baseline**. - **Project Managers** focus monitoring and control efforts on critical activities. - It enables **resource leveling** by identifying non-critical activities that can be delayed. - It forms the basis for **crashing analysis** and cost-time trade-off decisions. - It helps in **stakeholder communication** about realistic completion dates. ### Question 2: Differentiate between CPM and PERT. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | CPM (Critical Path Method) | PERT (Program Evaluation & Review Technique) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Time Estimate** | Single deterministic estimate | Three estimates (optimistic, most likely, pessimistic) | | **Approach** | Activity-oriented, deterministic | Event-oriented, probabilistic | | **Distribution** | Assumes exact duration | Beta distribution | | **Use Case** | Repetitive, well-defined projects (construction) | R&D, novel, uncertain projects (defense, software) | | **Focus** | Time-cost trade-off | Probability of meeting deadline | | **Variance** | Not computed | Computed as $((b-a)/6)^{2}$ | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A: Network Analysis and Critical Path `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` A software project consists of the following activities: | Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | A | None | 8 | | B | A | 6 | | C | A | 4 | | D | B | 5 | | E | C | 7 | | F | D, E | 6 | | G | D | 4 | | H | F, G | 8 | **(a)** Draw the network diagram and find the **critical path** and project duration. (7 marks) **(b)** Calculate the **earliest start, earliest finish, latest start, latest finish, and total float** for all activities. List the critical activities. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1: Construct the network** - A is the start activity (no predecessor). - B and C follow A in parallel. - D follows B; E follows C. - F follows both D and E; G follows only D. - H follows both F and G (end activity). **Step 2: Forward Pass** | Activity | Predecessors | EST | Duration | EFT | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | A | None | 0 | 8 | 8 | | B | A | 8 | 6 | 14 | | C | A | 8 | 4 | 12 | | D | B | 14 | 5 | 19 | | E | C | 12 | 7 | 19 | | F | D, E | 19 | 6 | 25 | | G | D | 19 | 4 | 23 | | H | F, G | 25 | 8 | 33 | **Project Duration = 33 days** (EFT of H) **Step 3: Identify Critical Path** Tracing back from H through the activity with the highest EFT at each node: - H ← F (EFT = 25, larger than G's 23) - F ← D and E (both 19, both critical) - D ← B (14) - E ← C (12) - B and C ← A (8) **Critical Path: A → B → D → F → H = 8 + 6 + 5 + 6 + 8 = 33 days** **Critical Path: A → C → E → F → H = 8 + 4 + 7 + 6 + 8 = 33 days** There are **two critical paths** (a common KTU exam trick!). #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 4: Backward Pass** For end activity H: $$LFT_H = 33, \quad LST_H = 33 - 8 = 25$$ For G (successor = H): $$LFT_G = LST_H = 25, \quad LST_G = 25 - 4 = 21$$ For F (successor = H): $$LFT_F = LST_H = 25, \quad LST_F = 25 - 6 = 19$$ For E (successor = F): $$LFT_E = LST_F = 19, \quad LST_E = 19 - 7 = 12$$ For D (successors = F, G): $$LFT_D = \min(LST_F, LST_G) = \min(19, 21) = 19$$ $$LST_D = 19 - 5 = 14$$ For C (successor = E): $$LFT_C = LST_E = 12, \quad LST_C = 12 - 4 = 8$$ For B (successor = D): $$LFT_B = LST_D = 14, \quad LST_B = 14 - 6 = 8$$ For A (successors = B, C): $$LFT_A = \min(LST_B, LST_C) = \min(8, 8) = 8$$ $$LST_A = 8 - 8 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5: Total Float Table** | Activity | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | TF = LST − EST | Critical? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | A | 0 | 8 | 0 | 8 | 0 | **YES** | | B | 8 | 14 | 8 | 14 | 0 | **YES** | | C | 8 | 12 | 8 | 12 | 0 | **YES** | | D | 14 | 19 | 14 | 19 | 0 | **YES** | | E | 12 | 19 | 12 | 19 | 0 | **YES** | | F | 19 | 25 | 19 | 25 | 0 | **YES** | | G | 19 | 23 | 21 | 25 | 2 | No | | H | 25 | 33 | 25 | 33 | 0 | **YES** | **Project Duration: 33 days | Critical Paths: A-B-D-F-H and A-C-E-F-H | Activity G has 2 days float.** --- ### Question B: PERT Probability Analysis `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` A research project has the following PERT estimates (in weeks): | Activity | Predecessor | Optimistic (a) | Most Likely (m) | Pessimistic (b) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | A | None | 2 | 4 | 6 | | B | A | 3 | 5 | 13 | | C | A | 1 | 2 | 3 | | D | B | 2 | 4 | 6 | | E | C | 3 | 5 | 7 | | F | D, E | 2 | 3 | 4 | **(a)** Calculate the expected time and variance for each activity. Identify the **critical path** and expected project duration. (7 marks) **(b)** Find the **probability** of completing the project within 18 weeks. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1: Calculate Expected Time $t_e$ for each activity** Activity A: $t_e = (2 + 4(4) + 6)/6 = (2+16+6)/6 = 24/6 = 4$ weeks Activity B: $t_e = (3 + 4(5) + 13)/6 = (3+20+13)/6 = 36/6 = 6$ weeks Activity C: $t_e = (1 + 4(2) + 3)/6 = (1+8+3)/6 = 12/6 = 2$ weeks Activity D: $t_e = (2 + 4(4) + 6)/6 = (2+16+6)/6 = 24/6 = 4$ weeks Activity E: $t_e = (3 + 4(5) + 7)/6 = (3+20+7)/6 = 30/6 = 5$ weeks Activity F: $t_e = (2 + 4(3) + 4)/6 = (2+12+4)/6 = 18/6 = 3$ weeks **Step 2: Calculate Variance $\sigma^2$ for each activity** Activity A: $\sigma^2 = ((6-2)/6)^2 = (4/6)^2 = 0.444$ Activity B: $\sigma^2 = ((13-3)/6)^2 = (10/6)^2 = 2.778$ Activity C: $\sigma^2 = ((3-1)/6)^2 = (2/6)^2 = 0.111$ Activity D: $\sigma^2 = ((6-2)/6)^2 = (4/6)^2 = 0.444$ Activity E: $\sigma^2 = ((7-3)/6)^2 = (4/6)^2 = 0.444$ Activity F: $\sigma^2 = ((4-2)/6)^2 = (2/6)^2 = 0.111$ **Step 3: Build Network with $t_e$ and Find Paths** | Path | Activities | Total Duration | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | A → B → D → F | 4 + 6 + 4 + 3 = **17 weeks** | | 2 | A → C → E → F | 4 + 2 + 5 + 3 = 14 weeks | **Critical Path: A → B → D → F with duration 17 weeks** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 4: Sum variances of critical path** $$\sigma_{cp}^{2} = \sigma_A^2 + \sigma_B^2 + \sigma_D^2 + \sigma_F^2$$ $$\sigma_{cp}^{2} = 0.444 + 2.778 + 0.444 + 0.111 = 3.777$$ $$\sigma_{cp} = \sqrt{3.777} = 1.943 \text{ weeks}$$ **Step 5: Calculate Z-Score** Given $T_s = 18$ weeks and $T_e = 17$ weeks: $$Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{cp}} = \dfrac{18 - 17}{1.943} = \dfrac{1}{1.943} = 0.515$$ **Step 6: Look up probability from Z table** For $Z = 0.515$, the cumulative probability from standard normal table is: $$P(Z \leq 0.515) \approx 0.6968 \text{ or } 69.68\%$$ **Answer: The probability of completing the project within 18 weeks is approximately 69.68%.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to divide by 6** in the PERT formula (students often write $a + 4m + b$ as the answer instead of dividing by 6). **Loss: 1-2 marks.** > 2. **Using variance formula incorrectly** — variance is $\left(\frac{b-a}{6}\right)^2$, NOT $\frac{b-a}{6}$. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 3. **Summing variances only on the critical path** — students sometimes sum variances of all activities. **Loss: 2 marks.** > 4. **Forgetting to take square root** before calculating Z-score. **Loss: 1 mark.** > 5. **Multiple critical paths in PERT** — if there are multiple critical paths, take the one with **higher variance** for a conservative probability estimate. **Loss: 2 marks.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE** - **Project Scheduling** converts the project plan into a time-phased operating chart; it directly affects NPV and cash flow. - **WBS** is the hierarchical decomposition; scheduling operates at the **work package level** (typically 8/80 rule — between 8 hours and 80 hours of work). - **Four Dependency Types:** FS (most common), SS, FF, SF. Default in most software is **FS with lag/lead**. - **CPM** uses a **single deterministic** time estimate — suited for construction and repetitive projects. - **PERT** uses **three estimates** $a, m, b$ with weights 1:4:1, giving $t_e = (a + 4m + b)/6$. - **Variance Formula:** $\sigma^2 = ((b-a)/6)^2$ — only summed for activities **on the critical path**. - **Critical Path** = longest path in network = minimum project duration. - **Total Float** $TF = LST - EST = LFT - EFT$. Critical activities have $TF = 0$. - **Free Float** is the time an activity can be delayed without delaying **any successor** (more restrictive than TF). - **Crashing Rule:** Crash the critical path activity with the **lowest cost slope** first, then recompute the network. - **Cost Slope** = (Crash Cost − Normal Cost) / (Normal Time − Crash Time), measured in ₹/day. - **Gantt Charts** are bar charts excellent for **status reporting**; they do not show dependencies explicitly (use linked Gantt in MS Project). - **Z-Score Formula** $Z = (T_s - T_e)/\sigma_{cp}$ — converts project time to a probability via standard normal tables. - **Multiple Critical Paths:** If two paths have the same duration, the project has **two critical paths** — both must be considered for crashing and variance calculation. - **Dummy Activity:** Used only in AOA networks to maintain logical dependencies without consuming time. - **Lead and Lag:** Lead allows an activity to start before its predecessor finishes (negative lag); Lag is a waiting period (positive lag). - **Schedule Compression Methods:** (1) **Crashing** (add resources, increase cost), (2) **Fast-tracking** (parallel execution, increase risk). - **Resource Leveling** smooths resource demand by delaying non-critical activities; may extend project duration. - **Resource Smoothing** maintains the critical path while smoothing resource usage. - **Milestones** have zero duration and represent significant checkpoints — they are NOT activities. - **PDM (Precedence Diagramming Method)** is the modern standard for AON networks in software like MS Project and Primavera P6. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Tip:** Always draw the network diagram cleanly, label forward pass values on the **top-left** of each node, and backward pass values on the **top-right**. Examiners reward neatness with partial marks even if minor computational errors occur.

topics.title: Introduction to PERT and CPM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to PERT and CPM # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique)** is a *probabilistic* project management tool developed in **1958** by the U.S. Navy (Booz, Allen & Hamilton) for the Polaris missile project. It models activities using **three-time estimates** to handle uncertainty, making it ideal for **research, development, and non-repetitive engineering projects**. **CPM (Critical Path Method)** is a *deterministic* project management technique developed in **1957** by DuPont and Remington Rand for chemical plant maintenance. It uses a **single time estimate** per activity and is best suited for **repetitive, well-defined construction and manufacturing projects**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** > PERT/CPM are network-based project scheduling techniques used to plan, schedule, coordinate, and control the various activities of a project. They help identify the **critical path** — the longest sequence of dependent tasks that determines the minimum project duration. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ### 🏠 Real-World Analogy: Building a House Imagine you are an architect building a house. Before laying a single brick, you must figure out: - **What tasks must happen first?** (e.g., you cannot paint walls before they are built). - **What is the longest chain of dependent tasks?** (the path that decides the completion date). - **Which tasks can be delayed without affecting the project?** (those that have "slack"). PERT is like a **weather forecast for your project** — it accepts that construction of the foundation might take anywhere from 5 to 15 days, with a most likely estimate of 8 days, and calculates a *probable* completion date. CPM is like a **fixed train timetable** — you know with certainty that pouring concrete takes exactly 3 days, so you plan rigidly around that. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction Students Must Memorize:** > - **PERT = Probabilistic = Three estimates (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) = R&D projects** > - **CPM = Deterministic = One estimate = Construction/repetitive projects** > - **Modern software (MS Project, Primavera) merges both → "PERT/CPM"** --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | **Optimistic Time** | $t_o$ | days/weeks | Shortest possible activity duration | | **Most Likely Time** | $t_m$ | days/weeks | Duration under normal working conditions | | **Pessimistic Time** | $t_p$ | days/weeks | Worst-case activity duration (excluding catastrophes) | | **Expected Time** | $t_e$ | days/weeks | Weighted average duration | | **Variance** | $\sigma^2$ | (days)² | Measure of uncertainty | | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma$ | days | Square root of variance | | **Earliest Start (ES)** | $ES$ | days | Earliest an activity can begin | | **Earliest Finish (EF)** | $EF$ | days | Earliest an activity can end | | **Latest Start (LS)** | $LS$ | days | Latest an activity can begin without delay | | **Latest Finish (LF)** | $LF$ | days | Latest an activity can end without delay | | **Total Float / Slack** | $TF$ | days | Time an activity can be delayed | | **Free Float** | $FF$ | days | Time an activity can be delayed without affecting successors | | **Critical Path** | $CP$ | — | Longest path; zero float activities | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The minimum project duration is mathematically equal to the **length of the critical path**. Any delay on a critical activity **delays the entire project**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown — The "Why & How" ### Step 1: Activity Decomposition (Work Breakdown Structure) Every project is broken into **activities (jobs/tasks)**. An activity consumes time and resources but produces no output that advances the project until completed. **Why?** Large projects are unmanageable as a single unit. Decomposition allows estimation, assignment, and tracking. ### Step 2: Establishing Logical Dependencies Determine which activity must precede another. This produces a **precedence network**. **How?** Use four logical relationships: - **Finish-to-Start (FS):** B cannot start until A finishes (most common). - **Start-to-Start (SS):** B cannot start until A starts. - **Finish-to-Finish (FF):** B cannot finish until A finishes. - **Start-to-Finish (SF):** B cannot finish until A starts (rare). ### Step 3: Network Construction (AOA or AON) Two drawing conventions exist: - **AOA (Activity on Arrow / Arrow Network):** Arrows = activities, Nodes = events/milestones. Requires **dummy activities** for parallel dependencies. - **AON (Activity on Node / Precedence Diagram):** Nodes = activities, Arrows = dependencies. **No dummy activities** required. Used by modern software. ### Step 4: Time Estimation - **For CPM:** Single deterministic time $t$. - **For PERT:** Three estimates → compute expected time. ### Step 5: Forward Pass (Calculate ES and EF) Starting from the project start (ES = 0), propagate forward: $$EF_i = ES_i + t_e$$ $$ES_j = \max(EF_i) \text{ for all predecessor activities } i$$ ### Step 6: Backward Pass (Calculate LS and LF) Starting from project end (LF = project duration for terminal activities), propagate backward: $$LS_i = LF_i - t_e$$ $$LF_i = \min(LS_j) \text{ for all successor activities } j$$ ### Step 7: Identify Critical Path Activities where **ES = LS and EF = LF (i.e., Total Float = 0)** are **critical**. The chain of critical activities from start to end forms the **critical path**. ### Step 8: Compute Floats $$TF_i = LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i$$ $$FF_i = \min(ES_{successors}) - EF_i$$ > [!NOTE] > **Float Interpretation:** A positive float means the activity is **non-critical** and can be delayed by that many days without delaying the project. A **zero float** means the activity is **critical**. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables | Unit | When to Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Expected Time (PERT)** | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | $t_o, t_m, t_p$ | days | PERT three-estimate problems | | 2 | **Variance (PERT)** | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ | $t_o, t_p$ | (days)² | Risk/uncertainty measurement | | 3 | **Standard Deviation** | $\sigma = \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}$ | $t_o, t_p$ | days | Project completion probability | | 4 | **Project Variance (Σ)** | $\sigma_{proj}^2 = \sum_{CP} \sigma_i^2$ | Sum of variances on CP | (days)² | Total project risk | | 5 | **Project Std Deviation** | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{proj}}$ | $T_s$ = scheduled, $T_e$ = expected | — | Z-score for probability lookup | | 6 | **Earliest Finish** | $EF_i = ES_i + t_e$ | — | days | Forward pass | | 7 | **Earliest Start** | $ES_j = \max(EF_{predecessors})$ | — | days | Forward pass | | 8 | **Latest Start** | $LS_i = LF_i - t_e$ | — | days | Backward pass | | 9 | **Latest Finish** | $LF_i = \min(LS_{successors})$ | — | days | Backward pass | | 10 | **Total Float** | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | — | days | Identify critical activities | | 11 | **Free Float** | $FF_i = ES_{successor} - EF_i$ | — | days | Delay without affecting successors | | 12 | **Crash Cost Slope** | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c}$ | $C_c$ = crash cost, $C_n$ = normal cost | ₹/day | Crashing analysis | | 13 | **Cost Slope Total** | $C(T) = \sum C_{normal} + \sum (\text{Slope}_i \times \Delta t_i)$ | — | ₹ | Minimum cost duration | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL FORMULA ALERT:** For KTU board exams, the formula $t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ is the **beta-distribution mean** and appears in nearly every PERT numerical. Memorize it in this exact form. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Software Development:** PERT/CPM schedules Agile sprints, identifies bottleneck features. - **Civil Engineering:** Schedules multi-story construction, highway laying, bridge building. - **Aerospace:** NASA uses PERT for mission planning (Apollo, Mars rovers). - **Manufacturing:** Plans assembly-line changeovers and product launches. - **IT Infrastructure:** Schedules data-center migrations and ERP rollouts. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the PERT Expected Time Formula The PERT expected time is derived from the **Beta Probability Distribution**, which is a continuous distribution bounded between $t_o$ and $t_p$. ### Assumptions: 1. The activity duration follows a Beta distribution. 2. $t_o$ and $t_p$ are the lower and upper bounds of the distribution. 3. $t_m$ is the mode (most likely value), not the mean. ### Derivation of the Mean of a Beta Distribution: The general Beta distribution PDF with parameters $\alpha, \beta > 0$ is: $$ f(t) = \frac{1}{B(\alpha, \beta)} (t - t_o)^{\alpha - 1} (t_p - t)^{\beta - 1} $$ where $B(\alpha, \beta) = \frac{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}$ is the Beta function. The **mean** of a Beta distribution in the interval $[a, b]$ is: $$ \mu = a + \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}(b - a) $$ The **mode** of a Beta distribution is: $$ \text{Mode} = a + \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2}(b - a) $$ **Step 1:** Set $a = t_o$, $b = t_p$. **Step 2:** Equate the mode to $t_m$: $$ t_m = t_o + \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2}(t_p - t_o) $$ **Step 3:** PERT assumes the distribution is **symmetric**, hence $\alpha - 1 = \beta - 1$, giving $\alpha = \beta$. **Step 4:** Substitute $\beta = \alpha$ into the mode equation: $$ t_m = t_o + \frac{\alpha - 1}{2\alpha - 2}(t_p - t_o) = t_o + \frac{1}{2}(t_p - t_o) $$ Wait — this gives $t_m = \frac{t_o + t_p}{2}$, which is **incorrect**. The standard PERT assumption is different. **Step 5 (Correct PERT Derivation):** PERT does NOT use the symmetric beta. Instead, it uses the empirical relationship where the **mean is approximated as a weighted average** of $t_o$, $t_m$, $t_p$ with weights 1, 4, 1 respectively. This weighted average is the **expected time**. Mathematically, the heuristic mean is: $$ t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6} $$ **Justification (1:4:1 ratio):** - The weight 4 on $t_m$ reflects that it is the **most frequently observed** duration. - The weights 1 on $t_o$ and $t_p$ reflect that these are **extreme (rare) values**. - Total weight = $1 + 4 + 1 = 6$ (denominator). **Verification using Beta distribution:** If we assume $\beta = \alpha$ and the mode equals the mean (approximation), then: $$ t_e = t_o + \frac{\alpha}{2\alpha}(t_p - t_o) = \frac{t_o + t_p}{2} $$ This is the simple average. The **empirical 1:4:1 weighting** gives a better fit to real project data (per Malcolm et al., 1959). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** The 1:4:1 weighting is **empirical**, not derived from pure mathematics. The exam may ask you to "state and justify" the formula — mention Malcolm's 1959 study. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Variance Formula The variance of a Beta distribution is: $$ \sigma^2 = \frac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha + \beta)^2 (\alpha + \beta + 1)} (b - a)^2 $$ **Step 1:** Assume $\alpha = \beta$ (symmetric distribution). **Step 2:** Substitute $\alpha = \beta$: $$ \sigma^2 = \frac{\alpha^2}{(2\alpha)^2 (2\alpha + 1)} (t_p - t_o)^2 = \frac{1}{4(2\alpha + 1)} (t_p - t_o)^2 $$ **Step 3:** As $\alpha \to \infty$ (the distribution concentrates around the mean), the variance becomes very small. PERT uses a **simpler empirical rule** based on the assumption that **99.7% of the distribution lies within $\pm 3\sigma$** (3-sigma rule, empirical). **Step 4:** Set $t_p - t_o = 6\sigma$ (the range covers 6 standard deviations): $$ \sigma = \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} $$ **Step 5:** Square to get variance: $$ \sigma^2 = \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 6-sigma rule is an assumption** — it states that the optimistic and pessimistic times are located at 3 standard deviations from the mean on either side. The range $t_p - t_o$ therefore equals $6\sigma$. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Complete Forward & Backward Pass **Project Data (AON Network):** | Activity | Predecessor | $t_e$ (days) | |---|---|---| | A | — | 4 | | B | A | 6 | | C | A | 3 | | D | B | 5 | | E | B, C | 7 | | F | C | 4 | | G | D, E | 6 | | H | E, F | 5 | | End | G, H | — | ### Step 1: Forward Pass (Calculate ES and EF) For **Activity A** (start node): $$ ES_A = 0, \quad EF_A = 0 + 4 = 4 $$ For **Activity B** (after A): $$ ES_B = EF_A = 4, \quad EF_B = 4 + 6 = 10 $$ For **Activity C** (after A): $$ ES_C = EF_A = 4, \quad EF_C = 4 + 3 = 7 $$ For **Activity D** (after B): $$ ES_D = EF_B = 10, \quad EF_D = 10 + 5 = 15 $$ For **Activity E** (after B and C — take max): $$ ES_E = \max(EF_B, EF_C) = \max(10, 7) = 10, \quad EF_E = 10 + 7 = 17 $$ For **Activity F** (after C): $$ ES_F = EF_C = 7, \quad EF_F = 7 + 4 = 11 $$ For **Activity G** (after D and E — take max): $$ ES_G = \max(EF_D, EF_E) = \max(15, 17) = 17, \quad EF_G = 17 + 6 = 23 $$ For **Activity H** (after E and F — take max): $$ ES_H = \max(EF_E, EF_F) = \max(17, 11) = 17, \quad EF_H = 17 + 5 = 22 $$ **Project Completion Time** = $\max(EF_G, EF_H) = \max(23, 22) = \mathbf{23}$ **days**. ### Step 2: Backward Pass (Calculate LS and LF) For terminal activities G and H, set $LF = 23$ (project duration): For **Activity G**: $$ LF_G = 23, \quad LS_G = 23 - 6 = 17 $$ For **Activity H**: $$ LF_H = 23, \quad LS_H = 23 - 5 = 18 $$ For **Activity E** (successors G, H — take min of LS): $$ LF_E = \min(LS_G, LS_H) = \min(17, 18) = 17, \quad LS_E = 17 - 7 = 10 $$ For **Activity D** (successor G only): $$ LF_D = LS_G = 17, \quad LS_D = 17 - 5 = 12 $$ For **Activity F** (successor H only): $$ LF_F = LS_H = 18, \quad LS_F = 18 - 4 = 14 $$ For **Activity B** (successors D, E — take min of LS): $$ LF_B = \min(LS_D, LS_E) = \min(12, 10) = 10, \quad LS_B = 10 - 6 = 4 $$ For **Activity C** (successors E, F — take min of LS): $$ LF_C = \min(LS_E, LS_F) = \min(10, 14) = 10, \quad LS_C = 10 - 3 = 7 $$ For **Activity A** (successors B, C — take min of LS): $$ LF_A = \min(LS_B, LS_C) = \min(4, 7) = 4, \quad LS_A = 4 - 4 = 0 $$ ### Step 3: Identify Critical Path Compute $TF = LS - ES$: | Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | ✅ | | B | 4 | 10 | 4 | 10 | **0** | ✅ | | C | 4 | 7 | 7 | 10 | 3 | ❌ | | D | 10 | 15 | 12 | 17 | 2 | ❌ | | E | 10 | 17 | 10 | 17 | **0** | ✅ | | F | 7 | 11 | 14 | 18 | 7 | ❌ | | G | 17 | 23 | 17 | 23 | **0** | ✅ | | H | 17 | 22 | 18 | 23 | 1 | ❌ | **Critical Path: A → B → E → G** with duration **23 days**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PERT/CPM Network Diagram (AON Format) ```mermaid graph LR Start([Project Start]):::startNode --> ActA["A
4 days"] ActA --> ActB["B
6 days"] ActA --> ActC["C
3 days"] ActB --> ActD["D
5 days"] ActB --> ActE["E
7 days"] ActC --> ActE ActC --> ActF["F
4 days"] ActD --> ActG["G
6 days"] ActE --> ActG ActE --> ActH["H
5 days"] ActF --> ActH ActG --> Finish([Project End 23 days]):::endNode ActH --> Finish classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef endNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef critical fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 class ActA,ActB,ActE,ActG critical ``` ## 4.2 PERT/CPM Methodology Workflow (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Step 1: Project Definition
and Scope Statement"]:::stepBox B["Step 2: Work Breakdown
Structure WBS"]:::stepBox C["Step 3: Activity Listing
and Sequencing"]:::stepBox D["Step 4: Network Construction
AOA or AON"]:::stepBox E["Step 5: Time Estimation
CPM single or PERT triple"]:::stepBox F["Step 6: Forward Pass
Compute ES and EF"]:::stepBox G["Step 7: Backward Pass
Compute LS and LF"]:::stepBox H["Step 8: Identify Critical Path
Float equals zero"]:::stepBox I["Step 9: Resource Leveling
and Cost Analysis"]:::stepBox J["Step 10: Schedule Monitoring
and Control"]:::stepBox A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J classDef stepBox fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Difference Matrix: PERT vs CPM (Visual) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph PERT["PERT Branch"] P1["Three Time Estimates"]:::pert P2["Probabilistic Model"]:::pert P3["Beta Distribution"]:::pert P4["Research and Development"]:::pert P5["Event Oriented"]:::pert end subgraph CPM["CPM Branch"] C1["Single Time Estimate"]:::cpm C2["Deterministic Model"]:::cpm C3["Normal Distribution"]:::cpm C4["Construction Projects"]:::cpm C5["Activity Oriented"]:::cpm end P1 --- C1 P2 --- C2 P3 --- C3 P4 --- C4 P5 --- C5 classDef pert fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cpm fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Float Calculation Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Activity i
ES_i, EF_i, LS_i, LF_i"]:::activity B["Total Float
TF = LS - ES
= LF - EF"]:::calc C["Free Float
FF = ES_successor
minus EF_i"]:::calc D["Independent Float
IF = TF minus FF
of predecessor"]:::calc E{"Float = 0?"}:::decision F["Critical Activity
on Critical Path"]:::critical G["Non-Critical
Has Slack Time"]:::noncritical A --> B A --> C A --> D B --> E E -->|Yes| F E -->|No| G classDef activity fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef calc fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef critical fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef noncritical fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Define PERT and CPM. List any four differences between them.** **Model Answer:** **PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique):** A probabilistic project management technique that uses three-time estimates (optimistic, most likely, pessimistic) to compute expected activity durations. It is event-oriented and used primarily for research and development projects. **CPM (Critical Path Method):** A deterministic project management technique that uses a single-time estimate for each activity. It is activity-oriented and used primarily for well-defined, repetitive projects like construction. | # | PERT | CPM | |---|---|---| | 1 | Probabilistic (three estimates) | Deterministic (one estimate) | | 2 | Used for R&D, non-repetitive work | Used for construction, repetitive work | | 3 | Event-oriented (AOA) | Activity-oriented (AON) | | 4 | Time-focused | Time and cost-focused (crashing) | | 5 | Beta distribution | Normal/step distribution | | 6 | Developed 1958, U.S. Navy | Developed 1957, DuPont | --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **What is the critical path? How is it identified in a network?** **Model Answer:** The **Critical Path** is the longest sequence of activities in a project network that determines the minimum time required to complete the project. It consists of activities with **zero total float (TF = 0)**. **Identification Steps:** 1. Perform a **forward pass** to compute ES and EF for all activities. 2. Perform a **backward pass** to compute LS and LF for all activities. 3. Compute **Total Float** for each activity: $TF = LS - ES = LF - EF$. 4. The path from project start to end passing through all activities with $TF = 0$ is the critical path. 5. **Project duration** = Sum of expected times of all activities on the critical path. > [!NOTE] > **Significance:** Any delay in a critical activity delays the entire project. Management focus should be on critical activities to control the project schedule. --- ## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` A software development project consists of the following activities: | Activity | Predecessors | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | A | — | 2 | 4 | 6 | | B | A | 3 | 5 | 7 | | C | A | 1 | 3 | 5 | | D | B | 4 | 6 | 8 | | E | B, C | 2 | 3 | 10 | | F | C | 3 | 5 | 7 | | G | D, E | 5 | 8 | 11 | | H | E, F | 4 | 6 | 8 | #### **(a) Construct the network and compute the expected time and variance for each activity.** [7 Marks] #### **(b) Find the critical path, project duration, and probability of completing the project in 25 days.** [7 Marks] --- #### 📝 Model Solution to Question A ### Part (a): Expected Time and Variance **Step 1:** Apply the formulas: $$ t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}, \quad \sigma^2 = \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2 $$ **Activity A:** $$ t_e = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days} $$ $$ \sigma^2_A = \left(\frac{6 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.444 $$ **Activity B:** $$ t_e = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days} $$ $$ \sigma^2_B = \left(\frac{7 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{36} = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.444 $$ **Activity C:** $$ t_e = \frac{1 + 4(3) + 5}{6} = \frac{1 + 12 + 5}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days} $$ $$ \sigma^2_C = \left(\frac{5 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.444 $$ **Activity D:** $$ t_e = \frac{4 + 4(6) + 8}{6} = \frac{4 + 24 + 8}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days} $$ $$ \sigma^2_D = \left(\frac{8 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.444 $$ **Activity E:** $$ t_e = \frac{2 + 4(3) + 10}{6} = \frac{2 + 12 + 10}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ days} $$ $$ \sigma^2_E = \left(\frac{10 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{9} \approx 1.778 $$ **Activity F:** $$ t_e = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days} $$ $$ \sigma^2_F = \left(\frac{7 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.444 $$ **Activity G:** $$ t_e = \frac{5 + 4(8) + 11}{6} = \frac{5 + 32 + 11}{6} = \frac{48}{6} = 8 \text{ days} $$ $$ \sigma^2_G = \left(\frac{11 - 5}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{6}{6}\right)^2 = 1^2 = 1.000 $$ **Activity H:** $$ t_e = \frac{4 + 4(6) + 8}{6} = \frac{4 + 24 + 8}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6 \text{ days} $$ $$ \sigma^2_H = \left(\frac{8 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{9} \approx 0.444 $$ **Summary Table:** | Activity | $t_e$ | $\sigma^2$ | |---|---|---| | A | 4 | 0.444 | | B | 5 | 0.444 | | C | 3 | 0.444 | | D | 6 | 0.444 | | E | 4 | 1.778 | | F | 5 | 0.444 | | G | 8 | 1.000 | | H | 6 | 0.444 | --- ### Part (b): Critical Path, Project Duration, and Probability **Step 1: Network Construction (AON)** — Refer to the network diagram in Section 4.1. **Step 2: Forward Pass (ES, EF):** | Activity | ES | EF = ES + $t_e$ | |---|---|---| | A | 0 | 4 | | B | EF_A = 4 | 4 + 5 = 9 | | C | EF_A = 4 | 4 + 3 = 7 | | D | EF_B = 9 | 9 + 6 = 15 | | E | max(EF_B, EF_C) = max(9,7) = 9 | 9 + 4 = 13 | | F | EF_C = 7 | 7 + 5 = 12 | | G | max(EF_D, EF_E) = max(15,13) = 15 | 15 + 8 = 23 | | H | max(EF_E, EF_F) = max(13,12) = 13 | 13 + 6 = 19 | **Project Duration (T_e) = max(EF_G, EF_H) = max(23, 19) = 23 days** **Step 3: Backward Pass (LS, LF):** | Activity | LF | LS = LF - $t_e$ | |---|---|---| | G | 23 | 23 - 8 = 15 | | H | 23 | 23 - 6 = 17 | | D | LS_G = 15 | 15 - 6 = 9 | | E | min(LS_G, LS_H) = min(15, 17) = 15 | 15 - 4 = 11 | | F | LS_H = 17 | 17 - 5 = 12 | | B | min(LS_D, LS_E) = min(9, 11) = 9 | 9 - 5 = 4 | | C | min(LS_E, LS_F) = min(11, 12) = 11 | 11 - 3 = 8 | | A | min(LS_B, LS_C) = min(4, 8) = 4 | 4 - 4 = 0 | **Step 4: Critical Path Identification (TF = 0):** | Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** ✅ | | B | 4 | 9 | 4 | 9 | **0** ✅ | | C | 4 | 7 | 8 | 11 | 4 | | D | 9 | 15 | 9 | 15 | **0** ✅ | | E | 9 | 13 | 11 | 15 | 2 | | F | 7 | 12 | 12 | 17 | 5 | | G | 15 | 23 | 15 | 23 | **0** ✅ | | H | 13 | 19 | 17 | 23 | 4 | **Critical Path: A → B → D → G, Project Duration = 23 days** **Step 5: Probability of Completion in 25 Days** Project variance (sum of variances on critical path A, B, D, G): $$ \sigma^2_{proj} = \sigma^2_A + \sigma^2_B + \sigma^2_D + \sigma^2_G = 0.444 + 0.444 + 0.444 + 1.000 = 2.332 $$ Project standard deviation: $$ \sigma_{proj} = \sqrt{2.332} \approx 1.527 \text{ days} $$ Z-score for $T_s = 25$ days: $$ Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{proj}} = \frac{25 - 23}{1.527} = \frac{2}{1.527} \approx 1.31 $$ From standard normal table: $$ P(Z \leq 1.31) \approx 0.9049 \approx 90.49\% $$ **Probability of completing the project in 25 days ≈ 90.49%** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` A construction project has the following activities with normal and crash times and costs: | Activity | Predecessor | Normal Time | Crash Time | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Cost (₹) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | — | 7 | 5 | 5000 | 8000 | | B | A | 6 | 4 | 4000 | 7000 | | C | A | 5 | 3 | 3000 | 5000 | | D | B, C | 8 | 6 | 6000 | 9000 | | E | D | 4 | 2 | 2000 | 4000 | Indirect cost = **₹1000 per day**. #### **(a) Draw the network, find the normal duration, and identify the critical path.** [7 Marks] #### **(b) Find the optimum duration and minimum project cost using crashing.** [7 Marks] --- #### 📝 Model Solution to Question B ### Part (a): Network, Normal Duration, Critical Path **Network:** A → B → D → E and A → C → D → E (converging paths). **Forward Pass:** | Activity | ES | EF (ES + Normal Time) | |---|---|---| | A | 0 | 7 | | B | 7 | 13 | | C | 7 | 12 | | D | max(13, 12) = 13 | 21 | | E | 21 | 25 | **Project Duration (Normal) = 25 days** **Backward Pass:** | Activity | LF | LS | |---|---|---| | E | 25 | 21 | | D | LS_E = 21 | 21 - 8 = 13 | | B | LS_D = 13 | 13 - 6 = 7 | | C | LS_D = 13 | 13 - 5 = 8 | | A | min(7, 8) = 7 | 7 - 7 = 0 | **Float Values:** | Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | 0 | 7 | 0 | 7 | **0** | ✅ | | B | 7 | 13 | 7 | 13 | **0** | ✅ | | C | 7 | 12 | 8 | 13 | 1 | ❌ | | D | 13 | 21 | 13 | 21 | **0** | ✅ | | E | 21 | 25 | 21 | 25 | **0** | ✅ | **Critical Path: A → B → D → E, Duration = 25 days** --- ### Part (b): Crashing Analysis **Step 1: Compute Cost Slopes** $$ \text{Cost Slope} = \frac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c} $$ | Activity | Normal Time | Crash Time | Normal Cost | Crash Cost | Slope (₹/day) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | 7 | 5 | 5000 | 8000 | $\frac{8000-5000}{7-5} = 1500$ | | B | 6 | 4 | 4000 | 7000 | $\frac{7000-4000}{6-4} = 1500$ | | C | 5 | 3 | 3000 | 5000 | $\frac{5000-3000}{5-3} = 1000$ | | D | 8 | 6 | 6000 | 9000 | $\frac{9000-6000}{8-6} = 1500$ | | E | 4 | 2 | 2000 | 4000 | $\frac{4000-2000}{4-2} = 1000$ | **Step 2: Initial Direct Cost** = $5000 + 4000 + 3000 + 6000 + 2000 = $₹20,000 **Step 3: Iteration-wise Crashing** **Iteration 1:** Critical path = A-B-D-E. Crash the activity on the critical path with the **lowest cost slope that is less than indirect cost (₹1000/day)**. Among A (1500), B (1500), D (1500), E (1000): only **E (₹1000/day)** is ≤ indirect cost. Crash E by 1 day. - New duration: 24 days - E: 4 → 3 - New project duration = 24 days - Direct cost increase = ₹1000, Indirect cost savings = ₹1000 → **no net change** **Iteration 2:** E can still be crashed. Crash E by 1 more day. - E: 3 → 2 (fully crashed) - New duration = 23 days - E is now fully crashed. **Iteration 3:** All critical activities (A, B, D, E) have slopes ≥ ₹1000, but E is now fully crashed. E's slope effectively becomes **∞**. We must now compare against indirect cost. Activities A, B, D have slope = ₹1500 each. The cheapest on the critical path is A, B, or D (all equal). However, we must also consider whether crashing a parallel activity (C) could shorten the project. **Re-examine the network:** A-B-D-E and A-C-D-E. - Path 1 (A-B-D-E): 7+6+8+4 = 25 - Path 2 (A-C-D-E): 7+5+8+4 = 24 **After E is fully crashed (E=2):** - Path 1: 7+6+8+2 = 23 - Path 2: 7+5+8+2 = 22 **Project duration = 23 days** (Path 1 still critical). **Iteration 3:** Critical path = A-B-D. Lowest slope on CP is ₹1500 (A, B, or D tie). Crash A by 1 day (max crash = 2 days). - A: 7 → 6, slope = ₹1500/day - Path 1: 6+6+8+2 = 22 - Path 2: 6+5+8+2 = 21 - Project duration = 22 days - Direct cost: +₹1500, Indirect cost saved: ₹1000 → **net +₹500** (cost increases) **Iteration 4:** Critical path still A-B-D. Continue crashing A. - A: 6 → 5 (fully crashed) - Path 1: 5+6+8+2 = 21 - Path 2: 5+5+8+2 = 20 - Project duration = 21 days - A is now fully crashed. **Iteration 5:** Critical path = B-D (slope ₹1500 each). Crash B by 1 day. - B: 6 → 5 - Path 1: 5+5+8+2 = 20 - Path 2: 5+5+8+2 = 20 (both paths now equal) - Project duration = 20 days - Cost: +₹1500 - ₹1000 = +₹500 (cost increases) **Iteration 6:** Both paths critical. To reduce project further, must crash activities on **both paths simultaneously** OR a common activity (D). - D is on both paths. Crash D by 1 day. - D: 8 → 7, slope = ₹1500/day - Path 1: 5+5+7+2 = 19 - Path 2: 5+5+7+2 = 19 - Project duration = 19 days - Cost: +₹1500 - ₹1000 = +₹500 **Iteration 7:** Crash D by 1 more day. - D: 7 → 6 (fully crashed) - Project duration = 18 days - Cost: +₹1500 - ₹1000 = +₹500 **Iteration 8:** All critical activities fully crashed. Cannot reduce further. **Optimum Duration Analysis:** | Duration | Direct Cost | Indirect Cost | Total Cost | |---|---|---|---| | 25 (normal) | ₹20,000 | ₹25,000 | ₹45,000 | | 24 (E -1) | ₹20,000 | ₹24,000 | ₹44,000 | | 23 (E -2) | ₹20,000 | ₹23,000 | ₹43,000 | | 22 (A -1) | ₹21,500 | ₹22,000 | ₹43,500 | | 21 (A -2) | ₹23,000 | ₹21,000 | ₹44,000 | | 20 (B -1) | ₹24,500 | ₹20,000 | ₹44,500 | | 19 (D -1) | ₹26,000 | ₹19,000 | ₹45,000 | | 18 (D -2) | ₹27,500 | ₹18,000 | ₹45,500 | **Minimum Total Cost = ₹43,000 at Duration = 23 days** > [!NOTE] > **Decision Rule:** Crash the project as long as the activity's cost slope is **less than** the indirect cost per day. When all slopes exceed indirect cost, the optimum is reached. **Optimum Project Duration = 23 days, Minimum Total Cost = ₹43,000** --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **TOP 5 MARK-LOSS MISTAKES IN PERT/CPM QUESTIONS:** > > **1. Forward Pass ES Calculation Error (–2 marks):** Students often forget to take the **MAX** of EF of all predecessors. When an activity has multiple predecessors, ES = MAX(EF of all predecessors), not just the first one. > > **2. Backward Pass LF Calculation Error (–2 marks):** Similarly, LF = **MIN** of LS of all successors. Confusing MIN with MAX here is the most common error. > > **3. Wrong PERT Formula (–3 marks):** Using $t_e = \frac{t_o + t_m + t_p}{3}$ (simple average) instead of the correct $\frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$. The 1:4:1 weighting is **mandatory**. > > **4. Probability Lookup Mistakes (–2 marks):** Computing Z-score correctly but reading the wrong value from the standard normal table. For $Z = 1.31$, the value is approximately **0.9049** (NOT 0.95, NOT 0.90). > > **5. Crashing Direction Error (–3 marks):** When parallel paths exist, students fail to check whether a non-critical activity becomes critical after crashing. **Always recompute the critical path after each iteration.** > > **6. Variance Summation Mistake (–2 marks):** Summing variances of ALL activities instead of only those on the **critical path**. Project variance = Σ(variances of activities on critical path). > > **7. Network Diagram Errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Drawing dummy activities incorrectly in AOA. Forgetting to add a dummy when two activities share a common successor but only one needs the dependency. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — INTRODUCTION TO PERT AND CPM** ### 🎯 Core Concepts to Memorize - **PERT** = **P**robabilistic, **E**vent-oriented, **R**esearch projects, **T**hree estimates. - **CPM** = **C**ertain, **P**lan-based, **M**anufacturing/construction, One estimate. - **Critical Path** = Longest path; determines minimum project duration. - **Float** = Slack time = $LS - ES = LF - EF$. - **Critical activities** always have **TF = 0**. ### 📊 Formulas (Non-Negotiable) - PERT Expected Time: $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ - PERT Variance: $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ - Forward Pass: $EF = ES + t_e$, $ES_j = \max(EF_{predecessors})$ - Backward Pass: $LS = LF - t_e$, $LF_i = \min(LS_{successors})$ - Project Variance: $\sigma_{proj}^2 = \sum_{CP} \sigma_i^2$ - Probability Z-score: $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{proj}}$ - Crash Cost Slope: $\dfrac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c}$ ### ⚙️ Network Rules - **AON (Precedence):** Nodes = Activities, Arrows = Dependencies. Preferred for modern software. - **AOA (Arrow):** Arrows = Activities, Nodes = Events. Requires **dummy activities** for logical clarity. - All networks must be **acyclic** (no loops). - Use **dummy activities** when AOA needs to represent two parallel activities sharing only a partial dependency. ### 🎲 Probability & Statistics Connection - PERT uses **Beta distribution**; CPM assumes deterministic times. - For project completion probability, use the **Standard Normal (Z) Table**. - Larger $\sigma_{proj}$ → higher uncertainty → wider probability spread. ### 💰 Crashing Decision Rule - **Crash** an activity if its cost slope is **less than** indirect cost per day. - Stop crashing when all critical activity slopes ≥ indirect cost. - After each crash, **recompute the critical path** — it may change. - When two paths are equally critical, consider crashing a **common activity** (efficiency) or crashing one activity on each path (costlier). ### 🧠 Common Exam Traps - "Activity with minimum float" vs "Activity with zero float" → minimum float = 0 for critical. - Don't confuse **Total Float (TF)** with **Free Float (FF)**. - $t_e$ is computed per activity; **project duration** is computed across the critical path. - When $t_o = t_m = t_p$ for all activities, PERT reduces to CPM. ### ✅ Quick Self-Test (5-Second Check) 1. Can you state the 1:4:1 formula and the 6-sigma rule? ✅ 2. Can you perform a forward and backward pass on a 6-activity network? ✅ 3. Can you identify the critical path from float values? ✅ 4. Can you compute Z-score and read the normal table? ✅ 5. Can you decide when to stop crashing a project? ✅ > **Master these and you will comfortably score 90%+ on any PERT/CPM problem in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination.**

topics.title: Critical Path Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Critical Path Calculation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In **IT Project Management**, the **Critical Path** is defined as the longest sequence of dependent activities in a project network diagram that determines the **minimum possible project duration**. It is the continuous chain of activities with **zero Total Float (TF = 0)**, meaning any delay in any of these activities will directly delay the entire project's completion. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Standard):** The Critical Path Method (CPM) is a deterministic project modeling technique used to identify the sequence of activities that represent the longest path through a project network, thereby establishing the shortest time within which the project can be completed. The three foundational pillars of Critical Path Calculation are: 1. **Forward Pass** — Computing the Earliest Start (ES) and Earliest Finish (EF) of every activity. 2. **Backward Pass** — Computing the Latest Start (LS) and Latest Finish (LF) of every activity. 3. **Float Analysis** — Computing Total Float (TF), Free Float (FF), and identifying the **zero-float chain**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Road Trip Analogy (KTU-Favourite Intuition):** > Imagine you are planning a road trip from **Kerala to Delhi**. You have a map with several routes that merge and split. Some segments are *toll highways* (fast) and some are *state roads* (slow). The **Critical Path** is the *slowest, longest possible route* that bottlenecks your journey. Even if all other roads are super fast, you cannot arrive in Delhi faster than the slowest bottleneck allows. In project terms, the **Critical Path = the chain of tasks that bottlenecks the entire project timeline**. A real IT example: *Before launching a software product, you must (1) code, (2) test, (3) deploy. The "code" phase is the bottleneck because testing and deployment cannot start until coding finishes. Hence, "code" lies on the critical path.* ## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Symbol | Plain Meaning | |------|--------|---------------| | Early Start | **ES** | The earliest time an activity can begin, after all its predecessors are done. | | Early Finish | **EF** | Earliest Start + Duration. | | Late Finish | **LF** | The latest time an activity can finish without delaying the project. | | Late Start | **LS** | Late Finish − Duration. | | Total Float | **TF** | How much an activity can be delayed without delaying the project. | | Free Float | **FF** | How much an activity can be delayed without delaying any successor. | > [!NOTE] > **Golden Rule of CPM:** *If TF = 0, the activity is on the Critical Path.* This is the single most-tested fact in KTU 2024 ESE papers for Module 1. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the CPM Engine The Critical Path Calculation engine operates in **three distinct phases**. Let us unpack each one with absolute clarity. ### Phase 1 — The Forward Pass (Left → Right traversal) - **Step 1:** For activities with **no predecessors (start nodes)**, set: $$\text{ES} = 0$$ - **Step 2:** For any other activity, **ES = maximum EF of all its immediate predecessors**. This enforces the logical dependency rule — you cannot start until the slowest predecessor is done. - **Step 3:** Compute **EF** for every activity using the master equation: $$\text{EF} = \text{ES} + \text{Duration}$$ - **Step 4:** The project completion time is the **maximum EF** of all terminal (end) activities. $$\text{Project Duration } (T_p) = \max(\text{EF}_{\text{terminal}})$$ ### Phase 2 — The Backward Pass (Right → Left traversal) - **Step 1:** For activities with **no successors (end nodes)**, set: $$\text{LF} = T_p$$ - **Step 2:** For any other activity, **LF = minimum LS of all its immediate successors**. This means an activity must finish just in time for the earliest-pressing successor. - **Step 3:** Compute **LS** using the master equation: $$\text{LS} = \text{LF} - \text{Duration}$$ ### Phase 3 — Float Analysis (Pathological Examination) - **Total Float (TF)** measures delay tolerance without delaying the project deadline. $$\text{TF} = \text{LS} - \text{ES} = \text{LF} - \text{EF}$$ - **Free Float (FF)** measures delay tolerance without delaying any direct successor's earliest start. $$\text{FF} = \min(\text{ES}_{\text{successors}}) - \text{EF}_{\text{current}}$$ - **Critical Path Identification:** Mark all activities where **TF = 0**. Connect them in chronological order. The chain forms the **Critical Path**, and its length equals $T_p$. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready) | # | Formula Name | Equation | Engineering Use | |---|--------------|----------|-----------------| | 1 | Early Start | $\text{ES}_i = \max(\text{EF}_{\text{predecessors}})$ | Computes earliest possible start time. | | 2 | Early Finish | $\text{EF}_i = \text{ES}_i + D_i$ | Adds task duration. | | 3 | Late Finish | $\text{LF}_i = \min(\text{LS}_{\text{successors}})$ | Tightest allowed finish. | | 4 | Late Start | $\text{LS}_i = \text{LF}_i - D_i$ | Computes latest start. | | 5 | Total Float | $\text{TF}_i = \text{LS}_i - \text{ES}_i$ | Project-level delay tolerance. | | 6 | Free Float | $\text{FF}_i = \min(\text{ES}_{\text{successors}}) - \text{EF}_i$ | Successor-level delay tolerance. | | 7 | Project Duration | $T_p = \max(\text{EF}_{\text{terminal}})$ | Minimum project finish time. | | 8 | Critical Path | $\{\text{Activities} \mid \text{TF} = 0\}$ | Bottleneck identification. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table Integrity Note (KTU Compliance):** All absolute-value or modulus bars in formulas are rendered using LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` to ensure no markdown table syntax breakage. **Never use the raw pipe symbol `|` inside a table cell.** ## 2.3 Why CPM Matters in Real IT Engineering - **Project Time Optimization:** Helps Project Managers (PMs) identify where to push resources. - **Risk Management:** Critical activities demand zero-tolerance monitoring. - **Resource Leveling:** Non-critical activities (high TF) can be deferred or re-allocated. - **Schedule Compression:** Crashing or fast-tracking decisions rely on critical path awareness. - **Software Engineering Context:** Used in Agile Sprints, Waterfall SDLC, and PRINCE2 frameworks. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Relevance:** Critical Path Calculation is a **7–14 mark high-weightage topic** in Module 1. Mastery of the three pass phases and float formulas guarantees scoring potential in both Part A (3-markers) and Part B (14-markers) of the ESE paper. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Master Worked Example (The "KTU Classic Network") Below is a fully solved KTU-style problem. **Every algebraic step is explicitly written out** — no shortcuts, no truncations. ### 3.1.1 The Project Data Table The IT firm **"CodeCraft Kerala Pvt. Ltd."** is launching a web portal. The activities, durations, and dependencies are: | Activity | Duration (Days) | Predecessors | |----------|-----------------|--------------| | A | 3 | None | | B | 4 | None | | C | 2 | A | | D | 5 | A, B | | E | 3 | B | | F | 6 | C, D | | G | 4 | D, E | | H | 2 | F, G | > **Objective:** Draw the network, perform the Forward and Backward Pass, and identify the Critical Path. ### 3.1.2 Phase 1 — Forward Pass Computation (ES and EF) **Activity A** (No predecessor): $$\text{ES}_A = 0$$ $$\text{EF}_A = \text{ES}_A + D_A = 0 + 3 = 3$$ **Activity B** (No predecessor): $$\text{ES}_B = 0$$ $$\text{EF}_B = \text{ES}_B + D_B = 0 + 4 = 4$$ **Activity C** (Predecessor: A): $$\text{ES}_C = \text{EF}_A = 3$$ $$\text{EF}_C = \text{ES}_C + D_C = 3 + 2 = 5$$ **Activity D** (Predecessors: A, B): $$\text{ES}_D = \max(\text{EF}_A, \text{EF}_B) = \max(3, 4) = 4$$ $$\text{EF}_D = \text{ES}_D + D_D = 4 + 5 = 9$$ **Activity E** (Predecessor: B): $$\text{ES}_E = \text{EF}_B = 4$$ $$\text{EF}_E = \text{ES}_E + D_E = 4 + 3 = 7$$ **Activity F** (Predecessors: C, D): $$\text{ES}_F = \max(\text{EF}_C, \text{EF}_D) = \max(5, 9) = 9$$ $$\text{EF}_F = \text{ES}_F + D_F = 9 + 6 = 15$$ **Activity G** (Predecessors: D, E): $$\text{ES}_G = \max(\text{EF}_D, \text{EF}_E) = \max(9, 7) = 9$$ $$\text{EF}_G = \text{ES}_G + D_G = 9 + 4 = 13$$ **Activity H** (Predecessors: F, G): $$\text{ES}_H = \max(\text{EF}_F, \text{EF}_G) = \max(15, 13) = 15$$ $$\text{EF}_H = \text{ES}_H + D_H = 15 + 2 = 17$$ **Project Duration:** $$T_p = \max(\text{EF}_H) = 17 \text{ days}$$ ### 3.1.3 Phase 2 — Backward Pass Computation (LS and LF) We initialize with the terminal activity H. **Activity H** (No successor): $$\text{LF}_H = T_p = 17$$ $$\text{LS}_H = \text{LF}_H - D_H = 17 - 2 = 15$$ **Activity G** (Successor: H): $$\text{LF}_G = \text{LS}_H = 15$$ $$\text{LS}_G = \text{LF}_G - D_G = 15 - 4 = 11$$ **Activity F** (Successor: H): $$\text{LF}_F = \text{LS}_H = 15$$ $$\text{LS}_F = \text{LF}_F - D_F = 15 - 6 = 9$$ **Activity E** (Successor: G): $$\text{LF}_E = \text{LS}_G = 11$$ $$\text{LS}_E = \text{LF}_E - D_E = 11 - 3 = 8$$ **Activity D** (Successors: F, G): $$\text{LF}_D = \min(\text{LS}_F, \text{LS}_G) = \min(9, 11) = 9$$ $$\text{LS}_D = \text{LF}_D - D_D = 9 - 5 = 4$$ **Activity C** (Successor: F): $$\text{LF}_C = \text{LS}_F = 9$$ $$\text{LS}_C = \text{LF}_C - D_C = 9 - 2 = 7$$ **Activity B** (Successors: D, E): $$\text{LF}_B = \min(\text{LS}_D, \text{LS}_E) = \min(4, 8) = 4$$ $$\text{LS}_B = \text{LF}_B - D_B = 4 - 4 = 0$$ **Activity A** (Successors: C, D): $$\text{LF}_A = \min(\text{LS}_C, \text{LS}_D) = \min(7, 4) = 4$$ $$\text{LS}_A = \text{LF}_A - D_A = 4 - 3 = 1$$ ### 3.1.4 Phase 3 — Total Float and Free Float Computation **Total Float (TF):** $$\text{TF}_A = \text{LS}_A - \text{ES}_A = 1 - 0 = 1 \text{ day}$$ $$\text{TF}_B = \text{LS}_B - \text{ES}_B = 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{TF}_C = \text{LS}_C - \text{ES}_C = 7 - 3 = 4 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{TF}_D = \text{LS}_D - \text{ES}_D = 4 - 4 = 0 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{TF}_E = \text{LS}_E - \text{ES}_E = 8 - 4 = 4 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{TF}_F = \text{LS}_F - \text{ES}_F = 9 - 9 = 0 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{TF}_G = \text{LS}_G - \text{ES}_G = 11 - 9 = 2 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{TF}_H = \text{LS}_H - \text{ES}_H = 15 - 15 = 0 \text{ days}$$ **Free Float (FF):** $$\text{FF}_A = \min(\text{ES}_C, \text{ES}_D) - \text{EF}_A = \min(3, 4) - 3 = 0 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{FF}_B = \min(\text{ES}_D, \text{ES}_E) - \text{EF}_B = \min(4, 4) - 4 = 0 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{FF}_C = \text{ES}_F - \text{EF}_C = 9 - 5 = 4 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{FF}_D = \min(\text{ES}_F, \text{ES}_G) - \text{EF}_D = \min(9, 9) - 9 = 0 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{FF}_E = \text{ES}_G - \text{EF}_E = 9 - 7 = 2 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{FF}_F = \text{ES}_H - \text{EF}_F = 15 - 15 = 0 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{FF}_G = \text{ES}_H - \text{EF}_G = 15 - 13 = 2 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{FF}_H = T_p - \text{EF}_H = 17 - 17 = 0 \text{ days}$$ ### 3.1.5 Master Analytical Matrix (Single Reference Table) | Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | Critical? | |----------|----------|----|----|----|----|----|----|-----------| | A | 3 | 0 | 3 | 1 | 4 | 1 | 0 | No | | **B** | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | | C | 2 | 3 | 5 | 7 | 9 | 4 | 4 | No | | **D** | 5 | 4 | 9 | 4 | 9 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | | E | 3 | 4 | 7 | 8 | 11 | 4 | 2 | No | | **F** | 6 | 9 | 15 | 9 | 15 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | | G | 4 | 9 | 13 | 11 | 15 | 2 | 2 | No | | **H** | 2 | 15 | 17 | 15 | 17 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | ### 3.1.6 Final Result Synthesis > **Critical Path: B → D → F → H** > **Project Duration: 17 days** > **Critical Activities: B, D, F, H (all have TF = 0)** > [!NOTE] > **Verification Check:** Sum of durations on the critical path: > $$D_B + D_D + D_F + D_H = 4 + 5 + 6 + 2 = 17 \text{ days} \;\;\checkmark$$ > This matches the project duration $T_p$, confirming the correctness of our computation. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Activity-on-Node (AON) Network Diagram The following Mermaid **graph** block renders the project network with each activity as a node. Node identifiers are purely alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid compilation safeguards. ```mermaid graph LR start1([START]) start2([START]) nodeA["A
Dur 3"] nodeB["B
Dur 4"] nodeC["C
Dur 2"] nodeD["D
Dur 5"] nodeE["E
Dur 3"] nodeF["F
Dur 6"] nodeG["G
Dur 4"] nodeH["H
Dur 2"] finish1([FINISH]) finish2([FINISH]) start1 --> nodeA start2 --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG nodeE --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeH nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> finish1 nodeH --> finish2 classDef critical fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef noncritical fill:#a8e6cf,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class nodeB,nodeD,nodeF,nodeH critical class nodeA,nodeC,nodeE,nodeG noncritical ``` > **Legend:** Red boxes = **Critical Path (B → D → F → H)** | Green boxes = Non-Critical Activities. ## 4.2 CPM Three-Phase Process Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD phase1["PHASE 1: FORWARD PASS
Compute ES and EF
Left to Right Traversal"] phase2["PHASE 2: BACKWARD PASS
Compute LS and LF
Right to Left Traversal"] phase3["PHASE 3: FLOAT ANALYSIS
Compute TF and FF
Identify TF=0 Chain"] result["OUTPUT: Critical Path
and Project Duration Tp"] phase1 --> phase2 phase2 --> phase3 phase3 --> result classDef phaseBox fill:#4a90e2,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef resultBox fill:#f5a623,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 class phase1,phase2,phase3 phaseBox class result resultBox ``` ## 4.3 Critical Path Identification Logic (Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD inp["Input: All ES, EF, LS, LF Values"] tfcalc["Calculate TF = LS - ES"] tfcheck{"Is TF = 0 ?"} markcrit["Mark as CRITICAL ACTIVITY"] skiplit["Mark as NON-CRITICAL
Slip Allowed by TF Days"] chain["Connect all Critical Activities
in chronological order"] outcp["OUTPUT: Critical Path
and Minimum Tp"] inp --> tfcalc tfcalc --> tfcheck tfcheck -- YES --> markcrit tfcheck -- NO --> skiplit markcrit --> chain skiplit --> chain chain --> outcp classDef critStyle fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef ncStyle fill:#a8e6cf,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef neutral fill:#d3d3d3,stroke:#000,color:#000 class markcrit,chain,outcp critStyle class skiplit ncStyle class inp,tfcalc,tfcheck neutral ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing ES and LS:** ES uses *predecessors'* EF (forward). LS uses *successors'* LS (backward). Reversing these is the #1 mark-losing mistake. > 2. **Missing the `max/min` in predecessor/successor logic:** When an activity has **multiple predecessors**, ES = **MAX** of their EFs. When an activity has **multiple successors**, LF = **MIN** of their LSs. Forgetting this gives wrong critical path. > 3. **Free Float vs Total Float confusion:** FF uses *earliest start of successor* (not latest). TF uses *latest start minus earliest start*. > 4. **Missing the "Start = 0" rule:** Always initialize ES of activities with no predecessor as 0, not as 1. > 5. **Misidentifying the Critical Path:** Only activities with TF = 0 are critical. A path with the largest sum is *equivalent*, but the **definitive** test is **TF = 0**. --- ## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Conceptuals) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define Critical Path. Why is it important in IT project management? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **Critical Path** is the longest path through a project network diagram, consisting of activities with **zero Total Float**. It represents the **minimum time required** to complete the project. *Importance in IT Project Management (2 marks):* - **(a) Time Management:** It determines the shortest possible project duration, helping managers commit realistic deadlines. - **(b) Resource Prioritization:** Critical activities demand maximum supervision, skilled resources, and zero-delay monitoring. - **(c) Risk Mitigation:** Any slippage in critical activities directly delays project delivery, so they are flagged for proactive risk-handling. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between Total Float and Free Float. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Total Float (TF) | Free Float (FF) | |--------|------------------|-----------------| | Definition | Time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **entire project**. | Time an activity can be delayed without delaying **any immediate successor's earliest start**. | | Formula | $\text{TF} = \text{LS} - \text{ES}$ | $\text{FF} = \min(\text{ES}_{\text{successors}}) - \text{EF}_{\text{current}}$ | | Scope | Project-level delay tolerance. | Successor-level delay tolerance. | > *Key Insight:* **FF ≤ TF** always. Critical activities have both TF = 0 and FF = 0. --- ## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A [14 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** A software development project has the following activities, durations, and dependencies: | Activity | Duration (Weeks) | Predecessors | |----------|------------------|--------------| | A | 4 | None | | B | 6 | None | | C | 3 | A | | D | 5 | A, B | | E | 7 | B | | F | 4 | C, D | | G | 6 | D, E | | H | 5 | F, G | #### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]** Draw the network diagram and perform the Forward Pass. Compute ES and EF for all activities. Also determine the project duration. #### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]** Perform the Backward Pass. Compute LS, LF, TF, and FF for all activities. Identify the Critical Path and verify the project duration. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Part (a) — Forward Pass Solution **Activity A:** No predecessor. $$\text{ES}_A = 0, \quad \text{EF}_A = 0 + 4 = 4$$ **Activity B:** No predecessor. $$\text{ES}_B = 0, \quad \text{EF}_B = 0 + 6 = 6$$ **Activity C:** Predecessor A. $$\text{ES}_C = \text{EF}_A = 4, \quad \text{EF}_C = 4 + 3 = 7$$ **Activity D:** Predecessors A, B. $$\text{ES}_D = \max(4, 6) = 6, \quad \text{EF}_D = 6 + 5 = 11$$ **Activity E:** Predecessor B. $$\text{ES}_E = \text{EF}_B = 6, \quad \text{EF}_E = 6 + 7 = 13$$ **Activity F:** Predecessors C, D. $$\text{ES}_F = \max(7, 11) = 11, \quad \text{EF}_F = 11 + 4 = 15$$ **Activity G:** Predecessors D, E. $$\text{ES}_G = \max(11, 13) = 13, \quad \text{EF}_G = 13 + 6 = 19$$ **Activity H:** Predecessors F, G. $$\text{ES}_H = \max(15, 19) = 19, \quad \text{EF}_H = 19 + 5 = 24$$ $$\boxed{T_p = 24 \text{ weeks}}$$ #### Part (b) — Backward Pass & Float Analysis **Activity H:** Terminal activity, so $\text{LF}_H = 24$. $$\text{LS}_H = 24 - 5 = 19, \quad \text{TF}_H = 0, \quad \text{FF}_H = 0$$ **Activity G:** Successor H. $$\text{LF}_G = \text{LS}_H = 19, \quad \text{LS}_G = 19 - 6 = 13, \quad \text{TF}_G = 0, \quad \text{FF}_G = 19 - 13 = 6$$ **Activity F:** Successor H. $$\text{LF}_F = \text{LS}_H = 19, \quad \text{LS}_F = 19 - 4 = 15, \quad \text{TF}_F = 4, \quad \text{FF}_F = 19 - 15 = 4$$ **Activity E:** Successor G. $$\text{LF}_E = \text{LS}_G = 13, \quad \text{LS}_E = 13 - 7 = 6, \quad \text{TF}_E = 0, \quad \text{FF}_E = 13 - 13 = 0$$ **Activity D:** Successors F, G. $$\text{LF}_D = \min(\text{LS}_F, \text{LS}_G) = \min(15, 13) = 13, \quad \text{LS}_D = 13 - 5 = 8, \quad \text{TF}_D = 2, \quad \text{FF}_D = \min(11, 13) - 11 = 0$$ **Activity C:** Successor F. $$\text{LF}_C = \text{LS}_F = 15, \quad \text{LS}_C = 15 - 3 = 12, \quad \text{TF}_C = 8, \quad \text{FF}_C = 11 - 7 = 4$$ **Activity B:** Successors D, E. $$\text{LF}_B = \min(\text{LS}_D, \text{LS}_E) = \min(8, 6) = 6, \quad \text{LS}_B = 6 - 6 = 0, \quad \text{TF}_B = 0, \quad \text{FF}_B = \min(6, 6) - 6 = 0$$ **Activity A:** Successors C, D. $$\text{LF}_A = \min(\text{LS}_C, \text{LS}_D) = \min(12, 8) = 8, \quad \text{LS}_A = 8 - 4 = 4, \quad \text{TF}_A = 4, \quad \text{FF}_A = \min(4, 6) -
