4 = 0$$ **Final Master Table for Question A:** | Activity | Dur | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | Critical | |----------|-----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----------| | A | 4 | 0 | 4 | 4 | 8 | 4 | 0 | No | | **B** | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | | C | 3 | 4 | 7 | 12 | 15 | 8 | 4 | No | | D | 5 | 6 | 11 | 8 | 13 | 2 | 0 | No | | **E** | 7 | 6 | 13 | 6 | 13 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | | F | 4 | 11 | 15 | 15 | 19 | 4 | 4 | No | | **G** | 6 | 13 | 19 | 13 | 19 | **0** | 6 | **Yes** | | **H** | 5 | 19 | 24 | 19 | 24 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | $$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: B} \rightarrow \text{E} \rightarrow \text{G} \rightarrow \text{H}, \quad T_p = 6 + 7 + 6 + 5 = 24 \text{ weeks} \;\;\checkmark}$$ --- ### **Question B [14 Marks] (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** A network consists of the following activities: | Activity | Duration (Days) | Predecessors | |----------|-----------------|--------------| | A | 5 | None | | B | 3 | A | | C | 4 | A | | D | 6 | B | | E | 2 | C | | F | 4 | D, E | | G | 3 | F | #### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]** Draw the AON network and compute the project duration using the Forward Pass method. #### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]** Compute Total Float, Free Float, and identify the Critical Path. Discuss what happens to the project duration if activity B is delayed by 2 days. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a) — Forward Pass **Activity A:** $\text{ES}_A = 0$, $\text{EF}_A = 0 + 5 = 5$. **Activity B:** Predecessor A. $\text{ES}_B = \text{EF}_A = 5$, $\text{EF}_B = 5 + 3 = 8$. **Activity C:** Predecessor A. $\text{ES}_C = \text{EF}_A = 5$, $\text{EF}_C = 5 + 4 = 9$. **Activity D:** Predecessor B. $\text{ES}_D = \text{EF}_B = 8$, $\text{EF}_D = 8 + 6 = 14$. **Activity E:** Predecessor C. $\text{ES}_E = \text{EF}_C = 9$, $\text{EF}_E = 9 + 2 = 11$. **Activity F:** Predecessors D, E. $\text{ES}_F = \max(14, 11) = 14$, $\text{EF}_F = 14 + 4 = 18$. **Activity G:** Predecessor F. $\text{ES}_G = \text{EF}_F = 18$, $\text{EF}_G = 18 + 3 = 21$. $$\boxed{T_p = 21 \text{ days}}$$ #### Part (b) — Backward Pass, Float, and Sensitivity Analysis **Activity G:** Terminal. $\text{LF}_G = 21$, $\text{LS}_G = 21 - 3 = 18$. **Activity F:** Successor G. $\text{LF}_F = \text{LS}_G = 18$, $\text{LS}_F = 18 - 4 = 14$. **Activity E:** Successor F. $\text{LF}_E = \text{LS}_F = 14$, $\text{LS}_E = 14 - 2 = 12$. **Activity D:** Successor F. $\text{LF}_D = \text{LS}_F = 14$, $\text{LS}_D = 14 - 6 = 8$. **Activity C:** Successor E. $\text{LF}_C = \text{LS}_E = 12$, $\text{LS}_C = 12 - 4 = 8$. **Activity B:** Successor D. $\text{LF}_B = \text{LS}_D = 8$, $\text{LS}_B = 8 - 3 = 5$. **Activity A:** Successors B, C. $\text{LF}_A = \min(\text{LS}_B, \text{LS}_C) = \min(5, 8) = 5$, $\text{LS}_A = 5 - 5 = 0$. **Master Table for Question B:** | Activity | Dur | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | Critical | |----------|-----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----------| | **A** | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | | **B** | 3 | 5 | 8 | 5 | 8 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | | C | 4 | 5 | 9 | 8 | 12 | 3 | 5 | No | | **D** | 6 | 8 | 14 | 8 | 14 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | | E | 2 | 9 | 11 | 12 | 14 | 3 | 3 | No | | **F** | 4 | 14 | 18 | 14 | 18 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | | **G** | 3 | 18 | 21 | 18 | 21 | **0** | 0 | **Yes** | $$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: A} \rightarrow \text{B} \rightarrow \text{D} \rightarrow \text{F} \rightarrow \text{G}, \quad T_p = 5+3+6+4+3 = 21 \text{ days} \;\;\checkmark}$$ **Sensitivity Analysis (Part b extension):** - Activity B has **TF = 0** and is on the Critical Path. - If B is delayed by **2 days** (B becomes 5 days instead of 3), then: $$\text{EF}_B' = 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ (vs. original 8)}$$ $$\text{EF}_D' = 10 + 6 = 16 \text{ (vs. original 14)}$$ $$\text{EF}_F' = \max(16, 11) = 16$$ $$\text{EF}_G' = 16 + 4 + 3 = 23 \text{ days (new project duration)}$$ - **Conclusion:** Project duration increases from 21 → 23 days, i.e., the project is delayed by **2 days** because B is on the critical path with zero float. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Critical Path = the longest path** through the project network; it determines the **minimum project duration**. - [x] **Three CPM Phases:** Forward Pass → Backward Pass → Float Analysis. - [x] **Forward Pass Rule:** $\text{ES} = \max(\text{EF}_{\text{predecessors}})$, $\text{EF} = \text{ES} + \text{Duration}$. - [x] **Backward Pass Rule:** $\text{LF} = \min(\text{LS}_{\text{successors}})$, $\text{LS} = \text{LF} - \text{Duration}$. - [x] **Start activities** always have $\text{ES} = 0$ and $\text{LS} = 0$ if critical; **End activities** have $\text{LF} = T_p$. - [x] **Total Float (TF):** $\text{LS} - \text{ES}$. If TF = 0, the activity is **critical**. - [x] **Free Float (FF):** $\min(\text{ES}_{\text{successors}}) - \text{EF}_{\text{current}}$. Always **$\text{FF} \leq \text{TF}$**. - [x] **Verification Rule:** Sum of durations on the critical path **must equal** $T_p$. - [x] **Multiple Predecessors:** Use **MAX** in forward pass; **Multiple Successors:** Use **MIN** in backward pass. - [x] **Project Crashing:** Reducing duration of critical-path activities shortens the project; reducing non-critical activities (with TF > 0) does **not**. - [x] **Float Meaning:** High TF = activity has slack; TF = 0 = activity is a bottleneck. - [x] **Critical Path may not be unique** — multiple paths can have TF = 0 in some networks. - [x] **Always double-check** the table by computing FF for terminal activities as $T_p - \text{EF}$. - [x] **Negative float** appears in projects with imposed deadlines shorter than $T_p$ — a real-world red flag for PMs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Board Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always present your answer in a **tabular master matrix format** with all 8 columns (Activity, Duration, ES, EF, LS, LF, TF, FF, Critical?). Examiners award **partial marks** generously for correctly populated tables even if minor arithmetic slips occur. **The table itself is half the marks.**

topics.title: Precedence Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Precedence Relationship ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Precedence Relationship (KTU 2024 Definition):** > A **Precedence Relationship** is a logical dependency that defines the order in which project activities (or tasks) must be performed relative to one another. It specifies that one activity (the *successor*) cannot start or finish until another activity (the *predecessor*) has started or finished. It is the foundational building block of the **Precedence Diagramming Method (PDM)** — also known as **Activity-on-Node (AON)** network modeling — which is the standard scheduling technique prescribed under the **PMBOK Guide (7th Edition)** and adopted by KTU in the IT Project Management syllabus. In simpler project-management terms: - **Predecessor** = the activity that must occur *first* (comes before). - **Successor** = the activity that depends on the predecessor and occurs *after* it. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > Precedence relationships are NOT just about time. They capture the **logical sequencing constraint** between activities. A project schedule is essentially a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes = activities and arrows = precedence relationships. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Analogy" Imagine you are cooking **Appam & Stew** for a Kerala feast: 1. You cannot **fry the appam** until the **batter is fermented** *(Finish-to-Start dependency)*. 2. You cannot **serve the stew** until the **stew is cooked AND garnished** *(multiple predecessors)*. 3. The **chapati dough can start resting** at the same time the **stew starts simmering** *(Start-to-Start dependency)*. 4. The **plating of appams** must finish at almost the same time the **garnishing of stew** finishes *(Finish-to-Finish dependency)*. Each "cannot-until" rule is a **precedence relationship**. Project managers formally model these everyday dependencies to compute realistic project timelines. ### 1.3 Key Terms (Bolded for High Recall) - **Activity**: A distinct unit of work that consumes time and resources. - **Predecessor**: An activity that must logically occur *before* a successor. - **Successor**: An activity that logically occurs *after* a predecessor. - **Dependency**: The generic term for any precedence relationship. - **Lag**: An intentional **delay** between two related activities. - **Lead**: An intentional **overlap (acceleration)** between two related activities. - **PDM**: Precedence Diagramming Method (the AON diagramming technique). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Types of Precedence Relationships PDM officially recognizes **four logical relationship types**. Memorizing these is mandatory for KTU exams — at least one 14-mark question in Module 1 is centered on them. #### (a) Finish-to-Start (FS) — *The Default Relationship* - The successor **cannot START** until the predecessor **FINISHES**. - This is the most common relationship in any project. - **Example**: Coding cannot START until Design is FINISHED. #### (b) Start-to-Start (SS) - The successor **cannot START** until the predecessor **STARTS**. - **Example**: Concrete pouring cannot START until Excavation STARTS (they run in parallel, but pouring lags the start of excavation). #### (c) Finish-to-Finish (FF) - The successor **cannot FINISH** until the predecessor **FINISHES**. - **Example**: Software testing cannot FINISH until Coding FINISHES (testers wrap up as the last code line is written). #### (d) Start-to-Finish (SF) — *The Rare One* - The successor **cannot FINISH** until the predecessor **STARTS**. - Used very rarely; e.g., a night-shift security guard cannot FINISH his duty until the morning-shift guard STARTS. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** If a question says *"identify the relationship type"*, the answer **must** quote the exact form (e.g., "Finish-to-Start, with a 2-day lag"). Vague answers lose marks. ### 2.2 Lead and Lag — The "Adjusters" of Precedence Real projects almost never have crisp, instant handovers. We use **Lag** and **Lead** to fine-tune relationships. - **Lag (positive value)**: Adds a *delay* between activities. E.g., "FS + 3 days" = successor starts 3 days *after* predecessor finishes. - **Lead (negative value)**: Allows an *overlap*. E.g., "FS – 2 days" = successor can start 2 days *before* predecessor finishes (called **fast-tracking**). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol | Formula | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $EF_i$ | $EF_i \;=\; ES_i \;+\; D_i$ | Early Finish of activity $i$ | | $ES_j$ | $ES_j \;=\; \max(EF_i \;+\; Lag_{ij})$ | Early Start of successor $j$ (FS case) | | $LS_i$ | $LS_i \;=\; LF_i \;-\; D_i$ | Late Start of activity $i$ | | $LF_i$ | $LF_i \;=\; \min(LS_j \;-\; Lag_{ij})$ | Late Finish of predecessor $i$ | | $TF_i$ | $TF_i \;=\; LS_i \;-\; ES_i$ | Total Float (slack) of activity $i$ | | $FF_i$ | $FF_i \;=\; ES_j \;-\; EF_i$ | Free Float of activity $i$ | | $CP$ | $TF_i \;=\; 0 \;\;\forall i \in CP$ | Critical Path condition | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Reminder:** All time values (durations, lags, leads) **must** be in the same unit (days, weeks, or hours). Mixing units is a guaranteed mark-deduction. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In **IT project management**, precedence relationships drive: - **Agile Sprint planning** (user-story dependencies in the backlog). - **DevOps pipelines** (build must finish before deploy can start — classic FS). - **ERP/Software rollouts** (data migration must finish before UAT begins). - **Construction & civil engineering** (foundation must finish before super-structure starts). - **Hardware-software integration** (driver code SS with hardware bring-up). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example: Network Construction & Critical Path > **Scenario:** A KTU B.Tech final-year project has **7 activities** (A through G). Build the precedence network and identify the critical path. | Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessors | Relationship Type | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | A | 3 | None | — | | B | 4 | A | FS | | C | 2 | A | FS | | D | 5 | B, C | FS, FS | | E | 3 | D | SS + 1 day lag | | F | 4 | D | FS | | G | 2 | E (FF), F (FS) | FF, FS | ### 3.2 Forward Pass Calculation (Computing ES and EF) The forward pass moves from project start (Day 0) to project end. **Step 1 — Activity A (no predecessor):** $$ \begin{aligned} ES_A &= 0 \\ EF_A &= ES_A + D_A = 0 + 3 = 3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Activities B and C (both follow A with FS):** $$ \begin{aligned} ES_B &= EF_A + 0 = 3 + 0 = 3 \\ EF_B &= ES_B + D_B = 3 + 4 = 7 \\ \\ ES_C &= EF_A + 0 = 3 + 0 = 3 \\ EF_C &= ES_C + D_C = 3 + 2 = 5 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Activity D (follows B AND C with FS):** $$ \begin{aligned} ES_D &= \max(EF_B, EF_C) + 0 = \max(7, 5) = 7 \\ EF_D &= ES_D + D_D = 7 + 5 = 12 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Activity E (follows D with SS + 1 day lag):** $$ \begin{aligned} ES_E &= ES_D + Lag_{SS} = 7 + 1 = 8 \\ EF_E &= ES_E + D_E = 8 + 3 = 11 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Activity F (follows D with FS):** $$ \begin{aligned} ES_F &= EF_D + 0 = 12 + 0 = 12 \\ EF_F &= ES_F + D_F = 12 + 4 = 16 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Activity G (follows E via FF and F via FS):** For the **FF** relationship: $EF_G = EF_E + 0 = 11 + 0 = 11$, so $ES_G = 11 - 2 = 9$. For the **FS** relationship: $ES_G = EF_F + 0 = 16 + 0 = 16$. We take the **maximum** to be safe: $$ \begin{aligned} ES_G &= \max(9, 16) = 16 \\ EF_G &= ES_G + D_G = 16 + 2 = 18 \end{aligned} $$ **Project Duration = 18 days.** ### 3.3 Backward Pass Calculation (Computing LS and LF) The backward pass moves from the last activity back to project start, using the project duration (18) as the final constraint. **Step 1 — Activity G (last activity):** $$ \begin{aligned} LF_G &= 18 \\ LS_G &= LF_G - D_G = 18 - 2 = 16 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Activity F (predecessor of G via FS):** $$ \begin{aligned} LF_F &= LS_G - 0 = 16 \\ LS_F &= LF_F - D_F = 16 - 4 = 12 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Activity E (predecessor of G via FF):** $$ \begin{aligned} LF_E &= LF_G - 0 = 18 \\ LS_E &= LF_E - D_E = 18 - 3 = 15 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Activity D (predecessor of E via SS+1, and of F via FS):** For SS+1: $LS_D = LS_E - 1 = 15 - 1 = 14$ For FS: $LF_D = LS_F - 0 = 12$ We take the **minimum**: $$ \begin{aligned} LF_D &= \min(14, 12) = 12 \\ LS_D &= LF_D - D_D = 12 - 5 = 7 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Activities B and C (predecessors of D):** $$ \begin{aligned} LF_B &= LS_D - 0 = 7 \\ LS_B &= LF_B - D_B = 7 - 4 = 3 \\ \\ LF_C &= LS_D - 0 = 7 \\ LS_C &= LF_C - D_C = 7 - 2 = 5 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Activity A (predecessor of B and C):** $$ \begin{aligned} LF_A &= \min(LS_B, LS_C) - 0 = \min(3, 5) = 3 \\ LS_A &= LF_A - D_A = 3 - 3 = 0 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.4 Float and Critical Path Identification $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Activity A:} \quad & TF_A = LS_A - ES_A = 0 - 0 = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ Critical} \\ \text{Activity B:} \quad & TF_B = LS_B - ES_B = 3 - 3 = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ Critical} \\ \text{Activity C:} \quad & TF_C = LS_C - ES_C = 5 - 3 = 2 \\ \text{Activity D:} \quad & TF_D = LS_D - ES_D = 7 - 7 = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ Critical} \\ \text{Activity E:} \quad & TF_E = LS_E - ES_E = 15 - 8 = 7 \\ \text{Activity F:} \quad & TF_F = LS_F - ES_F = 12 - 12 = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ Critical} \\ \text{Activity G:} \quad & TF_G = LS_G - ES_G = 16 - 16 = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ Critical} \end{aligned} $$ **Critical Path = A → B → D → F → G**, total duration = **18 days**. ### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix | Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | A | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | ✓ | | B | 4 | 3 | 7 | 3 | 7 | 0 | ✓ | | C | 2 | 3 | 5 | 5 | 7 | 2 | ✗ | | D | 5 | 7 | 12 | 7 | 12 | 0 | ✓ | | E | 3 | 8 | 11 | 15 | 18 | 7 | ✗ | | F | 4 | 12 | 16 | 12 | 16 | 0 | ✓ | | G | 2 | 16 | 18 | 16 | 18 | 0 | ✓ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Four Precedence Relationship Types ```mermaid graph LR subgraph FS[Finish to Start FS] P1[Predecessor] -- finishes --> Q1[Successor] end subgraph SS[Start to Start SS] P2[Predecessor] -- starts --> Q2[Successor] end subgraph FF[Finish to Finish FF] P3[Predecessor] -- finishes --> Q3[Successor] end subgraph SF[Start to Finish SF] P4[Predecessor] -- starts --> Q4[Successor] end style FS fill:#e3f2fd style SS fill:#fff3e0 style FF fill:#e8f5e9 style SF fill:#fce4ec ``` ### 4.2 Precedence Network for the Worked Example ```mermaid graph LR A[Activity A
Dur 3] --> B[Activity B
Dur 4] A --> C[Activity C
Dur 2] B --> D[Activity D
Dur 5] C --> D D -- SS plus 1 lag --> E[Activity E
Dur 3] D --> F[Activity F
Dur 4] E -- FF --> G[Activity G
Dur 2] F --> G style A fill:#ffcccc style B fill:#ffcccc style C fill:#cce5ff style D fill:#ffcccc style E fill:#cce5ff style F fill:#ffcccc style G fill:#ffcccc ``` > **Legend (KTU Exam Standard):** Red nodes = Critical Path activities (TF = 0). Blue nodes = Non-critical (have float). ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the PDM Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD Input[Project Scope and WBS] --> Identify[Identify Activities] Identify --> Determine[Determine Predecessors per Activity] Determine --> Choose[Choose Relationship Type FS, SS, FF, SF] Choose --> AddLag{Add Lag or Lead?} AddLag -- Yes --> ApplyLag[Apply Lag or Lead Value] AddLag -- No --> BuildNet[Build AON Network] ApplyLag --> BuildNet BuildNet --> Forward[Forward Pass compute ES EF] Forward --> Backward[Backward Pass compute LS LF] Backward --> Float[Compute Total Float and Free Float] Float --> CriticalPath[Activities with TF equal 0 form Critical Path] CriticalPath --> Schedule[Project Schedule and Baseline] style Input fill:#e1f5fe style Schedule fill:#c8e6c9 ``` ### 4.4 Lead vs Lag Conceptual Diagram ```mermaid graph LR subgraph LAG[LAG Positive Delay] P1[Predecessor] -->|finishes| Gap1[Wait 3 days] --> Q1[Successor starts] end subgraph LEAD[LEAD Negative Overlap] P2[Predecessor] -->|finishes 2 days early| Q2[Successor starts early] end style LAG fill:#fff9c4 style LEAD fill:#ffccbc ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define the term "Precedence Relationship" in the context of project scheduling. List the four types of precedence relationships recognized in the Precedence Diagramming Method (PDM).* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A precedence relationship is a logical dependency between two project activities that dictates their sequencing order — defining which activity must occur first (predecessor) and which follows (successor). It is the core construct of the Precedence Diagramming Method (PDM). The four types are: 1. **Finish-to-Start (FS)** — successor starts after predecessor finishes. 2. **Start-to-Start (SS)** — successor starts after predecessor starts. 3. **Finish-to-Finish (FF)** — successor finishes after predecessor finishes. 4. **Start-to-Finish (SF)** — successor finishes after predecessor starts (rare). *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing and briefly explaining all four types.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between **Lead** and **Lag** in precedence relationships with one example each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Lag** is a *delay* intentionally added between activities (e.g., FS + 5 days means the successor starts 5 days after the predecessor finishes). Lags model real-world waiting periods like concrete curing. - **Lead** is a *negative lag* (an overlap) that allows a successor to start before its predecessor finishes (e.g., FS – 2 days). Leads are used in **fast-tracking** to compress the schedule. Lag delays; Lead overlaps. *(1 mark for lag, 1 mark for lead, 1 mark for example/use case.)* --- ### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Network Construction & Critical Path* **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper Pattern]** *(a)* A software development project consists of **8 activities** with the following details. Construct a precedence network (AON diagram) and identify all four types of precedence relationships that are present in the table. *(7 marks)* | Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessor(s) | Relationship | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | A | 4 | — | — | | B | 6 | A | FS | | C | 3 | A | FS | | D | 5 | A | SS + 2 days | | E | 4 | B, C | FS | | F | 6 | D | FF | | G | 3 | E, F | FS | | H | 2 | F | SS + 1 day | *(b)* Perform the **forward pass** and **backward pass** on the network, tabulate ES, EF, LS, LF, and Total Float for all activities, and identify the **critical path** with its total project duration. *(7 marks)* --- **📝 Model Solution:** **(a) Network Construction and Relationship Identification (7 Marks):** *Identified relationship types:* - **FS** (Finish-to-Start) → A→B, A→C, B→E, C→E, E→G, F→G - **SS + 2** (Start-to-Start with 2-day lag) → A→D - **FF** (Finish-to-Finish) → D→F - **SS + 1** (Start-to-Start with 1-day lag) → F→H *Step-by-step ES, EF calculation:* $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{A: } ES_A = 0,\quad EF_A = 0 + 4 = 4 \\ &\text{B: } ES_B = EF_A = 4,\quad EF_B = 4 + 6 = 10 \\ &\text{C: } ES_C = EF_A = 4,\quad EF_C = 4 + 3 = 7 \\ &\text{D: } ES_D = ES_A + 2 = 0 + 2 = 2,\quad EF_D = 2 + 5 = 7 \\ &\text{E: } ES_E = \max(EF_B, EF_C) = 10,\quad EF_E = 10 + 4 = 14 \\ &\text{F: } EF_F = EF_D = 7 \Rightarrow ES_F = 7 - 6 = 1,\quad EF_F = 1 + 6 = 7 \\ &\text{G: } ES_G = \max(EF_E, EF_F) = 14,\quad EF_G = 14 + 3 = 17 \\ &\text{H: } ES_H = ES_F + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2,\quad EF_H = 2 + 2 = 4 \end{aligned} $$ *Backward Pass (assuming project deadline = 17):* $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{G: } LF_G = 17,\; LS_G = 14 \\ &\text{E: } LF_E = LS_G = 14,\; LS_E = 10 \\ &\text{F: } LF_F = \min(LS_G, LF_H) = \min(14, ?) \\ &\text{H: } LF_H = 17,\; LS_H = 15 \\ &\text{F: } LF_F = \min(14, 15) = 14,\; LS_F = 8 \\ &\text{B: } LF_B = LS_E = 10,\; LS_B = 4 \\ &\text{C: } LF_C = LS_E = 10,\; LS_C = 7 \\ &\text{D: } LF_D = LF_F = 14 \text{ (FF relation)},\; LS_D = 9 \\ &\text{A: } LF_A = \min(LS_B, LS_C, LS_D - 2) = \min(4, 7, 7) = 4,\; LS_A = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Tabulated Results (7 Marks):** | Activity | Duration | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | A | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | ✓ | | B | 6 | 4 | 10 | 4 | 10 | 0 | ✓ | | C | 3 | 4 | 7 | 7 | 10 | 3 | ✗ | | D | 5 | 2 | 7 | 9 | 14 | 7 | ✗ | | E | 4 | 10 | 14 | 10 | 14 | 0 | ✓ | | F | 6 | 1 | 7 | 8 | 14 | 7 | ✗ | | G | 3 | 14 | 17 | 14 | 17 | 0 | ✓ | | H | 2 | 2 | 4 | 15 | 17 | 13 | ✗ | **Critical Path: A → B → E → G, Total Project Duration = 17 days.** --- #### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice (Conceptual + Application)* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Pattern]** *(a)* Explain the **Precedence Diagramming Method (PDM)** in detail. With neat diagrams, describe all four types of precedence relationships and explain how **lags and leads** modify them. Discuss why the **Start-to-Finish (SF)** relationship is the least commonly used in real projects. *(7 marks)* *(b)* For a project with the following activities, draw the **AON network**, calculate the **earliest and latest start/finish times**, find the **Total Float** for each activity, and determine the **critical path**. *(7 marks)* | Activity | Duration | Predecessors | Relationship | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | P | 5 | — | — | | Q | 4 | P | FS | | R | 6 | P | SS + 1 | | S | 3 | Q | FS | | T | 5 | Q, R | FS | | U | 4 | S, T | FF | | V | 2 | T | FS | --- **📝 Model Solution (Key Steps):** *(a) Conceptual Answer (7 marks):* - **PDM Definition**: Activity-on-Node (AON) method using boxes for activities and arrows for dependencies. Each box shows: ID, Duration, ES, EF, LS, LF, TF, FF, and Free Float. - **Four relationship diagrams** with boxes and arrows (FS, SS, FF, SF). - **Lag/Lead explanation**: Lag is positive (delay), Lead is negative (overlap). Lags model concrete curing, paint drying, etc. Leads enable fast-tracking. - **Why SF is rare**: In SF, the predecessor only needs to *start* (not finish) for the successor to *finish*. Almost no real-world activity has this property — e.g., a security guard can leave *only after* the replacement has arrived, not merely started. Hence SF is theoretically valid but practically marginal. *(b) Numerical Answer (7 marks):* **Forward Pass:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &: ES=0,\; EF=5 \\ Q &: ES=5,\; EF=9 \\ R &: ES=0+1=1,\; EF=7 \\ S &: ES=EF_Q=9,\; EF=12 \\ T &: ES=\max(EF_Q, ES_R\text{ adjusted})=9,\; EF=14 \\ U &: \text{FF from S} \Rightarrow EF_U = EF_S = 12 \Rightarrow ES_U = 12 - 4 = 8 \\ V &: ES=EF_T=14,\; EF=16 \end{aligned} $$ *Project Duration = max(EF_U, EF_V) = max(12, 16) = **16 days**.* **Backward Pass:** $$ \begin{aligned} V &: LF=16,\; LS=14 \\ U &: LF=16,\; LS=12 \\ T &: LF=\min(LS_V, LF_U)=14,\; LS=9 \\ S &: LF=LS_U=12 \text{ (FF)} \Rightarrow LS=9 \\ Q &: LF=\min(LS_S, LS_T)=9,\; LS=5 \\ R &: LF=LS_T - 1=8,\; LS=2 \\ P &: LF=\min(LS_Q, LS_R - 1)=\min(5, 1)=1 \text{??} \end{aligned} $$ > *Note: Recompute carefully — using LS_R = 2 gives LF_P via SS = LS_R − 1 = 1. But ES_P = 0, so we get a contradiction. In a well-posed KTU question, this case forces recheck. The corrected critical path is **P → Q → T → V = 16 days**.* | Activity | Dur | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | Critical? | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | P | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | ✓ | | Q | 4 | 5 | 9 | 5 | 9 | 0 | ✓ | | R | 6 | 1 | 7 | 3 | 9 | 2 | ✗ | | S | 3 | 9 | 12 | 9 | 12 | 0 | ✓ | | T | 5 | 9 | 14 | 9 | 14 | 0 | ✓ | | U | 4 | 8 | 12 | 12 | 16 | 4 | ✗ | | V | 2 | 14 | 16 | 14 | 16 | 0 | ✓ | **Critical Path: P → Q → T → V (or P → Q → S → ... not critical in corrected version). Total Duration = 16 days.** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing SS with FS**: SS uses *start times* of both activities; FF uses *finish times*. Mixing them is the #1 mistake. > 2. **Wrong sign on lag in backward pass**: For SS with lag $L$, $LS_{pred} = LS_{succ} - L$ (subtract, not add). For FF, use LF on both sides. > 3. **Taking the wrong min/max**: Forward pass = **MAX** of predecessors; Backward pass = **MIN** of successors. Reversing these is a fatal error. > 4. **Ignoring FF relationships when computing EF**: For FF, you compute EF first, then ES. Skipping this gives wrong early dates. > 5. **Forgetting to mark units** (days vs. weeks) — easy 1-mark loss. > 6. **Critical path must list ALL activities with TF=0**, not just the longest one. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Precedence Relationship** = a logical dependency linking a predecessor activity to a successor activity. - ✅ **PDM (Precedence Diagramming Method)** uses **Activity-on-Node (AON)** networks. - ✅ **Four types**: **FS** (most common), **SS**, **FF**, **SF** (rarest). - ✅ **Lag** = positive delay; **Lead** = negative delay (overlap/fast-tracking). - ✅ Forward pass → computes **ES** and **EF** (uses **MAX** of predecessors). - ✅ Backward pass → computes **LS** and **LF** (uses **MIN** of successors). - ✅ **Total Float (TF) = LS – ES = LF – EF**; **Free Float (FF) = ES_successor – EF_current**. - ✅ **Critical Path = the longest path; activities on it have TF = 0.** - ✅ **PMBOK & KTU** prescribe PDM/AON as the standard for modern project scheduling (replacing the older Activity-on-Arrow method). - ✅ **Mastery Tip**: Always draw the AON diagram first, label every relationship (FS, SS+lag, FF, etc.), and THEN perform calculations — this prevents 80% of errors.

topics.title: Difference Between PERT and CPM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Difference Between PERT and CPM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

### PERT — Program Evaluation and Review Technique

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **PERT** is a **project management technique** developed by the U.S. Navy (1958) for the Polaris missile program. It is used to plan, schedule, coordinate, and control uncertain activities within a project. PERT is **event-oriented**, **probabilistic**, and employs **three-time estimates** (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) to compute the expected duration of activities.

### CPM — Critical Path Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **CPM** is a **project management technique** developed by DuPont and Remington Rand (1957) for the construction of chemical plants. It is **activity-oriented**, **deterministic**, uses a **single-time estimate** for each activity, and explicitly focuses on the **time-cost trade-off** (project crashing).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wedding vs. House-Building" Intuition

| Real-World Scenario | Equivalent Technique | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Planning a **wedding** (photography, catering, décor, music) — you don't know exact guest count, weather, vendor delays | **PERT** | High uncertainty, time estimates are guesses, only completion time matters |
| Building a **house** (excavation, foundation, brickwork, plastering) — each task has known labour & material cost | **CPM** | Repetitive, predictable, time AND cost are both critical |

**Plain-English Summary:**
- Think of **PERT** as *"I have no idea how long this will take, so let me guess three different durations and take an average."*
- Think of **CPM** as *"I know exactly how long each step takes, and I want to know which steps I can speed up by spending more money."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board High-Yield Point:**
> Modern software (MS Project, Primavera) **merges PERT and CPM**, but for exam purposes, you **must** treat them as conceptually distinct. Examiners frequently test the *origin, orientation, estimate type, and application area* of each.

---

## 1.3 Origin & Historical Context

| Parameter | PERT | CPM |
|---|---|---|
| **Developed by** | U.S. Navy (Special Projects Office) | DuPont & Remington Rand |
| **Year** | **1958** | **1957** |
| **Original Project** | Polaris Submarine Weapon System | Construction of chemical plants |
| **Primary Focus** | Time | Time and Cost |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Philosophy of Each Technique

### PERT (Probabilistic Model)
- Treats activity durations as **random variables** drawn from a **Beta Probability Distribution**.
- A manager provides three estimates because real-world research & development tasks lack historical data.
- The model computes a **weighted expected time** that emphasises the *most likely* estimate (weighted **4×**).
- Useful when activities are **one-time, non-repetitive, and uncertain** (e.g., R&D, defense, software prototyping).

### CPM (Deterministic Model)
- Treats activity durations as **fixed constants** based on historical productivity data.
- The model computes the **critical path** — the longest sequence of activities that determines the minimum project duration.
- Allows **crashing** (cost-based compression) — paying extra money to reduce activity time.
- Useful when activities are **repetitive and well-understood** (e.g., construction, manufacturing, maintenance).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_e = \dfrac{o + 4m + p}{6}$ | Expected time of an activity | **PERT only** |
| 2 | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{p - o}{6}\right)^2$ | Variance of an activity's duration | **PERT only** |
| 3 | $\sigma = \dfrac{p - o}{6}$ | Standard deviation | **PERT only** |
| 4 | $\sigma_{CP}^2 = \sum \sigma_i^2$ | Project variance = sum of variances along Critical Path | **PERT only** |
| 5 | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{CP}}$ | Probability factor (where $T_s$ = scheduled time) | **PERT only** |
| 6 | $\text{Slack (Float)} = LS - ES = LF - EF$ | Free time an activity can be delayed | **CPM only** |
| 7 | $C_t = C_n + C_c \cdot t$ | Direct cost vs. time linear relationship (crashing) | **CPM only** |
| 8 | $\text{Cost Slope} = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c}$ | Cost per unit time reduction | **CPM only** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Guide for KTU Board:**
> - $o$ = Optimistic time (best-case scenario)
> - $m$ = Most likely time (modal value)
> - $p$ = Pessimistic time (worst-case scenario)
> - $C_c$ = Crash cost | $C_n$ = Normal cost
> - $t_c$ = Crash time | $t_n$ = Normal time

---

## 2.3 Parameter Definitions Explained

- **Optimistic time ($o$):** The shortest possible time an activity can be completed if everything goes perfectly. Statistical probability of this is very low (≈ 1 in 100).
- **Most likely time ($m$):** The time an activity will most probably take under normal conditions. This is the **mode** of the Beta distribution.
- **Pessimistic time ($p$):** The longest possible time if everything goes wrong. Statistical probability of this is very low (≈ 1 in 100).
- **Earliest Start (ES):** The earliest time an activity can begin, equal to the maximum EF of all its predecessors.
- **Earliest Finish (EF):** $ES + \text{Duration}$.
- **Latest Start (LS):** The latest time an activity can begin without delaying the project: $LS = LF - \text{Duration}$.
- **Latest Finish (LF):** The minimum of LS values of all successor activities.
- **Critical Path:** The longest-duration path through the network; has **zero float/slack**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PERT** is used by ISRO, DRDO, NASA for satellite launch mission planning where R&D uncertainty dominates.
- **CPM** is used by L&T, Tata Projects, and construction giants for highway, bridge, and building projects where labour productivity is well-known.
- In modern **IT Project Management**, hybrid PERT-CPM is the default in **Agile-Waterfall hybrid models**, where the WBS is scheduled using CPM logic and effort estimates are PERT-style three-point estimates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of PERT Expected Time Formula $t_e$

### Why do we need an "expected" time?

In PERT, since activity duration is treated as a random variable, we need a **single representative value** to enter into the network diagram and compute the critical path. This single value is the **mean of the Beta distribution**, which PERT assumes underlies activity durations.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The Beta distribution's probability density function for activity duration $t$ is:

$$
f(t) = \dfrac{\Gamma(\alpha + \beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)} \cdot (t - a)^{\alpha - 1}(b - t)^{\beta - 1}
$$

where:
- $a$ = lower bound of duration
- $b$ = upper bound of duration
- $\alpha, \beta$ = shape parameters

**Step 1:** The mean (expected value) of the Beta distribution is:

$$
E(t) = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot b + \beta \cdot a}{\alpha + \beta}
$$

**Step 2:** In PERT, the relationship between shape parameters and the three estimates is established empirically as:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{4m + o - 5a}{b - a} \quad \text{and} \quad \beta = \dfrac{5b - o - 4m}{b - a}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $a = o$ (lower bound) and $b = p$ (upper bound) into $E(t)$:

$$
E(t) = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot p + \beta \cdot o}{\alpha + \beta}
$$

**Step 4:** Through algebraic simplification of the Beta distribution parameters, the final closed-form PERT formula is obtained:

$$
\boxed{\,t_e = \dfrac{o + 4m + p}{6}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check:** The *most likely* estimate $m$ gets a weight of **4** (the highest) because PERT assumes that the modal value is the most realistic. Optimistic and Pessimistic each get a weight of **1**, reflecting their low probability. Total weight = $1 + 4 + 1 = 6$ — hence the denominator.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of PERT Variance $\sigma^2$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The variance of the Beta distribution is:

$$
\sigma^2 = \dfrac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha + \beta)^2 (\alpha + \beta + 1)}
$$

**Step 2:** Substituting the PERT parameters and simplifying, the variance reduces to:

$$
\sigma^2 = \dfrac{(p - o)^2}{36}
$$

**Step 3:** Hence, the final PERT variance formula is:

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{p - o}{6}\right)^2\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Variance depends **only on the spread** between Optimistic and Pessimistic. A wider range ($p - o$) implies greater uncertainty, larger variance, and less reliable project completion estimate.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** An activity has Optimistic time $o = 4$ days, Most Likely time $m = 10$ days, Pessimistic time $p = 24$ days. Compute expected time and variance.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the expected time formula:**

$$
t_e = \dfrac{o + 4m + p}{6}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
t_e = \dfrac{4 + 4(10) + 24}{6}
$$

$$
t_e = \dfrac{4 + 40 + 24}{6}
$$

$$
t_e = \dfrac{68}{6} = 11.33 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the variance formula:**

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{p - o}{6}\right)^2
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{24 - 4}{6}\right)^2
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{20}{6}\right)^2
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = (3.33)^2 = 11.11 \text{ days}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{11.11} = 3.33 \text{ days}
$$

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive PERT vs CPM Comparison Matrix

| # | Parameter | **PERT** | **CPM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Orientation** | Event-oriented (nodes = events) | Activity-oriented (nodes = activities, usually arrow diagram) |
| 2 | **Estimate Type** | Three-time estimates ($o, m, p$) | Single-time estimate |
| 3 | **Nature** | Probabilistic / Stochastic | Deterministic |
| 4 | **Distribution** | Beta Distribution | None (assumes fixed duration) |
| 5 | **Focus** | Time only | Time AND Cost |
| 6 | **Crashing** | Not directly supported | Explicitly supported (cost-time trade-off) |
| 7 | **Project Type** | Non-repetitive, R&D, one-time jobs | Repetitive, construction, manufacturing |
| 8 | **Network Model** | Activity-on-Arrow (AOA) preferred | Activity-on-Node (AON) preferred |
| 9 | **Critical Path Computation** | Probabilistic, with confidence intervals | Deterministic, single value |
| 10 | **Dummy Activities** | Frequently required | Rarely required |
| 11 | **Information Required** | Time estimates of activities | Time & cost estimates of activities |
| 12 | **Suitability** | High uncertainty projects | Well-defined, predictable projects |
| 13 | **Output Metric** | Probability of completion by a deadline | Earliest & latest start/finish, slack, crashing cost |
| 14 | **Year of Origin** | **1958** | **1957** |
| 15 | **Originator** | U.S. Navy | DuPont & Remington Rand |
| 16 | **Slack/Float Usage** | Not central | Central concept |
| 17 | **Updating** | Frequently updated as data arrives | Updated when activities complete |
| 18 | **Resource Allocation** | Not a primary focus | Can be incorporated |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PERT Three-Estimates Conceptual Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Activity to be estimated] --> nodeB[Ask three questions]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Optimistic time o]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Most likely time m]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Pessimistic time p]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Compute te using formula o+4m+p over 6]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute variance using p minus o over 6 squared]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Sum variances along critical path]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compute project probability using Z formula]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Output: Probability of on-time completion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The flow shows that PERT begins with a single activity, gathers three subjective time estimates from the project manager, computes a weighted expected time, then propagates uncertainty through the critical path to give a probabilistic completion forecast.

---

## 4.2 CPM Cost-Time Trade-Off & Crashing Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Start: Activity with normal time tn and normal cost Cn] --> nodeB[Identify critical path]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute cost slope for each critical activity]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Select activity with lowest cost slope]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Crash activity by 1 time unit]
    nodeE --> nodeF{New critical path appears?}
    nodeF -->|No| nodeG[Continue crashing]
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeH[Recompute critical paths]
    nodeH --> nodeD
    nodeG --> nodeI[Stop at optimum project duration]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Output: Minimum cost schedule]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Note:** CPM crashing terminates when the marginal cost of crashing exceeds the penalty cost of project delay. This is the "optimum cost-time point" that CPM uniquely enables — something PERT cannot do directly.

---

## 4.3 PERT vs CPM — Block-Level Functional Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph blockA[PERT FUNCTIONAL ARCHITECTURE]
        pA1[Input: Three Time Estimates per Activity] --> pA2[Processor: Beta Distribution Engine]
        pA2 --> pA3[Output: Expected Time and Variance]
        pA3 --> pA4[Network Engine: Compute Critical Path]
        pA4 --> pA5[Output: Probabilistic Completion Forecast]
    end

    subgraph blockB[CPM FUNCTIONAL ARCHITECTURE]
        cB1[Input: Single Time and Cost per Activity] --> cB2[Processor: Forward and Backward Pass]
        cB2 --> cB3[Output: ES, EF, LS, LF, Slack]
        cB3 --> cB4[Crashing Engine: Cost Slope Optimisation]
        cB4 --> cB5[Output: Minimum Duration and Cost Schedule]
    end

    pA5 -. Hybrid Modern Use .-> cB1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two architectures are independent but complementary. Modern project management software feeds PERT's output (expected times) directly into CPM's deterministic engine — this is the basis of tools like MS Project and Primavera P6.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any **six differences** between PERT and CPM.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | PERT | CPM |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Event-oriented | Activity-oriented |
| 2 | Three-time estimates $(o, m, p)$ | Single-time estimate |
| 3 | Probabilistic (Beta distribution) | Deterministic |
| 4 | Time-focused only | Time-and-cost focused |
| 5 | Suitable for R&D, one-time jobs | Suitable for repetitive construction jobs |
| 6 | Developed in 1958 (U.S. Navy) | Developed in 1957 (DuPont) |

*(Any 6 of the 18 differences in the comparison matrix are acceptable — ½ mark each.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*An activity has $o = 2$, $m = 8$, $p = 18$ days. Compute the **expected time** and **variance**.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the expected time formula:

$$
t_e = \dfrac{o + 4m + p}{6} = \dfrac{2 + 4(8) + 18}{6} = \dfrac{2 + 32 + 18}{6} = \dfrac{52}{6} = 8.67 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the variance formula:

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{p - o}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{18 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{16}{6}\right)^2 = 7.11 \text{ days}^2
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_e = 8.67$ days, $\sigma^2 = 7.11$ days².

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **PERT technique** of project management in detail. State the PERT expected time and variance formulas with the meaning of each parameter.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The following table shows three-time estimates (in weeks) for activities in a small software project network:

| Activity | Optimistic $o$ | Most Likely $m$ | Pessimistic $p$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1-2 | 3 | 6 | 15 |
| 2-3 | 2 | 4 | 12 |
| 2-4 | 1 | 2 | 9 |
| 3-4 | 4 | 5 | 12 |
| 4-5 | 3 | 6 | 9 |

Compute the **expected time** and **variance** for each activity, identify the **critical path**, and the **expected project duration**.

---

### ⭐ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — PERT Explanation [7 Marks]

**Definition:** PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) is a probabilistic project management tool developed in 1958 by the U.S. Navy's Special Projects Office for the Polaris missile program. It is used to plan, schedule, and control projects where activity durations are uncertain. **[1 mark]**

**Three-Time Estimates:**

$$
o = \text{Optimistic time}, \quad m = \text{Most likely time}, \quad p = \text{Pessimistic time}
$$

**Expected Time Formula:**

$$
t_e = \dfrac{o + 4m + p}{6}
$$

The denominator 6 arises because the weights are $1 + 4 + 1 = 6$, where $m$ carries 4× weight (most realistic). **[2 marks]**

**Variance Formula:**

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{p - o}{6}\right)^2
$$

Variance measures the uncertainty in the estimate. A wider spread of $(p - o)$ implies higher uncertainty. **[1 mark]**

**Steps of PERT:**
1. Identify activities and their predecessors.
2. Collect three-time estimates from experts.
3. Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$ for each activity.
4. Draw the network diagram using $t_e$ values.
5. Perform forward and backward pass.
6. Identify the critical path (longest path).
7. Compute project variance by summing $\sigma^2$ along critical path.
8. Compute probability of completion using $Z$ formula:

$$
Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{CP}}
$$

where $T_s$ = scheduled time, $T_e$ = expected project time, $\sigma_{CP}$ = critical path standard deviation. **[3 marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$ for each activity** [3 marks]

**Activity 1-2:**

$$
t_e = \dfrac{3 + 4(6) + 15}{6} = \dfrac{42}{6} = 7.00 \text{ weeks}
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{15 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = (2.00)^2 = 4.00
$$

**Activity 2-3:**

$$
t_e = \dfrac{2 + 4(4) + 12}{6} = \dfrac{30}{6} = 5.00 \text{ weeks}
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{12 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = (1.67)^2 = 2.78
$$

**Activity 2-4:**

$$
t_e = \dfrac{1 + 4(2) + 9}{6} = \dfrac{18}{6} = 3.00 \text{ weeks}
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{9 - 1}{6}\right)^2 = (1.33)^2 = 1.78
$$

**Activity 3-4:**

$$
t_e = \dfrac{4 + 4(5) + 12}{6} = \dfrac{36}{6} = 6.00 \text{ weeks}
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{12 - 4}{6}\right)^2 = (1.33)^2 = 1.78
$$

**Activity 4-5:**

$$
t_e = \dfrac{3 + 4(6) + 9}{6} = \dfrac{36}{6} = 6.00 \text{ weeks}
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{9 - 3}{6}\right)^2 = (1.00)^2 = 1.00
$$

**Step 2: Network Diagram Paths** [1 mark]

The possible paths from node 1 to node 5 are:
- **Path 1:** 1-2-3-4-5 → $7 + 5 + 6 + 6 = 24$ weeks
- **Path 2:** 1-2-4-5 → $7 + 3 + 6 = 16$ weeks

**Step 3: Identify Critical Path** [1 mark]

The critical path is the **longest** path. Comparing 24 and 16 weeks, the critical path is:

$$
\boxed{\,1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5 \text{ with duration } 24 \text{ weeks}\,}
$$

**Step 4: Project Variance** [1 mark]

Sum of variances along the critical path (activities 1-2, 2-3, 3-4, 4-5):

$$
\sigma_{CP}^2 = 4.00 + 2.78 + 1.78 + 1.00 = 9.56 \text{ weeks}^2
$$

**Step 5: Project Standard Deviation** [1 mark]

$$
\sigma_{CP} = \sqrt{9.56} = 3.09 \text{ weeks}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Expected Project Duration** = **24 weeks**
- **Project Standard Deviation** = **3.09 weeks**
- **Critical Path** = 1-2-3-4-5

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CPM technique** of project management. Discuss the concept of **project crashing** and the **cost slope** with a suitable diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An activity has normal time $t_n = 10$ days, normal cost $C_n = \text{Rs. } 2000$, crash time $t_c = 6$ days, and crash cost $C_c = \text{Rs. } 3200$. Compute the **cost slope** and the **maximum number of days** the activity can be crashed.

---

### ⭐ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — CPM Explanation [7 Marks]

**Definition:** CPM (Critical Path Method) is a deterministic project management technique developed in 1957 by DuPont and Remington Rand for chemical plant construction. It is activity-oriented and uses single-time estimates based on historical productivity data. **[1 mark]**

**Key Concepts:** **[2 marks]**
- **Critical Path:** The longest-duration path through the network; determines the minimum project duration. Any delay on this path delays the whole project.
- **Float/Slack:** The time an activity can be delayed without affecting the project finish date. Critical activities have zero float.
- **Forward Pass:** Computes ES (Earliest Start) and EF (Earliest Finish).
- **Backward Pass:** Computes LS (Latest Start) and LF (Latest Finish).

**Project Crashing:** **[2 marks]**
Crashing is the technique of **shortening project duration** by adding extra resources (overtime, more labour, better equipment) at additional cost. The decision to crash is governed by the **cost slope** (also called cost gradient), defined as:

$$
\text{Cost Slope} = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c}
$$

where $C_c$ = crash cost, $C_n$ = normal cost, $t_c$ = crash time, $t_n$ = normal time. The unit is **currency per unit time** (e.g., Rs. per day).

**Crashing Procedure:** **[2 marks]**
1. Identify the critical path.
2. Compute cost slope for each activity on the critical path.
3. Select the activity with the **lowest cost slope** and crash it by one unit.
4. Recompute the network — if a new critical path appears, crash both paths simultaneously using the **combined cost slope**.
5. Stop when the marginal crashing cost exceeds the penalty for delay.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $t_n = 10$ days, $C_n = \text{Rs. } 2000$
- $t_c = 6$ days, $C_c = \text{Rs. } 3200$

**Step 1: Compute maximum crash days** [2 marks]

$$
\Delta t_{max} = t_n - t_c = 10 - 6 = 4 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2: Compute extra cost due to crashing** [2 marks]

$$
\Delta C = C_c - C_n = 3200 - 2000 = \text{Rs. } 1200
$$

**Step 3: Compute cost slope** [3 marks]

$$
\text{Cost Slope} = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c} = \dfrac{1200}{4} = \text{Rs. } 300 \text{ per day}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Maximum crash days** = **4 days**
- **Cost Slope** = **Rs. 300 per day**

**Interpretation:** The activity can be shortened by a maximum of 4 days, at an additional cost of Rs. 300 for every day crashed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing event-oriented and activity-oriented:** Examiners award **2 marks** for clearly stating PERT is event-oriented and CPM is activity-oriented. Many students write the opposite — **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong denominator:** Some students write $t_e = (o + 4m + p)/6$ as $(o + m + p)/3$ (simple average). This is the **arithmetic mean**, NOT the PERT expected time — **lose 3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting to add variances along the critical path:** In PERT probability problems, you must sum variances of **all activities on the critical path only** — not all activities. Wrong summation = **lose 2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing normal cost with crash cost in CPM:** $C_n$ is for **normal time $t_n$** (longer, cheaper), and $C_c$ is for **crash time $t_c$** (shorter, expensive). Reversing these breaks the cost slope formula — **lose 2 marks**.
> 5. **Not identifying the critical path explicitly:** Always state "The critical path is X → Y → Z with duration N weeks" as a separate bullet. Examiners want clarity — **lose 1 mark** if missing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

### 🔑 Must-Remember Points

- **PERT = Probabilistic, 3 estimates, Event-oriented, Time only, R&D projects, 1958 (US Navy).**
- **CPM = Deterministic, 1 estimate, Activity-oriented, Time & Cost, Construction projects, 1957 (DuPont).**
- The **PERT expected time formula** is $t_e = (o + 4m + p)/6$ — denominator is **6**, not 3.
- The **PERT variance formula** is $\sigma^2 = ((p - o)/6)^2$ — depends **only** on the spread between $p$ and $o$.
- The **critical path** is the **longest** path through the network; its duration equals the **minimum project duration**.
- **Slack/Float = LS − ES = LF − EF**; critical path activities have **zero slack**.
- In CPM, **cost slope** measures the extra money paid per day of time saved: $(C_c - C_n) / (t_n - t_c)$.
- **Project variance in PERT** = **sum of variances of activities on the critical path only**.
- The **Z formula** for probability of on-time completion is: $Z = (T_s - T_e) / \sigma_{CP}$.
- Modern IT project management uses a **PERT-CPM hybrid** — three-point estimates feed into a CPM network.

### 🚫 Frequently Confused Concepts

| Confusion | Clarification |
|---|---|
| "PERT gives exact dates" | **Wrong.** PERT gives **probabilities**, not exact dates. |
| "CPM cannot handle cost" | **Wrong.** CPM is **explicitly** designed for time-cost trade-off (crashing). |
| "Variance is the sum of durations" | **Wrong.** Variance is sum of **$\sigma^2$ of critical path activities**, not durations. |
| "All paths are critical" | **Wrong.** Only the **longest** path is critical; others have float. |

### 📚 KTU 2024 Board Exam Weightage (Indicative)

- **Direct "Differences" question:** 3–5 marks (high frequency in Part A).
- **Numerical on $t_e$ and $\sigma^2$:** 7 marks in Part B (almost every exam).
- **Critical path identification:** 5–7 marks (combined with above).
- **Crashing / cost slope numerical:** 7 marks in Part B (every alternate exam).
- **Conceptual "PERT vs CPM" essay:** 7–10 marks (Part B choice questions).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Float Calculation and Its Importance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Float Calculation and Its Importance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Project Network Scheduling** and the **Critical Path Method (CPM)**, **Float** (also known as **Slack**) is formally defined as the measure of the amount of time that a scheduled activity can be **delayed**, **postponed**, or **extended** without causing a delay to the **Project Completion Date** (Total Float) or any **immediate successor activity** (Free Float).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *IT Project Management (PBCBT604)*, Float is one of the most critical quantitative outputs of the **Forward Pass** and **Backward Pass** computations performed on a **Activity-On-Arrow (AOA)** or **Activity-On-Node (AON)** network diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PBCBT604, Module 1):**
> Float calculation is a foundational concept under *Project Scheduling and Network Techniques*. It directly determines the **Critical Path**, enables **Resource Leveling**, and supports **Risk Management** in IT projects.

## 1.2 Types of Float — The Four Pillars

| # | Float Type | Acronym | Definition (Rigor) |
|---|------------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Total Float** | **TF** | Time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **project finish date**. |
| 2 | **Free Float** | **FF** | Time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **early start of any successor**. |
| 3 | **Independent Float** | **IF** | Time an activity can be delayed when its **predecessors finish as late as possible** AND its **successors start as early as possible**. |
| 4 | **Interfering Float** | **IntF** | The portion of Total Float that, if consumed, **delays the project**. Mathematically: $IntF = TF - FF$. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Train Journey" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Indian Railways Reservation System:**
> Imagine you are planning a **12-hour train journey from Kochi to Delhi** with multiple halts. Each halt is an "activity."
> - **Total Float (TF):** If the train can still reach Delhi at the scheduled arrival time (e.g., 8:00 AM next day), any extra time you spend at any halt is your Total Float.
> - **Free Float (FF):** If a halt has a 15-minute buffer *before* the next train connection must depart, that 15 minutes is the Free Float — you can use it without making the next person miss their train.
> - **Independent Float (IF):** The buffer time you have when the *previous* train was already late AND the *next* train is scheduled to leave early. Usually the smallest of the three.
> - **Critical Activity:** A halt with **zero float** — the train *must* depart on time, or the entire journey (project) gets delayed.

## 1.4 Significance in IT Project Management

In **software development lifecycles (SDLC)** — Agile, Waterfall, or Hybrid — float values are used by Project Managers to:
1. **Identify the Critical Path** — activities with $TF = 0$.
2. **Level Resources** — re-assign developers from non-critical to critical tasks.
3. **Manage Risk** — activities with high float are *safer* to delay.
4. **Crash/Compress the Schedule** — activities with float can be shortened with minimal impact.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind Float — The "Forward and Backward" Duality

Float is calculated using a **two-pass algorithm** on the project network:

### 2.1.1 Forward Pass (Calculates Early Times)

The forward pass moves **left-to-right** through the network, computing the **earliest** possible start and finish times for every activity, given that all predecessors must complete first.

### 2.1.2 Backward Pass (Calculates Late Times)

The backward pass moves **right-to-left** through the network, computing the **latest** allowable start and finish times, ensuring that the project finishes on time.

## 2.2 Foundational Building Blocks (EST, EFT, LST, LFT)

Before computing any float, the student **must** compute the four key time parameters for every activity:

| Parameter | Symbol | Forward Pass Formula | Backward Pass Formula |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Early Start Time | **EST** | $\max(EST_{predecessors} + D_{predecessors})$ | — |
| Early Finish Time | **EFT** | $EST + D$ | — |
| Late Start Time | **LST** | — | $LFT - D$ |
| Late Finish Time | **LFT** | — | $\min(LST_{successors})$ |

Where $D$ = duration of the activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** For the **first activity** (no predecessor), $EST = 0$. For the **last activity** (project end), $LFT = \max(EFT_{all\ ending\ activities}) = Project\ Duration$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for KTU Exams:** Memorize these five formulas. They appear in **every** network-based question (3-mark, 7-mark, and 14-mark).

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Engineering Meaning |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Total Float (TF)** | $TF = LST - EST$ | Schedule flexibility without project delay |
| 2 | **Total Float (Alt Form)** | $TF = LFT - EFT$ | Same as above, alternative computation |
| 3 | **Free Float (FF)** | $FF = \min(EST_{successors}) - EFT$ | Delay allowed without affecting successor's early start |
| 4 | **Independent Float (IF)** | $IF = \min(EST_{successors}) - LFT - D$ | Float assuming worst-case predecessor & best-case successor |
| 5 | **Interfering Float (IntF)** | $IntF = TF - FF$ | Portion of float that becomes "project delay" if consumed |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For the **last activity** of a project, Free Float is conventionally **0**, not a positive value — because there is no successor whose early start would be impacted.

## 2.4 Why Float Matters — The Engineering & Managerial Utility

In real-world **IT Project Management**, float is not just an academic number. It is a **decision-making currency**:

1. **Resource Leveling:** If a developer is free during a non-critical activity's float window, the PM can re-allocate them to critical tasks without harming the schedule.
2. **Cost-Time Trade-off (Crashing):** Activities with float can be crashed (expedited) at lower cost first, before crashing critical path activities.
3. **Risk Management:** Activities with $TF > 0$ are **buffers** against unforeseen delays (e.g., bug fixes, server downtime, vendor delays).
4. **Earned Value Management (EVM):** The **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** is partially derived by comparing actual progress against float consumption.
5. **Agile/Sprint Planning:** The float concept translates into **slack in user stories** — a story with slack (float) is a candidate for deferral to a later sprint.

## 2.5 Theoretical Properties of Float

- $TF \geq FF \geq IF \geq 0$ (in a valid project network)
- $TF = 0 \implies$ Activity is on the **Critical Path**
- $IF$ is rarely positive in **series** networks but can be positive in **parallel/merge** structures
- The **sum of all Free Floats** of terminal activities equals the **sum of Total Floats** of all activities on non-critical paths

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — AON Network with 6 Activities

Consider the following **IT Project** with the activities and dependencies below (all durations in days):

| Activity | Duration (D) | Predecessors |
|----------|:-----------:|:------------:|
| A (Requirement Gathering) | 3 | — |
| B (Database Design) | 4 | A |
| C (UI Wireframe) | 2 | A |
| D (Backend API) | 5 | B |
| E (Frontend Code) | 6 | C |
| F (Integration Testing) | 3 | D, E |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Forward Pass (Calculating EST & EFT)

**Step 1 — Activity A (Start node, no predecessor):**
By boundary condition, the EST of the first activity is **0**.
$$
EFT_A = EST_A + D_A = 0 + 3 = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Activity B (Predecessor: A):**
B can start only after A finishes.
$$
EST_B = EFT_A = 3
$$
$$
EFT_B = EST_B + D_B = 3 + 4 = 7
$$

**Step 3 — Activity C (Predecessor: A):**
C also has A as the sole predecessor.
$$
EST_C = EFT_A = 3
$$
$$
EFT_C = EST_C + D_C = 3 + 2 = 5
$$

**Step 4 — Activity D (Predecessor: B):**
$$
EST_D = EFT_B = 7
$$
$$
EFT_D = EST_D + D_D = 7 + 5 = 12
$$

**Step 5 — Activity E (Predecessor: C):**
$$
EST_E = EFT_C = 5
$$
$$
EFT_E = EST_E + D_E = 5 + 6 = 11
$$

**Step 6 — Activity F (Predecessors: D AND E — Merge node):**
For merge nodes, EST is the **maximum** of all predecessor EFTs.
$$
EST_F = \max(EFT_D,\ EFT_E) = \max(12,\ 11) = 12
$$
$$
EFT_F = EST_F + D_F = 12 + 3 = 15
$$

**Project Duration = 15 days.**

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Backward Pass (Calculating LST & LFT)

**Step 7 — Activity F (End node, LFT = Project Duration):**
Boundary condition: $LFT_F = EFT_F = 15$
$$
LST_F = LFT_F - D_F = 15 - 3 = 12
$$

**Step 8 — Activity D (Successor: F):**
$$
LFT_D = LST_F = 12
$$
$$
LST_D = LFT_D - D_D = 12 - 5 = 7
$$

**Step 9 — Activity E (Successor: F):**
$$
LFT_E = LST_F = 12
$$
$$
LST_E = LFT_E - D_E = 12 - 6 = 6
$$

**Step 10 — Activity C (Successor: E):**
$$
LFT_C = LST_E = 6
$$
$$
LST_C = LFT_C - D_C = 6 - 2 = 4
$$

**Step 11 — Activity B (Successor: D):**
$$
LFT_B = LST_D = 7
$$
$$
LST_B = LFT_B - D_B = 7 - 4 = 3
$$

**Step 12 — Activity A (Successors: B AND C — Burst node):**
For burst nodes, LFT is the **minimum** of all successor LSTs.
$$
LFT_A = \min(LST_B,\ LST_C) = \min(3,\ 4) = 3
$$
$$
LST_A = LFT_A - D_A = 3 - 3 = 0
$$

## 3.4 Float Calculation Matrix — The Consolidated Result

Now we apply all five formulas from Section 2.3 to every activity:

| Activity | D | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | $TF = LST - EST$ | $FF = EST_{succ} - EFT$ | $IF$ | Critical? |
|:--------:|:-:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:----------------:|:----------------------:|:----:|:---------:|
| A | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **0** | $\min(3,3)-3 = 0$ | — | ✅ Yes |
| B | 4 | 3 | 7 | 3 | 7 | **0** | $7-7=0$ | — | ✅ Yes |
| C | 2 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 6 | **1** | $5-5=0$ | $5-6-2=-3\to 0$ | ❌ No |
| D | 5 | 7 | 12 | 7 | 12 | **0** | $12-12=0$ | — | ✅ Yes |
| E | 6 | 5 | 11 | 6 | 12 | **1** | $12-11=1$ | $12-12-6=-6\to 0$ | ❌ No |
| F | 3 | 12 | 15 | 12 | 15 | **0** | $15-15=0$ | — | ✅ Yes |

## 3.5 Interpretation of Results

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Path = A → B → D → F** (Total Project Duration = 15 days)
> - **Float of Activity C = 1 day:** Requirement UI Wireframe can be delayed by 1 day without delaying the project, but it will delay the start of Frontend (E) by 1 day.
> - **Float of Activity E = 1 day:** Frontend can be delayed by 1 day without delaying the project, but it will reduce the buffer for Integration Testing.
> - **Interfering Float for C:** $IntF = TF - FF = 1 - 0 = 1$ day. If C is delayed by 1 day, it will delay the project by 1 day (E consumes C's float and then becomes critical).
> - **Independent Float for C and E:** Both are negative, hence 0 (cannot be independently delayed without impacting the network).

## 3.6 Verification by Path Analysis

Sum of durations along each path from Start to End:
$$
\text{Path 1: } A \to B \to D \to F = 3 + 4 + 5 + 3 = 15 \text{ days (Critical)}
$$
$$
\text{Path 2: } A \to C \to E \to F = 3 + 2 + 6 + 3 = 14 \text{ days}
$$
$$
\text{Float of Path 2} = 15 - 14 = 1 \text{ day} \ \checkmark
$$

This confirms the TF of activities C and E is indeed 1 day.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Activity-on-Node (AON) Network Diagram

The following Mermaid **flowchart** represents the project network with all 6 activities, their durations, EST/EFT/LST/LFT values, and float calculations embedded into each node:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode([START]) --> nodeA["A: Req Gathering\nD=3\nES=0 EF=3\nLS=0 LF=3\nTF=0 FF=0"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["B: DB Design\nD=4\nES=3 EF=7\nLS=3 LF=7\nTF=0 FF=0"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["C: UI Wireframe\nD=2\nES=3 EF=5\nLS=4 LF=6\nTF=1 FF=0"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["D: Backend API\nD=5\nES=7 EF=12\nLS=7 LF=12\nTF=0 FF=0"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["E: Frontend Code\nD=6\nES=5 EF=11\nLS=6 LF=12\nTF=1 FF=1"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["F: Integration Test\nD=3\nES=12 EF=15\nLS=12 LF=15\nTF=0 FF=0"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> endNode([END - 15 days])

    classDef critical fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef noncritical fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef boundary fill:#339af0,stroke:#1864ab,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    class startNode,endNode boundary
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeD,nodeF critical
    class nodeC,nodeE noncritical
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:**
> - **Red nodes (Critical Path):** A → B → D → F — These have $TF = 0$. Any delay here **delays the project**.
> - **Green nodes (Non-Critical):** C → E — These have $TF = 1$ day. They can absorb minor delays.
> - **Blue nodes (Boundaries):** Start and End markers.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Float Calculation Engine

The following diagram illustrates the **internal data-flow architecture** of how a project management software (e.g., MS Project, Primavera P6) computes float internally. This is the **algorithmic engine** that students must conceptually understand for KTU exams:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        in1["Activity List\n(Name, Duration)"]
        in2["Precedence\nRelationships"]
        in3["Network Diagram\n(AOA or AON)"]
    end

    subgraph forwardPass["FORWARD PASS MODULE"]
        fp1["Initialize EST of\nStart Activity = 0"]
        fp2["For each activity:\nEST = max EFT of\nall predecessors"]
        fp3["Compute EFT =\nEST + Duration"]
        fp4["Project Duration =\nmax EFT of End node"]
    end

    subgraph backwardPass["BACKWARD PASS MODULE"]
        bp1["Initialize LFT of\nEnd Activity =\nProject Duration"]
        bp2["For each activity:\nLFT = min LST of\nall successors"]
        bp3["Compute LST =\nLFT - Duration"]
    end

    subgraph floatEngine["FLOAT CALCULATION ENGINE"]
        fl1["TF = LST - EST"]
        fl2["FF = min EST of\nsuccessors - EFT"]
        fl3["IF = min EST of\nsuccessors - LFT - D"]
        fl4["IntF = TF - FF"]
    end

    subgraph outputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        out1["Critical Path\n(TF = 0 activities)"]
        out2["Float Table\n(TF, FF, IF)"]
        out3["Gantt Chart with\nFloat Bars"]
        out4["Resource Leveling\nRecommendations"]
    end

    in1 --> fp1
    in2 --> fp2
    in3 --> fp2
    fp1 --> fp2 --> fp3 --> fp4
    fp4 --> bp1
    fp4 --> bp2
    bp1 --> bp2 --> bp3
    fp3 --> fl1
    bp3 --> fl1
    fp3 --> fl2
    fp3 --> fl3
    fl1 --> fl4
    fl1 --> out1
    fl1 --> out2
    fl2 --> out2
    fl3 --> out2
    fl2 --> out3
    fl1 --> out4

    classDef inputStyle fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef fpStyle fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#f59f00,color:#78350f
    classDef bpStyle fill:#f3d9fa,stroke:#9c36b5,color:#581c87
    classDef flStyle fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#14532d
    classDef outStyle fill:#ffe3e3,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#7f1d1d

    class in1,in2,in3 inputStyle
    class fp1,fp2,fp3,fp4 fpStyle
    class bp1,bp2,bp3 bpStyle
    class fl1,fl2,fl3,fl4 flStyle
    class out1,out2,out3,out4 outStyle
```

## 4.3 Float Type Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tfBlock["TOTAL FLOAT (TF)"]
        tfDef["Time delay allowed\nWITHOUT delaying\nproject completion"]
    end
    subgraph ffBlock["FREE FLOAT (FF)"]
        ffDef["Time delay allowed\nWITHOUT delaying any\nsuccessor's early start"]
    end
    subgraph ifBlock["INDEPENDENT FLOAT (IF)"]
        ifDef["Time delay allowed when\npredecessors finish LATE\nand successors start EARLY"]
    end
    subgraph intfBlock["INTERFERING FLOAT (IntF)"]
        intfDef["Portion of TF that\nbecomes project delay\nif consumed = TF - FF"]
    end

    tfBlock -->|"contains"| ffBlock
    ffBlock -->|"contains"| ifBlock
    tfBlock -.->|"subtracts"| intfBlock

    classDef tfColor fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#e8590c,color:#7c2d12
    classDef ffColor fill:#a5d8ff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef ifColor fill:#b2f2bb,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#14532d
    classDef intfColor fill:#fcc2d7,stroke:#c2255c,color:#831843

    class tfDef tfColor
    class ffDef ffColor
    class ifDef ifColor
    class intfDef intfColor
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Insight:** The nested structure $TF \supseteq FF \supseteq IF$ is a **containment hierarchy**. Every Free Float is also a Total Float, and every Independent Float is also a Free Float — but the reverse is not true.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Total Float and Free Float. How are they different from each other?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Total Float (TF):** It is the amount of time a non-critical activity can be delayed **without delaying the project completion date**. Mathematically, $TF = LST - EST$ or $TF = LFT - EFT$. **[1 Mark]**
- **Free Float (FF):** It is the amount of time an activity can be delayed **without delaying the early start of any of its immediate successor activities**. Mathematically, $FF = \min(EST_{successors}) - EFT$. **[1 Mark]**
- **Key Difference:** Total Float is measured against the **project finish date**, while Free Float is measured against the **successor's early start**. Therefore, $TF \geq FF$ always. Consuming Free Float does **not** delay the project, but consuming only Total Float (beyond FF) **does** delay the project. **[1 Mark]**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Critical Path? What is its relationship with Total Float?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Critical Path Definition:** The Critical Path is the **longest path** through the project network, which determines the **minimum project duration**. It consists of activities with zero scheduling flexibility. **[1.5 Marks]**
- **Relationship with Total Float:** All activities on the Critical Path have a **Total Float (TF) of zero**. This means any delay in a critical activity **directly delays the project finish date** by an equal amount. Conversely, any activity with $TF > 0$ is a **non-critical activity** lying on a path shorter than the critical path. The float of a path = (Project Duration) − (Sum of durations along that path). **[1.5 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** For the project network given below, draw the network diagram and compute the **EST, EFT, LST, and LFT** for each activity using the Forward Pass and Backward Pass method.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** From the values obtained in part (a), calculate the **Total Float, Free Float, and Independent Float** for each activity. Identify the **Critical Path** and the **Project Duration**.

| Activity | Duration (Days) | Predecessors |
|----------|:---------------:|:------------:|
| P | 5 | — |
| Q | 3 | P |
| R | 7 | P |
| S | 4 | Q |
| T | 6 | R |
| U | 2 | S, T |

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

##### **Part (a) — Forward & Backward Pass [7 Marks]**

**Forward Pass Calculations:**

**Activity P (Start, No predecessor):**
$$
EST_P = 0,\quad EFT_P = 0 + 5 = 5
$$

**Activity Q (Predecessor: P):**
$$
EST_Q = EFT_P = 5,\quad EFT_Q = 5 + 3 = 8
$$

**Activity R (Predecessor: P):**
$$
EST_R = EFT_P = 5,\quad EFT_R = 5 + 7 = 12
$$

**Activity S (Predecessor: Q):**
$$
EST_S = EFT_Q = 8,\quad EFT_S = 8 + 4 = 12
$$

**Activity T (Predecessor: R):**
$$
EST_T = EFT_R = 12,\quad EFT_T = 12 + 6 = 18
$$

**Activity U (Predecessors: S AND T — Merge):**
$$
EST_U = \max(EFT_S,\ EFT_T) = \max(12,\ 18) = 18
$$
$$
EFT_U = 18 + 2 = 20
$$

**Project Duration = 20 days** ✓

**Backward Pass Calculations:**

**Activity U (End node):**
$$
LFT_U = 20,\quad LST_U = 20 - 2 = 18
$$

**Activity S (Successor: U):**
$$
LFT_S = LST_U = 18,\quad LST_S = 18 - 4 = 14
$$

**Activity T (Successor: U):**
$$
LFT_T = LST_U = 18,\quad LST_T = 18 - 6 = 12
$$

**Activity Q (Successor: S):**
$$
LFT_Q = LST_S = 14,\quad LST_Q = 14 - 3 = 11
$$

**Activity R (Successor: T):**
$$
LFT_R = LST_T = 12,\quad LST_R = 12 - 7 = 5
$$

**Activity P (Successors: Q AND R — Burst):**
$$
LFT_P = \min(LST_Q,\ LST_R) = \min(11,\ 5) = 5
$$
$$
LST_P = 5 - 5 = 0
$$

##### **Part (b) — Float Calculations & Critical Path [7 Marks]**

| Activity | D | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | $TF$ | $FF$ | $IF$ | Critical? |
|:--------:|:-:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:----:|:----:|:----:|:---------:|
| P | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | **0** | $\min(11,5)-5=0$ | — | ✅ |
| Q | 3 | 5 | 8 | 11 | 14 | **6** | $8-8=0$ | $8-14-3=-9\to 0$ | ❌ |
| R | 7 | 5 | 12 | 5 | 12 | **0** | $12-12=0$ | — | ✅ |
| S | 4 | 8 | 12 | 14 | 18 | **6** | $12-12=0$ | $12-18-4=-10\to 0$ | ❌ |
| T | 6 | 12 | 18 | 12 | 18 | **0** | $18-18=0$ | — | ✅ |
| U | 2 | 18 | 20 | 18 | 20 | **0** | $20-20=0$ | — | ✅ |

**Critical Path Identification:**
Activities with $TF = 0$: **P → R → T → U**

**Project Duration = 5 + 7 + 6 + 2 = 20 days** ✓

**Path-wise verification:**
- Path 1: $P \to Q \to S \to U = 5+3+4+2 = 14$ days → Float = 6 days
- Path 2: $P \to R \to T \to U = 5+7+6+2 = 20$ days → Float = 0 (Critical)

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a suitable example the concepts of **Total Float, Free Float, and Independent Float**. State the formulas for each.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** "Float is a measure of scheduling flexibility, not a guarantee of project delay." Discuss this statement with reference to **resource leveling** and **critical path management** in IT projects.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

##### **Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation with Example [7 Marks]**

**Example Setup:** Consider a 3-activity software module project:
- **Activity X** (Coding): Duration = 6 days, no predecessor.
- **Activity Y** (Code Review): Duration = 2 days, predecessor = X.
- **Activity Z** (Deployment): Duration = 3 days, predecessor = Y.

**Forward Pass:** $X: ES=0, EF=6$; $Y: ES=6, EF=8$; $Z: ES=8, EF=11$.
**Backward Pass:** $Z: LF=11, LS=8$; $Y: LF=8, LS=6$; $X: LF=6, LS=0$.

**Float Calculations:**
- **Total Float (TF):** $TF_X = LS_X - ES_X = 0 - 0 = 0$. Since this is a series (linear) network, all activities have $TF = 0$, i.e., the entire path is critical. **[2 Marks]**
- **Free Float (FF):** $FF_X = \min(ES_Y) - EF_X = 6 - 6 = 0$. In a series network, FF = 0 for all activities because the successor's ES = current activity's EF. **[1 Mark]**
- **Independent Float (IF):** $IF_X = \min(ES_{successor}) - LF_X - D_X = 6 - 6 - 6 = -6 \to 0$. IF is computed and clamped to non-negative. **[1 Mark]**
- **General Formulas:** $TF = LST - EST$, $FF = EST_{succ} - EFT$, $IF = EST_{succ} - LFT - D$. **[3 Marks — for stating all formulas clearly]**

##### **Part (b) — Discussion on Float as Flexibility [7 Marks]**

**Statement Analysis:**
The statement emphasizes that **float is a planning tool**, not a guarantee. The presence of float on an activity does not mean the activity "can be delayed without consequence" — it means that within the float window, the PM has *choices*. **[1 Mark]**

**Relevance to Resource Leveling:**
- In an IT project, a developer assigned to a non-critical activity (say, with $TF = 4$ days) can be **re-allocated to a critical-path activity** for up to 4 days without delaying the project.
- This is called **Resource Leveling** — smoothing out the demand for resources across the project timeline.
- Example: If a back-end developer is idle during the float of a documentation activity, they can be assigned to a critical bug-fix task. **[3 Marks]**

**Relevance to Critical Path Management:**
- Float helps the PM **focus managerial attention** on critical activities. Critical path activities ($TF = 0$) need **continuous monitoring**.
- Non-critical activities with float can be **deliberately compressed or fast-tracked** to free up resources.
- However, float is **consumable** — if a non-critical activity consumes all its float, it becomes critical, and the **critical path may shift** to a new sequence of activities. This phenomenon is called **Critical Path Migration**. **[2 Marks]**

**Conclusion:**
Float is best understood as **schedule cushion** or **decision-making currency**. The PM's job is not to "waste" float but to **strategically deploy** it for risk mitigation, resource optimization, and cost-time trade-offs. **[1 Mark]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the max() at merge nodes and min() at burst nodes** — this is the #1 error. At a merge node, $EST = \max(EF_{predecessors})$, NOT just the immediate predecessor.
> 2. **Confusing EST with LST** — Students often compute both forward and backward passes and mistakenly mix up the columns in the final table.
> 3. **Setting $FF$ of the last activity as equal to $TF$** — Convention says $FF = 0$ for the last activity.
> 4. **Forgetting to clamp $IF$ to zero** — Negative $IF$ should be reported as 0, not the negative number.
> 5. **Not showing intermediate calculation steps** — In 14-mark questions, KTU examiners **require** step-by-step working. Writing only the final table without intermediate steps costs 3-4 marks.
