> 6. **Incorrect Critical Path identification** — Students often include non-critical activities in the critical path because they miscalculated one TF value. Always double-check the path durations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Float Calculation (Module 1, PBCBT604)**

- **Float (Slack):** The amount of time an activity can be delayed without affecting a specific milestone (project end, successor start, etc.).
- **Total Float (TF):** $TF = LST - EST = LFT - EFT$ — delay allowed without delaying project completion.
- **Free Float (FF):** $FF = \min(EST_{successors}) - EFT$ — delay allowed without delaying any successor's early start.
- **Independent Float (IF):** $IF = \min(EST_{successors}) - LFT - D$ — delay allowed under worst-case predecessor finish + best-case successor start (clamped to $\geq 0$).
- **Interfering Float (IntF):** $IntF = TF - FF$ — the portion of TF that *will* delay the project if consumed.
- **Critical Path:** The longest path through the network, with all activities having $TF = 0$. It defines the **minimum project duration**.
- **Hierarchy:** $TF \geq FF \geq IF \geq 0$.
- **Forward Pass:** Left-to-right, uses **max()** at merge nodes. Computes EST, EFT.
- **Backward Pass:** Right-to-left, uses **min()** at burst nodes. Computes LST, LFT.
- **Boundary Conditions:** $EST_{first} = 0$; $LFT_{last} = Project\ Duration = \max(EFT_{end\ activities})$.
- **Importance in IT PM:** Enables resource leveling, schedule compression (crashing), risk buffering, and EVM-based schedule tracking.
- **Critical Path Migration:** If float on a non-critical path is fully consumed, the critical path can **shift** to a new sequence of activities.
- **KTU 2024 Tip:** Always show the **network diagram** + **forward pass table** + **backward pass table** + **float table** as four distinct steps in 14-mark answers. This structured presentation typically fetches full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Reduction by Crashing of Activity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cost Reduction by Crashing of Activity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Crashing** is a controlled technique in Project Management whereby specific activities on the **Critical Path** of a project network are systematically accelerated (by allocating additional resources such as overtime, extra manpower, or improved technology) in order to **reduce the overall project duration**, subject to a defined cost budget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Reduction by Crashing** is the strategic process of reducing the *total project cost* by deliberately crashing certain activities. The trick is that while the **Direct Cost** increases, the **Indirect Cost** (which is time-dependent) decreases. The optimal point is the duration where the *Total Cost = Direct Cost + Indirect Cost* is **minimum**.

### Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Normal Time (NT)** | The standard, expected time required to complete an activity under normal working conditions. |
| **Crash Time (CT)** | The absolute minimum time an activity can be reduced to by applying extra resources. |
| **Normal Cost (NC)** | The direct cost of an activity when executed in Normal Time. |
| **Crash Cost (CC)** | The direct cost of an activity when executed in Crash Time. |
| **Cost Slope** | The amount of additional direct cost incurred per unit reduction in time. |
| **Direct Cost** | Cost directly attributable to project activities (labour, materials, equipment). |
| **Indirect Cost** | Overhead costs that vary with project duration (administration, rent, utilities). |
| **Critical Path** | The longest-duration path through the network; determines project completion time. |
| **Optimised Project Duration** | The duration that minimises the total project cost. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The "Express Delivery" Courier Service** 🚚
> Imagine you are sending a parcel that normally takes **5 days** by standard courier, costing you **₹100** in shipping. If you urgently need it in **2 days**, the courier offers an *express service* for **₹400**. The **cost slope** here is `(400 − 100) / (5 − 2) = ₹100 per day saved`. 
> 
> Similarly, in IT projects, crashing an activity is like paying a "premium" for faster delivery. If the daily *penalty* (indirect cost like lost revenue, server rent) of keeping the project running is **₹200 per day**, then it is *financially sensible* to pay ₹100/day to crash — you **save ₹100 net per day**. The art of *Cost Reduction by Crashing* is knowing exactly when to stop crashing — when the crash premium exceeds the daily savings.

## 1.3 Standard Metric for Cost Slope

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The standard crashing metric used in KTU examinations is the Cost Slope Formula:**
> $$\text{Cost Slope} = \frac{\text{Crash Cost} - \text{Normal Cost}}{\text{Normal Time} - \text{Crash Time}}$$
> The unit is **₹ per unit time (day/week)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Economics Behind Crashing

The total project cost is modelled as:

> **Total Project Cost (TPC) = Total Direct Cost + Total Indirect Cost**

- **Direct Cost**: Increases as we crash (steeper slope of crashing).
- **Indirect Cost**: Decreases linearly as project duration reduces.
- **TPC Curve**: A **U-shaped curve** — initially falls (indirect savings dominate), reaches a minimum, then rises (crash premium dominates).

### Conceptual Graph (Schematic U-Curve)

```
   Cost (₹)
     │
TPC  │\                          /
     │ \                        /
     │  \                      /
     │   \____ Minimum _______/
     │         ↑ Optimal Point
     │        /  \
     │       /    \
     │______/      \___________
              Duration (days)
```

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Crashing

1. **Construct the network diagram** and identify the critical path (CP).
2. **Compute the cost slope** for every activity using the standard formula.
3. **On the critical path**, find the activity with the **lowest cost slope**.
4. **Crash that activity by 1 unit** of time (or as much as allowed).
5. **Recompute all paths** and identify the new critical path. A new path may become critical — this is critical to detect.
6. **Repeat** steps 2–5 until one of the following stopping conditions is met:
   - The desired project duration is achieved.
   - The cheapest cost slope on the critical path **exceeds the indirect cost per day** (no net benefit).
   - All activities on the critical path are fully crashed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule of Thumb**: A crash is *economically justified* only when **Cost Slope < Indirect Cost per day**. If Cost Slope = Indirect Cost, crashing is a *break-even* move (no net gain, no net loss). If Cost Slope > Indirect Cost, **STOP crashing**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---------|------------|------|-------------|
| **Cost Slope** | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{C_C - C_N}{T_N - T_C}$ | ₹/day | Cost of crashing an activity by 1 day |
| **Total Direct Cost** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_{N_i} + \sum (\text{crash premium})$ | ₹ | All activity costs after crashing |
| **Total Indirect Cost** | $\text{Indirect Rate} \times \text{Project Duration}$ | ₹ | Time-dependent overhead |
| **Total Project Cost** | $\text{TDC} + \text{TIC}$ | ₹ | Ultimate cost minimisation objective |
| **Maximum Crash Days** | $T_N - T_C$ | days | Crash limit for one activity |
| **Critical Path Length** | $\max(\text{all path durations})$ | days | Project duration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition**: An activity **cannot be crashed below its Crash Time**, even if more money is provided. Once all activities on the critical path reach their crash times, the project is *fully crashed* and no further reduction is possible.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Crashing |
|----------|-------------------------|
| **Software / IT** | Compressing a release cycle from 6 months to 4 months to beat a competitor. |
| **Construction** | Adding a night shift to deliver a bridge before monsoon. |
| **Manufacturing** | Hiring temporary workers to meet festival-season demand. |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Expediting clinical trials to obtain faster drug approval. |
| **Aerospace** | Using parallel testing streams to launch a satellite ahead of schedule. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Standard Pattern)

A small IT project consists of **four activities**: A, B, C, D. The network is:

```
              ┌── A ──► (2) ── C ──►
   (Start) (1)│                        (End) (4)
              └── B ──► (3) ── D ──►
```

The table of normal and crash data is given below. The **indirect cost is ₹500 per day**. Determine the **optimum project duration and minimum total cost**.

| Activity | Predecessor | Normal Time (days) | Crash Time (days) | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Cost (₹) |
|----------|-------------|--------------------|-------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **A** (1–2) | Start | 5 | 3 | 1000 | 1400 |
| **B** (1–3) | Start | 7 | 5 | 1200 | 1800 |
| **C** (2–4) | A | 4 | 2 | 800 | 1200 |
| **D** (3–4) | B | 6 | 4 | 1500 | 2100 |

## 3.2 Step 0 — Compute the Cost Slopes

The cost slope is calculated using the formula from the cheat sheet:

$$
\text{Slope} = \frac{C_C - C_N}{T_N - T_C}
$$

Applying to each activity:

- **Activity A**: $\dfrac{1400 - 1000}{5 - 3} = \dfrac{400}{2} = 200$ ₹/day
- **Activity B**: $\dfrac{1800 - 1200}{7 - 5} = \dfrac{600}{2} = 300$ ₹/day
- **Activity C**: $\dfrac{1200 - 800}{4 - 2} = \dfrac{400}{2} = 200$ ₹/day
- **Activity D**: $\dfrac{2100 - 1500}{6 - 4} = \dfrac{600}{2} = 300$ ₹/day

> [!NOTE]
> The **indirect cost rate (₹500/day)** is our *decision threshold*. Since *all* cost slopes (200, 300) are less than ₹500, every crash on the critical path gives a *net positive saving* until paths become equal in length.

## 3.3 Step 1 — Initial (Normal) Schedule

Two paths exist in the network:
- **Path 1 (A–C)**: $5 + 4 = 9$ days
- **Path 2 (B–D)**: $7 + 6 = 13$ days

**Critical Path (CP)** = **B–D = 13 days**

Cost summary (Normal):
- Total Direct Cost (TDC) = $1000 + 1200 + 800 + 1500 = 4500$ ₹
- Total Indirect Cost (TIC) = $500 \times 13 = 6500$ ₹
- **Total Project Cost (TPC) = 4500 + 6500 = 11,000 ₹**

## 3.4 Step 2 — First Crash Iteration

On the critical path **B–D**, the available cost slopes are:
- B → 300 ₹/day
- D → 300 ₹/day

Both are equal, so we may crash either. We crash **B by 1 day** (B becomes 6).

**New Schedule**:
- **Path A–C**: $5 + 4 = 9$ days
- **Path B–D**: $6 + 6 = 12$ days

**New CP = B–D = 12 days**

Cost update:
- TDC = $4500 + 300 = 4800$ ₹ (added 300 for crashing B)
- TIC = $500 \times 12 = 6000$ ₹
- **TPC = 4800 + 6000 = 10,800 ₹** ✅ *(Net saving of ₹200)*

## 3.5 Step 3 — Second Crash Iteration

Critical path is still **B–D**. B can be crashed 1 more day (5 → 4 to 5; it has 2 days of crash capacity, already used 1). We crash **B by 1 more day** (B becomes 5, which is its **crash limit**).

**New Schedule**:
- **Path A–C**: $5 + 4 = 9$ days
- **Path B–D**: $5 + 6 = 11$ days

**New CP = B–D = 11 days**

Cost update:
- TDC = $4800 + 300 = 5100$ ₹
- TIC = $500 \times 11 = 5500$ ₹
- **TPC = 5100 + 5500 = 10,600 ₹** ✅ *(Net saving of ₹200)*

## 3.6 Step 4 — Third Crash Iteration

B is now **fully crashed (5 days)**. On critical path B–D, the only remaining crashable activity is **D** (slope 300). Crash D by 1 day (D = 5).

**New Schedule**:
- **Path A–C**: $5 + 4 = 9$ days
- **Path B–D**: $5 + 5 = 10$ days

**New CP = B–D = 10 days**

Cost update:
- TDC = $5100 + 300 = 5400$ ₹
- TIC = $500 \times 10 = 5000$ ₹
- **TPC = 5400 + 5000 = 10,400 ₹** ✅ *(Net saving of ₹200)*

## 3.7 Step 5 — Fourth Crash Iteration

Critical path is still **B–D**. B is fully crashed. D can be crashed 1 more day (5 → 4). Crash D by 1 day (D = 4, its **crash limit**).

**New Schedule**:
- **Path A–C**: $5 + 4 = 9$ days
- **Path B–D**: $5 + 4 = 9$ days

**Both paths are now CRITICAL at 9 days.**

Cost update:
- TDC = $5400 + 300 = 5700$ ₹
- TIC = $500 \times 9 = 4500$ ₹
- **TPC = 5700 + 4500 = 10,200 ₹** ✅ *(Net saving of ₹200)*

## 3.8 Step 6 — Stopping Condition Check

To reduce the project below 9 days, we must crash **both** critical paths simultaneously. The available activities are:

- On path **A–C**: A (slope 200) or C (slope 200)
- On path **B–D**: B is **fully crashed**; D is **fully crashed**

> [!WARNING]
> **No more crashing is possible on path B–D.** Therefore, the project cannot be reduced below 9 days. **STOP the crashing process.**

## 3.9 Final Analytical Matrix (Summary Table)

| Iteration | Activity Crashed | Days Crashed | Critical Path | Project Duration | TDC (₹) | TIC (₹) | TPC (₹) | Net Δ |
|-----------|------------------|--------------|---------------|------------------|---------|---------|---------|-------|
| 0 (Normal) | None | 0 | B–D | 13 | 4500 | 6500 | 11,000 | — |
| 1 | B | 1 | B–D | 12 | 4800 | 6000 | 10,800 | −200 |
| 2 | B | 1 | B–D | 11 | 5100 | 5500 | 10,600 | −200 |
| 3 | D | 1 | B–D | 10 | 5400 | 5000 | 10,400 | −200 |
| 4 | D | 1 | A–C, B–D (both) | 9 | 5700 | 4500 | **10,200** | −200 |

## 3.10 Conclusion of the Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimal Project Duration = 9 days**  
> **Minimum Total Project Cost = ₹10,200**  
> *All activities on path B–D are at their crash limits, and paths A–C and B–D are co-critical.*

The cost curve thus has its minimum (₹10,200) at duration = 9 days.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Project Network Diagram (Mermaid)

The network structure for our worked example, with normal/crash times annotated:

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1(("Start (1)")) -- "A : NT=5, CT=3" --> n2(("Node 2"))
    n1(("Start (1)")) -- "B : NT=7, CT=5" --> n3(("Node 3"))
    n2(("Node 2")) -- "C : NT=4, CT=2" --> n4(("End (4)"))
    n3(("Node 3")) -- "D : NT=6, CT=4" --> n4(("End (4)"))
```

**Path Summary Table** (rendered in Mermaid-compatible format):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["Path A-C : 5 + 4 = 9 days"]
    p2["Path B-D : 7 + 6 = 13 days CRITICAL"]
    p1 -.-> p2
```

## 4.2 Crashing Decision Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This flow maps the full decision logic of a crashing process — directly answering "when do we crash, and when do we stop?":

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["START: Construct Project Network"] --> s2["Compute All Path Durations"]
    s2 --> s3["Identify the Critical Path CP"]
    s3 --> s4["Calculate Cost Slope for Every Activity"]
    s4 --> s5["On CP, pick activity with LOWEST Cost Slope"]
    s5 --> s6{"Can this activity still be crashed?"}
    s6 -- No --> s7["Move to next lowest slope on CP"]
    s7 --> s5
    s6 -- Yes --> s8{"Is Cost Slope LESS THAN Indirect Cost per day?"}
    s8 -- Yes --> s9["CRASH the activity by 1 unit"]
    s9 --> s10["Update Direct Cost and Project Duration"]
    s10 --> s11["Recompute all paths, find new CP"]
    s11 --> s12{"New CP same as previous?"}
    s12 -- Yes --> s5
    s12 -- No --> s13["Replace CP with new CP and continue"]
    s13 --> s5
    s8 -- No --> s14["STOP Crashing: NOT Economical"]
    s14 --> s15["Compute Minimum Total Project Cost"]
```

## 4.3 Cost-Time Trade-off U-Curve (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1["Normal Duration: T_N"] --> a2["Direct Cost HIGH (No crash premium)"]
    a3["Crash Duration: T_C"] --> a4["Direct Cost MAX (Full crash premium)"]
    a5["Indirect Cost Line: Decreases with time"]
    a6["Total Cost U-Curve: Minimum at OPTIMAL DURATION"]
    a2 --> a6
    a4 --> a6
    a5 --> a6
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **U-curve** is the visual signature of cost-time trade-off. The **leftmost point** is the *fully crashed* (most expensive direct cost, lowest indirect cost), and the **rightmost point** is the *normal* schedule (lowest direct cost, highest indirect cost). The **bottom of the U** is the *optimal duration*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms: (a) Cost Slope (b) Indirect Cost (c) Crash Time.**

**Model Answer**:
- **(a) Cost Slope**: The additional direct cost incurred per unit reduction in activity time.  
  $\text{Cost Slope} = \dfrac{C_C - C_N}{T_N - T_C}$ (in ₹/day).
- **(b) Indirect Cost**: Costs that are not directly tied to a specific activity but vary with project duration — e.g., site rent, administration, supervisory overheads, insurance.
- **(c) Crash Time**: The absolute minimum time to which an activity can be reduced by allocating extra/crash resources, beyond which further reduction is physically impossible.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List any THREE assumptions of project crashing technique.**

**Model Answer**:
1. The cost slope of an activity is **linear** between normal and crash times.
2. All activities can be crashed **independently** (crashing one activity does not affect the cost slope of another).
3. The indirect cost varies **linearly** with project duration.
4. *Bonus*: The crash resources (manpower, equipment) are assumed to be **readily available** when required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions carry **14 marks**, usually split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Candidates answer **one full question** (either Question A or Question B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** What is **Project Crashing**? Explain the **step-by-step procedure** for crashing a project network to find the optimum duration. State the **stopping criteria**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:
**Definition**: Project crashing is a cost-time trade-off technique where activities on the critical path are accelerated to reduce the project duration at the minimum additional cost.

**Step-by-Step Procedure**:
1. Draw the project network and identify the **Critical Path (CP)**.
2. Compute the **Cost Slope** for every activity using the standard formula.
3. On the CP, select the activity with the **lowest cost slope**.
4. **Crash** that activity by 1 day (or its remaining crashable limit).
5. Update the **Direct Cost** and **Project Duration**.
6. **Re-identify the CP** — a new path may become critical.
7. **Repeat** steps 3–6 until any of the stopping criteria are met.

**Stopping Criteria**:
- The desired project duration is achieved.
- The cheapest cost slope on CP **exceeds the indirect cost rate** (no net saving).
- All activities on the CP are **fully crashed** (cannot reduce further).

---

**(b)** A project has the following data. Indirect cost is **₹400/day**. Determine the **optimum duration and minimum cost**. **(7 marks)**

| Activity | Normal Time | Crash Time | Normal Cost | Crash Cost |
|----------|-------------|------------|-------------|------------|
| 1–2 (A) | 6 | 4 | 800 | 1200 |
| 1–3 (B) | 8 | 5 | 1000 | 1450 |
| 2–3 (C) | 4 | 2 | 600 | 1000 |
| 2–4 (D) | 5 | 3 | 700 | 1100 |
| 3–4 (E) | 7 | 4 | 900 | 1500 |

Network: A, B start parallel. C: dummy/real 2→3. D: 2→4. E: 3→4. End at 4.
**Paths**: A–D (2–4) wait, let me restate: 1–2–3–4 via C, 1–2–4 via D, 1–3–4 via B–E. So:
- **Path P1**: A–C–E = 6+4+7 = **17**
- **Path P2**: A–D = 6+5 = **11**
- **Path P3**: B–E = 8+7 = **15**
- **CP = A–C–E = 17 days**

**Slopes**: A=200, B=150, C=200, D=200, E=200.

| Iteration | Activity Crashed | Duration | TDC | TIC (₹400/day) | TPC |
|-----------|------------------|----------|-----|----------------|-----|
| 0 | — | 17 | 4000 | 6800 | 10,800 |
| 1 | B (slope 150, lowest on CP? No, CP is A–C–E) | 17 | 4000 | 6800 | 10,800 |
| 1 (correct) | C (slope 200) | 16 | 4200 | 6400 | 10,600 |
| 2 | C → fully crashed at 2 | 15 | 4400 | 6000 | 10,400 |
| 3 | A (slope 200) | 14 | 4600 | 5600 | 10,200 |
| 4 | A → fully crashed at 4 | 13 | 4800 | 5200 | 10,000 |
| 5 | E (slope 200) | 12 | 5000 | 4800 | 9,800 ✅ |
| 6 | E → 5 | 11 | 5200 | 4400 | 9,600 ✅ |
| 7 | E → 4 (fully crashed) | 10 | 5400 | 4000 | 9,400 ✅ |
| 8 | A,C,E fully crashed; new CP? P1 = 4+2+4 = 10, P3 = B–E = ?  B(5)+E(4) = 9, so new CP? Check P1: 10, P3: 9, P2: 4+3 = 7. So CP = A–C–E = 10. Cannot reduce further (all on CP fully crashed). | 10 | 5400 | 4000 | **9,400** |

**Optimum: 10 days, ₹9,400**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: When multiple paths become critical after a crash, students often forget that **further reduction requires crashing on ALL critical paths simultaneously** (cost = sum of slopes). Always recheck all paths after every crash.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Cost-Time Trade-off** concept with the help of a **labelled diagram** of the U-curve. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:
The **Cost-Time Trade-off** analyses the relationship between project duration and total cost. It identifies the duration at which total cost is minimum.

**Components of the Trade-off**:
- **Direct Cost Curve**: Increases (concave) as duration decreases (more crash resources needed).
- **Indirect Cost Curve**: Decreases linearly as duration reduces (less overhead time).
- **Total Cost Curve** (sum of above) is **U-shaped**.
- The **vertex of the U-curve** is the **optimum duration** where total cost is minimum.
- **Crash Limit Point**: The fully crashed duration — beyond which crashing is physically impossible.

**Diagram**:
```
   Cost (₹)
     │ ●  ← Crash Limit
     │  \
     │   \
     │    \   Direct Cost Curve
     │     \____
     │          \____
     │   Total       \____
     │   Cost (U)        \____
     │_______/ Minimum     \______
     │        ↑             Direct Cost
     │      OPTIMUM
     │   _____/
     │  /   ↑
     │ /   Indirect Cost
     │/_____|________________________
              Duration (days)
              ↑ Normal    ↑ Crash
```

**Interpretation**:
- Left of optimum → crashing is over-aggressive (direct cost > indirect savings).
- Right of optimum → schedule is too relaxed (indirect cost > direct cost savings).
- At optimum → **balanced trade-off** is achieved.

---

**(b)** A project consists of **5 activities**. The data is given. Indirect cost is **₹300/day**. Find optimum duration and cost. **(7 marks)**

| Activity | NT | CT | NC | CC |
|----------|----|----|----|----|
| 1–2 | 3 | 2 | 500 | 700 |
| 1–3 | 4 | 2 | 600 | 800 |
| 2–4 | 5 | 3 | 800 | 1100 |
| 3–4 | 4 | 2 | 400 | 700 |
| 2–3 | 2 | 1 | 300 | 500 |

**Network**: 1–2–4 (A, C), 1–3–4 (B, D), 1–2–3–4 (A, dummy/E, D).

Let's simplify: Take it as 1 → 2 → 4 and 1 → 3 → 4 with a 2-3 activity.
- **Path P1**: A–C = 3+5 = 8
- **Path P2**: B–D = 4+4 = 8
- **Path P3**: A–E–D = 3+2+4 = 9 → **CP**

Slopes: A=200, B=200, C=150, D=150, E=200.
- TDC (normal) = 500+600+800+400+300 = 2600
- TIC (normal) = 300×9 = 2700
- TPC (normal) = 5300

| Iter | Crashed | New CP | Dur | TDC | TIC | TPC |
|------|---------|--------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| 0 | — | A–E–D | 9 | 2600 | 2700 | 5300 |
| 1 | C (slope 150, lowest on CP? CP is A-E-D, so C not on it. Lowest on A-E-D: A=200, E=200, D=150 → crash D) | A–E–D | 8 | 2750 | 2400 | 5150 ✅ |
| 2 | D → fully crashed at 2. Lowest on A–E–D now: A=200 or E=200. Crash E. | A–E–D | 7 | 2950 | 2100 | 5050 ✅ |
| 3 | E → fully crashed at 1. CP: A–E–D = 3+1+2 = 6. P1: A–C = 3+3 = 6 (now critical). Both critical. Lowest crash: A(200) on P1, but P3 needs crash. E gone. We must crash A and C? No, P1 has A and C. To reduce both, need to crash one from each path. On P1: A(200) or C(150). On P3: A(200) or E(fully crashed). So crash C (150) on P1 — but C is not on P3. Cannot reduce P3 by C. So we must crash A on both. Cost = 200. But A is on P1, and crashing A also reduces P3. So crash A by 1. New: P1: A–C = 2+3 = 5, P3: A–E–D = 2+1+2 = 5. Both 5. | A–C, A–E–D | 5 | 3150 | 1500 | 4650 ✅ |
| 4 | A fully crashed at 2. CP: P1 = 2+3=5, P3 = 2+1+2=5. To reduce P1: crash C (150). To reduce P3: A, E fully crashed. Cannot reduce P3. STOP. | — | 5 | 3150 | 1500 | **4650** |

**Optimum Duration = 5 days, Minimum Cost = ₹4,650**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students frequently lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Forgetting to recompute the critical path** after every crash (a previously non-critical path can become critical).
> 2. **Ignoring the indirect cost threshold** — continuing to crash even when cost slope > indirect rate.
> 3. **Forgetting dummy activities** in network diagrams, leading to wrong path identification.
> 4. **Confusing normal and crash costs** during summation — always re-verify which cost version applies to each activity at the current iteration.
> 5. **Not drawing the U-curve** for full-mark answers on cost-time trade-off questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cost Reduction by Crashing**

- **Definition**: Crashing = reducing project duration by accelerating activities, with the aim of *reducing total cost*.
- **Cost Slope Formula** (MUST memorise): $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{C_C - C_N}{T_N - T_C}$
- **Decision Rule**: Crash only if **Cost Slope < Indirect Cost per day**.
- **Break-even**: If Slope = Indirect Cost, crashing gives zero net benefit.
- **STOP condition**: If Slope > Indirect Cost, **do not crash**.
- **Critical Path Shift**: Always recompute all paths after a crash; new path may become critical.
- **Multiple Critical Paths**: To reduce duration further, must crash on **all** critical paths simultaneously (combined cost).
- **U-Curve**: Total Cost = Direct + Indirect. Has a **minimum (optimum)**.
- **Crash Limit**: Activity cannot be crashed below its **Crash Time** (physical/resource limit).
- **Assumptions**: Linear cost slopes, independent crashing, linear indirect cost.
- **Net Saving per crash** = Indirect Cost saved per day − Cost Slope.
- **Final answer format (KTU 2024)**: Always present a **summary table** with columns — Iteration | Activity Crashed | Duration | TDC | TIC | TPC — and conclude with the *optimum duration* and *minimum cost* highlighted in **bold**.
- **Total Direct Cost at any iteration** = Sum of (normal cost of each activity) + Sum of (cost slope × days crashed for each crashed activity).
- **Project Duration** = Length of the *longest* path at each iteration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost Control and Scheduling: Project Cost Control (PERT/Cost)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cost Control and Scheduling: Project Cost Control (PERT/Cost)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PERT/Cost** is a sophisticated project management control system that integrates the time-scheduling logic of the **Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)** with a comprehensive **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**-based cost-monitoring framework. It is a deterministic extension that allows project managers to plan, schedule, budget, and **control the financial performance** of a project against the baseline established at the time of network planning.

In KTU 2024 terminology, PERT/Cost is treated as a **Cost Control Subsystem** operating on top of the PERT network. While PERT alone answers *"When will the activity be done?"*, PERT/Cost answers the three crucial project finance questions:
1. *How much money has been authorized for this activity?*
2. *How much has actually been spent?*
3. *What is the cost variance, and is the project on budget?*

The system achieves this by assigning **Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled (BCWS)**, **Budgeted Cost of Work Performed (BCWP)**, and **Actual Cost of Work Performed (ACWP)** to every node and arc of the PERT network, enabling **Earned Value Analysis (EVA)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Family Wedding Planner"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Planning Your Sister's Wedding 🎊**
> Imagine you are the *de facto* project manager for your sister's wedding. You draw a small PERT chart: *Book Venue → Send Invites → Order Catering → Wedding Day*. Each activity has a planned date and a planned cost (say, ₹50,000 for catering).
>
> - **PERT alone** = The Gantt chart on your wall that says "Catering happens on 10th Dec."
> - **PERT/Cost** = Adding a *parallel cost-tracking sheet*. Now you also track: "On 10th Dec, were we supposed to have spent ₹50,000? Did we actually spend it? If yes, are we under or over?"
>
> If the caterer calls on the 8th and demands ₹60,000, PERT/Cost instantly flashes a **red signal** — a *cost overrun*. If he finishes early and bills only ₹40,000, PERT/Cost shows a *cost underrun / schedule saving*. This real-time financial feedback loop is precisely what PERT/Cost brings to large engineering and IT projects.

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Expansion | Plain-English Meaning |
|------|-----------|-----------------------|
| **BCWS** | Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled | "What we *planned* to spend by today" |
| **BCWP** | Budgeted Cost of Work Performed (Earned Value) | "What we *should* have spent for the work *actually* done" |
| **ACWP** | Actual Cost of Work Performed | "What we *actually* spent" |
| **CV** | Cost Variance = BCWP − ACWP | "Are we under or over budget?" |
| **SV** | Schedule Variance = BCWP − BCWS | "Are we ahead or behind schedule?" |
| **CPI** | Cost Performance Index = BCWP / ACWP | Efficiency of spending (≥ 1 is good) |
| **SPI** | Schedule Performance Index = BCWP / BCWS | Efficiency of time use (≥ 1 is good) |

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the **Earned Value Triangle** — BCWS, BCWP, ACWP. Every KTU numerical on PERT/Cost that appears in the **University Exam** is built around these three variables.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Architecture of PERT/Cost

The PERT/Cost system is built in **three hierarchical tiers** that map directly onto the Work Breakdown Structure:

### Tier 1 — *Account Level (Highest)*
The entire project is split into major **Accounts** (e.g., in an IT project: *Hardware Account*, *Software Account*, *Manpower Account*, *Training Account*). Each account is a high-level cost bucket.

### Tier 2 — *Work Package Level*
Each account is broken down into **Work Packages** — the smallest units for which a single department or vendor is responsible. *Example*: Under *Software Account* → *Module A Coding (40 person-days)*, *Module A Testing (15 person-days)*.

### Tier 3 — *Activity Level (Lowest)*
Each work package is decomposed into individual network **activities** that exist as nodes/arcs on the PERT diagram. This is the level at which time and cost data are physically linked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The PERT/Cost system functions because every **arc (activity)** in the PERT network has a **dual signature**: a *time estimate* (used by PERT) and a *cost estimate* (used by PERT/Cost). The two subsystems read the *same* network but produce *different* reports.

## 2.2 Two Dimensions of Project Cost

The total cost of any project is the sum of two fundamentally different cost categories:

### A. Direct Cost
- **Definition**: Expenditures that can be *physically and exclusively* attributed to a single project activity.
- **Examples in IT Projects**: Programmer salaries, software licenses, server hardware, outsourced development.
- **Behaviour with Time**: As the project duration is *crashed* (shortened by adding resources), the **direct cost rises sharply** due to overtime, expedited shipping, and parallel hiring.
- **Curve Shape**: Convex, monotonically increasing as project duration decreases.

### B. Indirect Cost (Overhead)
- **Definition**: Expenditures that *cannot* be traced to a single activity — they accrue with the *passage of calendar time*.
- **Examples**: Site rent, administrative salaries, utilities, insurance, depreciation of idle equipment.
- **Behaviour with Time**: Roughly **linear and decreasing** as project duration shortens. The earlier you finish, the less rent and salary you pay.
- **Curve Shape**: Approximately a straight line with a *negative slope*.

### C. Total Project Cost Curve
The grand total **C(T) = Direct Cost(T) + Indirect Cost(T)** is a **U-shaped (convex) curve**. The **minimum of this curve** gives the *cost-optimal project duration* — the single most important decision variable in PERT/Cost.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every numerical problem in the End Semester Examination is solved using one or more of these equations.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $C_{total}(T) = C_{direct}(T) + C_{indirect}(T)$ | Total project cost at duration T | ₹ / $ |
| 2 | $Cost \; Slope \; (S) = \dfrac{C_{crash} - C_{normal}}{T_{normal} - T_{crash}}$ | Marginal cost per day of crashing one activity | ₹ / day |
| 3 | $C_{crashed}(i) = C_{normal}(i) + S_{i} \times \Delta t$ | Cost of activity *i* after crashing by Δt days | ₹ / $ |
| 4 | $BCWS = \sum (\text{Budgeted cost of activities scheduled by date } t)$ | Planned value curve | ₹ / $ |
| 5 | $BCWP = \sum (\text{Budgeted cost of activities actually completed by date } t)$ | Earned value | ₹ / $ |
| 6 | $ACWP = \sum (\text{Actual cost of activities completed by date } t)$ | Actual cost | ₹ / $ |
| 7 | $CV = BCWP - ACWP$ | Cost Variance (positive = under budget) | ₹ / $ |
| 8 | $SV = BCWP - BCWS$ | Schedule Variance (positive = ahead) | ₹ / $ |
| 9 | $CPI = \dfrac{BCWP}{ACWP}$ | Cost Performance Index | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $SPI = \dfrac{BCWP}{BCWS}$ | Schedule Performance Index | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | Estimate at Completion (forecast final cost) | ₹ / $ |
| 12 | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | Variance at Completion | ₹ / $ |
| 13 | $T_{optimal} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{dC_{total}}{dT} = 0$ | Condition for cost-minimum duration | Days |

> [!WARNING]
> **Equation 2 (Cost Slope)** uses the *normal* minus *crash* time in the denominator. Many KTU answer scripts mistakenly invert it. If a 5-day activity can be crashed to 3 days at an extra cost of ₹4,000, slope = 4000 / (5−3) = **₹2,000 per day** — always positive.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the System

In real IT-industry deployments, PERT/Cost forms the analytical backbone of **Earned Value Management (EVM)** dashboards used by certified Project Management Professionals (PMPs). It is the only technique that converts *physical progress* (activities finished) into a *financial number* (earned value) that can be compared against the bank statement. Without PERT/Cost logic, a project manager can never know whether the project is **on time AND on budget simultaneously**.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cost-Slope Equation

The cost-slope concept is central to **project crashing** — the technique of selectively shortening critical activities to reduce project duration. Let us derive it from first principles.

### Given (per KTU convention)
For any single activity *i*:
- Normal time $T_{N}$ = duration under normal working conditions.
- Crash time $T_{C}$ = absolute minimum achievable duration (with extra resources).
- Normal cost $C_{N}$ = cost when executed at $T_{N}$.
- Crash cost $C_{C}$ = cost when executed at $T_{C}$.

### Assumptions of Linear Cost Behaviour
The cost-time relationship of an activity is **assumed linear** between the two extreme points $(T_{N}, C_{N})$ and $(T_{C}, C_{C})$. This linearity is the standard KTU assumption unless a non-linear function is explicitly supplied.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let $\Delta t = T_{N} - T_{C}$ be the maximum amount by which the activity can be crashed. The corresponding maximum increase in cost is $\Delta C = C_{C} - C_{N}$.

The **cost slope** $S$ is the *rate of change of cost per unit time saved*:

$$
S = \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta t} = \frac{C_{C} - C_{N}}{T_{N} - T_{C}}
$$

If we crash the activity by an arbitrary amount $x$ (where $0 \le x \le \Delta t$), the new cost is obtained by linear interpolation along the line joining the two extreme points:

$$
C(x) = C_{N} + S \cdot x
$$

Equivalently, if the new time is $T = T_{N} - x$, then:

$$
C(T) = C_{N} + S \cdot (T_{N} - T)
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \; C(T) = (C_{N} + S \cdot T_{N}) - S \cdot T
$$

This is the equation of a straight line with **negative slope $S$** when expressed in terms of activity duration. In *crashing* calculations we use the *positive* form $C(x) = C_{N} + Sx$ to add cost when time is reduced.

## 3.2 Full Worked Numerical — KTU Model Problem

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **archetype** of any 14-mark Part B numerical on PERT/Cost. Practising this one problem covers ~80% of all variants.

### **Problem Statement**
A small IT project consists of 7 activities **A** through **G** with the following network and cost data:

| Activity | Predecessor | Normal Time (days) | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Time (days) | Crash Cost (₹) |
|----------|-------------|--------------------|------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| A | — | 7 | 5,000 | 5 | 9,000 |
| B | A | 4 | 4,000 | 2 | 8,000 |
| C | A | 6 | 3,000 | 4 | 5,000 |
| D | B | 5 | 6,000 | 3 | 8,000 |
| E | C | 5 | 4,000 | 3 | 6,000 |
| F | D, E | 6 | 7,000 | 4 | 11,000 |
| G | F | 4 | 3,000 | 2 | 5,000 |

Indirect cost = **₹2,000 per day**. The contract carries a **penalty of ₹3,000 per day** for delay beyond the normal duration. Indirect cost is incurred for every day the project runs.

**Required:**
1. Compute the cost slope of each activity.
2. Find the **normal project duration** and the **normal total cost**.
3. **Crash the project step by step** until the cost-optimal duration is reached.
4. Identify the **cost-minimum duration** and the **minimum total cost**.

---

### **Step 1 — Compute Cost Slopes**

Using $S = (C_{C} - C_{N}) / (T_{N} - T_{C})$:

$$
S_{A} = \frac{9000 - 5000}{7 - 5} = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
S_{B} = \frac{8000 - 4000}{4 - 2} = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
S_{C} = \frac{5000 - 3000}{6 - 4} = \frac{2000}{2} = 1000 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
S_{D} = \frac{8000 - 6000}{5 - 3} = \frac{2000}{2} = 1000 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
S_{E} = \frac{6000 - 4000}{5 - 3} = \frac{2000}{2} = 1000 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
S_{F} = \frac{11000 - 7000}{6 - 4} = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
S_{G} = \frac{5000 - 3000}{4 - 2} = \frac{2000}{2} = 1000 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

| Activity | $T_{N}$ | $T_{C}$ | $C_{N}$ | $C_{C}$ | Slope $S$ (₹/day) |
|----------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------------------|
| A | 7 | 5 | 5,000 | 9,000 | **2,000** |
| B | 4 | 2 | 4,000 | 8,000 | **2,000** |
| C | 6 | 4 | 3,000 | 5,000 | **1,000** |
| D | 5 | 3 | 6,000 | 8,000 | **1,000** |
| E | 5 | 3 | 4,000 | 6,000 | **1,000** |
| F | 6 | 4 | 7,000 | 11,000 | **2,000** |
| G | 4 | 2 | 3,000 | 5,000 | **1,000** |

---

### **Step 2 — Find Normal Project Duration (Critical Path)**

List all paths from Start to End (where End is the completion of **G**):

$$
\text{Path 1: } A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow F \rightarrow G = 7+4+5+6+4 = 26 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\text{Path 2: } A \rightarrow C \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow G = 7+6+5+6+4 = 28 \text{ days}
$$

The **critical path is Path 2 (A–C–E–F–G)** with a duration of **28 days**.

**Normal Direct Cost** = 5,000 + 4,000 + 3,000 + 6,000 + 4,000 + 7,000 + 3,000 = **₹32,000**

**Normal Indirect Cost** = 28 × 2,000 = **₹56,000**

**Total Normal Project Cost** = 32,000 + 56,000 = **₹88,000**

---

### **Step 3 — Iterative Crashing**

**Crashing rule:** Crash the *cheapest critical activity* (lowest slope) provided the path remains critical. Stop when the *marginal cost of crashing* exceeds the *marginal saving in indirect cost* (₹2,000/day here). If a penalty exists, add it to the savings.

**Net marginal saving per day** = Indirect cost saved + Penalty avoided − Slope of activity crashed.

#### Iteration 1 — Crash C (slope 1,000) by 1 day
Path 2 reduces to 27. New cost:
- Direct: +₹1,000
- Indirect: −₹2,000
- **Net change: −₹1,000** (saving)
- New Total = 88,000 − 1,000 = **₹87,000**, Duration = 27 days.

#### Iteration 2 — Crash E (slope 1,000) by 1 day
Path 2 = 26. Net change −₹1,000. New Total = **₹86,000**, Duration = 26 days.

#### Iteration 3 — Crash F (slope 2,000) by 1 day
Path 2 = 25. Now Path 1 also = 26, Path 2 = 25. Path 1 becomes the new critical path. Net change = 0. New Total = **₹86,000**, Duration = 25 days.

#### Iteration 4 — Multiple critical paths exist. Must crash on **both paths**.
The cheapest combined option that reduces *both* paths:
- Crash F again (slope 2,000) and simultaneously reduce another activity on Path 1.
- Cheapest: Crash **B (slope 2,000)** on Path 1 + **F (slope 2,000)** on Path 2. Combined cost = **₹4,000/day**.
- This exceeds the ₹2,000 indirect saving. **STOP crashing.**

#### Iteration 5 — Alternative: Crash G (slope 1,000) — common to both paths
Crash G by 1 day: Both paths reduce by 1. Cost = +₹1,000. Indirect saved = ₹2,000. Net saving = ₹1,000.
- Apply until G hits its crash limit (2 days max). After 2 days, G is at $T_C = 2$.

#### Iteration 6 — Crash D (slope 1,000) on Path 1 and E (slope 1,000) on Path 2
Combined cost = ₹2,000/day. Equal to indirect saving. No benefit, no loss. Indifferent point.

---

### **Step 4 — Cost-Optimal Solution**

| Duration (days) | Direct Cost (₹) | Indirect Cost (₹) | Total Cost (₹) |
|------------------|------------------|--------------------|------------------|
| 28 | 32,000 | 56,000 | 88,000 |
| 27 | 33,000 | 54,000 | 87,000 |
| 26 | 34,000 | 52,000 | 86,000 |
| 25 | 34,000 | 50,000 | 84,000 |
| 24 | 35,000 | 48,000 | 83,000 ✅ |
| 23 | 36,000 | 46,000 | 82,000 ✅ |
| 22 | 37,000 | 44,000 | 81,000 ✅ |
| 21 (C,E,G all crashed) | 38,000 | 42,000 | 80,000 ✅ |

**Optimal duration ≈ 24 days**, **Minimum total cost ≈ ₹83,000** (any further crashing adds more to direct cost than it saves in indirect cost).

# 📒 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — PERT/Cost Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Initiation and WBS] --> A1[Decompose Project into Accounts]
    A1 --> A2[Decompose Accounts into Work Packages]
    A2 --> A3[Decompose Work Packages into Activities]
    A3 --> A4[Build PERT Network with Time Estimates]
    A4 --> A5[Identify Critical Path]
    A5 --> A6[Compute BCWS Curve for Each Activity]
    A6 --> A7[Assign Cost Slope to Each Activity]
    A7 --> A8[Compute Normal Total Cost equals Direct plus Indirect]
    A8 --> B1{Is Duration greater than Cost-Optimal Duration}
    B1 -- Yes --> B2[Select Cheapest Critical Activity to Crash]
    B2 --> B3[Reduce Duration by 1 Day]
    B3 --> B4[Add Slope Cost to Direct Cost]
    B4 --> B5[Subtract Indirect Cost Saved]
    B5 --> B6[Recompute Total Project Cost]
    B6 --> A5
    B1 -- No --> C1[STOP Crashing]
    C1 --> C2[Report Cost-Optimal Duration and Minimum Total Cost]
    A6 --> D1[During Execution Periodically Collect Actual Cost Data]
    D1 --> D2[Compute ACWP Curve]
    D2 --> D3[Compute BCWP Earned Value]
    D3 --> D4[Calculate CV equals BCWP minus ACWP]
    D4 --> D5[Calculate SV equals BCWP minus BCWS]
    D5 --> D6[Compute CPI equals BCWP divided by ACWP]
    D6 --> D7[Compute SPI equals BCWP divided by BCWS]
    D7 --> E1{CPI greater than 1}
    E1 -- Yes --> E2[Project is Under Budget]
    E1 -- No --> E3[Project is Over Budget]
    D5 --> F1{SV greater than 0}
    F1 -- Yes --> F4[Project is Ahead of Schedule]
    F1 -- No --> F5[Project is Behind Schedule]
    C2 --> G1[Final Earned Value Report to Stakeholders]
```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the PERT/Cost system. List any two of its advantages over conventional budgeting methods.**

**Model Answer:**
PERT/Cost is an integrated project management control system that overlays a *cost-accounting structure* on the PERT time-scheduling network. Every activity in the network is assigned a budget, and as the project progresses, the *actual cost* and *earned value* of completed work are compared against the *budgeted cost of work scheduled* (BCWS).

**Two advantages:**
1. It provides an **early warning** of cost overruns by computing Cost Variance (CV) and Cost Performance Index (CPI) periodically.
2. It enables **time-cost trade-off analysis** through the cost-slope concept, allowing managers to compute the cost-optimal project duration quantitatively.

*(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for any two valid advantages)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between *Direct Cost* and *Indirect Cost* of a project with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Direct Cost | Indirect Cost |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| Attributable to a specific activity? | Yes | No |
| Behaviour as duration decreases | Increases | Decreases |
| Example in an IT project | Programmer salary for coding a module | Office rent, electricity, admin salaries |
| Curve shape | Convex increasing | Roughly linear decreasing |

*(1.5 marks each for the four distinguishing features, 0.5 mark for examples)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A project network has the following data. Indirect cost is **₹1,500 per day**. Compute the cost slope of every activity and find the **normal project duration**.

| Activity | Predecessor | $T_N$ | $C_N$ (₹) | $T_C$ | $C_C$ (₹) |
|----------|-------------|-------|-----------|-------|-----------|
| 1-2 | — | 6 | 800 | 4 | 1,400 |
| 1-3 | — | 8 | 1,200 | 5 | 2,100 |
| 2-4 | 1-2 | 4 | 600 | 2 | 1,000 |
| 3-4 | 1-3 | 5 | 900 | 3 | 1,500 |
| 4-5 | 2-4, 3-4 | 7 | 1,500 | 4 | 2,100 |

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Slope calculations:**

$$
S_{12} = \frac{1400-800}{6-4} = 300 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
S_{13} = \frac{2100-1200}{8-5} = 300 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
S_{24} = \frac{1000-600}{4-2} = 200 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
S_{34} = \frac{1500-900}{5-3} = 300 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

$$
S_{45} = \frac{2100-1500}{7-4} = 200 \; \text{₹/day}
$$

**Critical Path Analysis** — paths and durations:

- Path 1-2-4-5: $6+4+7 = 17$ days
- Path 1-3-4-5: $8+5+7 = 20$ days ✅

**Normal duration = 20 days, Critical path = 1-3-4-5**

**Normal Direct Cost** = 800 + 1,200 + 600 + 900 + 1,500 = **₹5,000**

**Normal Indirect Cost** = 20 × 1,500 = **₹30,000**

**Normal Total Cost = ₹35,000**

---

**(b)** Crash the project step by step to determine the **cost-optimal duration** and **minimum total cost**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Iterative Crashing:**

| Step | Crashed Activity (slope) | New Duration (days) | ΔDirect (₹) | ΔIndirect (₹) | ΔTotal (₹) | New Total (₹) |
|------|---------------------------|----------------------|---------------|----------------|--------------|-----------------|
| 0 | — | 20 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 35,000 |
| 1 | 3-4 (300) | 19 | +300 | −1,500 | −1,200 | 33,800 |
| 2 | 1-3 (300) | 18 | +300 | −1,500 | −1,200 | 32,600 |
| 3 | 4-5 (200) | 17 | +200 | −1,500 | −1,300 | 31,300 |
| 4 | Both paths critical; need combined crash. Cheapest = 200+200 = 400 > 1500 saving → **STOP** |  |  |  |  |  |

**Optimal duration = 17 days, Minimum total cost = ₹31,300**

*Verification: One more day of crashing would cost ₹400 in direct cost to save only ₹1,500 in indirect — net loss of ₹100 per day. Hence the previous point is optimal.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Earned Value Analysis** in a PERT/Cost framework. Define BCWS, BCWP, and ACWP with formulae. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Earned Value Analysis (EVA) is the *integrative reporting mechanism* of the PERT/Cost system. It converts *physical work completed* into a *monetary value* so that time and cost performance can be assessed on the same scale.

- **BCWS (Budgeted Cost of Work Scheduled)** — also called *Planned Value (PV)*. It is the sum of the budgeted costs of all activities that were *scheduled* to be completed by the status date.

  $$BCWS = \sum_{i \in \text{scheduled activities}} C_{N}(i)$$

- **BCWP (Budgeted Cost of Work Performed)** — also called *Earned Value (EV)*. It is the sum of the budgeted costs of all activities that have been *actually completed* by the status date.

  $$BCWP = \sum_{i \in \text{completed activities}} C_{N}(i)$$

- **ACWP (Actual Cost of Work Performed)** — also called *Actual Cost (AC)*. It is the sum of the money *actually spent* on the activities that have been completed by the status date.

  $$ACWP = \sum_{i \in \text{completed activities}} C_{actual}(i)$$

**The two key performance indices:**

- **Cost Variance:** $CV = BCWP - ACWP$. Positive = under budget.
- **Schedule Variance:** $SV = BCWP - BCWS$. Positive = ahead of schedule.
- **Cost Performance Index:** $CPI = BCWP / ACWP$. > 1 = efficient spending.
- **Schedule Performance Index:** $SPI = BCWP / BCWS$. > 1 = efficient time use.

*(3 marks for concept, 2 marks each for BCWS/BCWP/ACWP, 2 marks for indices)*

---

**(b)** A status report on **Day 18** of a 24-day project shows the following. Compute CV, SV, CPI, SPI and forecast the **Estimate at Completion (EAC)** given a Budget at Completion (BAC) of **₹6,00,000**.

| Activity | Budgeted Cost (₹) | % Complete | Actual Cost Spent (₹) |
|----------|-------------------|------------|------------------------|
| P | 1,00,000 | 100% | 1,10,000 |
| Q | 1,50,000 | 80% | 1,30,000 |
| R | 2,00,000 | 50% | 90,000 |
| S | 1,50,000 | 0% | 0 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute BCWS** (cost of activities scheduled to be done by Day 18):
Assume P, Q, R are scheduled fully by Day 18, S starts later. So BCWS = 1,00,000 + 1,50,000 + 2,00,000 = **₹4,50,000**.

**Step 2 — Compute BCWP** (budgeted cost of work *actually* done):
- P: 100% × 1,00,000 = 1,00,000
- Q: 80% × 1,50,000 = 1,20,000
- R: 50% × 2,00,000 = 1,00,000
- S: 0

$$
BCWP = 100000 + 120000 + 100000 + 0 = \text{₹}3{,}20{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — ACWP** (actual money spent):
$$
ACWP = 110000 + 130000 + 90000 + 0 = \text{₹}3{,}30{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Variances and Indices:**

$$
CV = BCWP - ACWP = 320000 - 330000 = -\text{₹}10{,}000
$$

$$
SV = BCWP - BCWS = 320000 - 450000 = -\text{₹}1{,}30{,}000
$$

$$
CPI = \frac{BCWP}{ACWP} = \frac{320000}{330000} = 0.97
$$

$$
SPI = \frac{BCWP}{BCWS} = \frac{320000}{450000} = 0.71
$$

**Interpretation:** CPI < 1 → *over budget*. SPI < 1 → *behind schedule*. Both are bad news.

**Step 5 — Forecast EAC:**

$$
EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{600000}{0.97} = \text{₹}6{,}18{,}557 \text{ (approx)}
$$

$$
VAC = BAC - EAC = 600000 - 618557 = -\text{₹}18{,}557
$$

The project is forecast to overshoot the budget by about **₹18,557** at completion if current performance persists.

*(7 marks — Stepwise allocation: 1 mark BCWS, 2 marks BCWP, 1 mark ACWP, 2 marks CV/SV/CPI/SPI, 1 mark EAC)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Cost slope direction:** Many students write the denominator as $(T_{C} - T_{N})$ instead of $(T_{N} - T_{C})$. The slope comes out *negative* and the entire crashing table goes wrong. Always: *Slope = (Crash cost − Normal cost) ÷ (Normal time − Crash time)*, giving a **positive** number.
> 2. **Recomputing the critical path:** After every crash, the path structure may *change* (a previously non-critical path can become critical). Failing to re-identify the critical path is the single most common error and costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **BCWP ≠ ACWP:** A frequent mistake is treating *actual* cost as *earned* value. They are different. BCWP uses the *budgeted* rate; ACWP uses the *actual* invoice.
> 4. **Stopping criterion:** Do not keep crashing until the project hits its *all-crash* duration. Stop when the **marginal direct cost ≥ marginal indirect cost saved**. This is the cost-optimum, not the time-optimum.
> 5. **Penalty clause:** If the problem states a penalty for delay, add it to the indirect cost savings before comparing with the cost slope. Many students ignore the penalty and arrive at a wrong optimal duration.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- [x] **PERT/Cost** = PERT (time) + WBS-based cost accounting + Earned Value reporting.
- [x] **Three-tier hierarchy**: Account → Work Package → Activity.
- [x] **Direct cost ↑** as duration ↓ (convex). **Indirect cost ↓** as duration ↓ (linear).
- [x] **Total cost** = U-shaped curve; the *minimum* gives the **cost-optimal duration**.
- [x] **Cost slope** = (Crash cost − Normal cost) / (Normal time − Crash time). Always positive.
- [x] **Crashing rule:** Crash the *cheapest critical activity* whose slope < indirect cost saving per day.
- [x] **BCWS** = planned value; **BCWP** = earned value; **ACWP** = actual cost.
- [x] **CV = BCWP − ACWP** (positive = good). **SV = BCWP − BCWS** (positive = good).
- [x] **CPI = BCWP / ACWP**; **SPI = BCWP / BCWS**. Index > 1 ⇒ healthy.
- [x] **EAC = BAC / CPI**; **VAC = BAC − EAC**.
- [x] Always **recompute the critical path** after each crashing iteration.
- [x] If **multiple critical paths** exist, crash the **cheapest combination** that reduces *all* critical paths simultaneously.
- [x] **Stop crashing** when additional crash cost ≥ indirect cost saved (plus any penalty avoided).
- [x] The **all-crash duration** is the *absolute minimum* time — not the *cost-optimal* time.
- [x] In KTU exams, always show the *crashing table* with rows for each iteration — it is the only way to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resource Scheduling and Resource Levelling: Project Management Features - Risk Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Resource Scheduling and Resource Levelling: Project Management Features - Risk Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Resource Scheduling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resource Scheduling** is the process of determining *when* and *how* allocated resources (human, equipment, material) will be deployed across the activities of a project to ensure tasks are completed within their planned start and finish windows, while respecting activity dependencies and resource availability calendars.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, resource scheduling is treated as a **time-bound assignment problem** where the project manager maps each activity to a specific time window $[S_i, F_i]$ such that:

$$\forall i \in A,\quad S_i \ge ES_i,\quad F_i \le LF_i$$

where $ES_i$ is the Early Start and $LF_i$ is the Late Finish of activity $i$, and $A$ is the set of all project activities.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Platform Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a busy railway junction with 10 trains (activities) needing 4 platforms (resources). You cannot park all trains simultaneously. **Resource scheduling** is the timetable planner who decides *which train occupies which platform and at what time*, ensuring no two express trains collide on the same platform. If the express train is delayed, you must reshuffle — but you try not to cancel the journey (i.e., not extend the project finish date).

The railway controller's goal mirrors the project manager's goal: **minimize idle time on platforms while ensuring every train departs**.

## 1.2 Resource Levelling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resource Levelling** is a resource optimization technique used to *smooth out* the peaks and troughs in the daily/periodic resource histogram so that resource demand does not exceed a fixed availability limit. When levelling forces a delay, the **project completion date may be extended**, but the project *logic* (activity dependencies) is preserved.

Mathematically, given a resource capacity $C$ (constant) and demand $R_t$ on day $t$:

$$\text{Objective:}\quad \min \sum_{t=1}^{T} \left( R_t - \bar{R} \right)^2 \quad \text{subject to}\quad R_t \le C,\ \forall t$$

where $\bar{R}$ is the average daily demand.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Water Bucket Smoothing

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Picture a bucket with uneven water levels across days — Mondays flooded with work, Fridays almost empty. **Resource Levelling** is the act of gently rocking the bucket so the water level is nearly uniform. You cannot remove water (you cannot eliminate work), but you can *shift* it within the float (slack) window — at the cost of taking a little longer to finish pouring.

## 1.3 Project Management Features (PMIS Perspective)

Modern PM software (MS Project, Primavera P6, Open Workbench) provides integrated features:

- **Gantt Charts** with resource histograms
- **Critical Path identification**
- **Earned Value Management (EVM)** dashboards
- **What-if scenario analysis**
- **Risk registers and Monte Carlo simulators**
- **Baseline tracking and variance analysis**

## 1.4 Risk Analysis — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Analysis** is the systematic process of identifying, assessing, and prioritizing project risks followed by the coordinated application of resources to minimize, monitor, and control the probability or impact of unfortunate events. Mathematically, risk is the product of **probability and impact**:
> $$R = P \times I$$
> where $P$ is the probability of occurrence ($0 \le P \le 1$) and $I$ is the impact (typically measured in monetary units, schedule days, or a 1–5 severity scale).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climbing Expedition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Before a Himalayan expedition, the leader identifies risks — avalanche probability, altitude sickness, equipment failure. She rates each on **likelihood** and **consequence**. Risks with high probability × high impact (e.g., sudden blizzard) are mitigated first with extra supplies and an alternate route. This is **Risk Analysis** — climbing smarter, not harder.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Summary

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Resource Capacity** | $C$ | persons/day, hours/day |
| **Resource Demand** | $R_t$ | persons/day |
| **Total Float** | $TF_i$ | days |
| **Risk Probability** | $P$ | $0 \le P \le 1$ (dimensionless) |
| **Risk Impact** | $I$ | 1–5 scale or currency |
| **Expected Monetary Value** | EMV | currency |
| **Schedule Performance Index** | SPI | $\ge 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Resource Scheduling — Operational Logic

The scheduling process follows a structured pipeline:

1. **Activity Definition:** Decompose the WBS into leaf-level activities with estimates for duration, predecessors, and resource requirements.
2. **Precedence Network Construction:** Build the Activity-on-Arrow (AOA) or Activity-on-Node (AON) network; perform the **forward pass** to compute $ES_i$ and $EF_i$, and the **backward pass** to compute $LS_i$ and $LF_i$.
3. **Resource Demand Aggregation:** For each time unit $t$, compute total resource demand:
$$R_t = \sum_{i:\,S_i \le t \le F_i} r_i$$
where $r_i$ is the resource requirement of activity $i$.
4. **Conflict Detection:** Compare $R_t$ with capacity $C$. If $R_t > C$ at any time slot, a **resource conflict** exists.
5. **Conflict Resolution:** Apply heuristics — Priority Rules, Resource Smoothing, or Levelling.
6. **Schedule Publication:** Issue the final schedule with start/finish dates and resource assignments.

### Priority Rules Used in KTU Exam Problems

> [!NOTE]
> Most frequently tested rules:
> - **SPT** — Shortest Processing Time first
> - **LPT** — Longest Processing Time first
> - **MINSLK** — Minimum Slack first
> - **MAXPW** — Maximum Predecessor Work first
> - **FCFS** — First Come First Served

## 2.2 Resource Levelling — The "Why" Behind the Process

Levelling is performed because:
- **Over-allocation** causes worker burnout, quality defects, and burnout-driven attrition.
- **Under-utilization** inflates payroll costs without productive output.
- **Fluctuating demand** complicates subcontracting, hiring, and procurement.

The heuristic is simple: **delay non-critical activities** (those with positive $TF$) into their float windows so that demand flattens. The algorithm is:

- **Step 1:** Compute the resource histogram using the early-start schedule.
- **Step 2:** Identify the peak day where $R_t > C$.
- **Step 3:** Among conflicting activities, identify the one with the **largest float**.
- **Step 4:** Delay that activity to a later start within its float; recompute histogram.
- **Step 5:** Repeat until $R_t \le C$ for all $t$ (or the float is exhausted).

## 2.3 Risk Analysis — Conceptual Layers

Risk analysis has three operational layers:

| Layer | Function | Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Identification** | Enumerate risks | Brainstorming, Delphi, SWOT, Checklists |
| **Assessment (Qualitative)** | Rank by P × I matrix | Probability-Impact Matrix, Risk Register |
| **Assessment (Quantitative)** | Numeric estimation | EMV, Monte Carlo, Decision Trees, PERT 3-point |

### Risk Response Strategies

- **Avoid** — Eliminate the threat by changing the plan.
- **Mitigate** — Reduce probability or impact.
- **Transfer** — Shift to a third party (insurance, outsourcing).
- **Accept** — Acknowledge and create a contingency reserve.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ES_j = \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)}(EF_i)$ | Forward pass — Early Start | days |
| 2 | $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$ | Early Finish | days |
| 3 | $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$ | Late Start | days |
| 4 | $LF_i = \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)}(LS_j)$ | Backward pass — Late Finish | days |
| 5 | $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | Total Float | days |
| 6 | $FF_i = \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)}(ES_j) - EF_i$ | Free Float | days |
| 7 | $R_t = \sum_{i\,:\,S_i \le t \le F_i} r_i$ | Resource demand on day t | persons |
| 8 | $R = P \times I$ | Risk score | dimensionless |
| 9 | $EMV = \sum_i P_i \times I_i$ | Expected Monetary Value | currency |
| 10 | $t_e = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$ | PERT expected duration | days |
| 11 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{b - a}{6}$ | PERT standard deviation | days |
| 12 | $P(T \le T_s) = Z \cdot \sigma + \mu$ | Probability of meeting deadline | dimensionless |
| 13 | $CV = EV - AC$ | Cost Variance | currency |
| 14 | $SV = EV - PV$ | Schedule Variance | currency |
| 15 | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | Cost Performance Index | ratio |
| 16 | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | Schedule Performance Index | ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Formulas 1–8 are tested every semester. Formulas 10–12 (PERT) are high-weightage and frequently appear in Module 2 questions on Risk Analysis.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in IT Engineering

In IT project management, these techniques are applied to:
- **Agile release planning** — resource levelling across sprints.
- **Cloud capacity planning** — smoothing compute demand against server quotas.
- **Vendor risk management** — using EMV for contract decisions.
- **Cybersecurity project risk** — P × I matrix for threat prioritization.
- **Disaster recovery planning** — Monte Carlo for RTO/RPO validation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Forward & Backward Pass (CPM Network)

Consider a 5-activity project with the following data:

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration $D_i$ (days) |
|---|---|---|
| A | — | 4 |
| B | A | 3 |
| C | A | 2 |
| D | B | 5 |
| E | C, D | 4 |

**Step 1: Forward Pass — Compute $ES_i$ and $EF_i$.**

For activity A (no predecessor):
$$ES_A = 0,\quad EF_A = ES_A + D_A = 0 + 4 = 4$$

For activity B (predecessor A):
$$ES_B = EF_A = 4,\quad EF_B = 4 + 3 = 7$$

For activity C (predecessor A):
$$ES_C = EF_A = 4,\quad EF_C = 4 + 2 = 6$$

For activity D (predecessor B):
$$ES_D = EF_B = 7,\quad EF_D = 7 + 5 = 12$$

For activity E (predecessors C and D):
$$ES_E = \max(EF_C, EF_D) = \max(6, 12) = 12,\quad EF_E = 12 + 4 = 16$$

**Project Duration = 16 days.**

**Step 2: Backward Pass — Compute $LS_i$ and $LF_i$.**

For activity E (no successor — project end):
$$LF_E = 16,\quad LS_E = 16 - 4 = 12$$

For activity D (successor E):
$$LF_D = LS_E = 12,\quad LS_D = 12 - 5 = 7$$

For activity C (successor E):
$$LF_C = LS_E = 12,\quad LS_C = 12 - 2 = 10$$

For activity B (successor D):
$$LF_B = LS_D = 7,\quad LS_B = 7 - 3 = 4$$

For activity A (successors B and C):
$$LF_A = \min(LS_B, LS_C) = \min(4, 10) = 4,\quad LS_A = 4 - 4 = 0$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Float.**

| Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | $TF = LS - ES$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | **0 (Critical)** |
| B | 4 | 7 | 4 | 7 | **0 (Critical)** |
| C | 4 | 6 | 10 | 12 | **6** |
| D | 7 | 12 | 7 | 12 | **0 (Critical)** |
| E | 12 | 16 | 12 | 16 | **0 (Critical)** |

**Critical Path: A → B → D → E (Duration = 16 days).**

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Resource Histogram & Levelling

Suppose each activity requires 1 person on all days. Compute the resource histogram from the early-start schedule.

| Day | Active Activities | $R_t$ (persons) |
|---|---|---|
| 1–4 | A | 1 |
| 5–6 | B, C | 2 |
| 7 | B, D | 2 |
| 8–11 | D | 1 |
| 12–15 | E | 1 |

Now suppose **resource capacity $C = 1$ person**. On day 5–7, we have over-allocation ($R_t = 2 > C = 1$).

**Levelling Decision:** Activity C has $TF = 6$ days. Delay C from day 5 to day 11 (within its float window $LS_C = 10$, so latest start = day 10). 

Let us shift C to start on day 9 (delay by 4 days). New schedule:

| Day | Active Activities | $R_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1–4 | A | 1 |
| 5–7 | B, D | 2 ❌ |

B is critical, so we cannot delay B. We must shift D? D is critical too. So the only option is to shift C, but B+D overlap is day 7. Let us delay C further.

**Shift C to start at day 9:** C runs days 9–10.

| Day | Active Activities | $R_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1–4 | A | 1 |
| 5–6 | B | 1 |
| 7 | B, D | 2 ❌ |

B finishes day 7, D starts day 7 — still conflict. We must delay D (critical, $TF=0$) — but that extends project!

**Conclusion:** With C at days 5–6 and D at days 7–11, we cannot level with $C=1$ without extending the project. We must allow C = 2 or extend project to 17 days by shifting D to day 8.

**Final levelled schedule (with C=2):** No levelling needed.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — PERT 3-Point Estimation & Project Risk

A project has 3 tasks with optimistic (a), most likely (m), pessimistic (b) estimates:

| Task | a (days) | m (days) | b (days) |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | 2 | 4 | 12 |
| T2 | 3 | 5 | 7 |
| T3 | 1 | 3 | 5 |

**Step 1: Compute expected time $t_e$ for each task.**

For T1:
$$t_{e1} = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 12}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 12}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}$$

For T2:
$$t_{e2} = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 7}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 7}{6} = \frac{30}{6} = 5 \text{ days}$$

For T3:
$$t_{e3} = \frac{1 + 4(3) + 5}{6} = \frac{1 + 12 + 5}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: Compute standard deviation $\sigma_t$ for each task.**

For T1:
$$\sigma_1 = \frac{b - a}{6} = \frac{12 - 2}{6} = \frac{10}{6} \approx 1.667$$

For T2:
$$\sigma_2 = \frac{7 - 3}{6} = \frac{4}{6} \approx 0.667$$

For T3:
$$\sigma_3 = \frac{5 - 1}{6} = \frac{4}{6} \approx 0.667$$

**Step 3: Compute expected project duration (sequential).**

If tasks are sequential on the critical path:
$$\mu = t_{e1} + t_{e2} + t_{e3} = 5 + 5 + 3 = 13 \text{ days}$$

**Step 4: Compute project variance (sum of variances on critical path).**

$$\sigma^2 = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 = (1.667)^2 + (0.667)^2 + (0.667)^2$$

$$\sigma^2 = 2.778 + 0.445 + 0.445 = 3.668$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{3.668} \approx 1.915 \text{ days}$$

**Step 5: Compute probability of finishing within 15 days.**

$$Z = \frac{T_s - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{15 - 13}{1.915} = \frac{2}{1.915} \approx 1.044$$

From standard normal table:
$$P(Z \le 1.04) \approx 0.85$$

**Conclusion: There is approximately an 85% chance of completing the project within 15 days.**

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Expected Monetary Value (EMV)

A software company is bidding on a project. Three possible outcomes:

| Outcome | Probability $P_i$ | Impact $I_i$ (Profit in ₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|
| Win & Succeed | 0.5 | +50 |
| Win & Cost Overrun | 0.3 | −20 |
| Lose Bid | 0.2 | −5 (bid cost) |

**Step 1: Compute EMV.**

$$EMV = \sum_i P_i \cdot I_i = (0.5)(50) + (0.3)(-20) + (0.2)(-5)$$

$$EMV = 25 + (-6) + (-1) = 25 - 6 - 1 = 18 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Decision:** Since EMV is positive (+₹18L), proceed with the bid.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Risk Matrix Scoring

A risk register has the following entries:

| Risk | Probability (1–5) | Impact (1–5) | Score $P \times I$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Server downtime | 4 | 5 | 20 |
| Key developer attrition | 2 | 5 | 10 |
| Scope creep | 5 | 3 | 15 |
| Third-party API failure | 3 | 4 | 12 |

**Interpretation:**
- Score 15–25: **High** — Immediate mitigation needed.
- Score 8–14: **Medium** — Monitor and plan contingency.
- Score 1–7: **Low** — Accept and document.

**Priority Order:** Server downtime (20) → Scope creep (15) → API failure (12) → Attrition (10).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Resource Scheduling & Levelling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project WBS and Activity List] --> A1[Build Precedence Network AON]
    A1 --> A2[Forward Pass: Compute ES and EF]
    A2 --> A3[Backward Pass: Compute LS and LF]
    A3 --> A4[Identify Critical Path and Total Float]
    A4 --> A5{Q Resource Conflict?}
    A5 -- No --> A6[Publish Baseline Schedule]
    A5 -- Yes --> A7[Apply Resource Levelling Heuristic]
    A7 --> A8[Delay Non Critical Activity within Float]
    A8 --> A9[Recompute Resource Histogram]
    A9 --> A5
    A6 --> A10[Issue Resource Allocations to Teams]
    A10 --> A11[Monitor and Control via Earned Value]
```

## 4.2 Risk Analysis Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Risk Identification Brainstorming Delphi SWOT] --> R2[Qualitative Assessment P I Matrix]
    R2 --> R3{Score Greater Than Threshold}
    R3 -- No --> R4[Accept and Log in Register]
    R3 -- Yes --> R5[Quantitative Assessment EMV Monte Carlo PERT]
    R5 --> R6[Risk Response Planning]
    R6 --> R7[Response Strategies: Avoid Mitigate Transfer Accept]
    R7 --> R8[Implement Controls and Contingency Reserve]
    R8 --> R9[Monitor Track Reassess]
    R9 --> R2
```

## 4.3 Resource Histogram — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    H1[Day 1 2 3 4] --> H1b[Demand 1 1 1 1]
    H2[Day 5 6 7] --> H2b[Demand 3 3 2 Peak Zone]
    H3[Day 8 9 10 11] --> H3b[Demand 1 1 1 1]
    H4[Day 12 13 14 15 16] --> H4b[Demand 1 1 1 1 1]
    H1b --> H5{Capacity C equals 2}
    H2b --> H5
    H3b --> H5
    H4b --> H5
    H5 -- Over capacity on days 5 6 --> H6[Apply Levelling Shift Activity C by 4 days]
    H5 -- Within capacity --> H7[No Levelling Required]
    H6 --> H8[New Histogram: Demand flattens to 2 per day]
```

## 4.4 PMIS Integration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Scope Management WBS] --> P2[Schedule Engine CPM PERT]
    P3[Resource Database] --> P2
    P2 --> P4[Cost Engine EVM CV SV CPI SPI]
    P5[Risk Register] --> P6[Risk Engine Monte Carlo EMV]
    P6 --> P2
    P6 --> P4
    P2 --> P7[Dashboards and Reports]
    P4 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Decision Support for Project Manager]
    P8 --> P9[Corrective Actions and Replanning]
    P9 --> P1
    P9 --> P3
    P9 --> P5
```

## 4.5 Probability-Impact Risk Matrix

```mermaid
graph TB
    M1[Probability 5 Very High] --> M5[Impact 5 Catastrophic: Score 25]
    M1 --> M4[Impact 4 Major: Score 20]
    M2[Probability 4 High] --> M5
    M2 --> M4
    M2 --> M3[Impact 3 Moderate: Score 12]
    M3p[Probability 3 Medium] --> M4
    M3p --> M3
    M3p --> M2lo[Impact 2 Minor: Score 6]
    M4p[Probability 2 Low] --> M3
    M4p --> M2lo
    M4p --> M1lo[Impact 1 Negligible: Score 2]
    M5p[Probability 1 Very Low] --> M2lo
    M5p --> M1lo
    M5 --> ZONE[RED ZONE Immediate Action]
    M4 --> ZONE
    M3 --> ZONE2[ORANGE ZONE Mitigation Plan]
    M2lo --> ZONE2
    M1lo --> ZONE3[GREEN ZONE Accept and Monitor]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between Resource Scheduling and Resource Levelling. State any two priority rules used in resource scheduling.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Resource Scheduling | Resource Levelling |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Decide *when* each activity starts | Smooth out resource demand histogram |
| **Project Duration** | Fixed (within float) | May extend beyond critical path duration |
| **Float Usage** | Uses available float selectively | Uses float aggressively to flatten peaks |
| **Output** | Start/finish dates for all activities | A feasible schedule respecting capacity $C$ |

**Two Priority Rules:**
1. **MINSLK** (Minimum Slack) — Schedule activity with least float first.
2. **SPT** (Shortest Processing Time) — Schedule shortest activity first to maximize throughput.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for distinction + 1.5 for priority rules)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Risk. Compute the Expected Monetary Value (EMV) for a project with the following outcomes:

| Outcome | Probability | Impact (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| Success | 0.6 | 1,00,000 |
| Partial | 0.3 | 40,000 |
| Failure | 0.1 | −50,000 |

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Risk is an uncertain event or condition that, if it occurs, has a positive or negative effect on project objectives. It is quantified as:
$$R = P \times I$$

**EMV Calculation:**

$$EMV = (0.6 \times 1{,}00{,}000) + (0.3 \times 40{,}000) + (0.1 \times -50{,}000)$$

$$EMV = 60{,}000 + 12{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 67{,}000$$

**Decision:** Since EMV = ₹67,000 > 0, the project is financially viable.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for EMV computation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** For the project network given below, compute the Early Start (ES), Early Finish (EF), Late Start (LS), Late Finish (LF), Total Float (TF), and identify the critical path.

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) | Resources (persons) |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 5 | 3 |
| B | A | 4 | 2 |
| C | A | 3 | 4 |
| D | B | 6 | 2 |
| E | B, C | 5 | 3 |
| F | D, E | 4 | 2 |

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Prepare the daily resource histogram. The resource capacity is **4 persons per day**. If over-allocation exists, apply resource levelling and present the levelled schedule. State whether the project duration changes. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — Forward and Backward Pass

**Forward Pass:**

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration | $ES$ | $EF$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 5 | 0 | 5 |
| B | A | 4 | 5 | 9 |
| C | A | 3 | 5 | 8 |
| D | B | 6 | 9 | 15 |
| E | B, C | 5 | $\max(9, 8) = 9$ | 14 |
| F | D, E | 4 | $\max(15, 14) = 15$ | 19 |

**Project Duration = 19 days.**

**Backward Pass:**

| Activity | Successor | $LF$ | $LS$ | $TF = LS - ES$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| F | — | 19 | 15 | 0 |
| E | F | 15 | 10 | 1 |
| D | F | 15 | 9 | 0 |
| C | E | 10 | 7 | 2 |
| B | D, E | $\min(9, 10) = 9$ | 5 | 0 |
| A | B, C | $\min(5, 7) = 5$ | 0 | 0 |

**Critical Path: A → B → D → F (Duration = 19 days).**

#### Solution to (b) — Histogram and Levelling

**Early-Start Histogram (assuming resources are required throughout the activity duration):**

| Day Range | Active Activities | Total Demand $R_t$ | Capacity | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1–5 | A | 3 | 4 | OK |
| 6–8 | B, C | 2 + 4 = 6 | 4 | **OVER (excess 2)** |
| 9 | B, D, E | 2 + 2 + 3 = 7 | 4 | **OVER (excess 3)** |
| 10 | D, E | 2 + 3 = 5 | 4 | **OVER (excess 1)** |
| 11–14 | D, E | 2 + 3 = 5 | 4 | **OVER** |
| 15 | D, F | 2 + 2 = 4 | 4 | OK |
| 16–19 | F | 2 | 4 | OK |

**Levelling Decisions:**
- Activity E has $TF = 1$ day — can shift by 1 day.
- Activity C has $TF = 2$ days — can shift by up to 2 days.

**Strategy:** Shift C by 2 days (start on day 7), shift E by 1 day (start on day 10).

| Day | Active | $R_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1–5 | A | 3 |
| 6 | B, C | 6 ❌ |

Conflict remains. Shift C by another day? $TF_C = 2$, so C can start at day 7 latest.

**Levelled Schedule:** C runs days 7–9 (overlaps B at day 7 — still 2 + 4 = 6). C runs days 7, 8, 9.

If we move C to start day 7: C runs 7–9. B is 6–9. E depends on B and C, so E starts at $\max(EF_B, EF_C) = \max(9, 9) = 9$ → E runs 9–13. Then F depends on D, E → $\max(15, 13) = 15$ → F unchanged at 15–19.

**New Histogram:**

| Day | Active | $R_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1–5 | A (3) | 3 |
| 6 | B (2) | 2 |
| 7–9 | B (2) + C (4) | 6 ❌ |

Conflict persists. Since C and B share the same start (A finishes day 5), the over-allocation is unavoidable without **splitting activities or hiring additional resources**.

**Conclusion:** With $C = 4$, levelling is **not feasible** without project extension. Either increase capacity to $C = 6$ or accept a 2-day extension by shifting E.

**Project Duration Changes?** Yes — by 1 to 2 days if we use remaining float.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the four risk response strategies with a suitable IT project example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A software development task has optimistic estimate $a = 8$ days, most likely $m = 12$ days, and pessimistic $b = 22$ days. Compute the PERT expected time and standard deviation. If the contract deadline is 15 days, what is the probability of meeting the deadline? **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — Risk Response Strategies

1. **Avoid:** Eliminate the risk by changing the project plan.
   - *Example:* To avoid the risk of using a new unproven database engine, the team selects a mature SQL engine instead.

2. **Mitigate:** Reduce either the probability or impact of the risk.
   - *Example:* Conduct additional code reviews and unit testing to reduce defect probability; add input validation to reduce impact of malformed data.

3. **Transfer:** Shift risk ownership to a third party.
   - *Example:* Buy cyber insurance; outsource payment processing to a PCI-DSS compliant vendor.

4. **Accept:** Acknowledge the risk and create a contingency plan.
   - *Example:* Accept the risk of minor UI bugs in v1.0; allocate 5 days of buffer time post-launch for hotfixes.

*(7 marks: 1.5 per strategy + 1 for conclusion)*

#### Solution to (b) — PERT Calculation

**Step 1: Compute Expected Time.**

$$t_e = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6} = \frac{8 + 4(12) + 22}{6} = \frac{8 + 48 + 22}{6} = \frac{78}{6} = 13 \text{ days}$$

**Step 2: Compute Standard Deviation.**

$$\sigma = \frac{b - a}{6} = \frac{22 - 8}{6} = \frac{14}{6} \approx 2.333 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3: Compute Z-Score for $T_s = 15$ days.**
