$$Z = \frac{T_s - t_e}{\sigma} = \frac{15 - 13}{2.333} = \frac{2}{2.333} \approx 0.857$$

**Step 4: Look up Standard Normal Table.**

$$P(Z \le 0.86) \approx 0.8051 \text{ (or about 80.5\%)}$$

**Conclusion:** There is approximately an **80.5% probability** of completing the task within the 15-day deadline.

*(7 marks: 2 for $t_e$, 2 for $\sigma$, 3 for probability calculation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the formula correctly for Free Float vs Total Float** — Many students write $TF_i$ where $FF_i$ is required. Free float uses *early* dates of successors, not late dates.
> 2. **PERT variance mistake** — Variance of project = **sum** of variances on critical path, NOT sum of standard deviations. Many students add $\sigma$ values instead of $\sigma^2$.
> 3. **Resource levelling confusion** — Levelling allows *project extension*; *smoothing* does NOT. Using the terms interchangeably costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **EMV sign error** — Negative impacts (losses) must be subtracted, not added. A missing negative sign is a frequent deduction.
> 5. **Z-table misuse** — Z-table gives $P(Z \le z)$. Students often read $1 - P$ values incorrectly, reversing the conclusion.
> 6. **In CPM, missing the max/min** — In forward pass, $ES_j = \max$ of predecessors' $EF$; in backward pass, $LF_i = \min$ of successors' $LS$. Forgetting this is the #1 error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Resource Scheduling
- **CPM Forward Pass:** $ES_i = \max(\text{pred } EF)$, $EF_i = ES_i + D_i$.
- **CPM Backward Pass:** $LF_i = \min(\text{succ } LS)$, $LS_i = LF_i - D_i$.
- **Critical Path:** Activities with $TF = 0$ form the critical path; they dictate project duration.
- **Total Float:** $TF_i = LS_i - ES_i$ (or $LF_i - EF_i$ — both give same result).
- **Free Float:** $FF_i = \min(ES_{\text{succ}}) - EF_i$.

### Resource Levelling
- **Goal:** Smooth resource histogram so $R_t \le C$ for all $t$.
- **Cost:** Project duration may increase.
- **Method:** Delay non-critical activities within their float.
- **Output:** Feasible schedule respecting resource capacity.

### Risk Analysis
- **Risk Score:** $R = P \times I$.
- **EMV:** $EMV = \sum P_i \cdot I_i$.
- **PERT Expected Time:** $t_e = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$.
- **PERT Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \dfrac{b - a}{6}$.
- **Project Variance:** Sum of variances on critical path: $\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = \sum \sigma_i^2$.
- **Z-Score:** $Z = \dfrac{T_s - \mu_{\text{project}}}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}$.
- **Response Strategies:** **A**void, **M**itigate, **T**ransfer, **A**ccept (mnemonic: **AMTA**).

### PMIS Features (Quick Recall)
- **Gantt Charts, Network Diagrams, Histograms** for visualization.
- **EVM Metrics:** CV, SV, CPI, SPI for performance tracking.
- **What-If Analysis** for scenario planning.
- **Risk Registers and Dashboards** for governance.

### High-Yield Keywords for KTU Answers
- *Critical Path Method (CPM)*
- *Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)*
- *Expected Monetary Value (EMV)*
- *Resource Histogram*
- *Probability-Impact Matrix*
- *Total Float vs Free Float*
- *Monte Carlo Simulation*
- *Risk Register*
- *Contingency Reserve vs Management Reserve*
- *Earned Value Management (EVM)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Project Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Project Control — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Project Control** is the disciplined, continuous process of comparing actual project performance against the approved project management plan (baseline), analyzing variances, evaluating alternative courses of action, and taking timely corrective action to bring the project back on track in terms of **scope, schedule, cost, and quality**.

As per the PMBOK-aligned KTU terminology, project control is *not* a one-time event — it is an **integrated, iterative management function** performed throughout every phase of the project life cycle. It involves:

- **Monitoring** — collecting performance data (cost, schedule, scope, quality, risk).
- **Comparing** — measuring actuals against the baseline.
- **Analyzing** — identifying the *cause* of deviations (variances).
- **Taking Action** — corrective or preventive actions, change requests, and rebaselining when necessary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Under the *Project Control* topic, students are expected to master the **Planning–Control Cycle**, **Data Collection & Reporting**, **Earned Value Analysis**, **Forecasting**, and **Change Control** as part of the monitoring and controlling process group.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **driving a car from Kochi to Bengaluru (≈ 600 km)**. Before starting, you:
1. Plan the route (≈ **Project Plan / Baseline**).
2. Estimate fuel, time, and rest stops (≈ **Cost & Schedule Baseline**).
3. Start driving (≈ **Project Execution**).

While driving, you constantly:
- Glance at the **speedometer** (Are we going too slow? — *Schedule Tracking*).
- Check the **fuel gauge** (Are we burning more fuel than expected? — *Cost Tracking*).
- Compare your **GPS position** to the planned route (Are we on the correct path? — *Scope & Performance Tracking*).
- Decide whether to refuel, change route, or speed up (≈ **Corrective Action**).

> **This is Project Control.** The car trip is the project, the GPS plan is the baseline, the dashboard readings are performance data, and the steering/brake/accelerator inputs are corrective actions.

## 1.3 Why Project Control is Critical

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reasons (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - Detects problems **early**, when they are cheap to fix.
> - Provides **objective, data-driven** decision support (no "gut feel" management).
> - Maintains **stakeholder confidence** through transparent reporting.
> - Enables **forecasting** (predicting final cost & finish date while project is mid-flight).
> - Forms the **legal/contractual basis** for change orders and claims.

In IT projects — where requirements often drift and scope creep is rampant — **without control, every project eventually fails on the "triple constraint" of scope, time, and cost**.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Project Control

| Pillar | What is Measured | Baseline Compared Against |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost Control** | Actual expenditure (AC) | Cost Baseline (BAC) |
| **Schedule Control** | Actual progress (date) | Schedule Baseline (planned dates) |
| **Scope Control** | Work completed (deliverables) | Scope Baseline (WBS) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this for the exam:** *Project Control = Monitoring + Comparing + Analyzing + Correcting* — the **M-C-A-C loop** integrated into the project's life cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Planning and Control Cycle (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Project control operates as a continuous **closed-loop cycle**. The standard KTU-recommended cycle has **6 stages**:

1. **Plan** — Establish baseline (scope, schedule, cost, quality).
2. **Execute** — Perform the work as per the plan.
3. **Measure / Data Collection** — Gather actual performance data (hours, cost, deliverables produced).
4. **Evaluate / Compare** — Compare actuals against the baseline; compute variances.
5. **Take Corrective / Preventive Action** — Modify work, rebaseline, or escalate.
6. **Feed Forward** — Update estimates and forecasts for the remaining work; communicate to stakeholders.

> This is often called the **"Plan–Do–Check–Act" (PDCA) cycle** of project control, embedded inside the project's **Integrated Change Control** process.

## 2.2 Data Collection and Reporting

### 2.2.1 Data Collection Methods
- **Time sheets** (for hours/effort tracking).
- **Cost accounting reports** (for expenditure).
- **Progress reports** from team leads.
- **Defect logs** (for quality control).
- **Risk registers** updates.
- **Automated tools** (JIRA, MS Project, Primavera dashboards).

### 2.2.2 Types of Reports
| Report Type | Frequency | Audience | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Status Report** | Weekly | Project Manager, Team | What was done, what's next, issues |
| **Progress Report** | Bi-weekly/Monthly | Sponsor, Steering Committee | Earned value, milestones |
| **Exception Report** | As needed | Senior Management | Highlight major variances only |
| **Final Report** | End of project | All stakeholders | Lessons learned, closure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Reporting (KTU Examiner's favourite):** Reports must be **timely, accurate, brief, and actionable**. A report that arrives 3 weeks late defeats the entire purpose of control.

## 2.3 Earned Value Analysis (EVA) — The Heart of Project Control

**Earned Value Management (EVM)** is a *quantitative* project control technique that integrates **scope, schedule, and cost** into a single, unified performance measurement system. It answers three questions at any instant in the project:

1. **How much work did we plan to do by now?** → **PV (Planned Value)**
2. **How much work did we actually complete by now?** → **EV (Earned Value)**
3. **How much did we actually spend to do that work?** → **AC (Actual Cost)**

### 2.3.1 The Three Core EVM Metrics

$$PV = \text{Planned Value (BCWS)} = \text{Budgeted cost of work scheduled}$$

$$EV = \text{Earned Value (BCWP)} = \text{Budgeted cost of work performed}$$

$$AC = \text{Actual Cost (ACWP)} = \text{Actual cost of work performed}$$

### 2.3.2 The Four Variance and Index Metrics

$$SV = EV - PV \quad \text{(Schedule Variance)}$$

$$CV = EV - AC \quad \text{(Cost Variance)}$$

$$SPI = \frac{EV}{PV} \quad \text{(Schedule Performance Index)}$$

$$CPI = \frac{EV}{AC} \quad \text{(Cost Performance Index)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Rules (must memorize):**
> - $SV > 0$ or $SPI > 1$ → **Ahead of schedule** ✅
> - $SV < 0$ or $SPI < 1$ → **Behind schedule** ❌
> - $CV > 0$ or $CPI > 1$ → **Under budget** ✅
> - $CV < 0$ or $CPI < 1$ → **Over budget** ❌

### 2.3.3 The Four Forecasting Metrics

$$EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI} \quad \text{(Estimate at Completion — using current CPI)}$$

$$ETC = EAC - AC \quad \text{(Estimate to Complete)}$$

$$VAC = BAC - EAC \quad \text{(Variance at Completion)}$$

$$TCPI = \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} \quad \text{(To-Complete Performance Index)}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table and memorize all 11 formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on Project Control.**

| # | Metric | Formula | What it Tells You | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Planned Value | $PV$ | Budgeted cost of work scheduled | ₹ |
| 2 | Earned Value | $EV$ | Budgeted cost of work performed | ₹ |
| 3 | Actual Cost | $AC$ | Real money spent | ₹ |
| 4 | Cost Variance | $CV = EV - AC$ | Under/Over budget | ₹ |
| 5 | Schedule Variance | $SV = EV - PV$ | Ahead/Behind schedule | ₹ |
| 6 | Cost Performance Index | $CPI = EV / AC$ | Cost efficiency | Ratio |
| 7 | Schedule Performance Index | $SPI = EV / PV$ | Schedule efficiency | Ratio |
| 8 | Budget at Completion | $BAC$ | Total approved budget | ₹ |
| 9 | Estimate at Completion | $EAC = BAC / CPI$ | Forecasted final cost | ₹ |
| 10 | Estimate to Complete | $ETC = EAC - AC$ | Money still needed | ₹ |
| 11 | Variance at Completion | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | Expected final savings/loss | ₹ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pipe-Symbol Pitfall:** When writing EVM formulas, **never write** $\vert SV \vert$ with the vertical bar `|` in plain markdown text — it breaks tables. Use `\vert SV \vert` or `SV magnitude` instead.

## 2.5 Change Control (Integrated Change Control)

**Change Control** is the formal process of managing scope, schedule, or cost changes to the baseline. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the **Change Control Board (CCB)** concept.

### 2.5.1 Why Change Control is Needed
- Prevent **uncontrolled scope creep** (the #1 killer of IT projects).
- Ensure all changes are **evaluated for impact** on the triple constraint.
- Maintain **documented traceability** for audit and lessons learned.

### 2.5.2 The Change Control Process (CCB Workflow)

1. **Identify** the change (via a *Change Request* form).
2. **Document** the impact on scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk.
3. **Review** by the **Change Control Board (CCB)** — body of stakeholders + PM + sponsor.
4. **Approve / Reject / Defer** the change.
5. **Update** the baseline (if approved) — this is a *rebaseline*.
6. **Communicate** the decision to all stakeholders.
7. **Implement** the approved change in the next iteration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Concept:** A **rebaseline** is *not* the same as the original baseline. Rebaselining resets the measurement yardstick — but historical performance indices (CPI/SPI) are **not retroactively changed**. Future forecasting uses the new baseline.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In large IT projects (ERP rollouts, banking software, government e-governance systems), Project Control — particularly EVM — is the **single most defensible evidence** a project manager can present to:
- A sponsor demanding "Are we on track?".
- A vendor negotiating penalties for delays.
- An auditor reviewing public-sector IT expenditure (e.g., Smart City, Digital India projects).

> **Real-world example:** A ₹10 Crore banking software project at month 6 of a 12-month schedule shows $CPI = 0.80$ and $SPI = 0.95$. Without EVM, the sponsor has no clue the project is heading toward a **₹2.5 Crore overrun** ($EAC = 12.5$ Cr). With EVM, the PM raises a red flag at month 4 and implements corrective action.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — EVM Computation (Standard 14-Mark KTU Pattern)

### Problem Statement
A project has a **Budget at Completion (BAC) of ₹ 5,00,000** and a planned duration of **12 months**. At the end of **month 6**, the project manager has the following data:
- Planned Value (PV) by month 6 = **₹ 2,50,000**
- Actual Cost (AC) incurred by month 6 = **₹ 2,80,000**
- Earned Value (EV) of work actually completed = **₹ 2,30,000**

**Required:** Calculate SV, CV, SPI, CPI, EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI. Interpret the project status.

### Step 1 — Schedule Variance (SV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
SV &= 2{,}30{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000 \\
SV &= -20{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $SV$ is **negative** → the project is **behind schedule** by the equivalent of ₹ 20,000 worth of planned work.

### Step 2 — Cost Variance (CV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
CV &= 2{,}30{,}000 - 2{,}80{,}000 \\
CV &= -50{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $CV$ is **negative** → the project is **over budget** by ₹ 50,000.

### Step 3 — Schedule Performance Index (SPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} \\
SPI &= \frac{2{,}30{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} \\
SPI &= 0.92
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $SPI = 0.92 < 1.00$ → For every **1 hour** of work planned, only **0.92 hours** worth of work is being completed. The project is **8 % behind schedule**.

### Step 4 — Cost Performance Index (CPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} \\
CPI &= \frac{2{,}30{,}000}{2{,}80{,}000} \\
CPI &= 0.8214
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $CPI \approx 0.82 < 1.00$ → We are getting only **₹ 0.82 of work for every ₹ 1.00 spent**. The project is **18 % over budget** in efficiency terms.

### Step 5 — Estimate at Completion (EAC) — using current CPI

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} \\
EAC &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.8214} \\
EAC &= 6{,}08{,}571
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** If the project continues at its current inefficient rate, it will end up costing **₹ 6,08,571** instead of the budgeted **₹ 5,00,000** — a projected **overrun of ₹ 1,08,571** (≈ 21.7 %).

### Step 6 — Estimate to Complete (ETC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ETC &= EAC - AC \\
ETC &= 6{,}08{,}571 - 2{,}80{,}000 \\
ETC &= 3{,}28{,}571
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** To complete the remaining work, the project will need an **additional ₹ 3,28,571** if no corrective action is taken.

### Step 7 — Variance at Completion (VAC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAC &= BAC - EAC \\
VAC &= 5{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}08{,}571 \\
VAC &= -1{,}08{,}571
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project is **forecasted to be ₹ 1,08,571 over budget** at completion (negative = overrun).

### Step 8 — To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPI &= \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} \\
TCPI &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}30{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}80{,}000} \\
TCPI &= \frac{2{,}70{,}000}{2{,}20{,}000} \\
TCPI &= 1.2273
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $TCPI > 1.00$ → To meet the original BAC of ₹ 5,00,000, the *remaining* work must be performed at an efficiency of **1.23** — i.e., get ₹ 1.23 of value for every ₹ 1.00 spent. This is **unrealistically high** given the current CPI of 0.82. **Conclusion:** The BAC target is no longer achievable — the PM should consider **rebaselining** or scope reduction.

### Step 9 — Final Status Matrix

| Metric | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| SV | ₹ –20,000 | ❌ Behind Schedule |
| CV | ₹ –50,000 | ❌ Over Budget |
| SPI | 0.92 | ❌ Behind |
| CPI | 0.82 | ❌ Over Budget |
| EAC | ₹ 6,08,571 | ⚠️ Forecasted overrun |
| ETC | ₹ 3,28,571 | ⚠️ Money still needed |
| VAC | ₹ –1,08,571 | ❌ Will overshoot by ₹ 1.08 L |
| TCPI | 1.23 | ⚠️ BAC unachievable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's verdict on this problem:** The project is in **CRITICAL state** — both schedule and cost are negative, TCPI > 1.0, and the BAC is unachievable. The PM must (a) escalate to the sponsor, (b) request scope reduction or budget increase, and (c) consider rebaselining.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — What Action to Take

| Observed State | CV | SV | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Healthy | $CV > 0$ | $SV \geq 0$ | Continue as planned; capture lessons |
| Over-budget, on time | $CV < 0$ | $SV \geq 0$ | Cost reduction: optimize resources, renegotiate vendor |
| On budget, behind schedule | $CV \geq 0$ | $SV < 0$ | Crashing/fast-tracking; add resources |
| Critical | $CV < 0$ | $SV < 0$ | **Rebaseline**; escalate; scope reduction |
| Earned more, spent less | $CV > 0$ | $SV > 0$ | Excellent — share best practices |

## 3.3 Sensitivity Check — Effect of EAC Formulas

There are **multiple EAC formulas** depending on assumption. KTU 2024 tests the most common three:

| Assumption | Formula | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| Past performance is representative | $EAC = BAC / CPI$ | Default; CPI-based |
| Past + remaining both at budget | $EAC = AC + (BAC - EV)$ | If anomalies are temporary |
| New estimate for remaining work | $EAC = AC + ETC_{new}$ | PM has new bottom-up estimate |

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Formula 1 (CPI-based):} \quad EAC_1 &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{0.8214} = 6{,}08{,}571 \\[4pt]
\text{Formula 2 (Budgeted remaining):} \quad EAC_2 &= AC + (BAC - EV) = 2{,}80{,}000 + 2{,}70{,}000 = 5{,}50{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{Formula 3 (New ETC):} \quad EAC_3 &= AC + ETC_{new}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If the question says *"assuming current performance continues"*, use **Formula 1**. If it says *"future work will be done at planned rate"*, use **Formula 2**. If it says *"the PM re-estimates the remaining work"*, use **Formula 3**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Project Planning & Control Cycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: PLAN — Establish baseline scope, schedule, cost] --> B[Step 2: EXECUTE — Perform project work]
    B --> C[Step 3: MEASURE — Collect actual data: cost, hours, deliverables]
    C --> D[Step 4: EVALUATE — Compare actuals vs baseline: compute PV, EV, AC, variances]
    D --> E{Step 5: Are variances within acceptable limits?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Step 6a: CONTINUE — Update forecasts and proceed]
    E -- No --> G[Step 6b: TAKE ACTION — Corrective or preventive action]
    G --> H[Step 7: UPDATE BASELINE or ESCALATE to CCB]
    H --> C
    F --> C
```

## 4.2 Earned Value Triangle — The Core EVM Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PV[PV — Planned Value BCWS] -->|EV greater than PV = ahead| META{Project Status}
    EV[EV — Earned Value BCWP] --> META
    AC[AC — Actual Cost ACWP] -->|EV greater than AC = under budget| META
    META -->|SV positive, CV positive| HEALTHY[Project Healthy]
    META -->|SV negative, CV negative| CRITICAL[Project Critical]
    META -->|SV positive, CV negative| OBCOST[Over Budget On Time]
    META -->|SV negative, CV positive| BEHIND[Behind Schedule On Budget]
```

## 4.3 Integrated Change Control Workflow (CCB)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REQ[Change Request Raised by Stakeholder] --> DOC[Document Impact: Scope, Schedule, Cost, Quality, Risk]
    DOC --> REVIEW{CCB Review Meeting}
    REVIEW -- Approved --> UPD[Update Project Management Plan and Baseline]
    REVIEW -- Rejected --> NOTIFY1[Inform Requester with Reasons]
    REVIEW -- Deferred --> BACKLOG[Add to Backlog for Future Phase]
    UPD --> COMM[Communicate to All Stakeholders]
    COMM --> IMPL[Implement Change in Next Iteration]
    IMPL --> LOG[Log in Change Log and Lessons Learned]
    NOTIFY1 --> LOG
    BACKLOG --> LOG
    LOG --> CLOSE[Close Change Request]
```

## 4.4 EVM Metrics Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BASE[3 Base Metrics: PV, EV, AC] --> VAR[4 Variance and Index Metrics: SV, CV, SPI, CPI]
    VAR --> FORECAST[4 Forecasting Metrics: EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI]
    FORECAST --> DEC[Management Decision: Continue, Rebaseline, or Escalate]
    BASE --> INTERP[Status Interpretation: Schedule and Cost Position]
    INTERP --> DEC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The hierarchy shows the logical flow of EVM analysis — start with 3 raw metrics, derive 4 performance measures, then use 4 forecasting metrics to drive the management decision. This is a very common KTU 14-mark question structure.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Project Control. List any four activities involved in the project control process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Project Control is the process of monitoring project performance against the approved baseline (scope, schedule, cost, quality), analyzing variances, and taking corrective actions to ensure the project meets its objectives.

**Four Activities (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Monitoring** — collecting actual performance data (cost, schedule, scope).
2. **Measuring** — comparing actuals against the planned baseline.
3. **Analyzing** — identifying the cause and magnitude of variances (using EVM).
4. **Correcting** — taking corrective/preventive action, updating the plan, or escalating to the CCB.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between *Cost Variance (CV)* and *Schedule Variance (SV)*. State the formula and the interpretation rule for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cost Variance (CV) | Schedule Variance (SV) |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $CV = EV - AC$ | $SV = EV - PV$ |
| **Unit** | Rupees (₹) | Rupees (₹) |
| **Positive means** | Under budget (good) | Ahead of schedule (good) |
| **Negative means** | Over budget (bad) | Behind schedule (bad) |
| **Index version** | $CPI = EV/AC$ | $SPI = EV/PV$ |

> **Final line for full marks:** *CV* measures **cost efficiency**, while *SV* measures **schedule efficiency** — both expressed in monetary terms using the EV technique.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU Standard):** *Answer any one full question. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]****

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the **Planning and Control Cycle** in detail. List and briefly describe its phases.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A software project has a Budget at Completion (BAC) of **₹ 8,00,000** and a planned duration of **10 months**. At the end of **month 5**, the following data is observed:
- Planned Value (PV) = ₹ 4,00,000
- Earned Value (EV) = ₹ 3,60,000
- Actual Cost (AC) = ₹ 4,20,000

Calculate the following and interpret the project status:
1. Schedule Variance (SV) and Schedule Performance Index (SPI)
2. Cost Variance (CV) and Cost Performance Index (CPI)
3. Estimate at Completion (EAC) and Variance at Completion (VAC)

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

The **Planning and Control Cycle** is a continuous, closed-loop process used by project managers to keep projects on track. It has **6 phases**:

1. **Plan (1.0 mark):** Establish the project baseline — scope, schedule, cost, and quality. This becomes the yardstick for measurement.
2. **Execute (1.0 mark):** Carry out the work as per the plan, using resources, executing tasks, and producing deliverables.
3. **Measure / Data Collection (1.0 mark):** Gather actual performance data — hours worked, money spent, deliverables produced, defects logged.
4. **Evaluate / Compare (1.0 mark):** Compare actuals against the baseline. Compute PV, EV, AC, and the variances (SV, CV) and indices (SPI, CPI).
5. **Take Corrective Action (1.5 marks):** If variances exceed thresholds, take corrective/preventive action. This may include crashing, fast-tracking, rebaselining, or escalating to the CCB.
6. **Feed Forward / Forecast (1.5 marks):** Update the forecast for the remaining work (EAC, ETC, VAC) and communicate the new projection to stakeholders.

> **Closing statement (for full marks):** *The cycle is iterative and is repeated at every status checkpoint (typically weekly or bi-weekly) until project closure.*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $BAC = 8{,}00{,}000$; $PV = 4{,}00{,}000$; $EV = 3{,}60{,}000$; $AC = 4{,}20{,}000$

**(1) Schedule Variance and SPI (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV = 3{,}60{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000 = -40{,}000 \\
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{3{,}60{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $SV < 0$ and $SPI = 0.90 < 1$ → Project is **behind schedule** (only 90 % of planned work completed).

**(2) Cost Variance and CPI (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC = 3{,}60{,}000 - 4{,}20{,}000 = -60{,}000 \\
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{3{,}60{,}000}{4{,}20{,}000} = 0.857
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $CV < 0$ and $CPI \approx 0.86 < 1$ → Project is **over budget** (₹ 0.86 of value per ₹ 1 spent).

**(3) EAC and VAC (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{0.857} = 9{,}33{,}488 \\
VAC &= BAC - EAC = 8{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}33{,}488 = -1{,}33{,}488
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Interpretation:** The project is **forecasted to overshoot its budget by ₹ 1,33,488 (≈ 16.7 %)** if current performance continues. **Recommended Action:** Rebaseline or scope reduction; escalate to the CCB.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]****

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the concept of **Earned Value Management (EVM)**. List and define the **three base metrics** and **four derived metrics** used in EVM.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain the **Integrated Change Control** process in detail. What is the role of the **Change Control Board (CCB)**?

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Earned Value Management (EVM) — Concept (2 marks):**

EVM is a quantitative project control technique that integrates **scope, schedule, and cost** into a unified performance measurement system. It replaces traditional "actual cost vs budget" comparisons with a **three-dimensional view**: planned value, earned value, and actual cost — measured at the same point in time.

**Three Base Metrics (2.5 marks — ~0.83 each):**
- **PV (Planned Value / BCWS):** The budgeted cost of work *scheduled* to be done by a given date.
- **EV (Earned Value / BCWP):** The budgeted cost of work *actually performed* by a given date.
- **AC (Actual Cost / ACWP):** The actual money spent to complete the work performed by a given date.

**Four Derived Metrics (2.5 marks — ~0.625 each):**
- **SV = EV − PV** (Schedule Variance; positive = ahead)
- **CV = EV − AC** (Cost Variance; positive = under budget)
- **SPI = EV / PV** (Schedule Performance Index; > 1 = ahead)
- **CPI = EV / AC** (Cost Performance Index; > 1 = efficient)

> **Conclusion (for full marks):** *EVM enables forecasting (EAC, ETC, VAC) and provides early warning of cost/schedule deviations — a critical tool for any project manager handling projects above ₹ 50 Lakhs.*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Integrated Change Control — Definition (1.5 marks):**

Integrated Change Control is the process of reviewing **all** change requests, approving/rejecting changes, and managing changes to the deliverables, project management plan, baselines, and other project documents. It runs **throughout the entire project life cycle**.

**Why it is needed (1 mark):** Prevents uncontrolled scope creep, ensures impact analysis, maintains stakeholder alignment.

**Process Steps (3 marks — ~0.5 each):**
1. **Identify** the change via a formal Change Request.
2. **Evaluate** the impact on scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk.
3. **Review** by the Change Control Board (CCB).
4. **Decide** — Approve, Reject, or Defer.
5. **Update** the project management plan/baseline.
6. **Communicate** decision to all stakeholders.
7. **Implement** the change and update the change log.

**Role of the Change Control Board (1.5 marks):**
- The CCB is a **formal group** (typically PM + sponsor + key stakeholders + SMEs) responsible for **reviewing, evaluating, and deciding** on change requests.
- It has the **authority** to approve or reject changes that affect the project baseline.
- It ensures that **only justified, value-adding changes** are incorporated — protecting the project from scope creep.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on Project Control Questions (KTU 2024):**
> 1. **Mixing up the formulas:** Writing $CV = EV - PV$ instead of $CV = EV - AC$ costs **1 full mark** in a 7-mark sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting to interpret the values:** KTU expects both the *numerical answer* AND a *plain-English interpretation* (e.g., "$SPI = 0.9$ means **behind schedule**"). Skipping interpretation = −1 mark.
> 3. **Using the wrong EAC formula:** Three different EAC formulas exist (CPI-based, budgeted-remaining, new-ETC). Always read the question carefully to identify the assumption.
> 4. **Confusing PV with EV:** $PV$ = what was *planned*; $EV$ = what was *earned* (completed). Many students substitute these incorrectly.
> 5. **Skipping the CCB explanation:** When asked about Change Control, students often describe the process but **forget the CCB's role** — this costs 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Unit errors:** Writing "$CPI = 0.86$" without a clear unit (it's a **ratio**, dimensionless) is acceptable; but writing "$SV = -40{,}000$ rupees worth of work" is the safer, KTU-preferred phrasing.
> 7. **No final recommendation:** EVM questions almost always end with "interpret and recommend" — a one-line *corrective action* closes the answer for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Project Control (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Project Control =** Monitoring + Comparing + Analyzing + Correcting (closed-loop).
- ✅ **Six phases of the Planning & Control Cycle:** Plan → Execute → Measure → Evaluate → Correct → Forecast.
- ✅ **Three EVM base metrics:** **PV, EV, AC** — measured at the same point in time.
- ✅ **Four EVM derived metrics:** **SV, CV, SPI, CPI** — SV/CV in ₹, SPI/CPI as ratios.
- ✅ **Four forecasting metrics:** **EAC, ETC, VAC, TCPI** — used to predict the final cost.
- ✅ **Interpretation rules:** $SPI > 1$ = ahead; $SPI < 1$ = behind; $CPI > 1$ = efficient; $CPI < 1$ = inefficient.
- ✅ **EAC (default) = BAC / CPI** — assumes current performance continues.
- ✅ **TCPI > 1** → original BAC is unachievable; consider rebaseline.
- ✅ **Change Control Board (CCB)** = formal body that approves/rejects scope changes.
- ✅ **Rebaselining** ≠ retroactive fixing of CPI/SPI — it only resets the *future* baseline.
- ✅ **Types of project reports:** Status, Progress, Exception, Final — each serves a different audience.
- ✅ **Scope creep** is the #1 enemy of IT projects — change control is the antidote.
- ✅ **Earned Value** integrates scope + schedule + cost in *one unified number* (EV) — the most powerful project control metric.
- ✅ **EVM works only with a valid baseline** — if the original plan was unrealistic, EVM will report "overrun" but the *real* issue is bad planning, not bad execution.
- ✅ **Rule of thumb:** A project with $CPI < 0.85$ for **two consecutive months** should be escalated to senior management.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *PV tells you the plan. EV tells you the truth. AC tells you the cost. The variance tells you the story.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Project Audit and Project Termination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Project Audit and Project Termination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Project Audit — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Audit (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Project Audit is a formal, systematic, and independent examination of a project — its deliverables, processes, performance, financial records, and management practices — conducted at defined checkpoints or at project closure, with the objective of verifying compliance, assessing performance against the project management plan, identifying lessons learned, and providing assurance to stakeholders.*

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned Outcome-Based Education framework, project audits are mapped to **Course Outcomes (CO)** such as *CO2: Apply project planning, scheduling, and control techniques* and *CO3: Evaluate project performance using auditing and termination frameworks.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Health Check-Up" Model

Think of a project like a **long road trip in a bus carrying 50 passengers**:

- **The Project Manager** is the driver.
- **The Project Team** is the engine and passengers.
- **The Project Audit** is a **mandatory highway check-post inspection** where an independent mechanic (auditor) climbs in, checks the brakes, fuel consumption, route adherence, ticket collection, and whether the destination is still reachable on time.

If the bus is breaking down, the auditor flags it. If the bus is fine, the auditor signs off and recommends it continue. The mechanic doesn't *drive* the bus — they just *inspect* it. This independence is the **cornerstone of audit credibility**.

### 1.3 Standard Categories & Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Audit Dimensions:**
> Every project audit in the IT/Software domain must evaluate the **Triple Constraint Triangle**: **Scope**, **Time**, and **Cost**, along with two additional modern dimensions — **Quality** and **Risk**. These five axes form the **Pentagon of Audit Evaluation (PAE)** and constitute the standard framework for any KTU-level audit analysis question.

The standard performance metrics used in audits (industry standard, KTU-accepted):

- **SPI (Schedule Performance Index)** = Earned Value (EV) / Planned Value (PV)
- **CPI (Cost Performance Index)** = Earned Value (EV) / Actual Cost (AC)
- **CV (Cost Variance)** = EV − AC
- **SV (Schedule Variance)** = EV − PV

> [!NOTE]
> These four metrics (SPI, CPI, CV, SV) are the **most frequently tested numerical questions** in KTU ESE for this module. Memorize their decision rules: SPI ≥ 1.0 and CPI ≥ 1.0 indicate a healthy project; values below 1.0 signal trouble.

### 1.4 Project Termination — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Project Termination (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Project Termination is the formal, controlled, and documented process of ending a project — or a project phase — which includes validating all deliverables, completing administrative closure, settling contracts, releasing resources, archiving documentation, and capturing lessons learned for organizational learning.*

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy — The "End of a Movie Production"

When a **movie production** ends, the director doesn't just walk off the set. The crew:
1. Wraps up the final shoot.
2. Hands over raw footage, costumes, and props.
3. Settles payments with actors, vendors, and the studio.
4. Files tax returns and contracts.
5. Holds a **wrap party** (post-project review) where everyone shares what worked and what didn't.

This entire "wrap-up" is **Project Termination**. The movie is the *deliverable*; the wrap-up is the *process*. Both are essential.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** A project is NOT considered *complete* in KTU/PMBOK terminology until **formal termination is signed off**. Premature stopping without closure is treated as **project failure** or **premature termination**, not termination.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Project Audits

Project audits are classified along **three independent dimensions**, and KTU questions frequently ask students to compare any two.

### Dimension 1: By Timing

| Audit Type | When Conducted | Purpose | KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-Project Audit** | Before project initiation | Validates feasibility, charter, business case | Low (1–3 marks) |
| **Mid-Project (In-Process) Audit** | During execution phase | Reviews progress, identifies deviations, recommends corrections | High (7–14 marks) |
| **Post-Project Audit** | After project closure | Captures lessons learned, evaluates success criteria | High (7–14 marks) |

### Dimension 2: By Source / Independence

| Type | Auditor Source | Objectivity | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal Audit** | Employee of the same organization | Moderate — may have bias | **Low** |
| **External Audit** | Hired from outside consulting firm | **High** — fully independent | **High** |
| **Third-Party / Regulatory Audit** | Government or standards body (e.g., ISO, CMMi) | Highest — legally mandated | Highest |

### Dimension 3: By Subject Matter

- **Financial Audit** — Verifies budgetary compliance, cost controls, ROI.
- **Compliance Audit** — Checks adherence to laws, contracts, organizational policies.
- **Performance / Management Audit** — Assesses efficiency, effectiveness of PM practices.
- **Technical / Quality Audit** — Validates technical deliverables, code quality, testing coverage.
- **Risk Audit** — Reviews risk register, mitigation effectiveness, contingency usage.

## 2.2 The Five Phases of a Project Audit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Framework:** Every KTU answer on the audit process MUST follow these five logical phases in sequence. Examiners award marks for *sequencing* and *terminology*.

1. **Phase 1 — Audit Initiation & Scoping**
   - Define audit objectives.
   - Identify scope, boundaries, stakeholders.
   - Select auditors (internal/external).
   - Establish the **Audit Charter**.

2. **Phase 2 — Audit Planning**
   - Develop the **Audit Plan** with checklists.
   - Identify data sources: project plan, WBS, schedules, budgets, risk register.
   - Define evaluation criteria and KPIs (SPI, CPI, CV, SV).

3. **Phase 3 — Data Collection & Evidence Gathering**
   - Interview project team, stakeholders, sponsors.
   - Review documents (charter, plans, status reports, logs).
   - Inspect deliverables, test artifacts.
   - Use techniques like **sampling, observation, walkthroughs**.

4. **Phase 4 — Analysis & Evaluation**
   - Compare actual vs. planned performance.
   - Calculate variances (CV, SV).
   - Identify root causes using techniques like **Pareto Analysis, Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram, 5 Whys**.

5. **Phase 5 — Reporting & Follow-Up**
   - Draft the **Audit Report** with findings, conclusions, recommendations.
   - Distribute to stakeholders.
   - Track implementation of corrective actions.

## 2.3 The Audit Report — Standard Structure

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked:** *"List the contents of a project audit report."* (3–4 marks)

A professionally structured audit report contains:

1. **Executive Summary** — One-page overview for top management.
2. **Audit Objectives & Scope** — What was audited and why.
3. **Methodology** — How the audit was conducted.
4. **Findings** — Factual observations (positive and negative).
5. **Analysis** — Root cause analysis and impact assessment.
6. **Recommendations** — Specific, actionable corrective actions.
7. **Conclusions** — Overall opinion (Unqualified, Qualified, Adverse, Disclaimer).
8. **Appendices** — Supporting data, checklists, raw interview notes.

## 2.4 Project Termination — The Four Types

This is the **single most tested sub-topic** under Project Termination in KTU papers.

### Type 1: Extinction (Natural Death)
- The project **completes all deliverables** as planned.
- Resources are released, contracts closed, archives completed.
- **Most desirable** form of termination.
- *Example:* A new HR software is built, deployed, accepted by the client.

### Type 2: Addition (Merger into Operations)
- The project deliverables are **handed over to an existing operations team** within the organization.
- The project team dissolves, but the *product* continues under operations.
- *Example:* A custom-built billing system is merged into the IT operations department.

### Type 3: Integration (Merger into another Project)
- The project's deliverables or resources are **absorbed by another ongoing project**.
- The project effectively becomes a sub-component of a larger effort.
- *Example:* A security module developed in Project A is integrated into the larger Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) project.

### Type 4: Starvation (Slow Death)
- The project **loses funding or resources gradually** and dies a slow death.
- Often caused by **budget cuts, leadership change, or strategic pivot**.
- Considered a *failed* form of termination.
- *Example:* A blockchain R&D project loses funding quarter after quarter until it is silently abandoned.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **Starvation with Extinction**. The key distinction is:
> - **Extinction** = planned, completed, success.
> - **Starvation** = unplanned, incomplete, failure due to resource withdrawal.

## 2.5 Reasons for Early (Premature) Termination

| Category | Specific Reasons |
|---|---|
| **Technical** | Infeasible technology, performance targets unmet, obsolete by completion |
| **Financial** | Budget overrun beyond acceptable threshold, ROI negative |
| **Strategic** | Business case invalidated, market shift, change in leadership |
| **Organizational** | Loss of sponsor, team attrition, political conflict |
| **External** | Regulatory change, competitor launches better product, pandemic disruption |

## 2.6 The Termination Process — Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Process Question:** *"Explain the steps in project termination."* — Use this 6-step sequence.

1. **Step 1 — Termination Decision** — Triggered by completion, success, or failure.
2. **Step 2 — Deliverable Validation** — Verify all outputs meet acceptance criteria.
3. **Step 3 — Administrative Closure** — Document final status, archive records.
4. **Step 4 — Contract Closure** — Settle all vendor/contractor obligations.
5. **Step 5 — Resource Release** — Reassign or release team members, equipment.
6. **Step 6 — Post-Project Review & Lessons Learned** — Conduct retrospective, feed into Knowledge Base.

## 2.7 Admin Closure vs Contract Closure

| Aspect | Administrative Closure | Contract Closure |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Entire project (internal + external) | Specific contracts only |
| **Focus** | Documentation, archives, lessons | Legal, financial, obligations |
| **Owner** | Project Manager | Procurement / Legal / Contracts Manager |
| **Output** | Final project report, lessons learned doc | Contract closure letter, final payments |
| **KTU Weight** | High | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point for KTU:** Every project undergoes **Admin Closure**, but only projects involving external contracts undergo **Contract Closure**. A purely internal IT project (e.g., internal tool development) skips contract closure.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — Audit Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Healthy Range | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Planned Value (PV)** | Planned work × Planned cost rate | — | Baseline |
| **Earned Value (EV)** | Completed work × Planned cost rate | — | Actual progress in monetary terms |
| **Actual Cost (AC)** | Sum of all incurred costs | — | Real money spent |
| **Cost Variance (CV)** | $CV = EV - AC$ | CV ≥ 0 | CV < 0 → Over Budget |
| **Schedule Variance (SV)** | $SV = EV - PV$ | SV ≥ 0 | SV < 0 → Behind Schedule |
| **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** | $CPI = \frac{EV}{AC}$ | CPI ≥ 1.0 | CPI < 1 → Cost Inefficiency |
| **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** | $SPI = \frac{EV}{PV}$ | SPI ≥ 1.0 | SPI < 1 → Schedule Slippage |
| **Estimate at Completion (EAC)** | $EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI}$ | EAC ≤ BAC | EAC > BAC → Cost Overrun Projected |
| **Variance at Completion (VAC)** | $VAC = BAC - EAC$ | VAC ≥ 0 | VAC < 0 → Budget Deficit Expected |
| **To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)** | $TCPI = \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | TCPI ≤ 1.0 | TCPI > 1 → Future efficiency must improve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"Is the project healthy?"* — Compute **both CPI and SPI**. If CPI ≥ 1 and SPI ≥ 1 → On Track. If CPI < 1 and SPI < 1 → In Trouble. If one is healthy and the other is not → mention both findings, not just one. **Single-metric answers lose marks.**

## 2.9 Real-World Utility

These audit and termination frameworks are used by:

- **IT companies** (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) — for project governance and CMMI audits.
- **Government PSUs** — for mandatory C&AG (Comptroller & Auditor General) audits.
- **Banks & Financial Institutions** — for RBI-mandated project audits.
- **Defense and Aerospace projects** — for stage-gate audits (DRDO, ISRO).

The framework is **industry-portable** and forms the **foundation of Project Governance Offices (PMO)** in mature organizations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Worked Example — Earned Value Audit Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 — Typical ESE Question Type:** *"A project is 60% complete by schedule and 55% complete by cost. The budget at completion (BAC) is ₹20,00,000. Actual cost incurred is ₹12,00,000. Calculate CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, VAC, and interpret the project health."* — **Solve below step by step.**

### Given Data

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BAC} &= \text{Rs. } 20{,}00{,}000 \\
\text{Planned \% Completion (PV factor)} &= 0.60 \\
\text{Actual \% Completion (Earned)} &= 0.55 \\
\text{Actual Cost (AC)} &= \text{Rs. } 12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Calculate Planned Value (PV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV &= \text{BAC} \times \text{Planned \% Completion} \\
PV &= 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 \\
PV &= \text{Rs. } 12{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** PV represents the value of work that was *supposed* to be done by this time. It is based on the schedule, not actual progress.

### Step 2 — Calculate Earned Value (EV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
EV &= \text{BAC} \times \text{Actual \% Completion} \\
EV &= 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.55 \\
EV &= \text{Rs. } 11{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** EV represents the monetary value of work *actually* completed. It is the most important metric because it measures true progress.

### Step 3 — Calculate Cost Variance (CV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC \\
CV &= 11{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 \\
CV &= -\text{Rs. } 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** CV is **negative**, meaning the project is **over budget by ₹1,00,000**.

### Step 4 — Calculate Schedule Variance (SV)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SV &= EV - PV \\
SV &= 11{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000 \\
SV &= -\text{Rs. } 1{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** SV is **negative**, meaning the project is **behind schedule**.

### Step 5 — Calculate Cost Performance Index (CPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPI &= \frac{EV}{AC} \\
CPI &= \frac{11{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \\
CPI &= 0.917
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** CPI < 1.0. The project is getting only **₹0.917 worth of work for every ₹1 spent** — i.e., cost-inefficient.

### Step 6 — Calculate Schedule Performance Index (SPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPI &= \frac{EV}{PV} \\
SPI &= \frac{11{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} \\
SPI &= 0.917
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** SPI < 1.0. The project has achieved only **91.7% of the planned schedule progress** — i.e., schedule slippage.

### Step 7 — Calculate Estimate at Completion (EAC)

Assuming the current cost performance continues:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAC &= \frac{BAC}{CPI} \\
EAC &= \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{0.917} \\
EAC &= \text{Rs. } 21{,}81{,}027 \text{ (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** If the current trend continues, the project will **overshoot the budget by approximately ₹1,81,027**.

### Step 8 — Calculate Variance at Completion (VAC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
VAC &= BAC - EAC \\
VAC &= 20{,}00{,}000 - 21{,}81{,}027 \\
VAC &= -\text{Rs. } 1{,}81{,}027
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The project is expected to end **₹1,81,027 over budget** if corrective action is not taken.

### Step 9 — To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TCPI &= \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} \\
TCPI &= \frac{20{,}00{,}000 - 11{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000 - 12{,}00{,}000} \\
TCPI &= \frac{9{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \\
TCPI &= 1.125
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The remaining work (₹9,00,000) must be completed with only ₹8,00,000 remaining budget. The team must achieve **₹1.125 of work for every ₹1 spent from now on** to meet the original BAC. This is a **tough target** (> 1), signaling budget recovery is difficult.

### Final Audit Verdict

| Metric | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| CV | −₹1,00,000 | Over Budget |
| SV | −₹1,00,000 | Behind Schedule |
| CPI | 0.917 | Cost-Inefficient |
| SPI | 0.917 | Schedule Slippage |
| EAC | ₹21,81,027 | Will exceed BAC |
| VAC | −₹1,81,027 | Budget overrun projected |
| TCPI | 1.125 | Recovery requires > 100% efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Audit Recommendation:** Based on the calculation, the project is in **DOUBLE TROUBLE** (both schedule and cost overrun). The audit should recommend one of three actions: **(a) Crash/Fast-Track schedule with additional resources, (b) Renegotiate scope with sponsor, (c) Initiate controlled project termination if recovery is not feasible.** This triad of options is the **standard KTU expected answer** for double-trouble projects.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Audit vs. Termination Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Tool:** Use this decision matrix in 14-mark analytical questions.

| Condition | Recommendation | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| SPI ≥ 1, CPI ≥ 1, all deliverables done | **Extinction (Natural)** | Healthy project, complete |
| SPI ≥ 1, CPI ≥ 1, ongoing operations needed | **Addition (Merge to Ops)** | Project success, continue as BAU |
| SPI < 1, CPI < 1, salvageable | **Recovery (Fast-Track/Crash)** | Double trouble but not fatal |
| SPI < 1, CPI < 1, low ROI on remaining work | **Premature Termination** | Sunk cost, abandon now |
| Funding cut externally | **Starvation** (avoidable) | Strategic failure |
| Acquired by another project | **Integration** | Strategic realignment |
| Tech obsolete, mid-development | **Extinction (Forced)** | Strategic failure |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Audit Report Findings (Conceptual)

> **Scenario:** A KTU hypothetical question: *"An IT project is in its 6th month of a 12-month plan. The project manager reports: 'We are 40% complete, but we have spent 55% of the budget. There are 3 major bugs in production and 2 key developers resigned this month.' Identify audit findings and recommend actions."*

### Audit Findings (with Severity Tags):

1. **F1 — Cost Overrun [HIGH]:** SPI = 0.80, CPI = 0.73 → Severe cost inefficiency.
2. **F2 — Schedule Slippage [HIGH]:** Only 40% done in half the time → 20% behind schedule.
3. **F3 — Quality Defect [MEDIUM]:** 3 major bugs in production → testing process gap.
4. **F4 — Resource Attrition [HIGH]:** 2 of N key developers lost → knowledge loss risk.
5. **F5 — Risk Management Gap [MEDIUM]:** Attrition was not in risk register.

### Recommended Actions:

1. Conduct a **scope-baseline review** with sponsor.
2. **Reallocate budget** — request 15% contingency.
3. **Hire replacements** within 30 days; initiate knowledge transfer.
4. **Add regression testing** to the QA cycle.
5. **Update the risk register** and escalate to PMO.
6. If recovery is unviable → **recommend controlled termination** to the steering committee.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Insight:** A 14-mark answer must include **at least 3 findings, 3 recommendations, AND a final decision** (continue vs. terminate). Answers that stop at findings lose up to 4 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Project Audit Process (5-Phase Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase 1: Audit Initiation and Scoping] --> B[Phase 2: Audit Planning and Checklist Design]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Data Collection and Evidence Gathering]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Analysis and Evaluation SPI CPI CV SV]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Reporting and Follow-Up Corrective Actions]
    E --> F{Project Status}
    F -->|Healthy| G[Continue Execution]
    F -->|At Risk| H[Recovery Action Plan]
    F -->|Critical| I[Recommend Termination]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the **canonical KTU flow** for any audit-process question (typically 7 marks). The diamond at node F represents the **decision point** where the auditor's recommendation is finalized.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Project Termination Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Project in Distress or Complete] --> Q1{Deliverables Accepted?}
    Q1 -->|Yes All| Q2{Operations Continue?}
    Q1 -->|No or Partial| Q3{Recovery Feasible?}
    Q1 -->|No Critical| TermFail[Forced Termination]

    Q2 -->|Yes Internal Ops| T1[Addition Type]
    Q2 -->|No Standalone Done| T2[Extinction Type]

    Q3 -->|Yes Funds Available| T3[Recovery via Crash or Fast-Track]
    Q3 -->|No Resources Gone| TermFail
    Q3 -->|Strategic Pivot| T4[Starvation Type]

    Start --> Q4{Absorbed by Another Project?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| T5[Integration Type]
    Q4 -->|No| Start
```

> **Reading Guide:** This decision tree maps the **four classical termination types** (Extinction, Addition, Integration, Starvation) to project conditions. KTU often asks: *"Given a scenario, which type of termination applies?"* — Use this tree to justify your answer.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Administrative vs Contract Closure (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AdministrativeClosure[Administrative Closure Internal Focus]
        A1[Final Project Report] --> A2[Archive Documents]
        A2 --> A3[Lessons Learned]
        A3 --> A4[Knowledge Base Update]
    end

    subgraph ContractClosure[Contract Closure External Focus]
        C1[Verify Contract Terms] --> C2[Settle Payments]
        C2 --> C3[Sign Closure Letter]
        C3 --> C4[Legal Compliance]
    end

    ProjectComplete[Project Completion Trigger] --> AdministrativeClosure
    ProjectComplete --> ContractClosure
    AdministrativeClosure --> Final[Formal Sign-off]
    ContractClosure --> Final
```

> **Reading Guide:** This diagram visualizes the **dual-track closure process**. Note that Administrative Closure is **mandatory for every project**, while Contract Closure runs in parallel **only if external contracts exist**.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Audit Report Flow (Input → Output)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1[Project Charter] --> AuditProcess[Audit Process]
    In2[Project Management Plan] --> AuditProcess
    In3[Status Reports] --> AuditProcess
    In4[Risk Register] --> AuditProcess
    In5[Financial Records] --> AuditProcess

    AuditProcess --> Out1[Executive Summary]
    AuditProcess --> Out2[Findings Positive and Negative]
    AuditProcess --> Out3[Root Cause Analysis]
    AuditProcess --> Out4[Recommendations]
    AuditProcess --> Out5[Conclusions]

    Out1 --> FinalReport[Final Audit Report]
    Out2 --> FinalReport
    Out3 --> FinalReport
    Out4 --> FinalReport
    Out5 --> FinalReport

    FinalReport --> Stakeholders[Sponsor PMO Steering Committee]
    FinalReport --> FollowUp[Corrective Action Tracking]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The five **Inputs** map to standard PMBOK artifacts. The five **Outputs** map to the **Audit Report Structure** defined in Section 2.3. This is the **complete input-output model** suitable for a 7-mark KTU question.

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Audit Classification Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AuditTypes[Project Audit Types] --> D1[By Timing]
    AuditTypes --> D2[By Source]
    AuditTypes --> D3[By Subject Matter]

    D1 --> T1[Pre-Project Audit]
    D1 --> T2[Mid-Project Audit]
    D1 --> T3[Post-Project Audit]

    D2 --> S1[Internal Audit]
    D2 --> S2[External Audit]
    D2 --> S3[Regulatory Audit]

    D3 --> M1[Financial Audit]
    D3 --> M2[Compliance Audit]
    D3 --> M3[Performance Audit]
    D3 --> M4[Technical Audit]
    D3 --> M5[Risk Audit]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the **three-dimensional classification of audits** — frequently asked in 3-mark definition questions. Use this exact tree structure in your KTU answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Project Audit. List any four types of project audits. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Project Audit** is a formal, systematic, and independent examination of a project to verify compliance, assess performance, and identify lessons learned, conducted by qualified auditors at predefined checkpoints.

**Four types of project audits:**

1. **Financial Audit** — verifies budget compliance and cost control.
2. **Compliance Audit** — checks adherence to laws, contracts, and policies.
3. **Performance / Management Audit** — evaluates efficiency of PM processes.
4. **Technical / Quality Audit** — validates technical deliverables and quality standards.

*(2 marks for definition; 1 mark for correctly listing four types.)*

---

### Question 2: What is project starvation? How does it differ from extinction? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Project Starvation** is a form of premature, unplanned project termination that occurs when a project loses its funding, resources, or executive support gradually, eventually causing it to die a slow death. It is a **failure-mode** of termination.

**Difference from Extinction:**

| Aspect | Starvation | Extinction |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Unplanned failure | Planned, natural end |
| Cause | Resource/funding withdrawal | Successful completion |
| Status | Incomplete deliverables | All deliverables accepted |
| Outcome | Considered a failure | Considered a success |

*(1.5 marks for definition; 1.5 marks for clean differentiation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q.A (a)** Explain the five phases of a project audit in detail. *(7 marks)*

**Q.A (b)** Discuss the four types of project termination with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer for Q.A (a): Five Phases of a Project Audit

**Phase 1 — Audit Initiation and Scoping (1.5 marks):**
The audit begins with a clear definition of objectives — what is being audited and why. The auditor identifies the audit's scope, boundaries, and key stakeholders. A formal **Audit Charter** is created, which authorizes the audit and grants the auditor access to project artifacts. For example, in an IT software project, the scope may include the design phase, testing phase, and budget compliance, but exclude post-deployment operations.

**Phase 2 — Audit Planning (1.5 marks):**
The auditor develops a detailed **Audit Plan** containing checklists, timelines, and resource requirements. Data sources are identified: the project charter, WBS, Gantt chart, risk register, and financial ledgers. KPIs are defined — primarily SPI, CPI, CV, and SV. The plan is approved by the audit sponsor.

**Phase 3 — Data Collection and Evidence Gathering (1.5 marks):**
Evidence is collected through **document reviews, interviews, observations, and walkthroughs**. Sampling techniques are used for large datasets. The auditor interviews the project manager, team members, sponsor, and key vendors. Inspect actual deliverables (e.g., source code, test reports, deployment logs) to verify stated progress.

**Phase 4 — Analysis and Evaluation (1.5 marks):**
The auditor compares actual performance against the baseline. Earned Value metrics are computed. Root cause analysis is performed for any deviation using tools such as **Pareto Analysis, Fishbone Diagram, and 5-Whys**. The auditor identifies systemic issues, not just symptoms.

**Phase 5 — Reporting and Follow-Up (1 mark):**
A formal **Audit Report** is drafted, reviewed, and distributed to the sponsor, PMO, and steering committee. The report contains findings, root causes, and actionable recommendations. A follow-up schedule is established to track the implementation of corrective actions. The audit cycle is complete only when corrective actions are verified.

---

#### Model Answer for Q.A (b): Four Types of Project Termination

**Type 1: Extinction (2 marks):**
Extinction is the **natural, planned, and successful** end of a project. All deliverables have been accepted by the customer, contracts are closed, and resources are formally released. *Example:* A custom e-commerce website developed for a client is delivered, accepted, and the project team is disbanded.

**Type 2: Addition (1.5 marks):**
Addition occurs when the project's deliverables are **transferred to an existing operational unit** within the organization for ongoing maintenance and support. The project team is dissolved, but the product lives on. *Example:* A new HR management system is developed by a project team and then handed over to the IT Operations department, which takes over its maintenance.

**Type 3: Integration (1.5 marks):**
Integration happens when a project's outputs or resources are **absorbed into another ongoing project**. The project essentially becomes a sub-project of a larger initiative. *Example:* A mobile app security module developed in Project Alpha is integrated into the larger Enterprise Mobility project, and the security team joins that project's resources.

**Type 4: Starvation (2 marks):**
Starvation is the **unplanned, slow death of a project due to gradual resource or funding withdrawal**. It is considered a failure mode of termination. *Example:* A research project on quantum cryptography loses funding in three consecutive budget cycles and is eventually abandoned without formal closure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Table (optional for full marks):**

| Type | Planned? | Success? | Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extinction | Yes | Yes | Natural completion |
| Addition | Yes | Yes | Operational handover |
| Integration | Yes | Yes/Mixed | Project merger |
| Starvation | No | No | Resource withdrawal |

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q.B (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure and contents of a project audit report. *(7 marks)*

**Q.B (b)** An IT project has BAC = ₹50,00,000. At the 50% time mark, the project is 40% complete and has spent ₹25,00,000. Calculate CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC, VAC, and TCPI. Interpret the project health and recommend an action. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer for Q.B (a): Project Audit Report

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Inputs                  →  Audit Process  →  Report Sections
─────────                ───────────────     ───────────────
Project Charter                            • Executive Summary
Project Management Plan                    • Objectives and Scope
Status Reports              →              • Methodology
Risk Register                              • Findings
Financial Records                          • Analysis (Root Cause)
                                           • Recommendations
                                           • Conclusions
                                           • Appendices
```

**Explanation of Each Section (5 marks):**

1. **Executive Summary** — A concise, one-page overview for top management. Highlights key findings and the auditor's overall opinion.

2. **Audit Objectives and Scope** — States why the audit was conducted, what was covered, and what was explicitly excluded (e.g., "scope covers design and development phases only").

3. **Methodology** — Describes the techniques used: document review, interviews, sampling, observation, walkthroughs. Establishes audit credibility.

4. **Findings** — Factual observations categorized as **positive** (best practices) and **negative** (deficiencies, non-compliance, variances). Each finding should reference evidence.

5. **Analysis** — Root cause analysis using techniques like Fishbone or 5-Whys. Identifies whether issues are systemic or one-off.

6. **Recommendations** — Specific, actionable, time-bound corrective actions. Each recommendation should have an owner and a deadline.

7. **Conclusions** — The auditor's formal opinion:
   - **Unqualified** (clean — all good)
   - **Qualified** (minor issues)
   - **Adverse** (major issues)
   - **Disclaimer** (insufficient evidence)

8. **Appendices** — Supporting data: raw interview notes, detailed calculations, sample checklists, photographs/screenshots.

---

#### Model Answer for Q.B (b): Earned Value Calculations

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BAC &= \text{Rs. } 50{,}00{,}000 \\
PV \text{ factor} &= 0.50 \quad \text{(planned 50% at this time)} \\
EV \text{ factor} &= 0.40 \quad \text{(actually 40% complete)} \\
AC &= \text{Rs. } 25{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Planned Value:**

$$
PV = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = \text{Rs. } 25{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Earned Value:**

$$
EV = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = \text{Rs. } 20{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Cost Variance:**

$$
CV = EV - AC = 20{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = -\text{Rs. } 5{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Schedule Variance:**

$$
SV = EV - PV = 20{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000 = -\text{Rs. } 5{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — CPI:**

$$
CPI = \frac{EV}{AC} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 0.80
$$

**Step 6 — SPI:**

$$
SPI = \frac{EV}{PV} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 0.80
$$

**Step 7 — EAC:**

$$
EAC = \frac{BAC}{CPI} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{0.80} = \text{Rs. } 62{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 8 — VAC:**

$$
VAC = BAC - EAC = 50{,}00{,}000 - 62{,}50{,}000 = -\text{Rs. } 12{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 9 — TCPI:**

$$
TCPI = \frac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000 - 25{,}00{,}000} = \frac{30{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} = 1.20
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Metric | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| CV | −₹5,00,000 | Over Budget |
| SV | −₹5,00,000 | Behind Schedule |
| CPI | 0.80 | Cost-Inefficient |
| SPI | 0.80 | Schedule Slippage |
| EAC | ₹62,50,000 | Will exceed BAC by 25% |
| VAC | −₹12,50,000 | Major budget overrun |
| TCPI | 1.20 | Recovery needs 120% efficiency |

**Audit Interpretation and Recommendation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The project is in **DOUBLE TROUBLE** (both SPI and CPI < 1.0). The performance is severely degraded — 20% behind schedule and 20% over budget at the halfway point. If uncorrected, the project will end **25% over budget (₹12,50,000 overrun)** and require **20% better future efficiency** (TCPI = 1.20) to recover.

**Recommended Actions (in order of priority):**

1. **Escalate to the Steering Committee** — Present audit findings formally.
2. **Conduct a Scope-Budget Review** with the sponsor — Renegotiate deliverables.
3. **Fast-Track or Crash the schedule** — Add resources, parallelize tasks.
4. **Implement stricter cost controls** — Freeze non-essential expenditures.
5. **If recovery is unviable** — Recommend **controlled project termination** to the sponsor with formal sunk-cost analysis.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Most common error — Missing the interpretation step.** Students compute CV, SV, CPI, SPI correctly but write *"therefore the project is failing"* without quantifying what that means. Always **state the magnitude** (e.g., "20% behind schedule, 25% over budget").
>
> 2. **Second common error — Wrong EAC formula.** Some students use $EAC = AC + (BAC - EV)$ which is for *independent* estimates. The KTU-expected formula is $EAC = BAC / CPI$ for *current trend* continuation. Use the one that matches the question's assumption.
>
> 3. **Third common error — Omitting TCPI.** TCPI is the "recovery target" and is a **favourite 1–2 mark sub-question**. Always compute it when the question asks about *future* project health.
>
> 4. **Fourth common error — Skipping the recommendation.** A 7-mark numerical question without a recommendation is treated as **incomplete**. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for missing actionable advice.
>
> 5. **Unit omission.** Always write **"₹" or "Rs."** with every monetary value. Bare numbers lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Project Audit and Project Termination**

### Project Audit Essentials:
- ✅ Project audit is a **formal, independent, systematic review** of a project.
- ✅ Classified **three ways**: by timing (pre/mid/post), by source (internal/external/regulatory), and by subject (financial/compliance/performance/technical/risk).
- ✅ Five-phase process: **Initiation → Planning → Data Collection → Analysis → Reporting & Follow-Up**.
- ✅ Audit report contains **8 sections**: Executive Summary, Objectives, Methodology, Findings, Analysis, Recommendations, Conclusions, Appendices.
- ✅ Auditors must be **independent** of the project team for credibility.

### Earned Value Formulas (MUST MEMORIZE):
- ✅ $CV = EV - AC$ — Negative = Over Budget.
- ✅ $SV = EV - PV$ — Negative = Behind Schedule.
- ✅ $CPI = EV / AC$ — Below 1.0 = Inefficient.
- ✅ $SPI = EV / PV$ — Below 1.0 = Behind Schedule.
- ✅ $EAC = BAC / CPI$ — Expected total cost at current trend.
- ✅ $VAC = BAC - EAC$ — Expected final variance.
- ✅ $TCPI = (BAC - EV) / (BAC - AC)$ — Future efficiency target.

### Project Termination Essentials:
- ✅ **Four types**: Extinction, Addition, Integration, Starvation.
- ✅ Extinction = **planned success**; Starvation = **unplanned failure**.
- ✅ Termination process has **6 steps**: Decision → Validation → Admin Closure → Contract Closure → Resource Release → Lessons Learned.
- ✅ **Admin Closure** is mandatory for all projects; **Contract Closure** only for projects with external contracts.
- ✅ Premature termination can be **forced** (extinction type) or **slow** (starvation type).
- ✅ **Post-Project Review** captures lessons learned for organizational knowledge base.

### KTU Examination Strategy:
- ✅ For 3-mark questions — Use **bullet points** and **bold keywords**; do not write essays.
- ✅ For 7-mark sub-parts — Use **headings, sub-points, and at least one example**.
- ✅ For 14-mark questions — Combine **definitions, classifications, process flow, examples, and a comparison table**.
- ✅ Always include **recommendations** in audit questions — examiners reward forward-looking analysis.
- ✅ **Sequencing matters** — Use the canonical 5-phase (audit) and 6-step (termination) frameworks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Agile Project Management: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Agile Project Management: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Agile Project Management (APM)** is an iterative and incremental approach to managing software and IT projects, grounded in the **Agile Manifesto (2001)**. It emphasizes adaptive planning, evolutionary delivery, and continuous improvement through short, time-boxed cycles called **iterations** or **sprints** (typically **2 to 4 weeks**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Agile Project Management is a value-driven framework that delivers project outputs in incremental, frequent, and consumable increments, prioritizing customer collaboration, working software, and responsiveness to change over rigid planning and extensive documentation.

The approach is rooted in the philosophy of **adaptive leadership** and **lean thinking**, where uncertainty is embraced, customer feedback drives delivery, and self-organizing cross-functional teams produce high-quality outcomes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Building a House the Agile Way"**
> 
> Imagine you want to build a house. The **Traditional (Waterfall) Approach** requires you to finalize the entire blueprint, the budget, and every material before laying a single brick. If you change your mind about the kitchen layout after construction has started, it costs a fortune.
> 
> The **Agile Approach** is like building the house in **small livable phases**: first, you build and furnish one room completely (a working "increment"). The family lives in it, gives feedback ("We want more windows in the bedroom!"), and then the next room is built incorporating that feedback. By the end, the entire house is built, but it was shaped by the actual people who live in it.

This analogy captures the essence of Agile: **build a little, test a little, learn a lot, and adapt continuously**.

---

## 1.3 The Agile Manifesto (2001) — The Four Core Values

In February **2001**, **17 software practitioners** met at **Snowbird, Utah**, and authored the **Agile Manifesto**, which formalizes four foundational values:

| # | Agile Value | Counter-Tradition (But Still Has Value) |
|---|------------|----------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Individuals and Interactions** | over processes and tools |
| 2 | **Working Software** | over comprehensive documentation |
| 3 | **Customer Collaboration** | over contract negotiation |
| 4 | **Responding to Change** | over following a plan |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The word **"over"** does **not** mean the right-hand items are unimportant. It means the **left-hand items are valued more highly**. Documentation still exists, plans still exist — but they are subordinate to people, working output, collaboration, and adaptability.

---

## 1.4 The 12 Principles of the Agile Manifesto

These principles operationalize the four values:

1. **Customer satisfaction** through early and continuous delivery of valuable software.
2. **Welcome changing requirements**, even late in development — Agile processes harness change for the customer's competitive advantage.
3. **Deliver working software frequently** (weeks rather than months).
4. **Business people and developers** must work together daily throughout the project.
5. Build projects around **motivated individuals**; give them the environment and support they need, and trust them to get the job done.
6. **Face-to-face conversation** is the most effective method of conveying information.
7. **Working software** is the primary measure of progress.
8. Agile processes promote **sustainable development** — maintain a constant pace indefinitely.
9. Continuous attention to **technical excellence and good design** enhances agility.
10. **Simplicity** — the art of maximizing the amount of work not done — is essential.
11. The best architectures, requirements, and designs emerge from **self-organizing teams**.
12. **Regular reflection** on how to become more effective, then tune and adjust behavior.

---

## 1.5 Core Metrics in Agile

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Agile Metrics (bolded as constants):**
> - **Sprint Length:** **2 weeks** (most common) — bounded time-box for a single iteration.
> - **Velocity:** Amount of work (story points) a team completes in one sprint.
> - **Burndown Chart:** Visual representation of remaining work over time.
> - **Lead Time:** Time from requirement creation to delivery.
> - **Cycle Time:** Time a work item spends in active development.
