### Step 4 — Add Practices

Life-critical Crystal Orange demands:

$$
P_{L40} = P_{D40} \cup \{\text{Formal Reviews, Defect Tracking, Redundancy}\}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define the **Crystal methodology**. Who invented it, and what is its core philosophy?

**Model Answer:**
Crystal is a family of lightweight, agile software development methodologies invented by **Alistair Cockburn**. Its core philosophy is that **projects are inherently uncertain**, and uncertainty is best managed through **effective communication, frequent delivery, and the human skills of the team**. Crystal emphasizes that there is **no one-size-fits-all** process; the methodology must be tailored to the team's size and project criticality.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the difference between **Crystal Clear** and **Crystal Orange** in terms of team size and required deliverables?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Crystal Clear | Crystal Orange |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| Team Size (D-number) | D6 (up to 6 members) | D40 (21–40 members) |
| Number of Deliverables | 4 (Mission, Schedule, Status, Code) | 12 (includes configuration plans, integration testing) |
| Iteration Length | 1–2 months | 2–3 months |
| Sponsorship | Active sponsor | Senior sponsor |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** A software company is developing a **life-critical patient monitoring system** for an ICU. The team consists of **15 developers**, and the project must comply with **FDA medical software regulations**. The project is expected to run for **2 years**.

#### (a) Identify the appropriate **Crystal variant** for this project. Justify your selection based on Cockburn's principles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Team Size Identification**
$$
\text{Team Size} = 15 \text{ developers}
$$
Round up to the nearest D-number:
$$
D = 20
$$

**Step 2 — Criticality Assessment**
The project is **life-critical** (failure could lead to patient death). Therefore:
$$
\text{Criticality} = L \ (\text{Life})
$$

**Step 3 — Combine Parameters**
$$
\text{Crystal Variant} = L20 = \text{Crystal Yellow, Life-Critical}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification**
- **Frequent Delivery:** Every 1–3 months, working software is delivered for clinical trials.
- **Reflective Improvement:** Mandatory after every iteration to improve patient safety features.
- **Osmotic Communication:** Co-located ICU software team ensures informal flow of safety-critical knowledge.
- **Personal Safety:** Developers must feel safe to report defects that could affect patient lives.
- **Easy Access to Expert Users:** Doctors and nurses are part of the feedback loop.
- **Technical Environment:** Automated test suites, static analysis tools, and CI/CD pipelines are essential for FDA compliance.
- **LDC-PC Triangle:** Since life is at stake, the **L (Life)** parameter is maximized, requiring extra rigor.

**Conclusion:** **Crystal Yellow (L20)** is the most appropriate variant for this project.

#### (b) Describe **Cockburn's LDC-PC Project Triangle**. How does it influence methodology selection in Crystal? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Alistair Cockburn proposed that every project must balance **five competing priorities**, collectively known as **LDC-PC**:

| Priority | Full Form | Meaning |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **L** | Life | Expected lifetime of the system |
| **D** | Discretionary | How much stretch/overhead is allowed |
| **C** | Criticality | Impact of system failure |
| **P** | Priority | Urgency of delivery |
| **Com** | Comfort | Team's comfort with the methodology |

**Mathematical Representation:**
$$
L + D + C + P + Com = 100\%
$$

**Influence on Crystal Selection:**
- A project with **high C (Criticality)** requires **more discipline** (more artifacts, formal reviews) → chooses heavier Crystal variants (Orange, Red).
- A project with **high P (Priority)** may require **more frequent deliveries** → affects iteration length.
- A project with **high Com (Comfort)** allows the team to use a **lighter** variant (Crystal Clear).
- **L (Life)** dictates documentation depth and reverse-engineering requirements.

**Example:** For the ICU monitoring system, **L + C dominate**, requiring a heavier variant like Crystal Yellow (L20) with extra practices.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** A **fintech startup** wants to build a mobile banking app using Crystal methodology. The team has **5 developers**, all working from the same office. The project is **non-critical (Comfort level)**, with a 6-month deadline.

#### (a) Identify the appropriate **Crystal variant** and list all **mandatory artifacts** and **practices**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Team Size**
$$
\text{Team Size} = 5 \implies D = 6
$$

**Step 2 — Criticality**
$$
\text{Criticality} = C \ (\text{Comfort})
$$

**Step 3 — Selection**
$$
\text{Crystal Variant} = C6 = \text{Crystal Clear (Comfort, 6 members)}
$$

**Step 4 — Mandatory Artifacts (4 total):**

| # | Artifact | Purpose |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Mission Statement** | Defines project goals |
| 2 | **Schedule (Iteration Plan)** | Lists delivery dates |
| 3 | **Status (Burn-down Chart)** | Tracks progress |
| 4 | **Code (Source + Tests)** | Working software with automated tests |

**Step 5 — Mandatory Practices (7 total):**
1. Frequent Delivery (every 1 month)
2. Reflective Improvement (post-iteration)
3. Osmotic Communication (co-located office)
4. Personal Safety (no-blame culture)
5. Focus (single task per developer)
6. Easy Access to Expert Users (banking analysts on call)
7. Technical Environment (Git, Jenkins, automated unit tests)

#### (b) Explain **Osmotic Communication** and **Personal Safety** with a real-world example. Why are they critical in Crystal? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Osmotic Communication:**
Osmotic communication refers to the **passive flow of information** that occurs when team members are co-located. Like **osmosis in chemistry** (where molecules diffuse across a semi-permeable membrane), in Crystal, **ideas, bugs, and design decisions diffuse** naturally among team members through:
- Overhearing discussions
- Whiteboard conversations
- Casual pair-programming
- Visual task boards

**Real-World Example:** In the fintech startup, when a developer is debugging a payment API and casually mentions a bug, the senior developer overhears and offers an immediate suggestion — **without a formal meeting**. This is osmotic communication.

**Personal Safety:**
Personal safety means that every team member feels **psychologically secure** to:
- Admit mistakes
- Raise concerns about deadlines
- Question decisions
- Suggest innovative ideas

**Real-World Example:** A junior developer notices a **security vulnerability** in the mobile banking app. Because of personal safety, he reports it immediately, even though the project deadline is tight. In a culture without safety, he would have hidden the bug, leading to a potential **data breach**.

**Why Critical in Crystal:**
- Crystal relies on **frequent delivery**; if team members hide defects, deliveries will fail.
- Crystal emphasizes **reflection**; without safety, no one admits mistakes to learn from them.
- Cockburn believed **human skills + communication** are the most important success factors in any project.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Crystal with Extreme Programming (XP) or Scrum.** Crystal is a separate agile methodology. While both are agile, Crystal emphasizes communication, while Scrum emphasizes roles and ceremonies.
> 2. **Always round UP** the team size to the nearest D-number. For example, 7 developers → D20, not D10. This is a frequent error in numerical questions.
> 3. **Forgetting the Criticality Prefix:** A 6-person team for a life-critical project is **L6 (Crystal Clear, Life)**, NOT plain Crystal Clear. The criticality letter comes FIRST.
> 4. **Confusing LDC-PC triangle with priority triangles in other methodologies.** LDC-PC is unique to Crystal/Cockburn.
> 5. **Inadequate explanation of "Osmotic Communication":** Do not just say "informal communication." Explain the **analogy with chemical osmosis** and how information diffuses through team proximity.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Crystal is a family of agile methodologies**, not a single one.
- **Invented by Alistair Cockburn** in the late 1990s.
- **Color codes** correspond to team size:
  - Clear (D6), Yellow (D20), Orange (D40), Red (D80), Maroon (D200).
- **Criticality prefixes:** C (Comfort), D (Discretionary), M (Money), L (Life).
- **Three Primary Principles:** Frequent Delivery, Reflective Improvement, Osmotic Communication.
- **Seven Secondary Properties:** + Personal Safety, Focus, Easy Access to Expert Users, Technical Environment.
- **LDC-PC Triangle:** L(ife), D(iscretionary), C(riticality), P(riority), Com(fort) = 100%.
- **Crystal Clear requires only 4 artifacts**; higher variants require more.
- **Iteration length is typically 1–3 months** depending on team size.
- **Always combine Team Size + Criticality** to get the full Crystal variant name (e.g., L20, C6).
- **Osmotic Communication requires physical co-location** within ~8–10 meters.
- **Personal Safety is non-negotiable** — it is the foundation of honest communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Crystal = Cockburn + Colors + Communication + Criticality."* If you remember this, you will never lose marks on the Crystal topic in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Various Terminologies Used in Scrum (Sprint)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Various Terminologies Used in Scrum (Sprint)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Scrum Framework** (as defined in the *Scrum Guide* by Ken Schwaber and Jeff Sutherland, which is the authoritative source prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus), the **Sprint** is the **heart of Scrum**. A Sprint is a **time-boxed** iteration of **one month or less** during which a "Done", usable, and potentially releasable product Increment is created. Within the Sprint, multiple terminologies are used to describe events, artifacts, and engineering practices that govern the execution of work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Agile & Scrum):**
> A student must be able to define, differentiate, and correctly apply the following Sprint-related terminologies: **Sprint Goal, Sprint Backlog, Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, Definition of Done, Velocity, Story Points, Burndown Chart, Product Increment, Time-box, Capacity,** and **Impediment**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are participating in a **cooking show competition** with a fixed time limit of **30 minutes** (the Sprint). At the start:

- The **judge (Product Owner)** announces: *"Make a 3-course Italian meal"* — this is your **Sprint Goal**.
- You and your teammates pick the recipes (User Stories) from the menu (Product Backlog) and plan who cooks what — this is **Sprint Planning**.
- Every 15 minutes, you huddle and say, *"I am chopping onions, next I will sauté"* — this is the **Daily Scrum**.
- You measure how much work is left on a whiteboard with a downward graph — the **Sprint Burndown Chart**.
- At the end, the judges taste the food. If approved, it becomes a **Product Increment**.
- After the show, you sit down and ask, *"What went well? What didn't?"* — this is the **Sprint Retrospective**.

Just like every term in the cooking show is a **bounded ritual**, every Sprint term in Scrum is a **bounded, intentional ceremony or artifact**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** All Sprint terminologies exist to make the team's progress **visible**, **predictable**, and **inspectable** — three pillars of empirical process control in Scrum.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Term | Standard Value (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|
| Sprint Duration | **2 to 4 weeks** (recommended, never exceeds **1 month**) |
| Daily Scrum Duration | **15 minutes** (hard time-box) |
| Sprint Planning Duration | **Maximum 8 hours** (for a 1-month Sprint) |
| Sprint Review Duration | **Maximum 4 hours** (for a 1-month Sprint) |
| Sprint Retrospective Duration | **Maximum 3 hours** (for a 1-month Sprint) |
| Recommended Team Size | **10 or fewer people** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Breakdown of Sprint Terminologies

### A. Sprint Events (The Five Formal Events)

1. **The Sprint Itself**
   - A container for all other four events.
   - Has a **fixed duration (time-box)** that does not change.
   - Ends with a usable product **Increment**.

2. **Sprint Planning**
   - **Why:** To define what can be delivered in the Sprint and how that work will be achieved.
   - **How:** The whole Scrum Team collaborates. The Product Owner discusses the **Sprint Goal**; the Development Team forecasts the work.

3. **Daily Scrum (Stand-up)**
   - **Why:** To inspect progress toward the Sprint Goal and adapt the plan.
   - **How:** A 15-minute event for the Development Team. Each member answers: *What did I do yesterday? What will I do today? What impediments do I face?*

4. **Sprint Review**
   - **Why:** To inspect the Increment and adapt the Product Backlog.
   - **How:** The team demonstrates the work to stakeholders and gathers feedback.

5. **Sprint Retrospective**
   - **Why:** To inspect and improve the team's process, people, and definition of "Done".
   - **How:** The team reflects on the past Sprint using formats like *Start / Stop / Continue* or *Mad / Sad / Glad*.

### B. Sprint Artifacts (Information Radiators)

1. **Sprint Backlog** — The set of Product Backlog items, an actionable plan (often a *Sprint Goal*, the *Product Backlog items* selected, and the *work plan*), held by the Development Team.
2. **Product Increment** — The sum of all Product Backlog items completed during a Sprint, integrated with the work of all previous Sprints.
3. **Definition of Done (DoD)** — A shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete. **Every Increment must meet this standard.**

### C. Engineering & Measurement Terminologies

1. **Story Points** — A unit of measure expressing an estimate of the overall effort required to fully implement a Product Backlog item.
2. **Velocity** — The amount of work (in Story Points) a team can complete in a single Sprint.
3. **Capacity** — The total available person-hours / person-days the team has for the Sprint, factoring in holidays, leaves, and meetings.
4. **Burndown Chart** — A line chart showing the remaining work (y-axis) over the days of the Sprint (x-axis).
5. **Sprint Goal** — A single objective for the Sprint that provides focus and encourages cohesion.
6. **Impediment** — Any obstacle that prevents the team from making progress.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

In IT project management practice, these Sprint terminologies allow distributed software teams to:
- Forecast delivery dates using historical **velocity** data.
- Identify bottlenecks via **burndown charts**.
- Maintain quality via a strict **Definition of Done**.
- Continuously improve through **retrospectives**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The *Sprint* is **not** just a work cycle — it is a **container event** that holds four other events. Examiners frequently frame 7-mark questions around this hierarchical relationship.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Velocity | $V = \dfrac{\sum \text{Story Points Completed}}{\text{Number of Sprints}}$ | Story Points / Sprint | Stabilizes after 3–4 Sprints |
| Sprint Burndown | $R(t) = R(0) - \sum_{i=1}^{t} C_i$ | Story Points | $R(t) \ge 0$ |
| Sprint Goal Success | $SG = \dfrac{\text{SP Completed}}{\text{SP Committed}} \times 100$ | Percentage | $SG \ge 80\%$ is healthy |
| Effective Capacity | $C_{eff} = (T_{total} - T_{meetings} - T_{PTO}) \times F$ | Person-hours | $F$ = Focus Factor (typically 0.6 to 0.8) |
| Forecast Sprints | $N = \dfrac{\text{Total Remaining SP}}{V_{avg}}$ | Number | $N$ is rounded up |
| Sprint Duration | $D_s \le 1 \text{ month}$ | Calendar time | 2–4 weeks is the industry norm |
| Daily Scrum Time-box | $T_{ds} = 15 \text{ min}$ | Minutes | Strict, non-extendable |
| Definition of Done | Boolean gate: $DoD : \text{Increment} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$ | Logical | $DoD = 1$ for shippable work |

> [!NOTE]
> The formulas above are **process formulas** (no physical constants like $c$ or $G$). They are pure KTU-relevant performance metrics. The **vertical bar notation** is written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown tables.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Velocity Computation Across Sprints

The **Velocity** metric is the cornerstone of Sprint forecasting. Below is the step-by-step analytical derivation that the KTU valuation key expects.

**Step 1 — Define Story Points per Sprint.**
Let $SP_i$ be the total Story Points completed in Sprint $i$, where $i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$.

**Step 2 — Sum the Completed Story Points.**
The cumulative completed work after $n$ Sprints is:

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} SP_i
$$

**Step 3 — Average Velocity (Arithmetic Mean).**
The Average Velocity $V_{avg}$ is defined as:

$$
V_{avg} = \dfrac{S_{total}}{n} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} SP_i
$$

**Step 4 — Forecasting Remaining Work.**
If the Product Backlog has $SP_{rem}$ Story Points remaining, the number of Sprints required is:

$$
N = \dfrac{SP_{rem}}{V_{avg}}
$$

If $N$ is not a whole number, it is rounded up using the ceiling function:

$$
N_{final} = \lceil N \rceil
$$

**Step 5 — Forecasting the Release Date.**
If each Sprint duration is $D_s$ days and the start date is $D_0$, the estimated release date is:

$$
D_{release} = D_0 + N_{final} \times D_s
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

A team has completed the following Story Points in the last 4 Sprints:
- Sprint 1: **18 SP**
- Sprint 2: **22 SP**
- Sprint 3: **20 SP**
- Sprint 4: **24 SP**

The remaining Product Backlog contains **70 SP**. Each Sprint is 2 weeks. Find: **(a)** Average Velocity, **(b)** Number of Sprints to finish, **(c)** Release date if today is *01-Mar-2024*.

### Solution

**Step (a) — Average Velocity**

$$
S_{total} = 18 + 22 + 20 + 24 = 84 \text{ SP}
$$

$$
V_{avg} = \dfrac{84}{4} = 21 \text{ SP / Sprint}
$$

**Step (b) — Forecast Sprints**

$$
N = \dfrac{70}{21} = 3.33 \text{ Sprints}
$$

$$
N_{final} = \lceil 3.33 \rceil = 4 \text{ Sprints}
$$

**Step (c) — Release Date Calculation**

$$
D_{release} = \text{01-Mar-2024} + 4 \times 14 \text{ days} = \text{01-Mar-2024} + 56 \text{ days}
$$

$$
D_{release} = \text{26-Apr-2024}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always show the **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in your answer — examiners award 1 mark specifically for justifying that partial Sprints cannot exist.

---

## 3.3 Burndown Chart Analytical Framework

A Sprint Burndown Chart is derived from the cumulative work remaining.

Let $R(0)$ be the total work committed at the start of the Sprint. After each day $t$, the work completed is $C_t$. The remaining work is:

$$
R(t) = R(0) - \sum_{i=1}^{t} C_i
$$

The **ideal burndown** (perfectly linear) follows:

$$
R_{ideal}(t) = R(0) \times \left(1 - \dfrac{t}{D_s}\right)
$$

The team's **deviation** from the ideal at day $t$ is:

$$
\Delta(t) = R(t) - R_{ideal}(t)
$$

A positive $\Delta(t)$ indicates the team is **behind schedule**; a negative value indicates the team is **ahead of schedule**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sprint Terminology Classification

| Terminology | Category | Primary Owner | Duration / Metric | Output Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sprint Goal | Commitment | Product Owner | One statement | Sprint Focus |
| Sprint Backlog | Artifact | Development Team | Updated daily | Visible plan |
| Sprint Planning | Event | Scrum Master facilitates | $\le 8$ hours | Sprint Backlog |
| Daily Scrum | Event | Development Team | $15$ minutes | Updated plan |
| Sprint Review | Event | Scrum Team | $\le 4$ hours | Updated Backlog |
| Sprint Retrospective | Event | Scrum Master facilitates | $\le 3$ hours | Improvement actions |
| Definition of Done | Quality Gate | Scrum Team | Boolean check | Shippable Increment |
| Velocity | Metric | Calculated | Story Points | Forecast input |
| Story Points | Estimation Unit | Development Team | Fibonacci scale | Effort estimate |
| Burndown Chart | Visualization | Scrum Master | Line graph | Progress insight |
| Impediment | Blocker | Scrum Master removes | Variable | Unblocked work |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sprint Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **chronological flow** of all Sprint events and the supporting terminologies that operate within them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SPRINT CONTAINER 0 to 1 Month] --> B[SPRINT PLANNING Max 8 Hours]
    B --> C[SPRINT BACKLOG Created]
    C --> D[DAILY SCRUM Every 24 Hours Max 15 Min]
    D --> E{Is Sprint End Reached?}
    E -- No --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[SPRINT REVIEW Max 4 Hours]
    F --> G[PRODUCT INCREMENT Demonstrated]
    G --> H[SPRINT RETROSPECTIVE Max 3 Hours]
    H --> I[DEFINITION OF DONE Verified]
    I --> J[VELOCITY RECORDED]
    J --> K[BURNDOWN CHART ARCHIVED]
    K --> L[New Sprint Begins]
    L --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The Sprint is a **looping container**. Inside it, four formal events (Planning, Daily Scrum, Review, Retrospective) operate in sequence. The artifacts (Backlog, Increment, Burndown Chart) and metrics (Velocity, DoD) are produced as outputs of these events.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Sprint Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PO[PRODUCT OWNER] -->|Defines Priority| PB[PRODUCT BACKLOG]
    PB -->|Pulls Items| SP[SPRINT PLANNING EVENT]
    SP -->|Produces| SB[SPRINT BACKLOG]
    SB -->|Reference| DS[DAILY SCRUM]
    DS -->|Tracks via| BC[BURNDOWN CHART]
    DS -->|Identifies| IM[IMPEDIMENTS]
    IM -->|Removes| SM[SCRUM MASTER]
    DS -->|Burns Down| PI[PRODUCT INCREMENT]
    PI -->|Checked Against| DOD[DEFINITION OF DONE]
    DOD -->|Passed| SR[SPRINT REVIEW]
    SR -->|Feedback to| PB
    DOD -->|Reflected On| SR2[SPRINT RETROSPECTIVE]
    SR2 -->|Improvements to| SP
    DS -->|Measures| V[VELOCITY]
    V -->|Forecasts| PB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation Guide:** This block diagram shows the **functional relationship** between roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master), events (Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, etc.), and artifacts (Backlogs, Increment, Burndown Chart). A 7-mark question may ask you to map any three of these blocks and explain their interaction.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Hierarchy of Sprint Time-Boxes

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1[DAILY SCRUM 15 Minutes] --> T2[SPRINT PLANNING Max 8 Hours]
    T2 --> T3[SPRINT REVIEW Max 4 Hours]
    T3 --> T4[SPRINT RETROSPECTIVE Max 3 Hours]
    T4 --> T5[SPRINT ITSELF Max 1 Month]
```

This nested diagram visually demonstrates that the **Sprint is the largest time-box** and the **Daily Scrum is the smallest**. The ratio is approximately:

$$
\dfrac{T_{Sprint}}{T_{DailyScrum}} = \dfrac{30 \text{ days}}{15 \text{ min}} = \dfrac{43200 \text{ min}}{15 \text{ min}} = 2880
$$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define the term "Sprint" as used in Scrum. State the standard duration of a Sprint.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Sprint** is a **time-boxed iteration** of **one month or less** during which a "Done", usable, and potentially releasable product Increment is created. It is the **heart of Scrum** and acts as a container for all other Scrum events.

**Standard Duration:** The maximum duration of a Sprint is **one month (typically 2 to 4 weeks)**. A new Sprint begins immediately after the conclusion of the previous Sprint.

*(3 marks: 2 for definition + 1 for duration)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is a "Definition of Done" (DoD) in Scrum? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Definition of Done (DoD)** is a **shared understanding within the Scrum Team** of the **quality criteria** that a Product Backlog item must meet before it is considered complete. It is a **formal, documented checklist** that the team adheres to.

**Importance:**
- Ensures **transparency** of work quality.
- Provides a **uniform standard** for all increments.
- Prevents the release of **incomplete or defective** product features.
- Forms the basis for the Sprint Review and Release decisions.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for importance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse "Sprint Review" with "Sprint Retrospective."** Review is for the *product*; Retrospective is for the *process*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this swap.
> 2. **Do not write "Sprint duration is 2 weeks" as a rigid rule** — write "2 to 4 weeks, never exceeding 1 month." Mark loss on this is common.
> 3. **Velocity is NOT a target set by management** — it is an *observed outcome*. Writing it as a target loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Always show the ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in numerical forecast problems.
> 5. **Story Points are NOT hours** — they are a relative unit of effort.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q (a)** Explain the term **"Sprint Goal"**. How does it provide focus and promote cohesion within a Scrum Team? Discuss the consequences of a team working without a Sprint Goal. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A Scrum Team has the following velocity history:

| Sprint | Story Points Completed |
|---|---|
| Sprint 1 | 20 |
| Sprint 2 | 25 |
| Sprint 3 | 22 |
| Sprint 4 | 28 |
| Sprint 5 | 30 |

The remaining Product Backlog is **120 Story Points**. If each Sprint is 3 weeks long and the current date is 15-Jan-2024, calculate: (i) Average Velocity, (ii) Number of Sprints needed, (iii) Estimated Release Date. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

The **Sprint Goal** is a **single, concise objective** set during Sprint Planning that the Sprint is intended to achieve. It provides **focus** and **cohesion** by:

1. **Unifying the Team:** All team members align their efforts toward one measurable outcome rather than completing isolated tasks. *(1 mark)*
2. **Enabling Flexibility in Scope:** The team can adapt the *plan* (Sprint Backlog) as long as the *goal* is preserved. *(1 mark)*
3. **Promoting Collaboration:** Members help each other when individual tasks stall, knowing the common target. *(1 mark)*
4. **Creating Transparency:** Stakeholders and the Product Owner can clearly see Sprint progress. *(1 mark)*

**Consequences of working without a Sprint Goal (2 marks):**
- The team operates as a group of individuals working on disconnected tasks.
- The Daily Scrum becomes a status report rather than a coordination meeting.
- The Sprint Review lacks a clear demonstration theme.
- The team loses its ability to **adapt scope** while preserving the objective.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The Sprint Goal is the *commitment* of the Scrum Team to deliver a coherent, valuable increment within the time-box.

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

$$
SP_1 = 20,\; SP_2 = 25,\; SP_3 = 22,\; SP_4 = 28,\; SP_5 = 30
$$

$$
SP_{rem} = 120, \quad D_s = 3 \text{ weeks}, \quad D_0 = \text{15-Jan-2024}
$$

**Step 1 — Average Velocity (2 marks)**

$$
V_{avg} = \dfrac{20 + 25 + 22 + 28 + 30}{5} = \dfrac{125}{5} = 25 \text{ SP / Sprint}
$$

**Step 2 — Forecast Sprints (2 marks)**

$$
N = \dfrac{120}{25} = 4.8 \text{ Sprints}
$$

$$
N_{final} = \lceil 4.8 \rceil = 5 \text{ Sprints}
$$

**Step 3 — Release Date (3 marks)**

$$
\text{Total duration} = 5 \times 3 \text{ weeks} = 15 \text{ weeks}
$$

$$
15 \text{ weeks} = 105 \text{ days}
$$

$$
D_{release} = \text{15-Jan-2024} + 105 \text{ days}
$$

Adding 105 days to 15-Jan-2024 (31 days in Jan + 29 Feb 2024 leap year + 31 Mar + 14 Apr) = **30-Apr-2024**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Jul 2023]

**Q (a)** Define and differentiate between **Sprint Review** and **Sprint Retrospective**. Include in your answer: purpose, participants, time-box, and output artifacts. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Explain the concept of **"Velocity"** in Scrum. How is it used for release forecasting? Demonstrate with a numerical example where the team has completed 18, 22, 25, and 27 Story Points in the last four Sprints and the Product Backlog contains 96 SP. Each Sprint is 2 weeks long. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

| Aspect | Sprint Review | Sprint Retrospective |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Inspect the *Increment* and gather stakeholder feedback. | Inspect and improve the team's *process* and relationships. |
| **Participants** | Scrum Team + Stakeholders + Customers. | Scrum Team only (internal). |
| **Time-box** | Maximum **4 hours** (for a 1-month Sprint). | Maximum **3 hours** (for a 1-month Sprint). |
| **Output Artifact** | Updated Product Backlog with new items/feedback. | List of process improvement actions for the next Sprint. |
| **Focus** | The **Product** ("Are we building the right thing?"). | The **Process** ("Are we building it the right way?"). |

*(5 marks for the table; 2 marks for concluding note on why both events are essential to empirical process control — the three pillars: Transparency, Inspection, Adaptation.)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Concept (3 marks):**
**Velocity** is the **measure of work completed per Sprint**, expressed in Story Points. It is an *empirical metric* — not a target set by management. It stabilizes after **3 to 4 Sprints** of consistent performance and serves as the **primary input for release forecasting**.

**Numerical Demonstration (4 marks):**

$$
V_{avg} = \dfrac{18 + 22 + 25 + 27}{4} = \dfrac{92}{4} = 23 \text{ SP / Sprint}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{96}{23} = 4.17 \text{ Sprints}
$$

$$
N_{final} = \lceil 4.17 \rceil = 5 \text{ Sprints}
$$

**Release Duration:**

$$
5 \times 2 \text{ weeks} = 10 \text{ weeks} = 70 \text{ days}
$$

**Conclusion:** With an average velocity of 23 SP per Sprint, the team will require 5 Sprints (10 weeks) to deliver the remaining 96 Story Points.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4 – Sprint Terminologies):**

- **Sprint** = Time-boxed iteration, $\le 1$ month, produces a usable Increment.
- **Sprint Goal** = Single, unifying objective; provides focus and adaptability.
- **Sprint Backlog** = Owned by the Development Team; visible, real-time plan.
- **Product Backlog** = Owned by the Product Owner; prioritized list of everything that might be needed.
- **Product Increment** = Sum of all completed Backlog items; must meet the DoD.
- **Sprint Planning** = $\le 8$ hours; produces the Sprint Backlog.
- **Daily Scrum** = 15 minutes; for the Development Team; inspects progress.
- **Sprint Review** = $\le 4$ hours; for the *product*; involves stakeholders.
- **Sprint Retrospective** = $\le 3$ hours; for the *process*; Scrum Team only.
- **Definition of Done (DoD)** = Shared quality standard; non-negotiable for Increment.
- **Story Points** = Relative effort estimate; Fibonacci scale (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21).
- **Velocity** = $\sum SP / n$; empirical, not a target.
- **Capacity** = Available person-hours minus PTO minus meetings times a *focus factor* (0.6 to 0.8).
- **Burndown Chart** = Remaining work vs. time; ideal line is linear.
- **Impediment** = Any blocker; Scrum Master's responsibility to remove.
- **Time-box** = A hard, non-negotiable maximum duration for an event.
- **Sprint Forecast Formula:** $N_{final} = \lceil SP_{rem} / V_{avg} \rceil$.
- **Scrum Team Size** = $\le 10$ people (recommended).
- **Sprint is a CONTAINER event** — it holds all four other events.
- **No scope change inside a Sprint** that compromises the Sprint Goal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked to "explain Sprint terminologies," always group your answer under three headers — *Events, Artifacts, Metrics* — and present at least one formula. This structured approach consistently scores full marks in the 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Backlog
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Product Backlog

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Product Backlog (Scrum Guide 2020 Definition):**
> The **Product Backlog** is an **emergent, ordered list of what is needed to improve the product**. It is the **single source of work** undertaken by the Scrum Team. Items at the top of the Product Backlog are clearer and more detailed than items at the bottom. As the product and the environment in which it is used evolves, the Product Backlog evolves as well. The Product Backlog is **dynamic**; it constantly changes to identify what the product needs to be appropriate, competitive, and useful.

The **Product Owner** is the person solely accountable for the **content, availability, and ordering** of the Product Backlog. The Product Backlog is managed by the Product Owner but is **co-created** with the entire Scrum Team to ensure technical feasibility, business value alignment, and continuous refinement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — The "Shopping List for Your Dream House":**
> 
> Imagine you are building a custom dream house with an architect (the **Product Owner**) and a construction team (the **Development Team**).
> - Instead of a single rigid blueprint decided on day one, you maintain a **living shopping list** of every feature, room, and improvement.
> - At the top of the list are the most important items — *"Build the kitchen, install plumbing in master bathroom."* — these are crystal clear and ready to start tomorrow.
> - At the bottom are vague future ideas — *"Maybe a rooftop garden someday?"*
> - Every week, you and the team review the list (this is **Backlog Refinement**), adding new ideas, removing bad ones, and getting more clarity on the items that will be built next.
> - This list is the **Product Backlog** — it is the single source of truth for "what we are building and why."

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **PBI (Product Backlog Item):** Any item on the Product Backlog (user story, bug, technical task, spike, etc.).
- **DEEP Qualities:** **D**etailed Appropriately, **E**stimated, **E**mergent, **P**rioritized.
- **INVEST Criteria:** **I**ndependent, **N**egotiable, **V**aluable, **E**stimable, **S**mall, **T**estable.
- **Refinement (Grooming):** The act of adding detail, estimates, and order to Product Backlog items.
- **Definition of Ready (DoR):** A shared understanding of the conditions that a PBI must meet before it can be selected for a Sprint.
- **Product Owner Accountability:** The Product Owner is **one person**, not a committee.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — The Anatomy of a Product Backlog

The Product Backlog is not a flat to-do list. It is a **prioritized, living artifact** with a specific anatomy. Below is a structured breakdown of its operational logic:

### Step 1: The Product Backlog Contains a Mix of Item Types
A healthy Product Backlog holds a heterogeneous mix of items:
- **User Stories** (functional features described from the end-user's perspective)
- **Bugs / Defects** (broken functionality that must be fixed)
- **Technical Tasks / Enablers** (architecture, refactoring, infrastructure)
- **Spikes** (time-boxed research to reduce uncertainty)
- **Non-Functional Requirements** (performance, security, usability)
- **Epics and Themes** (large bodies of work that get decomposed into stories)

### Step 2: Items are Continuously Refined (The Refinement Loop)
- **Top of the backlog:** Highly refined, small, estimated, "Ready" for the next Sprint.
- **Middle of the backlog:** Less clear, roughly estimated, slated for future Sprints.
- **Bottom of the backlog:** Coarse, unestimated, "someday/maybe" ideas.
- **Refinement Time-box:** Scrum Guide caps this at **no more than 10% of the capacity of the Development Team**.

### Step 3: The Product Owner Owns the "What" and the "Why"
- The Product Owner **orders** items by value, risk, and dependencies.
- The **Development Team** owns the "How" (technical implementation).
- The **Scrum Master** ensures the refinement process is healthy.

### Step 4: Emergent Nature — It Is Never "Done"
- New items are added as the market changes, customers speak, or technology evolves.
- Old items are removed when they are no longer valuable.
- Items move up or down based on shifting priorities.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Product Backlog Properties (DEEP)

| Property | Full Form | What It Means in Practice | KTU Exam Cue |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D** | Detailed Appropriately | Items near the top have enough detail to be worked on; items at the bottom can be vague. | "Items at the top of the Product Backlog are more refined than items at the bottom." |
| **E** | Estimated | Items at the top are sized in effort/points; bottom items may not be estimated yet. | Estimates appear progressively. |
| **E** | Emergent | The backlog is never complete; it evolves continuously with new learning. | "Product Backlog is a living artifact." |
| **P** | Prioritized | Items are ordered; the sequence reflects business value, risk, and dependencies. | The Product Owner is accountable for ordering. |

---

## 2.3 INVEST Criteria for High-Quality User Stories

| Letter | Criterion | Test Question |
|---|---|---|
| **I** | Independent | Can this story be developed without depending on another unfinished story? |
| **N** | Negotiable | Is the story a placeholder for conversation, not a rigid contract? |
| **V** | Valuable | Does it deliver clear value to a user, customer, or stakeholder? |
| **E** | Estimable | Can the team assign a size (story points) to it? |
| **S** | Small | Can it fit within a single Sprint? |
| **T** | Testable | Can we write clear pass/fail acceptance criteria? |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real-world Agile software development (used by Amazon, Spotify, Microsoft, and most product-based firms), the Product Backlog:
- **Replaces the traditional "Waterfall Requirements Specification"** with a living, prioritized inventory.
- **Enables Continuous Value Delivery** — the team always works on the highest-value items.
- **Decouples big, vague ideas (Epics) from small, shippable units (Stories)** through progressive elaboration.
- **Provides a single source of truth** for product roadmaps, sprint planning, and release forecasting.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — From Vision to Backlog

## 3.1 Hierarchical Decomposition: Epic → Feature → Story → Task

The Product Backlog typically holds items at multiple levels of granularity. Below is the **exhaustive hierarchical derivation** showing how a high-level product vision is progressively broken down into shippable units.

### Stage 1: The Product Vision (Top of the Pyramid)
- **Input:** A strategic vision statement (e.g., *"Build a cloud-based expense reporting tool for small businesses"*).
- **Output:** A clear, single-sentence north star.

### Stage 2: Decomposition into Epics
- An **Epic** is a large body of work that can be broken down, often spanning multiple Sprints.
- **Example Epic:** *"User Authentication Module"* or *"Reporting & Analytics Dashboard"*.

### Stage 3: Epic Decomposed into Features
- A **Feature** is a distinct functionality within an Epic.
- **Example Feature (under Reporting Epic):** *"PDF Export of Monthly Expense Reports"*.

### Stage 4: Feature Decomposed into User Stories
- A **User Story** describes a small, user-centric increment of value.
- **Standard Template:**
$$
\text{As a } \langle \text{role} \rangle \text{, I want } \langle \text{goal} \rangle \text{ so that } \langle \text{benefit} \rangle
$$
- **Example Story:** *"As a small business owner, I want to export my monthly expenses to PDF so that I can share them with my accountant."*

### Stage 5: Story Decomposed into Tasks (Optional)
- During Sprint Planning, the Development Team breaks stories into technical **Tasks** (e.g., design schema, write API endpoint, build UI, write tests).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Building a "User Login" Feature (Analytical Matrix)

Let us derive a complete Product Backlog refinement walkthrough for a real-world scenario.

> **Scenario:** A startup is building a SaaS HR portal. The Product Owner wants to add a **"Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)"** capability.

### Step 1 — Capture the Raw Idea (Bottom of Backlog)
$$
\text{Initial Entry: } \text{"Add MFA for security"}
$$
- **Refinement status:** Coarse, no detail, no estimate, no priority. Lives at the bottom.

### Step 2 — Apply INVEST to Convert into a User Story
$$
\text{As a registered user, I want to enable Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) using my mobile app,} \\ \text{so that my account is protected even if my password is stolen.}
$$
- **I (Independent):** ✅ Can be built without other unfinished stories.
- **N (Negotiable):** ✅ Specifics like app type (Google Authenticator, Authy) are left open for conversation.
- **V (Valuable):** ✅ Clearly increases security and customer trust.
- **E (Estimable):** ✅ Team can size it (e.g., 5 story points).
- **S (Small):** ✅ Fits within one Sprint.
- **T (Testable):** ✅ Can write pass/fail tests (e.g., OTP invalid → login denied).

### Step 3 — Add Acceptance Criteria (Definition of Ready)
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AC1: } & \text{User can scan a QR code using a standard TOTP authenticator app.} \\
\text{AC2: } & \text{An OTP is required after entering the correct password.} \\
\text{AC3: } & \text{Invalid OTP blocks login and shows a clear error message.} \\
\text{AC4: } & \text{User can disable 2FA from settings after re-verification.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Estimate Using Story Points
- The Development Team, using **Planning Poker**, agrees on **5 story points** based on relative effort, complexity, and risk.
- This estimate is added to the PBI, making it more refined — it moves **up** the Product Backlog.

### Step 5 — Prioritize (Order by Value)
- The Product Owner, knowing a major enterprise client has demanded MFA before signing a contract, places this story **at the top** of the Product Backlog — ahead of lower-value cosmetic improvements.

### Step 6 — Refinement Time-Box Check
- If the team spends more than **10% of Sprint capacity** refining the backlog, the Scrum Master intervenes to maintain the limit set by the Scrum Guide.

---

## 3.3 Prioritization Techniques (Analytical Toolkit)

The KTU examiner frequently asks students to list or compare prioritization techniques. Below is the exhaustive breakdown:

| Technique | Core Idea | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **MoSCoW** | Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have (this time) | When stakeholders need a clear release scope. |
| **WSJF (Weighted Shortest Job First)** | $\text{WSJF} = \dfrac{\text{Business Value} + \text{Time Criticality} + \text{Risk Reduction}}{\text{Job Size}}$ | SAFe framework; large-scale Agile. |
| **Kano Model** | Classifies features as Basic, Performance, or Excitement | When balancing must-haves vs. delighters. |
| **Cost of Delay** | $\text{CoD} = \text{User-Business Value} + \text{Time Criticality} + \text{Risk Reduction}$ | When justifying ordering decisions. |
| **Value vs. Effort Matrix** | Plot items on a 2x2 grid (High/Low Value × High/Low Effort) | Quick, lightweight prioritization for small teams. |
| **Stack Ranking** | Simply order items 1, 2, 3, … by gut feel + data | Early-stage products with limited data. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If asked to *derive* the order of items, KTU 2024 expects you to explicitly state that **the Product Owner is accountable for the ordering** and that the decision is a **balancing of business value, risk, dependencies, and learning**.

---

## 3.4 Definition of Ready (DoR) — Analytical Derivation

A **Definition of Ready** is a checklist that signals a PBI is "ready" to be pulled into a Sprint. A typical DoR includes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DoR Checklist: } & \\
1. & \text{ User story follows the standard format (As a / I want / So that).} \\
2. & \text{ INVEST criteria are satisfied.} \\
3. & \text{ Acceptance criteria are written and testable.} \\
4. & \text{ Story is estimated (story points assigned).} \\
5. & \text{ Dependencies are identified and resolved or accepted.} \\
6. & \text{ Story is small enough to be completed in one Sprint.} \\
7. & \text{ The team has a shared understanding (refinement session done).}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Product Backlog — Order of Refinement Pyramid

The following block-level functional architecture flow illustrates the **progressive elaboration** of the Product Backlog from vague ideas at the bottom to fully ready items at the top.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["STRATEGIC VISION"] --> B["EPICS Large coarse-grained initiatives"]
    B --> C["FEATURES Distinct functionalities within an Epic"]
    C --> D["USER STORIES Small user-centric increments of value"]
    D --> E["TASKS Technical work items created during Sprint Planning"]
    F["TOP OF BACKLOG Ready small estimated detailed valuable items"] --> G["MIDDLE BACKLOG Roughly estimated less clear items"]
    G --> H["BOTTOM OF BACKLOG Vague someday or maybe ideas"]
    H --> B
```

## 4.2 Product Backlog Refinement Lifecycle (Continuous Flow)

The following diagram shows how a Product Backlog Item (PBI) moves through the refinement pipeline in a never-ending loop.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["NEW IDEA Captured as raw backlog item"] --> P2["DECOMPOSITION Epic to Feature to Story"]
    P2 --> P3["INVEST VALIDATION Check Independent Negotiable Valuable Estimable Small Testable"]
    P3 --> P4["ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA Definition of Ready checklist"]
    P4 --> P5["ESTIMATION Planning Poker story points assigned"]
    P5 --> P6["PRIORITIZATION Ordered by Product Owner value risk dependency"]
    P6 --> P7{"Sprint Planning: Item pulled into Sprint Backlog?"}
    P7 -- YES --> P8["DEVELOPMENT Sprint execution"]
    P7 -- NO --> P9["STAYS IN PRODUCT BACKLOG Continuously refined"]
    P9 --> P3
```

## 4.3 Roles and Responsibilities in Product Backlog Management

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PO["PRODUCT OWNER Owns content availability and ordering of Product Backlog"]
    DEV["DEVELOPMENT TEAM Owns the how estimates and technical decomposition"]
    SM["SCRUM MASTER Facilitates refinement removes impediments enforces time-box"]
    STK["STAKEHOLDERS CUSTOMERS USERS Provide input on value and acceptance"]

    PO <--> DEV
    PO <--> STK
    SM --> PO
    SM --> DEV
    DEV --> SM
```

## 4.4 Product Backlog vs. Sprint Backlog — Boundary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PB["PRODUCT BACKLOG Owned by Product Owner Long-term horizon"]
        P1["Item 1 Top: highest value"]
        P2["Item 2"]
        P3["Item 3"]
        P4["Item N Bottom: vague ideas"]
    end

    subgraph SB["SPRINT BACKLOG Owned by Development Team Short-term horizon 1 Sprint"]
        S1["Story pulled from PB Item 1"]
        S2["Tasks derived from Story 1"]
        S3["Tasks derived from Story 1"]
    end

    P1 -- Selected during Sprint Planning --> S1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> 
> **Q1. Define Product Backlog. List its key characteristics.**
> 
> **Model Answer:**
> The **Product Backlog** is an emergent, ordered list of what is needed to improve the product. It is the single source of work for the Scrum Team and is managed by the Product Owner.
> 
> Key characteristics (DEEP):
> 1. **Detailed Appropriately** — items at the top are clearer than those at the bottom.
> 2. **Estimated** — items at the top have size estimates.
> 3. **Emergent** — it evolves continuously and is never "done."
> 4. **Prioritized** — items are ordered by value, risk, and dependencies.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> 
> **Q2. What is Backlog Refinement? Mention its time-box limit as per the Scrum Guide.**
> 
> **Model Answer:**
> **Backlog Refinement** (also called Grooming) is the continuous activity of adding detail, estimates, and order to Product Backlog items. It is a collaborative effort involving the Product Owner, Development Team, and Scrum Master.
> 
> As per the **Scrum Guide**, refinement typically consumes **no more than 10% of the capacity of the Development Team** to ensure that the team does not spend excessive time on backlog work at the cost of Sprint execution.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write "Product Backlog is owned by the Scrum Master" — **WRONG**. The Product Backlog is owned by the **Product Owner**, though the Scrum Team contributes to refinement.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Conflating **Product Backlog** with **Sprint Backlog**. The Product Backlog is long-term and owned by the Product Owner; the Sprint Backlog is short-term (one Sprint) and owned by the Development Team.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing that backlog items are "frozen" once the Sprint starts. In reality, only the **Sprint Goal** is frozen; the Product Backlog continues to evolve.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Model Question**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks, Module 4]**
> 
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a Product Backlog in detail. Discuss its key characteristics (DEEP) and the role of the Product Owner in managing it.
> 
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the INVEST criteria for writing good user stories. With a suitable example, show how a raw requirement is refined into a ready user story.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Product Backlog — Concept, DEEP Characteristics, Product Owner's Role**

The **Product Backlog** is a dynamic, prioritized list of all known requirements, enhancements, bug fixes, and technical work needed to build and improve a product. It serves as the **single source of truth** for "what the Scrum Team will work on."

**DEEP Characteristics:**

1. **Detailed Appropriately:** Items at the top are clear, small, and have acceptance criteria. Items at the bottom may be one-liners or vague ideas. Detail grows as items move up.
2. **Estimated:** Items at the top have story-point estimates; bottom items may have **T-shirt sizes** (S/M/L) or no estimate at all.
3. **Emergent:** The Product Backlog is a **living artifact** — items are added, removed, and re-ordered continuously as the market, customers, and technology evolve.
4. **Prioritized:** The list is ordered so that the most valuable, most risky, or most dependent items are at the top.

**Role of the Product Owner:**
- **Accountable for the content, availability, and ordering** of the Product Backlog.
- Represents the **voice of the customer** and the business.
- Ensures **Maximum Value Delivery** by ordering items based on business value, risk, and dependencies.
- Decides **what** gets built; the Development Team decides **how**.
- Regularly **engages with stakeholders** to refine priorities.

**(b) INVEST Criteria with Worked Example**

**INVEST Criteria Explained:**

- **I — Independent:** A story should be self-contained and not depend on other incomplete stories.
- **N — Negotiable:** A story is a placeholder for conversation, not a rigid contract.
- **V — Valuable:** A story must deliver clear value to a user, customer, or stakeholder.
- **E — Estimable:** A story must be clear enough to assign a size estimate.
- **S — Small:** A story should fit within a single Sprint.
- **T — Testable:** A story must have clear pass/fail acceptance criteria.

**Worked Example — Refining a Raw Requirement:**

**Raw Requirement (bottom of backlog):**
- *"Add password reset to the login page."*

**Refined User Story (top of backlog):**

> *"As a registered user, I want to reset my forgotten password via a secure email link, so that I can regain access to my account without contacting support."*

**Acceptance Criteria (Definition of Ready):**
- AC1: User clicks "Forgot Password" and enters a registered email.
- AC2: A unique, time-limited reset link is emailed to the user.
- AC3: The link expires after 30 minutes.
- AC4: The new password must meet complexity rules (length, special characters).
- AC5: A confirmation message is shown after a successful reset.

**Estimate:** Team assigns **3 story points** using Planning Poker.

**Refinement Verification Using INVEST:**
- **I:** Independent — can be built without other stories. ✅
- **N:** Negotiable — exact expiry time and complexity rules are open for conversation. ✅
- **V:** Valuable — directly improves user experience. ✅
- **E:** Estimable — team can size it. ✅
- **S:** Small — fits in one Sprint. ✅
- **T:** Testable — all five ACs are testable. ✅

The story is now "Ready" and can be pulled into the next Sprint.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks, Module 4]**
> 
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Product Backlog** and **Sprint Backlog**. Explain who owns each and how items flow from one to the other.
> 
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable example, explain the process of **Backlog Refinement**. Mention the time-box limit and the roles involved.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Product Backlog vs. Sprint Backlog**

| Basis of Comparison | Product Backlog | Sprint Backlog |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Long-term, contains everything that could ever be done. | Short-term, contains only items for the current Sprint. |
| **Owner** | **Product Owner** (sole accountability). | **Development Team** (collectively). |
| **Content** | User stories, bugs, spikes, epics, technical tasks. | A selected **Sprint Goal** plus the stories and tasks pulled in for that Sprint. |
| **Time Horizon** | Can span weeks, months, or even years. | A single Sprint (typically 1–4 weeks). |
| **Evolution** | Continuously evolving; new items added anytime. | Frozen for the duration of the Sprint (only the Sprint Goal is fixed). |
| **Visibility** | Visible to all stakeholders. | Visible to the Scrum Team primarily. |

**Flow of Items:**
- During **Sprint Planning**, the Development Team pulls the **highest-priority, ready items** from the Product Backlog into the Sprint Backlog.
- These items are then decomposed into **technical tasks** by the team.
- Once the Sprint starts, the Product Backlog continues to be refined for **future** Sprints.

**(b) Backlog Refinement — Process, Time-box, and Roles**

**Definition:**
Backlog Refinement (Grooming) is the **ongoing activity** of adding detail, estimates, and order to Product Backlog items so that the next Sprint's worth of work is "Ready."

**Process Steps (with example):**

1. **Select an Item to Refine:** The team picks a coarse item from the middle of the Product Backlog, e.g., *"Allow users to upload profile pictures."*
2. **Collaborative Discussion:** The Product Owner explains the **why** (business value), the Development Team asks **how** (technical approach), and acceptance criteria are drafted.
3. **Split if Needed:** If the item is too large, it is decomposed — e.g., split into *"Upload from device"*, *"Crop image"*, *"Set as default avatar"*.
4. **Write Acceptance Criteria:** ACs are added (file size limit, supported formats, etc.).
5. **Estimate:** The team assigns story points using **Planning Poker**.
6. **Re-prioritize:** The Product Owner places the refined items higher up the backlog.

**Time-box Limit:**
As per the Scrum Guide, the Development Team should spend **no more than 10% of their Sprint capacity** on refinement activities. For a 2-week Sprint, this is roughly **4 hours per person** across the Sprint.

**Roles Involved:**
- **Product Owner:** Clarifies the "what" and the "why," accepts or rejects refined items.
- **Development Team:** Owns the "how," provides estimates, flags technical dependencies.
- **Scrum Master:** Facilitates the refinement session, ensures the time-box is respected, and coaches the team on healthy practices.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Product Backlog (KTU 2024)**

- **Definition:** Emergent, ordered list of what is needed to improve the product. Single source of work.
- **Owner:** The **Product Owner** is solely accountable for its **content, availability, and ordering**.
- **DEEP Properties:** **D**etailed Appropriately, **E**stimated, **E**mergent, **P**rioritized.
- **INVEST Criteria for User Stories:** Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable.
- **User Story Format:** *As a `<role>`, I want `<goal>`, so that `<benefit>`.*
- **Refinement Time-box:** ≤ **10% of Development Team capacity** per Sprint.
- **Hierarchy:** Vision → Epic → Feature → User Story → Task.
- **Product Backlog ≠ Sprint Backlog:** Long-term vs. short-term; Product Owner vs. Development Team.
- **Prioritization Techniques:** MoSCoW, WSJF, Kano Model, Cost of Delay, Value vs. Effort Matrix, Stack Ranking.
- **Definition of Ready (DoR):** Shared checklist that signals a PBI is ready to be pulled into a Sprint.
- **Emergent Nature:** The Product Backlog is **never complete** and **never frozen** — it evolves continuously.
- **Examiner's Trap:** Do NOT say the Scrum Master owns the Product Backlog, and do NOT say the backlog is "done" at any point.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sprint Backlog
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sprint Backlog

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **Sprint Backlog (Scrum Guide 2020 Definition)**
> The **Sprint Backlog** is the set of Product Backlog items selected for the Sprint, plus a **plan for delivering the product Increment** and realizing the **Sprint Goal**. It is a highly visible, real-time picture of the work that the Development Team plans to accomplish during the Sprint, and it belongs solely to the Development Team.

In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, the Sprint Backlog is recognized as a **formal Scrum Artifact** (one of the three: Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment) and is a direct deliverable of the **Sprint Planning** event. It is **owned and updated exclusively by the Development Team**, while the Product Owner and Scrum Master may influence it but cannot dictate its internal composition.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4 – Agile Project Management)**
> The Sprint Backlog is a high-weightage topic under *Scrum Framework Artifacts*. Students must be able to distinguish it from the Product Backlog, explain its composition, describe the Sprint Planning event that creates it, and link it to tracking tools such as the **Sprint Burndown Chart**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **chef preparing for a single dinner service in a restaurant**:

- The **Product Backlog** = the *entire recipe book of the restaurant* (hundreds of possible dishes, prioritized by customer demand).
- The **Sprint Backlog** = the *chopping board and prep list for tonight's service*. The head chef (Development Team) selects 5–6 specific dishes (User Stories) from the recipe book, decides the order of cooking, the prep work needed (chopping onions, marinating, boiling water), and the estimated time each task will take.
- The **Sprint Goal** = the *theme of tonight's service* (e.g., "Italian Night" – every dish must feel authentically Italian).
- During service, the chef constantly updates the board — *"onions chopped ✔, sauce simmering, pasta boiling"* — this is the **daily update** of the Sprint Backlog.

> **Key Insight:** Just as the chef cannot suddenly add an entirely new dish mid-service (that would be *scope creep*), the Development Team cannot add new items to the Sprint Backlog mid-Sprint without renegotiating with the Product Owner.

---

## 1.3 Core Terms You Must Memorize

| Term | Plain-English Meaning | KTU-Standard Term |
|------|----------------------|-------------------|
| **Sprint Goal** | A single objective for the Sprint | The *why* of the Sprint |
| **Product Backlog Item (PBI)** | A user need/feature/story | The *what* to be done |
| **Task** | A piece of work needed to complete a PBI | The *how* it will be done |
| **Definition of Done (DoD)** | The agreed completion criteria | The *done* contract |
| **Capacity** | Total developer-hours available | The *constraint* |

> [!NOTE] **Standard Metric (Bolded Constant)**
> The standard **Sprint length is 2 weeks (10 working days)** in most KTU case-study questions. The **recommended team size is 7 ± 2 members**, and the **recommended Daily Scrum time-box is 15 minutes**.

---

## 1.4 Relationship Map of Scrum Artifacts

The Sprint Backlog sits **between** the Product Backlog and the Increment:

$$\text{Product Backlog} \xrightarrow{\text{Sprint Planning}} \text{Sprint Backlog} \xrightarrow{\text{Execution by Dev Team}} \text{Increment}$$

This transparency-driven chain is a recurring KTU question pattern (often worth **3–5 marks**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Components of the Sprint Backlog

The Sprint Backlog is **not just a list of tasks**. It is a composite artifact made of three interrelated layers:

1. **The Sprint Goal** — A short statement of *why* the Sprint is valuable. It provides coherence and focus, encouraging the team to work collaboratively rather than on isolated tasks.
2. **The Selected Product Backlog Items** — A subset of items pulled from the top of the Product Backlog. These are typically high-priority User Stories that align with the Sprint Goal.
3. **The Plan for Delivering the Increment** — A decomposition of each PBI into engineering tasks (often 4–16 hours of work each), with estimates and ownership.

> **Why these three together?** Without the *Goal*, the team optimizes locally. Without the *Items*, there is nothing concrete. Without the *Plan*, the work cannot be tracked daily.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Lifecycle

The Sprint Backlog follows a precise operational flow inside the Scrum framework:

- **Step 1 — Sprint Planning (Part 1: *What*):** The Product Owner presents the highest-priority Product Backlog items. The Development Team forecasts which ones can be completed in the Sprint → forms the **initial Sprint Backlog**.
- **Step 2 — Sprint Planning (Part 2: *How*):** The Development Team breaks each selected PBI into **tasks** (sub-units of 4–16 hours). Tasks are estimated and self-assigned.
- **Step 3 — Sprint Backlog Refinement (Throughout the Sprint):** As the team learns, new tasks are added and obsolete tasks are removed. **Scope may be clarified and renegotiated** with the Product Owner, but the Sprint Goal remains **sacrosanct**.
- **Step 4 — Daily Scrum Updates:** Every day, the team inspects the Sprint Backlog (typically visualized on a **Task Board** or **Burndown Chart**) and adapts the plan for the next 24 hours.
- **Step 5 — Sprint Review & Retrospective:** At Sprint end, the Sprint Backlog is **discarded** after retrospective lessons are captured. A new Sprint Backlog is created in the next Sprint Planning.

> [!NOTE] **Critical KTU Point**
> The Sprint Backlog is **empirically transparent**. It must be visible to all stakeholders — typically on a wall, whiteboard, or digital tool (Jira, Azure DevOps, Trello). Opacity violates the Scrum pillar of **Transparency**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula, metric, and boundary condition associated with the Sprint Backlog that students may be tested on.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Team Velocity** | $V = \dfrac{\sum \text{Story Points Completed in Last } n \text{ Sprints}}{n}$ | Story Points / Sprint | Typically averaged over 3 Sprints |
| **Sprint Capacity** | $C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (H_i \times F_i)$ | Person-Hours | $H_i$ = available hours, $F_i$ = focus factor (commonly **0.6 to 0.8**) |
| **Sprint Commitment** | $S_c = \text{Total Story Points pulled into Sprint}$ | Story Points | Should be $\leq$ Velocity with buffer |
| **Ideal Burn Rate** | $R_{ideal} = \dfrac{S_c}{D_{sprint}}$ | Points / Day | Linear ideal burndown line |
| **Actual Remaining Work** | $RW_t = S_c - \sum \text{Points Completed up to Day } t$ | Story Points | Plotted on Y-axis of Burndown |
| **Focus Factor** | $F = \dfrac{\text{Productive Hours}}{\text{Available Hours}}$ | Dimensionless | Industry standard: **0.60** |
| **Task Size Limit** | $T_{max} \leq 16 \text{ hours}$ | Hours | Anything larger must be decomposed |
| **Sprint Length Range** | $1 \text{ to } 4 \text{ weeks}$ | Weeks | KTU default assumption: **2 weeks** |
| **Daily Scrum Time-box** | $\leq 15 \text{ minutes}$ | Minutes | Hard limit; exceeded = violation |
| **Sprint Planning Time-box** | $\leq 8 \text{ hours (for 1-month Sprint)}$ | Hours | Scales linearly: 4 hours for 2-week Sprint |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas drive real-world **release forecasting** in IT companies. A Scrum Master uses velocity and capacity formulas to advise stakeholders on *how many Sprints* a project will take — directly feeding into contract negotiations and budget planning.

---

## 2.4 The Sprint Goal – The Spine of the Sprint Backlog

Every Sprint Backlog must be anchored to **one Sprint Goal**. This single sentence provides:

- **Focus:** If two items conflict, the team defers to the goal.
- **Flexibility:** The team can swap tasks as long as the goal is met.
- **Collaboration:** Encourages team-level work rather than individual silos.

> [!NOTE] **Example (KTU Exam Style)**
> *Sprint Goal:* "Enable customers to checkout using a saved credit card."
> *Sprint Backlog Items aligned:* (1) Save card API, (2) PCI-compliant storage, (3) UI for card selection, (4) Test automation for payment flow.
> Notice that an unrelated bug fix (e.g., "fix footer color") would **not** belong in this Sprint unless the Product Owner agrees to expand scope.

---

## 2.5 Task Board – Visual Representation of the Sprint Backlog

The Sprint Backlog is most commonly visualized as a **Task Board** with three (or more) columns:

$$\text{To Do} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{In Progress} \quad \longrightarrow \quad \text{Done}$$

Some teams add **"Verification / Testing"** between In Progress and Done to enforce the **Definition of Done (DoD)**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition of Done (DoD) vs Acceptance Criteria**
> - **Acceptance Criteria** = defined by the Product Owner, item-specific (e.g., "card must be tokenized").
> - **Definition of Done** = defined by the team, applies to *every* item (e.g., "code reviewed, unit tested, deployed to staging, documentation updated").
> Conflating these is a **favourite KTU trick question** worth 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating the Ideal Burndown Line

The **Sprint Burndown Chart** is the visual companion of the Sprint Backlog. It plots the *remaining work* on the Y-axis against the *Sprint days* on the X-axis. The ideal line is a straight line from the starting point to zero on the last day.

Let the following variables be defined:

- $S_c$ = Total Story Points committed at the start of the Sprint
- $D_{sprint}$ = Total duration of the Sprint in working days
- $t$ = Day index (where $t = 0$ is Day 1 and $t = D_{sprint}$ is the last day)
- $RW_t$ = Remaining work at the end of day $t$

**Step 1:** The ideal burn rate (slope of the ideal line) is the total work divided by the total duration:

$$R_{ideal} = \frac{S_c}{D_{sprint}}$$

**Step 2:** At day $t$, the *ideal* remaining work is the initial commitment minus the cumulative ideal work completed in $t$ days:

$$RW_t^{ideal} = S_c - (R_{ideal} \times t) = S_c - \left(\frac{S_c}{D_{sprint}}\right) \times t$$

**Step 3:** Simplifying the expression:

$$RW_t^{ideal} = S_c \left(1 - \frac{t}{D_{sprint}}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Boundary condition check:
- At $t = 0$: $RW_0^{ideal} = S_c \left(1 - 0\right) = S_c$ ✔
- At $t = D_{sprint}$: $RW_{D}^{ideal} = S_c \left(1 - 1\right) = 0$ ✔

This confirms the ideal line is a straight descending line from $(0, S_c)$ to $(D_{sprint}, 0)$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A 2-week (10 working days) Sprint has the team commit to **40 Story Points** in the Sprint Backlog. The Focus Factor of each developer is **0.6**, and there are **5 developers** each working **8 hours/day**.
> **(a)** Calculate the team's capacity in person-hours.
> **(b)** Plot the ideal burndown values for Day 1, Day 5, and Day 10.
> **(c)** If by end of Day 5, 16 points remain, what is the actual burn rate per day so far?

### Solution

**Part (a) — Capacity Calculation**

**Step 1:** Compute the available hours per developer across the Sprint:

$$H_{developer} = 8 \text{ hours/day} \times 10 \text{ days} = 80 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the focus factor to get productive hours per developer:

$$H_{productive} = 80 \times 0.6 = 48 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by the team size:

$$C_{team} = 48 \times 5 = 240 \text{ person-hours}$$

> **Answer (a):** The team's productive capacity is **240 person-hours**.

---

**Part (b) — Ideal Burndown Values**

**Step 1:** Compute the ideal daily burn rate:

$$R_{ideal} = \frac{S_c}{D_{sprint}} = \frac{40}{10} = 4 \text{ points/day}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $RW_t^{ideal}$ for the requested days:

- Day 1 ($t = 1$): $RW_1^{ideal} = 40 - (4 \times 1) = 36$ points
- Day 5 ($t = 5$): $RW_5^{ideal} = 40 - (4 \times 5) = 20$ points
- Day 10 ($t = 10$): $RW_{10}^{ideal} = 40 - (4 \times 10) = 0$ points

> **Answer (b):** Ideal burndown = **36, 20, 0 points** at Days 1, 5, and 10 respectively.

---

**Part (c) — Actual Burn Rate After 5 Days**

**Step 1:** Calculate points actually completed:

$$\text{Completed} = S_c - RW_5 = 40 - 16 = 24 \text{ points}$$

**Step 2:** Compute actual daily burn rate:

$$R_{actual} = \frac{24}{5} = 4.8 \text{ points/day}$$

**Step 3:** Compare with ideal rate:

$$R_{actual} - R_{ideal} = 4.8 - 4.0 = +0.8 \text{ points/day}$$

> **Answer (c):** The team is burning **0.8 points/day faster** than the ideal — the Sprint is **ahead of schedule** and is on track to complete early unless the Product Owner adds new items.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sprint Backlog vs Other Scrum Elements

This comparison matrix is a **high-frequency KTU exam question**. Master this table.

| Dimension | Product Backlog | Sprint Backlog | Increment |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Owner** | Product Owner | Development Team | Development Team |
| **Time Horizon** | Entire product lifecycle | Single Sprint (1–4 weeks) | End of each Sprint |
| **Granularity** | Coarse (Epics, Features) | Medium (User Stories, Tasks) | Fine (Working software) |
| **Volatility** | Continuously refined | Frozen after Sprint Planning | Frozen after Sprint Review |
| **Visibility** | Visible to all stakeholders | Visible to all stakeholders | Demonstrated at Sprint Review |
| **Commitment Type** | Ordered (not committed) | Committed (forecast for the Sprint) | Done (per DoD) |
| **Created In** | Continuous Backlog Refinement | Sprint Planning Event | Sprint Execution |
| **Discarded When** | Never (until product sunset) | End of Sprint | End of Sprint (or kept as baseline) |
| **KPI Tracked** | Backlog Grooming Rate | Burndown, Velocity | Release Readiness |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Story Point to Task Decomposition

> **Problem:** A User Story *"As a user, I want to reset my password via email"* is pulled into the Sprint Backlog. The team decomposes it into tasks. Given the team's velocity is **30 points/Sprint** and the story is estimated at **5 points**, calculate what percentage of the Sprint this single story consumes.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the percentage of total commitment:

$$\% \text{ consumed} = \frac{\text{Story Points of Story}}{\text{Sprint Velocity}} \times 100$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$\% \text{ consumed} = \frac{5}{30} \times 100 = 16.67\%$$

**Step 3:** For a 10-day Sprint, this means the team spends approximately:

$$\text{Days} = \frac{16.67}{100} \times 10 = 1.67 \text{ days}$$

> **Answer:** The story consumes **~16.67%** of the team's capacity, equivalent to **~1.67 working days**.

---

## 3.5 Capacity Buffer & Commitment Safety Margin

A well-formed Sprint Backlog does **not** consume 100% of the team's calculated capacity. The Scrum Guide implicitly assumes a buffer for:

- Unplanned production support
- Bug triage
- Team ceremonies
- Learning curve on new tech

> **Rule of Thumb (Bolded Standard):** Commit to **70%–80%** of theoretical capacity, leaving a **20%–30% buffer** for the above.

$$\text{Safe Commitment} = 0.75 \times C_{team}$$

This explains why a 240-hour team should not pull 40 tasks of 6 hours each (240/6 = 40) — they should pull only **~30 such tasks**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Sprint Backlog Composition & Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Backlog] --> B[Sprint Planning Event]
    B --> C[Sprint Goal Definition]
    B --> D[Selected PBIs Pulled]
    B --> E[Task Decomposition and Estimation]
    C --> F[Sprint Backlog]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Daily Scrum Inspection]
    G --> H{Any Impediments?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Scrum Master Removes Impediment]
    H -- No --> J[Continue Sprint Work]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Working Increment]
    K --> L[Sprint Review Demo]
    L --> M[Sprint Retrospective]
    M --> N[Next Sprint Planning]
    N --> A
    F --> P[Sprint Burndown Chart Visualization]
    F --> Q[Task Board To Do In Progress Done]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Sprint Backlog Layered Structure

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SBL[Sprint Backlog Artifact]
        SG[Sprint Goal: Single sentence objective]
        subgraph PBI[Selected Product Backlog Items]
            US1[User Story 1 - 5 SP]
            US2[User Story 2 - 8 SP]
            US3[User Story 3 - 3 SP]
            US4[User Story 4 - 5 SP]
        end
        subgraph TASK[Task Breakdown for Each PBI]
            T1A[Task - Backend API - 6h]
            T1B[Task - Frontend UI - 4h]
            T1C[Task - Unit Tests - 3h]
            T2A[Task - DB Schema - 4h]
            T2B[Task - Integration Test - 5h]
        end
    end
    SG --- PBI
    PBI --- TASK
    DoD[Definition of Done: Applied to every task] -.-> TASK
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Sprint Backlog State Transitions

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Draft : Sprint Planning creates initial Sprint Backlog
    Draft --> Committed : Team commits to Sprint Goal and items
    Committed --> InProgress : Day 1 - Execution begins
    InProgress --> Refined : Daily Scrum adds or removes tasks
    Refined --> InProgress : Loop until Sprint end
    InProgress --> Completed : Sprint Review demonstrates Increment
    Completed --> Discarded : Lessons captured in Retrospective
    Discarded --> [*]
    Committed --> Cancelled : Sprint is abnormally terminated
    Cancelled --> [*]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Sprint Backlog in the Scrum System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PO[Product Owner] -->|Prioritizes PBIs| PB[Product Backlog]
    PB -->|Top Items Pulled| SP[Sprint Planning]
    SM[Scrum Master] -->|Facilitates| SP
    DT[Development Team] -->|Forecasts and Plans| SP
    SP -->|Outputs| SBL2[Sprint Backlog]
    SBL2 -->|Inspected Daily| DS[Daily Scrum]
    SBL2 -->|Visualized on| BC[Burndown Chart]
    SBL2 -->|Visualized on| TB[Task Board]
    DT -->|Executes Tasks| SBL2
    SBL2 -->|Produces| INC[Increment]
    INC -->|Demonstrated| SR[Sprint Review]
    SR -->|Feedback| PO
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagrams**
> Notice that the **Sprint Backlog is the central hub** connecting planning, execution, tracking, and delivery. Every Scrum ceremony either feeds into it or inspects it. This centrality is why KTU questions often use the Sprint Backlog as a pivot point to test your understanding of the entire Scrum framework.

---

## 4.5 Textual Schematic: Task Board Layout

```
+--------------------+--------------------+--------------------+--------------------+
|     TO DO          |   IN PROGRESS      |   TESTING          |     DONE           |
+--------------------+--------------------+--------------------+--------------------+
| US-101: Card UI    | US-100: Tokenize   | US-099: Login API  | US-098: Logout     |
|   T1: Wireframe    |   T1: Encryption   |   T1: Integration  |   T1: Backend      |
|   T2: CSS Styling  |   T2: Storage      |   T2: Security     |   T2: Frontend     |
|                    |                    |                    |                    |
| US-102: Email Notif|                    |                    | US-097: Header Fix |
|   T1: SMTP Setup   |                    |                    |   T1: Responsive   |
+--------------------+--------------------+--------------------+--------------------+
| Total: 18 SP       | Total: 13 SP       | Total: 8 SP        | Total: 21 SP       |
+--------------------+--------------------+--------------------+--------------------+
```

This layout is **directly transcribable into KTU answer sheets** for 14-mark diagram questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Q: Define Sprint Backlog. List its three main components.**
