- Code is deployed to production but kept dormant via configuration flags
- Enables "decoupling deployment from release" — a key DevOps principle

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Formula Reference Table** — Key metrics and equations for Continuous Deployment calculations.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|------------------|------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | **Deployment Frequency (DF)** | $DF = \dfrac{N_{deployments}}{T_{period}}$ | Number of deployments per unit time | deployments/day |
| 2 | **Lead Time for Changes (LT)** | $LT = T_{production} - T_{commit}$ | Time from code commit to production | minutes/hours |
| 3 | **Change Failure Rate (CFR)** | $CFR = \dfrac{N_{failed\_changes}}{N_{total\_changes}} \times 100$ | Percentage of changes causing failures in production | % |
| 4 | **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{recovery}}{N_{incidents}}$ | Average time to restore service after failure | minutes |
| 5 | **Deployment Success Rate (DSR)** | $DSR = \dfrac{N_{successful\_deploys}}{N_{total\_deploys}} \times 100$ | Reliability of automated deployments | % |
| 6 | **Test Coverage (TC)** | $TC = \dfrac{L_{covered}}{L_{total}} \times 100$ | Percentage of code exercised by tests | % |
| 7 | **Rollback Time (RT)** | $RT = T_{restore} - T_{failure\_detected}$ | Time elapsed from failure detection to system restoration | seconds/minutes |
| 8 | **Pipeline Efficiency (PE)** | $PE = \dfrac{T_{value\_added}}{T_{total\_pipeline}} \times 100$ | Proportion of time spent on productive activities | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics form the **DORA Four Key Metrics** (Deployment Frequency, Lead Time, Change Failure Rate, MTTR) which are globally recognized benchmarks for DevOps and Continuous Deployment maturity.

## 2.4 Continuous Delivery vs. Continuous Deployment

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse these two terms. They are NOT the same.

| Aspect | Continuous Delivery | Continuous Deployment |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Production Trigger** | Manual approval required | Fully automatic |
| **Human Gate** | Yes (release manager click) | No (zero-touch) |
| **Risk Tolerance** | Moderate | Low (requires extensive testing) |
| **Deployment Frequency** | Daily to weekly | Multiple times per day |
| **Maturity Level** | Intermediate | Advanced (highest DevOps maturity) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Scenario: KTU Numerical Problem Walkthrough

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU often tests the DORA metrics and pipeline efficiency calculations. Practice deriving these step-by-step.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
A software company practicing Continuous Deployment made **240 deployments** over a period of **30 days**. Out of these, **18 deployments** caused production failures that required rollback. The total time spent on recovery across all incidents was **540 minutes**. The pipeline runs for a total of **720 hours per month**, of which **540 hours** are spent on value-adding activities (coding, testing, deploying). Calculate:
- (i) Deployment Frequency
- (ii) Change Failure Rate
- (iii) Mean Time to Recovery
- (iv) Pipeline Efficiency

### 3.1.2 Given Data Extraction

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{deployments} &= 240 \\
T_{period} &= 30 \text{ days} \\
N_{failed\_changes} &= 18 \\
\sum T_{recovery} &= 540 \text{ minutes} \\
T_{total\_pipeline} &= 720 \text{ hours} \\
T_{value\_added} &= 540 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Part (i) — Deployment Frequency

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF &= \frac{N_{deployments}}{T_{period}} \\
&= \frac{240 \text{ deployments}}{30 \text{ days}} \\
&= 8 \text{ deployments/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $DF = 8$ deployments per day — indicating an elite-performing CD pipeline.

### 3.1.4 Part (ii) — Change Failure Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
CFR &= \frac{N_{failed\_changes}}{N_{total\_changes}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{18}{240} \times 100 \\
&= 0.075 \times 100 \\
&= 7.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $CFR = 7.5\%$ — well within the elite performer benchmark (0%–15%).

### 3.1.5 Part (iii) — Mean Time to Recovery

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= \frac{\sum T_{recovery}}{N_{incidents}} \\
&= \frac{540 \text{ minutes}}{18 \text{ incidents}} \\
&= 30 \text{ minutes/incident}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $MTTR = 30$ minutes — indicates a mature incident response capability.

### 3.1.6 Part (iv) — Pipeline Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE &= \frac{T_{value\_added}}{T_{total\_pipeline}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{540}{720} \times 100 \\
&= 0.75 \times 100 \\
&= 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $PE = 75\%$ — a healthy pipeline with 25% time spent on waiting, manual handoffs, or rework.

### 3.1.7 DORA Performance Classification Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Reference:** DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment) classifies organizations into performance tiers based on the four key metrics.

| Metric | Elite | High | Medium | Low |
|--------|-------|------|--------|-----|
| **Deployment Frequency** | On-demand (multiple/day) | Weekly to monthly | Monthly to biannually | > 6 months |
| **Lead Time** | < 1 hour | 1 day – 1 week | 1 week – 1 month | > 1 month |
| **Change Failure Rate** | 0% – 15% | 16% – 30% | 31% – 45% | > 45% |
| **MTTR** | < 1 hour | < 1 day | < 1 week | > 1 week |

**Our Solution Classification:** Based on the derived values, this organization qualifies as an **Elite Performer** in Continuous Deployment maturity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Continuous Deployment End-to-End Pipeline Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete automated flow from a developer's code commit to production release and feedback loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer Code Commit] --> B[Git Repository Webhook Trigger]
    B --> C[Automated Build Stage]
    C --> D[Unit Test Execution]
    D --> E{Tests Passed?}
    E -- No --> F[Notify Developer - Fix Required]
    F --> A
    E -- Yes --> G[Integration Test Stage]
    G --> H[Static Code Analysis]
    H --> I[Security Vulnerability Scan]
    I --> J[Deploy to Staging Environment]
    J --> K[Automated UAT Suite]
    K --> L{All Quality Gates Cleared?}
    L -- No --> M[Auto Rollback to Last Stable]
    M --> F
    L -- Yes --> N[Blue-Green Production Switch]
    N --> O[Canary Release to 5 Percent Users]
    O --> P{Health Metrics OK?}
    P -- No --> Q[Instant Traffic Reroute to Blue]
    Q --> M
    P -- Yes --> R[Full Production Rollout]
    R --> S[Real-Time Monitoring and Logging]
    S --> T[User Feedback Collection]
    T --> U[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    U --> A
```

## 4.2 Deployment Strategy Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StrA[Blue-Green Deployment]
        A1[Blue Environment - Live Traffic] -->|Switch Router| A2[Green Environment - New Version]
        A2 -->|Verify| A3[Full Traffic to Green]
    end
    subgraph StrB[Canary Release]
        B1[Version V1 - 95 Percent Users] -->|Monitor| B2[Version V2 - 5 Percent Users]
        B2 -->|Healthy| B3[Expand to 50 Percent]
        B3 -->|Healthy| B4[Full 100 Percent Rollout]
    end
    subgraph StrC[Rolling Deployment]
        C1[Server 1 V1] -->|Update| C2[Server 2 V1]
        C2 -->|Update| C3[Server 3 V1]
        C3 -->|Update| C4[All Servers V2]
    end
```

## 4.3 Continuous Deployment Maturity Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Level 1 - Manual Deployment] --> P2[Level 2 - Continuous Integration]
    P2 --> P3[Level 3 - Continuous Delivery]
    P3 --> P4[Level 4 - Continuous Deployment]
    P4 --> P5[Level 5 - Autonomous Operations and AI-Driven Deployment]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Continuous Deployment and explain how it differs from Continuous Delivery.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Continuous Deployment (CD) is a software release practice where every code change that passes the automated test suite is **automatically deployed to the production environment** without any manual approval or human intervention. It is the final, fully-automated stage of the CI/CD pipeline.

> **Differentiation (1 mark):** The key difference is the **human gate**. In **Continuous Delivery**, the code is built, tested, and packaged so that it is *always ready* for production release, but a **manual approval** (typically by a release manager) is required to trigger the actual deployment. In **Continuous Deployment**, this manual step is eliminated — if all automated tests pass, the code goes live automatically. Continuous Deployment requires a higher level of test coverage, monitoring maturity, and organizational trust in automation.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any three deployment strategies used in Continuous Deployment and briefly explain one.**

**Model Answer:**

Three deployment strategies used in Continuous Deployment are:
1. **Blue-Green Deployment** — Two identical production environments (Blue and Green) with traffic switching
2. **Canary Release** — Gradual rollout to a small percentage of users before full deployment
3. **Rolling Deployment** — Incremental update across multiple servers with zero downtime

> **Explanation of Blue-Green Deployment (1 mark):** In this strategy, the current live version runs in the **Blue environment**, while the new version is deployed to the **Green environment**. Once the Green environment is verified through automated tests, a router switches all user traffic from Blue to Green instantly. If any issues arise, traffic can be switched back to Blue, providing **instant rollback capability** and zero-downtime deployments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Internal choice is provided between Question A and Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Continuous Deployment pipeline in detail. Describe each stage from code commit to production release with suitable diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the benefits and challenges of implementing Continuous Deployment in an IT project. How does it impact project timelines, quality, and team productivity? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question A

### Part (a) — Continuous Deployment Pipeline Architecture (7 Marks)

A Continuous Deployment pipeline is an **automated sequence of stages** through which every code change traverses from the developer's local machine to the live production environment. The architecture consists of the following interconnected stages:

**Stage 1: Source Code Management and Commit (1 mark)**
- The developer writes code locally and pushes it to a shared version control repository (e.g., **Git**, **GitHub**, **GitLab**, or **Bitbucket**)
- A **webhook** automatically triggers the pipeline upon detecting a new commit
- This eliminates manual initiation and ensures every change is evaluated

**Stage 2: Build Automation (1 mark)**
- The CI server (e.g., **Jenkins**, **GitLab CI**, **CircleCI**) compiles the source code
- Dependencies are resolved using package managers (Maven, npm, pip)
- A deployable artifact (JAR, Docker image, WAR) is produced
- Build failures immediately notify the developer via email or chat tools

**Stage 3: Automated Testing — The Quality Gate (2 marks)**
This is the **most critical stage** in CD and includes multiple test layers:
- **Unit Tests:** Individual functions/methods tested in isolation
- **Integration Tests:** Inter-module communication verified
- **Regression Tests:** Ensures new changes don't break existing functionality
- **Performance Tests:** Load and stress testing under simulated conditions
- **Security Scans:** Static Application Security Testing (SAST) and dependency vulnerability checks
- If any test fails, the pipeline **halts** and the change is rejected automatically

**Stage 4: Staging Deployment and Acceptance Testing (1 mark)**
- The artifact is deployed to a **staging environment** that mirrors production
- Automated User Acceptance Tests (UAT) and end-to-end tests are executed
- Feature flags may be used to control visibility of new functionality

**Stage 5: Production Deployment (1 mark)**
- Upon passing all quality gates, the code is automatically released to production
- **Deployment strategies** (Blue-Green, Canary, Rolling) are employed to minimize risk
- The traffic is gradually or instantly shifted to the new version

**Stage 6: Monitoring, Observability, and Feedback (1 mark)**
- Real-time monitoring tools (Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog) track application health
- Automated alerts trigger rollback if anomalies are detected
- User feedback and business metrics feed back into the next development cycle

> **Reference:** The architectural flow is illustrated in the Mermaid pipeline diagram provided in Section 4.1 of these notes.

### Part (b) — Benefits and Challenges of Continuous Deployment (7 Marks)

**Benefits (4 marks):**

1. **Accelerated Time-to-Market (1 mark):** Features reach end users within minutes or hours instead of weeks. Organizations practicing CD can deploy **multiple times per day**, giving them a significant competitive advantage.

2. **Reduced Deployment Risk (1 mark):** Since changes are **small and incremental**, they are easier to test, debug, and roll back. A single deployment contains fewer modifications, meaning fewer potential points of failure.

3. **Improved Software Quality (1 mark):** Continuous feedback loops and automated testing catch defects early in the development cycle. The cost of fixing a bug in production is estimated to be **6× higher** than fixing it during development.

4. **Enhanced Developer Productivity and Customer Satisfaction (1 mark):** Developers spend less time on deployment logistics and more time on innovation. End users receive rapid bug fixes and feature updates, improving satisfaction and retention.

**Challenges (3 marks):**

1. **High Initial Investment (1 mark):** Setting up a robust CD pipeline requires significant investment in **infrastructure, tooling, and training**. Organizations may struggle with the cultural shift from traditional release management.

2. **Requires Comprehensive Test Automation (1 mark):** Without high-quality automated test suites (coverage typically **≥ 80%**), the risk of deploying broken code to production increases dramatically. "Garbage in, garbage out" applies directly to CD.

3. **Organizational and Cultural Resistance (1 mark):** Teams accustomed to manual release processes may resist the loss of control. Security and compliance teams may have concerns about audit trails and governance in fully automated deployments.

**Impact on IT Project Management:**

| Dimension | Impact |
|-----------|--------|
| **Timelines** | Compressed release cycles; project milestones align with continuous delivery cadences |
| **Quality** | Improved through automated testing and immediate feedback |
| **Team Productivity** | Increased focus on value-adding activities; reduced context-switching |
| **Risk** | Lower per-deployment risk; aggregated risk management through monitoring |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 — Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the Blue-Green Deployment and Canary Release strategies in detail. Compare their advantages and disadvantages in the context of Continuous Deployment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A company practices Continuous Deployment. Over 90 days, they made 900 deployments. 45 deployments caused production failures. Total recovery time was 1800 minutes. The total pipeline time was 1500 hours, with 1200 hours of value-added time. Compute: (i) Deployment Frequency, (ii) Change Failure Rate, (iii) MTTR, (iv) Pipeline Efficiency. Classify the company as per DORA metrics. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Question B

### Part (a) — Blue-Green Deployment vs. Canary Release (7 Marks)

**Blue-Green Deployment (3.5 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Blue-Green Deployment is a release strategy that maintains **two identical production environments** — the **Blue environment** (currently serving live user traffic) and the **Green environment** (idle, where the new version is deployed). Once the Green environment passes all tests, a **load balancer or router switches** all user traffic from Blue to Green instantaneously.

> **Advantages (1 mark):** (i) **Zero downtime** during deployment; (ii) **Instant rollback** capability by switching back to Blue; (iii) Simplifies testing in a production-like environment.

> **Disadvantages (1 mark):** (i) **Doubles infrastructure costs** (two production environments must run simultaneously); (ii) Database schema migrations can be complex; (iii) Not suitable for applications with large stateful sessions.

**Canary Release (3.5 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Canary Release is a progressive deployment strategy where the new version is released to a **small subset of users (typically 5% to 10%)** — the "canaries" — before being rolled out to the entire user base. Performance and error metrics of this subset are closely monitored, and if no regressions are detected, the rollout expands gradually (e.g., 10% → 25% → 50% → 100%).

> **Advantages (1 mark):** (i) **Real-user validation** with minimal blast radius; (ii) Early detection of issues with specific user segments (devices, geographies); (iii) Cost-effective compared to Blue-Green.

> **Disadvantages (1 mark):** (i) **Slower full rollout** compared to instant switching; (ii) Requires sophisticated **traffic-splitting infrastructure**; (iii) Monitoring and observability must be highly granular to detect issues in the small canary cohort.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Blue-Green | Canary Release |
|-----------|------------|----------------|
| Rollout Speed | Instant | Gradual |
| Infrastructure Cost | High (2x) | Moderate |
| Rollback Speed | Instant | Fast (revert canary) |
| Risk Exposure | Low (isolated) | Low (small user subset) |
| Best Use Case | Critical applications | Feature experimentation |

### Part (b) — DORA Metrics Computation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{deployments} &= 900 \\
T_{period} &= 90 \text{ days} \\
N_{failed\_changes} &= 45 \\
\sum T_{recovery} &= 1800 \text{ minutes} \\
T_{total\_pipeline} &= 1500 \text{ hours} \\
T_{value\_added} &= 1200 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Deployment Frequency (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DF &= \frac{N_{deployments}}{T_{period}} \\
&= \frac{900 \text{ deployments}}{90 \text{ days}} \\
&= 10 \text{ deployments/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Change Failure Rate (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CFR &= \frac{N_{failed\_changes}}{N_{total\_changes}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{45}{900} \times 100 \\
&= 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) MTTR (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTR &= \frac{\sum T_{recovery}}{N_{incidents}} \\
&= \frac{1800 \text{ minutes}}{45 \text{ incidents}} \\
&= 40 \text{ minutes/incident}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Pipeline Efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PE &= \frac{T_{value\_added}}{T_{total\_pipeline}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1200}{1500} \times 100 \\
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**DORA Classification (1 mark):**

| Metric | Value | Tier |
|--------|-------|------|
| DF | 10/day | **Elite** (on-demand) |
| CFR | 5% | **Elite** (0–15%) |
| MTTR | 40 min | **High** (< 1 hour, close to Elite) |

**Conclusion:** The company is an **Elite Performer** in Continuous Deployment maturity based on DORA benchmarks. The 80% pipeline efficiency indicates minimal waste and a well-optimized CD process.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Continuous Delivery with Continuous Deployment** — Most common 2-mark deduction. Remember: CD = zero manual gate; Continuous Delivery = manual approval gate.
> 2. **Formula Unit Inconsistency** — When calculating Deployment Frequency, ensure both numerator and denominator use consistent time units. MTTR and Lead Time should be expressed in the same unit (minutes or hours).
> 3. **Missing Diagram** — For pipeline architecture questions, KTU expects a **labeled diagram**. Skipping the diagram costs at least 2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting DORA Classification** — Numerical problems often expect you to classify the organization as Elite/High/Medium/Low. Omitting this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring Challenges** — When asked about benefits, many students forget to discuss **challenges**. KTU questions are designed to test balanced understanding.
> 6. **Pipeline Stage Misordering** — Ensure stages are in the correct logical order: Commit → Build → Test → Stage → Deploy → Monitor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Continuous Deployment (Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Continuous Deployment = Every passing build is **automatically** released to production — no human gate required.
- **Pipeline Stages (in order):** Commit → Build → Unit Test → Integration Test → Security Scan → Stage → UAT → **Production Deploy** → Monitor → Feedback.
- **Predecessor Stages:** Continuous Integration (CI) → Continuous Delivery → **Continuous Deployment (CD)**.
- **Key Difference:**
   - Continuous Delivery = Manual approval to deploy
   - Continuous Deployment = Fully automatic deployment
- **DORA Four Key Metrics:**
   - **DF** (Deployment Frequency) → How often
   - **LT** (Lead Time) → How fast
   - **CFR** (Change Failure Rate) → How risky
   - **MTTR** (Mean Time to Recovery) → How resilient
- **Deployment Strategies to Memorize:**
   - **Blue-Green:** Two parallel environments, instant switch
   - **Canary:** Small percentage rollout (5–10%)
   - **Rolling:** Incremental server-by-server update
   - **Feature Flags:** Decouple deployment from release
- **Required Test Coverage:** **≥ 80%** is the industry benchmark for safe CD.
- **Pipeline Efficiency Formula:** $PE = \dfrac{T_{value\_added}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ — ideal is **≥ 75%**.
- **Benefits:** Speed, quality, risk reduction, productivity, customer satisfaction.
- **Challenges:** Tooling cost, test automation maturity, cultural resistance, compliance concerns.
- **Maturity Pyramid:** Manual → CI → Continuous Delivery → **Continuous Deployment** → Autonomous/AI-driven operations.
- **KTU Numerical Focus:** Be prepared to compute all four DORA metrics and classify the organization into performance tiers.
- **Examiner's Keyword Triggers:** Always use the terms "automated pipeline," "quality gate," "rollback strategy," and "feedback loop" in descriptive answers for higher marks.
- **Tooling Examples (for reference):** Jenkins, GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, CircleCI, ArgoCD, Spinnaker, Docker, Kubernetes, Prometheus, Grafana.
- **One-Line Definition for 2-mark Questions:** *"Continuous Deployment is the DevOps practice of automatically releasing every code change that passes automated tests directly to the production environment, enabling rapid, reliable, and risk-mitigated software delivery."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automated Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automated Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automated Monitoring** in IT Project Management refers to the systematic use of software tools, scripts, dashboards, and algorithmic processes to continuously collect, measure, analyze, and report on project performance metrics—**without requiring manual, repetitive human intervention**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) curriculum, automated monitoring encompasses the real-time observation of project health parameters such as schedule adherence, budget consumption, resource utilization, defect density, build success rates, code quality, and stakeholder communication latency.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Automated Monitoring is the deployment of programmable, rule-based, and AI-augmented observability systems that gather project telemetry data continuously, evaluate it against predefined thresholds, and trigger corrective workflows or stakeholder notifications in near real-time.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are driving a modern car equipped with a **digital dashboard**. As you drive:
- The **speedometer** instantly tells you if you are exceeding the speed limit.
- The **fuel gauge** warns you before the tank runs dry.
- The **engine temperature sensor** alerts you if the car is overheating.
- The **GPS navigation system** automatically reroutes you if you deviate from the planned path.

You don't have to manually check the engine, the wheels, or the fuel pipe every minute—the car's **embedded sensors and automation systems** do it for you. If something is wrong, the car beeps or flashes a warning light so you can take corrective action.

**Automated Monitoring in IT Project Management works exactly the same way:**
- The **project** is the car.
- The **software sensors** are monitoring tools like Jira, Jenkins, Nagios, Grafana, Power BI, or Azure DevOps.
- The **dashboard** is the project status report.
- The **warning beeps** are automated alerts (email, SMS, Slack notifications).
- The **driver** is the Project Manager, who is notified only when attention is required.

This frees the Project Manager from "constantly looking under the hood" and allows them to focus on **strategic decision-making**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology in Bold

The following are the standard **KTU-recommended** key performance indicators (KPIs) tracked under automated monitoring:

- **SPI (Schedule Performance Index):** $\text{SPI} = \dfrac{\text{Earned Value (EV)}}{\text{Planned Value (PV)}}$
- **CPI (Cost Performance Index):** $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{Earned Value (EV)}}{\text{Actual Cost (AC)}}$
- **Defect Density (DD):** $\text{DD} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size of Module (KLOC or FP)}}$
- **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD):** Average time to discover a defect.
- **Mean Time To Resolve (MTTR):** Average time to fix a defect.
- **Build Success Rate (BSR):** $\text{BSR} = \dfrac{\text{Successful Builds}}{\text{Total Builds}} \times 100\%$
- **Code Coverage (CC):** $\text{CC} = \dfrac{\text{Lines of Code Tested}}{\text{Total Lines of Code}} \times 100\%$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Automated monitoring is **NOT** just about tracking—it is a closed-loop system involving **Observe → Analyze → Alert → Act → Re-observe**. The "act" phase often triggers automated workflows (e.g., auto-rollback of failed deployments).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Automated Monitoring

Automated monitoring operates on a layered architecture. Below is the structured logic used in KTU Board examination answers:

**Layer 1 — Data Collection Layer (Sensors)**
- **What it does:** Gathers raw metrics from project artifacts (commits, tickets, builds, deployments).
- **Tools:** Jira REST API, Git hooks, Jenkins log parsers, SonarQube scanners, AWS CloudWatch.
- **Why it matters:** Eliminates human error in data entry. Provides a **single source of truth**.

**Layer 2 — Data Aggregation & Storage Layer**
- **What it does:** Normalizes, cleans, and stores the raw data in a centralized warehouse.
- **Tools:** Elasticsearch, InfluxDB, Prometheus, SQL data marts, BigQuery.
- **How it works:** Uses ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines to convert heterogeneous project data into a uniform schema.

**Layer 3 — Analysis & Rule Engine Layer**
- **What it does:** Applies predefined thresholds, statistical models, and machine learning algorithms.
- **Examples:** "If CPI < 0.9 for 3 consecutive days, trigger a 'Budget Risk' alert."
- **Why it matters:** Converts raw numbers into **actionable intelligence**.

**Layer 4 — Visualization & Reporting Layer**
- **What it does:** Renders dashboards, heat-maps, burn-down charts, and trend graphs.
- **Tools:** Grafana, Power BI, Tableau, Kibana, native Jira dashboards.
- **Why it matters:** Cognitive load on stakeholders is reduced—**a picture is worth a thousand rows of data**.

**Layer 5 — Alerting & Action Layer**
- **What it does:** Sends notifications through email, SMS, Slack, Microsoft Teams, or PagerDuty.
- **Advanced Feature:** Auto-remediation (e.g., restart a crashed server, roll back a faulty deployment).

**Layer 6 — Feedback & Continuous Improvement Layer**
- **What it does:** Retrospectively evaluates whether the monitoring system itself is effective.
- **Concept:** Known as "**Monitoring the Monitor**" or **Meta-Monitoring**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Acronym | Full Form | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| EV | Earned Value | Budgeted cost of work actually performed | Currency (₹ / $) |
| PV | Planned Value | Budgeted cost of work scheduled | Currency (₹ / $) |
| AC | Actual Cost | Real cost incurred for work performed | Currency (₹ / $) |
| SPI | Schedule Performance Index | $\text{EV} / \text{PV}$ | $\vert \text{SPI} \vert \geq 0$; ideal = 1.0 |
| CPI | Cost Performance Index | $\text{EV} / \text{AC}$ | $\vert \text{CPI} \vert \geq 0$; ideal = 1.0 |
| CV | Cost Variance | $\text{EV} - \text{AC}$ | Currency; positive = under budget |
| SV | Schedule Variance | $\text{EV} - \text{PV}$ | Currency; positive = ahead of schedule |
| EAC | Estimate At Completion | $\text{AC} + \dfrac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{CPI}}$ | Currency |
| ETC | Estimate To Complete | $\text{EAC} - \text{AC}$ | Currency |
| TCPI | To-Complete Performance Index | $\dfrac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}}$ | Ratio |
| DD | Defect Density | $\dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Size}}$ | $\text{per KLOC}$ or $\text{per FP}$ |
| BSR | Build Success Rate | $\dfrac{\text{Successful Builds}}{\text{Total Builds}} \times 100$ | Percentage (0–100%) |
| MTTD | Mean Time To Detect | $\dfrac{\sum \text{Detection Time}}{\text{Number of Defects}}$ | Hours / Days |
| MTTR | Mean Time To Resolve | $\dfrac{\sum \text{Resolution Time}}{\text{Number of Defects}}$ | Hours / Days |
| SLA | Service Level Agreement | Uptime commitment (e.g., 99.9%) | Percentage |
| RAG | Red, Amber, Green | Traffic-light status indicator | Categorical |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always quote the **threshold** of an indicator when defining it. For example: *"An SPI value below 0.9 indicates a schedule slippage warning."* Examiners reward contextual numerical benchmarks.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the IT industry, automated monitoring is the **backbone of DevOps, SRE (Site Reliability Engineering), and Agile project management**. Practical utilities include:

1. **Predictive Risk Management:** ML models trained on historical EV data forecast future CPI/SPI trends 2 weeks ahead.
2. **Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (CI/CD):** Jenkins monitors code commits and triggers automated builds + tests.
3. **Cloud Cost Governance:** AWS Cost Explorer + CloudWatch alarms stop overspending in real-time.
4. **SLA Compliance:** Telecom and SaaS companies automate uptime monitoring to honour customer SLAs (e.g., 99.99% guaranteed).
5. **Regulatory Compliance:** Banking and healthcare IT projects use automated audit logs to comply with **RBI, HIPAA, GDPR, and ISO 27001** standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: How Automated Monitoring Calculates a Composite Project Health Score

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to **derive the composite project health index** using multiple KPIs. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

**Step 1 — Normalize each KPI to a 0–1 scale (where 1 = perfect).**

For ratio-based KPIs (SPI, CPI), use:

$$
N_{\text{SPI}} = \min\left(\frac{\text{SPI}}{1.0},\ 1.0\right)
$$

This caps the normalized value at 1.0, so an SPI of 1.2 still scores 1.0 (no over-rewarding for over-performance in monitoring logic).

**Explanation:** We divide the actual SPI by the ideal target (1.0) so that meeting the target exactly produces a normalized value of 1.0. Anything below the target scales down proportionally. The $\min(\cdot, 1.0)$ function prevents an over-performing project from skewing the composite score.

**Step 2 — Normalize percentage KPIs (BSR, CC, SLA).**

$$
N_{\text{BSR}} = \frac{\text{BSR}_{\text{actual}}}{100}
$$

$$
N_{\text{CC}} = \frac{\text{CC}_{\text{actual}}}{100}
$$

**Explanation:** Since these are already percentages, we simply convert to a decimal fraction. The boundary condition is $0 \le N \le 1$.

**Step 3 — Apply weighted aggregation to compute the Composite Health Index (CHI).**

$$
\text{CHI} = w_1 N_{\text{SPI}} + w_2 N_{\text{CPI}} + w_3 N_{\text{BSR}} + w_4 N_{\text{CC}} + w_5 N_{\text{SLA}}
$$

where the weights satisfy:

$$
w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 + w_5 = 1.0
$$

**Explanation:** Each KPI contributes to the overall health according to its organizational priority. For example, a safety-critical aerospace project may assign $w_4 = 0.5$ to Code Coverage, while a cost-driven outsourcing project may assign $w_2 = 0.5$ to CPI.

**Step 4 — Map the CHI to a RAG (Red, Amber, Green) status.**

$$
\text{Status} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Green} & \text{if } \text{CHI} \ge 0.85 \\
\text{Amber} & \text{if } 0.70 \le \text{CHI} < 0.85 \\
\text{Red} & \text{if } \text{CHI} < 0.70
\end{cases}
$$

**Explanation:** A 3-tier RAG indicator is industry standard because it provides instant cognitive recognition to executives reviewing a dashboard.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Style):** A project has the following metrics at the end of Week 8:

$$
\text{SPI} = 0.92, \quad \text{CPI} = 0.88, \quad \text{BSR} = 95\%, \quad \text{CC} = 78\%, \quad \text{SLA} = 99.5\%
$$

The organization uses weights:

$$
w_1 = 0.20,\ w_2 = 0.30,\ w_3 = 0.15,\ w_4 = 0.20,\ w_5 = 0.15
$$

Compute the Composite Health Index and determine the RAG status.

**Solution:**

**Step (i)** Normalize SPI:

$$
N_{\text{SPI}} = \min\!\left(\frac{0.92}{1.0},\ 1.0\right) = 0.92
$$

**Step (ii)** Normalize CPI:

$$
N_{\text{CPI}} = \min\!\left(\frac{0.88}{1.0},\ 1.0\right) = 0.88
$$

**Step (iii)** Normalize BSR:

$$
N_{\text{BSR}} = \frac{95}{100} = 0.95
$$

**Step (iv)** Normalize CC:

$$
N_{\text{CC}} = \frac{78}{100} = 0.78
$$

**Step (v)** Normalize SLA:

$$
N_{\text{SLA}} = \frac{99.5}{100} = 0.995
$$

**Step (vi)** Compute CHI:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CHI} &= (0.20)(0.92) + (0.30)(0.88) + (0.15)(0.95) + (0.20)(0.78) + (0.15)(0.995) \\
&= 0.184 + 0.264 + 0.1425 + 0.156 + 0.14925 \\
&= 0.89575
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (vii)** Determine status:

$$
\text{CHI} = 0.89575 \ge 0.85 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Status} = \textbf{Green}
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite a slightly low CPI (0.88) and modest code coverage (78%), the project is **Green** because the high build success rate and strong SLA compliance pull the composite score above the 0.85 threshold.

### 3.3 Derivation of the EAC Formula

The Estimate At Completion is critical in monitoring budget overruns.

**Starting point:** Assuming the project will continue at its current cost efficiency (CPI):

$$
\text{Cost Efficiency Ratio} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} = \text{CPI}
$$

**Step 1 — Remaining work in budget terms:**

$$
\text{Remaining Budget} = \text{BAC} - \text{EV}
$$

**Step 2 — Project remaining work to actual cost using current CPI:**

$$
\text{ETC} = \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{CPI}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total project cost (EAC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAC} &= \text{AC} + \text{ETC} \\
&= \text{AC} + \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{CPI}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\text{EAC} = \text{AC} + \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{CPI}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for EAC:** This formula assumes **future cost performance mirrors past performance**. If management commits to corrective action (e.g., hiring cheaper resources), a different formula using $\text{TCPI}$ is used instead.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Automated Monitoring Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **six-layer functional architecture** of an automated project monitoring system, mapping the data flow from raw collection to stakeholder dashboards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Source 1: Jira Tickets] --> B1[Layer 1: Data Collection Sensors]
    A2[Source 2: Git Repositories] --> B1
    A3[Source 3: Jenkins CI Builds] --> B1
    A4[Source 4: Cloud Cost Logs] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Layer 2: Aggregation and ETL Pipeline]
    C1 --> D1[Layer 3: Rule Engine and ML Analytics]
    D1 --> E1[Layer 4: Dashboard Visualization]
    D1 --> F1[Layer 5: Alerting and Auto Remediation]
    E1 --> G1[Stakeholder 1: Project Manager]
    E1 --> G2[Stakeholder 2: Sponsor and Client]
    F1 --> H1[Email and SMS Channel]
    F1 --> H2[Slack and Teams Channel]
    F1 --> H3[PagerDuty On Call Engineer]
    E1 --> I1[Layer 6: Meta Monitoring and Feedback Loop]
    I1 --> D1
```

### 4.2 RAG Status Decision Flow

The diagram below shows the rule-based decision logic an automated monitoring engine uses to assign a project's RAG (Red, Amber, Green) status.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J1[Start: Read Project KPIs] --> K1{Compute CHI}
    K1 --> K2{CHI greater or equal 0.85}
    K2 -- Yes --> L1[Status equals Green]
    K2 -- No --> K3{CHI between 0.70 and 0.85}
    K3 -- Yes --> L2[Status equals Amber]
    K3 -- No --> L3[Status equals Red]
    L1 --> M1[Display on Executive Dashboard]
    L2 --> M1
    L3 --> N1[Trigger Risk Mitigation Workflow]
    N1 --> M1
```

### 4.3 Automated Alert Escalation Matrix

This block diagram shows how an alert escalates from a junior team member to senior management based on severity and time elapsed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Defect Detected] --> P1{Severity Level}
    P1 -- Low --> Q1[Notify Developer via Slack]
    P1 -- Medium --> Q2[Notify Team Lead and PM]
    P1 -- High --> Q3[Notify PM and Sponsor]
    P1 -- Critical --> Q4[Page On Call and CIO]
    Q1 --> R1{Resolved within 4 hours}
    R1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 --> R2{Resolved within 8 hours}
    R2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 --> R3{Resolved within 2 hours}
    R3 -- No --> Q4
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define Automated Monitoring. List any four tools used for automated project monitoring.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Automated Monitoring is the use of software tools, scripts, dashboards, and rule-based engines to continuously collect, measure, analyze, and report project performance metrics without requiring manual, repetitive human intervention. It provides real-time visibility into schedule, cost, quality, and risk parameters, and triggers automated alerts when thresholds are breached.

**Four Tools (1 Mark — ¼ × 4):**

1. **Jira** — Tracks tickets, sprint velocity, and burn-down charts.
2. **Jenkins** — Monitors CI/CD build success rates and deployment frequency.
3. **Grafana** — Renders real-time dashboards from time-series data.
4. **Power BI** — Generates executive-level business intelligence reports.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*What is the meaning of RAG status in automated monitoring? Mention the threshold values used in industry for each colour.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Meaning (1 Mark):**
RAG stands for **Red, Amber, Green**. It is a traffic-light indicator used in automated monitoring dashboards to instantly communicate the health of a project. Green indicates healthy progress, Amber indicates caution, and Red indicates critical risk requiring immediate intervention.

**Thresholds (2 Marks):**
The standard industry thresholds, based on a Composite Health Index (CHI) score between 0 and 1, are:

$$
\text{Green:}\ \text{CHI} \ge 0.85, \quad \text{Amber:}\ 0.70 \le \text{CHI} < 0.85, \quad \text{Red:}\ \text{CHI} < 0.70
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write *"RAG is used in project management"* without defining the **three colours and their corresponding numerical thresholds**. Always state the CHI numerical range. **No threshold = 1 mark deducted.**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **six-layer functional architecture** of an automated project monitoring system with a neat block diagram. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** The following data is collected for a software project at the end of Month 5:
$\text{EV} = 45\ \text{lakhs},\ \text{PV} = 50\ \text{lakhs},\ \text{AC} = 55\ \text{lakhs},\ \text{BAC} = 120\ \text{lakhs}$

Calculate SPI, CPI, CV, SV, EAC, ETC, and TCPI. Interpret the project status. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

The six-layer architecture of an automated project monitoring system is as follows:

**Layer 1: Data Collection Layer (Sensors) (1 Mark)**
This layer uses APIs, agents, and webhooks to collect raw data from project artifacts. Examples include Jira REST API for ticket data, Git hooks for commit data, and Jenkins log parsers for build data. Its purpose is to eliminate human error in data entry and provide a single source of truth.

**Layer 2: Aggregation and Storage Layer (1 Mark)**
This layer normalizes the heterogeneous data and stores it in a centralized data warehouse using ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines. Tools used include Elasticsearch, InfluxDB, and Prometheus.

**Layer 3: Rule Engine and Analytics Layer (1 Mark)**
This layer applies predefined thresholds, statistical models, and machine learning algorithms to the normalized data. For example, a rule may state: *"If CPI < 0.9 for three consecutive days, generate a budget risk alert."*

**Layer 4: Visualization Layer (1 Mark)**
This layer renders dashboards, burn-down charts, and heat-maps using tools such as Grafana, Power BI, or Tableau. The aim is to reduce cognitive load on stakeholders.

**Layer 5: Alerting and Auto-Remediation Layer (1 Mark)**
This layer sends notifications through email, SMS, Slack, or PagerDuty when thresholds are breached. Advanced systems also trigger auto-remediation workflows such as rolling back a faulty deployment.

**Layer 6: Meta-Monitoring Layer (1 Mark)**
This layer evaluates the effectiveness of the monitoring system itself, ensuring that the alerts are accurate and that the system is not generating false positives or missing critical events.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark):**
*(Refer to the diagram in SECTION 4.1 of these notes.)*

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**

$$
\text{EV} = 45\ \text{lakhs},\ \quad \text{PV} = 50\ \text{lakhs},\ \quad \text{AC} = 55\ \text{lakhs},\ \quad \text{BAC} = 120\ \text{lakhs}
$$

**Step 1: Compute SPI (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{SPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} = \frac{45}{50} = 0.90
$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{SPI} < 1.0$, so the project is **behind schedule**.

**Step 2: Compute CPI (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} = \frac{45}{55} = 0.818
$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{CPI} < 1.0$, so the project is **over budget**.

**Step 3: Compute CV (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 45 - 55 = -10\ \text{lakhs}
$$

**Interpretation:** Negative CV confirms cost overrun.

**Step 4: Compute SV (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 45 - 50 = -5\ \text{lakhs}
$$

**Interpretation:** Negative SV confirms schedule delay.

**Step 5: Compute EAC (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{EAC} = \text{AC} + \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{CPI}} = 55 + \frac{120 - 45}{0.818}
$$

$$
\text{EAC} = 55 + \frac{75}{0.818} = 55 + 91.69 = 146.69\ \text{lakhs}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project is now expected to cost ₹146.69 lakhs against the original budget of ₹120 lakhs — a **22% overrun**.

**Step 6: Compute ETC (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{ETC} = \text{EAC} - \text{AC} = 146.69 - 55 = 91.69\ \text{lakhs}
$$

**Step 7: Compute TCPI (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{TCPI} = \frac{\text{BAC} - \text{EV}}{\text{BAC} - \text{AC}} = \frac{120 - 45}{120 - 55} = \frac{75}{65} = 1.1538
$$

**Interpretation:** To complete the project within the original budget, the remaining work must be performed at a CPI of **1.15**, which is highly aggressive given the current CPI of 0.82. This indicates that the **original budget is unattainable**, and a revised budget (EAC) of ₹146.69 lakhs should be approved.

**Final Status:** The project is in the **Red** zone — both schedule slippage and cost overrun are significant.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **benefits and limitations** of automated monitoring in IT project management. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A project uses weights $w_1 = 0.25,\ w_2 = 0.25,\ w_3 = 0.20,\ w_4 = 0.15,\ w_5 = 0.15$ for the KPIs SPI, CPI, BSR, CC, and SLA respectively. The observed values are:

$\text{SPI} = 1.05,\ \text{CPI} = 0.95,\ \text{BSR} = 88\%,\ \text{CC} = 82\%,\ \text{SLA} = 99.9\%$

Compute the **Composite Health Index (CHI)** and determine the **RAG status**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Benefits (4 Marks):**

1. **Real-Time Visibility (1 Mark):** Automated tools provide dashboards updated every minute, enabling proactive decision-making rather than reactive firefighting.

2. **Reduced Human Error (1 Mark):** Automation eliminates manual data entry, which is a leading cause of inaccurate status reports in traditional project management.

3. **Early Risk Detection (1 Mark):** Threshold-based alerts warn the Project Manager of cost or schedule deviations days or weeks before they become crises.

4. **Improved Stakeholder Communication (1 Mark):** Auto-generated reports and dashboards keep sponsors, clients, and teams aligned without scheduling frequent status meetings.

**Limitations (3 Marks):**

1. **High Initial Setup Cost (1 Mark):** Tools like ServiceNow, Azure DevOps, or custom Grafana dashboards require significant investment in licensing, training, and integration.

2. **Alert Fatigue (1 Mark):** Poorly tuned thresholds can flood teams with false-positive alerts, causing critical warnings to be ignored.

3. **Inability to Capture Qualitative Factors (1 Mark):** Automated systems struggle to measure team morale, customer satisfaction, or political risk — factors that still require human judgment.

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Normalize each KPI (3 Marks — ½ × 6)**

$$
N_{\text{SPI}} = \min\!\left(\frac{1.05}{1.0},\ 1.0\right) = 1.00
$$

$$
N_{\text{CPI}} = \min\!\left(\frac{0.95}{1.0},\ 1.0\right) = 0.95
$$

$$
N_{\text{BSR}} = \frac{88}{100} = 0.88
$$

$$
N_{\text{CC}} = \frac{82}{100} = 0.82
$$

$$
N_{\text{SLA}} = \frac{99.9}{100} = 0.999
$$

**Step 2: Apply weighted aggregation (3 Marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CHI} &= (0.25)(1.00) + (0.25)(0.95) + (0.20)(0.88) + (0.15)(0.82) + (0.15)(0.999) \\
&= 0.2500 + 0.2375 + 0.1760 + 0.1230 + 0.14985 \\
&= 0.93635
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Determine RAG status (1 Mark)**

$$
\text{CHI} = 0.93635 \ge 0.85 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Green}
$$

**Interpretation:** The project is healthy across all five dimensions, with a particularly strong SPI of 1.05 (slightly ahead of schedule) and excellent SLA compliance of 99.9%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Automated Monitoring Questions:**
> 1. **Never write "monitoring is good"** without quantifying. Examiners expect **numbers, formulas, and thresholds**.
> 2. **Always pair each KPI with its formula.** Writing *"CPI measures cost performance"* scores 0.5 marks; writing *"CPI = EV / AC"* scores 1 mark.
> 3. **Do not skip the diagram.** Even in 7-mark sub-questions, a **block diagram is mandatory** for full marks. Hand-drawn or Mermaid-rendered — both are accepted.
> 4. **In numerical problems, always interpret the result.** Computing SPI = 0.90 without saying *"behind schedule"* loses 0.5 marks.
> 5. **Boundary conditions must be stated.** E.g., *"SPI ≥ 0 indicates progress"* or *"BSR ranges from 0% to 100%"*.
> 6. **Common mistake:** Students confuse EAC and ETC. **EAC = total expected project cost.** **ETC = cost needed to finish remaining work.** Memorize this distinction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Automated Monitoring** is the **continuous, tool-driven, real-time observation** of project KPIs without manual intervention.
- The **six-layer architecture** is: **Collection → Aggregation → Analysis → Visualization → Alerting → Meta-Monitoring**.
- Core **EVM (Earned Value Management)** formulas to memorize:
  - $\text{SPI} = \text{EV} / \text{PV}$
  - $\text{CPI} = \text{EV} / \text{AC}$
  - $\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC}$
  - $\text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}$
  - $\text{EAC} = \text{AC} + (\text{BAC} - \text{EV}) / \text{CPI}$
  - $\text{TCPI} = (\text{BAC} - \text{EV}) / (\text{BAC} - \text{AC})$
- **RAG Thresholds** (industry standard): **Green ≥ 0.85**, **Amber 0.70–0.85**, **Red < 0.70**.
- **Composite Health Index (CHI)** is a weighted sum of normalized KPIs, with weights summing to **1.0**.
- **Key Tools:** Jira, Jenkins, Grafana, Power BI, SonarQube, PagerDuty, Azure DevOps, AWS CloudWatch.
- **Benefits:** Real-time visibility, reduced human error, early risk detection, improved communication.
- **Limitations:** High setup cost, alert fatigue, inability to measure qualitative factors.
- **Advanced Concepts:** Auto-remediation, ML-based predictive monitoring, meta-monitoring, RAG dashboards, SLA governance.
- **Boundary conditions:** SPI, CPI, BSR, CC are all bounded between **0 and 1** (after normalization); RAG status is a **categorical** output.
- **Always interpret numerical results** in the context of **schedule health, cost health, quality health, and risk health**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study Suggestion on Project Topics: A Mini-Project to be Identified in the Given Domain (Crowd Source System)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study Suggestion on Project Topics: A Mini-Project to be Identified in the Given Domain (Crowd Source System)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Crowd Source System (CSS)** is a distributed problem-solving and production model in which an organization obtains required services, ideas, content, or financial contributions by soliciting input from a large, open, and often geographically dispersed group of individuals — typically via a web-based or mobile platform. In the context of KTU's B.Tech IT Project Management (PBCBT604, 2024 Scheme), a *mini-project* in the Crowd Source domain is a **bounded, deliverable, semester-scale software engineering exercise** in which a small student team (typically 4 members) applies the full **PMBOK / Software Project Management lifecycle** — Initiation → Planning → Execution → Monitoring & Control → Closure — to conceptualize, prototype, and document a software solution that operationalizes one or more *crowd-sourcing primitives* (crowd creation, crowd voting, crowd solving, crowd funding, or crowd sensing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> *"A mini-project on a Crowd Source System is a semester-long, team-based software engineering artifact that demonstrates the application of project management knowledge areas (Scope, Schedule, Cost, Quality, Risk, Communication) to a real-world, contributor-driven application such as civic reporting, opinion aggregation, or collaborative knowledge platforms."*

The **Crowd Source System** as a *Domain* in the KTU mini-project bank typically belongs to the **Web / Mobile Application Development** category and integrates concepts from distributed systems, human-computer interaction, and software engineering economics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Town Hall Notice Board vs. Wikipedia"

Imagine the difference between a **single-author notice board** in a small Kerala town panchayat office and **Wikipedia**:

| Aspect | Notice Board (Traditional) | Wikipedia (Crowd Source System) |
|---|---|---|
| Content creator | One clerk | Millions of volunteers |
| Verification | Pre-publication review | Post-publication peer review |
| Scalability | Limited by physical wall | Limited only by internet bandwidth |
| Cost model | Fixed salary | Volunteer / micro-reward |
| Failure recovery | Single point of failure | Distributed resilience |

A **mini-project on a Crowd Source System** is, in essence, the student team's attempt to build a *small-scale Wikipedia* for a specific niche — for example, a **civic-issue reporting platform for Kerala local bodies**, a **campus event aggregation app**, or a **price-comparison community platform**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PBCBT604 — Module 4):**
> The case study suggestion expects the student to (a) **identify a viable mini-project topic** within the assigned domain, (b) **justify the selection** with feasibility reasoning, and (c) **map the topic to PMBOK knowledge areas**. Marks are awarded for the *reasoning behind selection*, not merely for listing topics.

## 1.3 Key Terms in the Domain (Bolded for Examination Recall)

- **Crowd Source System (CSS)** — A web/mobile platform that aggregates contributions from an undefined, large group.
- **Mini-Project** — A semester-scale (typically 12–16 week) deliverable, 4-member team, 200–500 person-hour effort, as per KTU norms.
- **Domain** — The application area; here, *Crowd Source System*.
- **Crowd Creation** — Contributors *generate* content (e.g., Wikitravel, OpenStreetMap).
- **Crowd Voting** — Contributors *rank* content (e.g., Reddit, Stack Overflow reputation).
- **Crowd Solving** — Contributors *solve* a posted problem (e.g., InnoCentive).
- **Crowd Funding** — Contributors *fund* a proposal (e.g., Kickstarter, Ketto).
- **Crowd Sensing** — Contributors *sense/report* environmental data (e.g., Waze traffic).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** Always tie your mini-project topic back to **one of the five crowd-sourcing primitives** above. Topics that cannot be mapped to a primitive are penalized as "vague" in valuation.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of the Topic

The KTU examiner expects the student to demonstrate, in a 14-mark answer, a **structured methodology** for converting a *domain* (Crowd Source System) into a *mini-project statement*. This methodology has **five operational stages**:

### Stage 1 — Domain Familiarization
- Read **3–5 reference papers** on the domain (e.g., Jeff Howe, *Wired Magazine*, 2006 — coined the term "crowdsourcing").
- Identify the **problem class** the domain addresses (information asymmetry, distributed sensing, mass ideation).

### Stage 2 — Problem Narrowing
- Apply the **SMART filter** to the domain:
  - **S**pecific — A narrow sub-problem (e.g., "civic pothole reporting" rather than "smart city").
  - **M**easurable — A success metric (e.g., "≥ 100 verified reports in 30 days").
  - **A**chievable — Within a 4-member, 16-week scope.
  - **R**elevant — Solves a real problem in the campus / locality.
  - **T**ime-bound — One academic semester.
- Document this in a **Project Charter**.

### Stage 3 — Stakeholder Identification
- Typical CSS stakeholders: *End-users (contributors), Moderators, System Administrators, Sponsoring Organization, Government/NGO Partners*.

### Stage 4 — Feasibility Analysis (TCOF)
- **Technical** — Web stack feasibility (LAMP / MERN / MEAN).
- **Cost** — Mostly free-tier cloud (Firebase, Vercel, GitHub Pages).
- **Operational** — Single admin; 4-student team.
- **Schedule** — 16-week Gantt chart.

### Stage 5 — Risk Register
- Low contributor engagement → **Gamification** (badges, leaderboards).
- Spam / abuse → **Moderation queue** with ML flagging.
- Data privacy → **Anonymization** layer.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **estimation and control formulas** a KTU 2024 student must know when scoping a mini-project in *any* domain, including Crowd Source Systems.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Unit | Application in Mini-Project |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = a \times (KLOC)^{b}$ | $E$=Effort, $KLOC$=Thousand Lines of Code, $a,b$=COCOMO constants | Person-Months | Basic COCOMO for effort sizing |
| 2 | $D = c \times E^{d}$ | $D$=Duration, $E$=Effort, $c,d$=COCOMO constants | Months | Project schedule sizing |
| 3 | $FP = UFP \times CAF$ | $FP$=Function Points, $UFP$=Unadjusted FP, $CAF$=Complexity Adjustment Factor | Function Points | Size estimation for non-KLOC projects |
| 4 | $EV = \% \text{ Complete} \times BAC$ | $EV$=Earned Value, $BAC$=Budget at Completion | Currency / Hours | Earned Value Management |
| 5 | $CV = EV - AC$ | $CV$=Cost Variance, $AC$=Actual Cost | Currency | Negative CV = over-budget |
| 6 | $SV = EV - PV$ | $SV$=Schedule Variance, $PV$=Planned Value | Currency / Hours | Negative SV = behind schedule |
| 7 | $CPI = \dfrac{EV}{AC}$ | $CPI$=Cost Performance Index | Ratio | $CPI < 1$ = poor cost performance |
| 8 | $SPI = \dfrac{EV}{PV}$ | $SPI$=Schedule Performance Index | Ratio | $SPI < 1$ = behind schedule |
| 9 | $EAC = \dfrac{BAC}{CPI}$ | $EAC$=Estimate at Completion | Currency | Forecasted total cost |
| 10 | $TCPI = \dfrac{BAC - EV}{BAC - AC}$ | $TCPI$=To-Complete Performance Index | Ratio | Cost efficiency required to finish on budget |
| 11 | $Risk\ Score = P \times I$ | $P$=Probability, $I$=Impact | Score (1–25) | Quantitative risk prioritization |
| 12 | $Productivity = \dfrac{Size}{Effort}$ | Size in FP/KLOC | FP per PM | Team productivity benchmarking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **COCOMO Constants Quick-Reference Table (Organic Mode — typical mini-project):**
>
> | Project Mode | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Organic (small team, familiar) | 2.4 | 1.05 | 2.5 | 0.38 |
> | Semi-detached | 3.0 | 1.12 | 2.5 | 0.35 |
> | Embedded | 3.6 | 1.20 | 2.5 | 0.32 |
>
> *For a 4-member, 16-week mini-project, the **Organic mode** is the KTU-preferred classification.*

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the IT industry, the **Crowd Source System** pattern underpins:
- **OpenStreetMap** (geospatial crowd creation — valued at \$BNs).
- **Waze** (crowd sensing for traffic — acquired by Google for ~\$1.3 B).
- **Stack Overflow** (crowd solving for programming — 100M+ users).
- **Kerala State IT Mission's** *K-Smart* civic complaint platform (a literal crowd-sensing application).
- **iFixit** (crowd creation for repair manuals).

By completing a mini-project in this domain, the student demonstrates **portfolio-grade** proficiency in:
- Distributed system design.
- User-generated content (UGC) moderation.
- Incentive mechanism design (gamification).
- Cloud-deployed full-stack web/mobile engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this domain scores well in KTU evaluation:** The crowd source domain is *observable* (examiners can easily test the working model), *constrained* (fits 16-week scope), and *socially relevant* (aligned with NEP 2020's emphasis on societal engagement).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: From Domain to Mini-Project Statement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section demonstrates the **complete, exhaustive methodology** that a 14-mark KTU answer requires. Every transition is explicitly written.

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Walkthrough — "Kerala Civic Issue Reporting Platform (KCIRP)"

### Step 1: Receive the Domain Assignment
The KTU examiner (or the guide) assigns the domain: **Crowd Source System**.

### Step 2: Brainstorm Crowd-Sourcing Primitives Available
List the five primitives (Creation, Voting, Solving, Funding, Sensing) and select one primary primitive. For a *civic reporting* platform, the chosen primitive is **Crowd Sensing** — users *report* civic issues (potholes, streetlight failures, water-logging).

### Step 3: Apply the SMART Filter

$$\text{Problem} = \text{"Civic Issue Reporting for Kerala Municipalities"}$$

Evaluating against SMART:

- **Specific** — Narrowed to *pothole, streetlight, and waste-management* reports.
- **Measurable** — Target: *500 verified reports from 100 unique users in 30 days* post-launch.
- **Achievable** — A 4-member team can build a MERN-stack web app + Android wrapper in 16 weeks.
- **Relevant** — Kerala municipalities spend millions annually on a manual complaint system; an automated platform reduces response time.
- **Time-bound** — One semester, 16 weeks.

### Step 4: Estimate Effort Using Basic COCOMO

Assume the estimated code size, post-requirements analysis, is **$KLOC = 3.0$** (3,000 source lines). The project is *organic*.

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= a \times (KLOC)^{b} \\
&= 2.4 \times (3.0)^{1.05} \\
&= 2.4 \times 3.1553 \\
&\approx 7.57 \text{ Person-Months}
\end{aligned}$$

Total **person-months** is approximately 7.57. With a 4-member team:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Team-months} &= \dfrac{7.57}{4} \\
&\approx 1.89 \text{ calendar months of full-time work}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to **hours** (1 PM ≈ 152 working hours in industry standard):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Effort in Hours} &= 7.57 \times 152 \\
&\approx 1,151 \text{ person-hours}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Derive Schedule Using COCOMO Duration Formula

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= c \times E^{d} \\
&= 2.5 \times (7.57)^{0.38} \\
&= 2.5 \times 2.2138 \\
&\approx 5.53 \text{ Months}
\end{aligned}$$

The 5.53-month schedule comfortably fits the 16-week (≈ 4-month) semester *only* if parallel workstreams are introduced. Hence the schedule needs **task-level parallelization** — a Project Schedule Network Diagram is required (see Section 4).

### Step 6: Compute Function Points (Cross-Check)

For a web app with 4 external inputs, 2 external outputs, 3 external inquiries, 5 internal logical files, 4 external interface files, the UFP is computed:

| Function Type | Count | Weight (Average) | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| External Inputs (EI) | 4 | 4 | 16 |
| External Outputs (EO) | 2 | 5 | 10 |
| External Inquiries (EQ) | 3 | 4 | 12 |
| Internal Logical Files (ILF) | 5 | 10 | 50 |
| External Interface Files (EIF) | 4 | 7 | 28 |

$$\begin{aligned}
UFP &= 16 + 10 + 12 + 50 + 28 \\
&= 116 \text{ Function Points}
\end{aligned}$$

Assuming $CAF = 1.05$ (mild complexity):

$$FP = UFP \times CAF = 116 \times 1.05 = 121.8 \text{ Function Points}$$

### Step 7: Risk Quantification — Sample 5 Risks

| Risk | Probability ($P$) | Impact ($I$) | $P \times I$ | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Low user adoption | 0.4 | 4 | 1.6 | Gamification + college tie-up |
| Spam reports | 0.6 | 3 | 1.8 | Image-hash + CAPTCHA |
| Server downtime | 0.2 | 5 | 1.0 | Multi-region deploy on free tier |
| Data leakage | 0.1 | 5 | 0.5 | AES-256 encryption |
| Team member dropout | 0.2 | 3 | 0.6 | Pair programming + documentation |

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** A risk register with $P \times I > 1.0$ is considered a "high-priority risk" by KTU evaluators. Always categorize each risk into **High / Medium / Low** bands.

### Step 8: Earned Value Snapshot — End of Week 8

Assume at the end of week 8:
- $BAC$ (Budget at Completion) = ₹60,000 (in kind labor value).
- $PV$ (Planned Value) at week 8 = ₹30,000 (50% planned).
- $AC$ (Actual Cost incurred) = ₹34,000 (team worked extra hours).
- $EV$ (Earned Value) — 45% of work actually completed = ₹27,000.

$$\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC = 27{,}000 - 34{,}000 = -7{,}000 \\
SV &= EV - PV = 27{,}000 - 30{,}000 = -3{,}000 \\
CPI &= \dfrac{EV}{AC} = \dfrac{27{,}000}{34{,}000} \approx 0.79 \\
SPI &= \dfrac{EV}{PV} = \dfrac{27{,}000}{30{,}000} = 0.90 \\
EAC &= \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{0.79} \approx 75{,}949
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $CV < 0$ → **Over budget** by ₹7,000.
- $SV < 0$ → **Behind schedule**.
- $CPI < 1$ → Cost performance is poor; for every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.79 of work was earned.
- $SPI < 1$ → Schedule performance is lagging.
- $EAC$ has **risen** above $BAC$ → corrective action (scope cut or extra resource) is needed.

### Step 9: Final Project Statement (For the KTU Report)

> *"The **Kerala Civic Issue Reporting Platform (KCIRP)** is a 16-week, 4-member mini-project that operationalizes a **Crowd Sensing** primitive of the Crowd Source System domain. The platform enables Kerala citizens to geo-tag, photograph, and submit civic complaints (potholes, streetlight failures, waste accumulation) via an Android app. A moderation queue, gamified contributor leaderboard, and a municipal-officer dashboard constitute the core modules. The project is estimated at 7.57 person-months (≈ 1,151 person-hours) using Basic COCOMO organic-mode parameters, validated by 121.8 Function Points. Project success is defined as ≥ 500 verified reports from ≥ 100 unique contributors within 30 days of go-live."*

This single paragraph is the **deliverable KTU expects** in a 14-mark answer.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1. Define a Crowd Source System. List its five primary primitives with one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *Crowd Source System (CSS)* is a distributed model in which a large, open group of contributors (the "crowd") provides ideas, content, funds, or data, typically via a web platform, to solve a problem or create value. The five primary primitives are: **(i) Crowd Creation** — contributors generate content (e.g., *OpenStreetMap*); **(ii) Crowd Voting** — contributors rank content (e.g., *Reddit*); **(iii) Crowd Solving** — contributors solve a posted challenge (e.g., *InnoCentive*); **(iv) Crowd Funding** — contributors pledge money (e.g., *Kickstarter*); **(v) Crowd Sensing** — contributors report environmental/real-time data (e.g., *Waze*).
> *(1 mark definition + 2 marks for the table — 5 primitives with examples.)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2. State the SMART filter used in mini-project topic identification. Apply it to one Crowd Source System sub-topic.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **SMART filter** is a goal-setting framework requiring the topic to be **S**pecific, **M**easurable, **A**chievable, **R**elevant, and **T**ime-bound. Applied to a *civic pothole-reporting app* (Crowd Sensing): **S** — focuses only on potholes (not all civic issues); **M** — target = 100 verified reports in 30 days; **A** — a 4-member team can build it in 16 weeks using free-tier cloud; **R** — solves a real problem for Kerala municipalities; **T** — bounded to one academic semester.
> *(1 mark for SMART definition + 2 marks for the application.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern, 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks

**Q.A (a)** Identify a viable mini-project topic in the Crowd Source System domain for a 4-member B.Tech team. Justify your selection using the **SMART filter** and the **5 crowd-sourcing primitives**. Construct a **Project Charter** with the following sections: *Project Title, Objectives, Scope, Deliverables, Stakeholders, Success Criteria.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The identified mini-project is the **"Kerala Civic Issue Reporting Platform (KCIRP)"**.

- **Project Title:** KCIRP — A Crowd Sensing Platform for Kerala Municipalities.
- **Project Objectives:**
  1. Build a web + mobile app where citizens geo-tag civic issues (potholes, broken streetlights, waste dumping).
  2. Provide a moderation queue for municipal officers.
  3. Implement gamification (badges, leaderboards) to drive contributor retention.
- **Scope:** *In-scope* — user registration, report submission with photo + GPS, moderation, leaderboard, admin dashboard. *Out-of-scope* — payment integration, multi-language i18n, hardware IoT sensors.
- **Deliverables:** SRS document, design diagrams, working prototype, test report, final project report, viva-voce demonstration.
- **Stakeholders:** Contributors (citizens), Municipal Officers, Project Guide, System Administrator, Kerala IT Mission (potential partner).
- **Success Criteria:** ≥ 100 unique contributors, ≥ 500 verified reports, ≤ 2-second median response time, deployed on a public URL by week 16.

**SMART Justification:** Topic is *Specific* (potholes, streetlights, waste), *Measurable* (500 reports target), *Achievable* (MERN stack in 16 weeks), *Relevant* (Kerala civic-tech priority), *Time-bound* (one semester).

**Primitive Mapping:** Primary primitive is **Crowd Sensing**; auxiliary primitives are **Crowd Voting** (upvote critical issues) and **Crowd Creation** (municipal officer posts resolution updates).

*(Mark split: 2 for topic identification + 2 for SMART + 3 for Project Charter.)*

---

**Q.A (b)** For the mini-project identified in Q.A(a), estimate the **effort and duration** using the **Basic COCOMO model** in Organic mode. Assume $KLOC = 3.0$. Also compute the **Function Point** estimate assuming $CAF = 1.05$ and an UFP of 116. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given $KLOC = 3.0$, organic mode: $a = 2.4$, $b = 1.05$, $c = 2.5$, $d = 0.38$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= a \times (KLOC)^{b} \\
&= 2.4 \times (3.0)^{1.05} \\
&= 2.4 \times 3.1553 \\
&\approx 7.57 \text{ Person-Months}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= c \times E^{d} \\
&= 2.5 \times (7.57)^{0.38} \\
&= 2.5 \times 2.2138 \\
&\approx 5.53 \text{ Months}
\end{aligned}$$

With a 4-member team, the **calendar duration** becomes:

$$\text{Calendar Months} = \dfrac{7.57}{4} \approx 1.89 \text{ months of pure effort}$$

**Function Point Calculation:**

$$FP = UFP \times CAF = 116 \times 1.05 = 121.8 \text{ Function Points}$$

**Productivity Benchmark:**

$$\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{121.8 \text{ FP}}{7.57 \text{ PM}} \approx 16.1 \text{ FP / PM}$$

*(Mark split: 2 for COCOMO effort + 2 for COCOMO duration + 2 for FP + 1 for productivity interpretation.)*

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **methodology** a B.Tech team should follow to identify, narrow, and justify a mini-project topic in the Crowd Source System domain. Use a **block diagram** to illustrate the steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The methodology has **six sequential steps**:

1. **Domain Familiarization** — Read 3–5 reference papers; understand crowd-sourcing primitives.
2. **Primitive Selection** — Choose one of Creation / Voting / Solving / Funding / Sensing.
3. **SMART Filtering** — Narrow the topic using SMART criteria.
4. **Feasibility Study (TCOF)** — Technical, Cost, Operational, Schedule feasibility.
5. **Estimation** — COCOMO + Function Points.
6. **Charter Preparation** — Document objectives, scope, stakeholders, success criteria.

**Block Diagram (textual, since the answer sheet cannot host Mermaid):**

`[Domain] → [Reference Papers] → [Primitive Selection] → [SMART Filter] → [Feasibility TCOF] → [Estimation COCOMO/FP] → [Charter]`

Each step produces an artifact: step 1 produces a *literature note*, step 2 produces a *primitive matrix*, step 3 produces a *narrowed statement*, step 4 produces a *feasibility report*, step 5 produces an *effort/duration estimate*, step 6 produces the *Project Charter*.

*(Mark split: 2 for the methodology explanation + 3 for the block diagram + 2 for the artifacts.)*

---

**Q.B (b)** For the same mini-project, develop a **Risk Register** with at least 5 risks, applying the $P \times I$ scoring model. Suggest a **mitigation** for each risk. Compute the **Earned Value** metrics ($CV, SV, CPI, SPI, EAC$) at the end of week 8, given $BAC = 60{,}000$, $PV = 30{,}000$, $AC = 34{,}000$, $EV = 27{,}000$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Risk Register:**

| # | Risk | $P$ | $I$ | $P \times I$ | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Low user adoption | 0.4 | 4 | 1.6 | College tie-up, gamified badges |
| 2 | Spam / fake reports | 0.6 | 3 | 1.8 | CAPTCHA + image-hash + manual moderation |
| 3 | Server downtime | 0.2 | 5 | 1.0 | Multi-region free-tier (Vercel + Render) |
| 4 | Data privacy breach | 0.1 | 5 | 0.5 | AES-256 encryption at rest |
| 5 | Team member dropout | 0.2 | 3 | 0.6 | Pair programming + shared documentation |

**Earned Value Calculations at end of Week 8:**

$$\begin{aligned}
CV &= EV - AC = 27{,}000 - 34{,}000 = -7{,}000 \\
SV &= EV - PV = 27{,}000 - 30{,}000 = -3{,}000 \\
CPI &= \dfrac{EV}{AC} = \dfrac{27{,}000}{34{,}000} \approx 0.794 \\
SPI &= \dfrac{EV}{PV} = \dfrac{27{,}000}{30{,}000} = 0.900 \\
EAC &= \dfrac{BAC}{CPI} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{0.794} \approx 75{,}567
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $CV = -7{,}000$ → project is **over budget** by ₹7,000.
- $SV = -3{,}000$ → project is **behind schedule**.
- $CPI = 0.79 < 1$ → for every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.79 of work is earned; corrective action needed.
- $SPI = 0.90 < 1$ → schedule efficiency is at 90%; fast-tracking is recommended.
- $EAC \approx 75{,}567$ exceeds $BAC$ (₹60,000) → final cost overrun of ~26% is forecasted.

*(Mark split: 2 for risk register + 3 for EV computations + 2 for interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Listing topics without justification** — Naming "Kerala pothole app" without mapping it to a *primitive* and *SMART* filter scores **zero** on justification marks.
> 2. **Skipping the COCOMO constants** — Many students write $E = 2.4 \times KLOC^{1.05}$ but forget the constants table; you lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing $EV$ with $PV$** — $EV$ is *what you have actually completed*, $PV$ is *what you planned to complete*. Mix-ups cost full marks in EV problems.
> 4. **No block diagram** — A 14-mark answer without a block diagram loses 2–3 marks. Even a hand-drawn ASCII flowchart is acceptable.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — $E$ is in *Person-Months*, $D$ in *Months*, $FP$ in *Function Points*. Writing bare numbers = -1 mark.
> 6. **Vague feasibility analysis** — A TCOF (Technical / Cost / Operational / Schedule feasibility) study is mandatory; missing it is a -2 mark penalty.
