| 5 | **Sprint Retrospective** | ≤ 3 hrs (for 4-week sprint) | Inspect the *process* (not the product) and improve it | Scrum Team only | Improvement Action Items |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application Heuristic:** *Each event is a formal opportunity to inspect and adapt.* Skipping the Retrospective is the #1 reason Scrum "degenerates into a mini-Waterfall" in real projects — a common KTU exam trap.

### 2.3.1 Application Walkthrough — Sprint Planning (Two Sub-Parts)

- **Part A — *What* can be done this Sprint?**
  - The Product Owner discusses the objective of the Sprint (the **Sprint Goal**).
  - The Developers select items from the top of the Product Backlog.
  - *Negotiation* happens until the team is confident about the **forecast**.

- **Part B — *How* will the work get done?**
  - The Developers break down backlog items into **tasks** (≤ 1 day each).
  - The team collectively creates the **Sprint Backlog** (a plan for the Sprint).

### 2.3.2 Application Walkthrough — Daily Scrum

The classic **three-question format** (Ken Schwaber, original Scrum framework):
1. What did I do **yesterday** that helped the team meet the Sprint Goal?
2. What will I do **today** to help the team meet the Sprint Goal?
3. Do I see any **impediment** that prevents me or the team from meeting the Sprint Goal?

> [!NOTE]
> The 2020 Scrum Guide de-emphasized the three questions in favor of an *open-ended* structure focused on the Sprint Goal. However, KTU examiners still reward the explicit mention of the three questions as evidence of foundational knowledge.

## 2.4 The Three Scrum Artifacts — Application in a Project

| Artifact | Commitment Associated | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Product Backlog** | **Product Goal** | The *what* — emergent, ordered list of work. |
| **Sprint Backlog** | **Sprint Goal** | The *what* + *how* for the current Sprint. |
| **Increment** | **Definition of Done** | The *result* — a concrete stepping stone toward the Product Goal. |

The **"three artifacts, three commitments"** mapping was added in the 2020 Scrum Guide and is a frequent KTU exam topic.

## 2.5 Applied Scrum Practices in a Project — Step-wise Operational Logic

1. **Project Inception / Pre-Sprint 0**
   - Create the **Product Vision** and identify the **Product Goal**.
   - Identify the **Scrum Master** and **Product Owner**.
   - Form the **cross-functional Development Team**.
   - Build an **initial Product Backlog** (user stories + epics + technical debt).

2. **Sprint 1 Begins**
   - Conduct **Sprint Planning** → commit to a Sprint Goal.
   - Hold **Daily Scrums** → track progress, surface impediments.
   - The Scrum Master removes blockers.

3. **End of Sprint 1**
   - Conduct **Sprint Review** → demo the increment to stakeholders.
   - Conduct **Sprint Retrospective** → identify process improvements.
   - Increment is **Done** (passes the Definition of Done).
   - Begin **Sprint 2 Planning** immediately (no gap).

4. **Repeat** for the duration of the project until the **Product Goal is achieved** or funding runs out.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **core quantitative metrics** frequently asked in KTU Module 4 problems. Memorize the formulas, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Metric / Formula | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Velocity** | $V = \frac{\sum \text{Story Points Completed in Sprint}}{\text{Number of Sprints (for average)}}$ | Story Points / Sprint |
| 2 | **Average Velocity** | $V_{avg} = \frac{\sum V_i}{n}$ | Story Points / Sprint (over *n* sprints) |
| 3 | **Sprint Burndown** | $R(t) = W_0 - \sum_{i=1}^{t} C_i$ | $R(t)$ = Remaining work at day $t$, $W_0$ = total work, $C_i$ = completed on day $i$ |
| 4 | **Release Forecast** | $\text{Sprints Required} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Total Backlog Points}}{V_{avg}} \right\rceil$ | Always round **up** |
| 5 | **Capacity** | $C = N \times D \times F$ | $N$ = team members, $D$ = available days, $F$ = **focus factor (typically 0.6 – 0.8)** |
| 6 | **Planned vs. Actual Variance** | $\Delta V = V_{actual} - V_{planned}$ | Negative ⇒ over-commitment |
| 7 | **Defect Density** | $DD = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Size (in KLOC or FP or Story Points)}}$ | Used in quality tracking |
| 8 | **Story Point to Ideal Hour (planning poker heuristic)** | 1 Point $\approx$ 4 – 6 Ideal Hours | Empirical; varies per team |
| 9 | **Cumulative Flow (work-in-progress)** | $WIP(t) = \text{Started}(t) - \text{Finished}(t)$ | Used in CFD diagram |
| 10 | **Sprint Goal Achievement Rate** | $SGA\% = \frac{\text{Sprints achieving goal}}{\text{Total Sprints}} \times 100$ | Healthy teams: 80%+ |
| 11 | **Scrum Anti-pattern Count (Project Health Index)** | $PHI = \text{Anti-patterns observed}$ | Lower is better; $PHI \leq 2$ considered healthy |
| 12 | **Sprint Goal Attainment Index** | $SGAI = \frac{\text{Achieved Sprint Goals}}{\text{Total Sprints}}$ | Ideal $\geq 0.8$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rules, all absolute-value bars in the formulas above are rendered using $\lvert$ / $\rvert$ or the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ — **never** use the vertical pipe `|` inside a markdown table row, as it breaks the markdown table parser.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The application of Scrum practices in real engineering projects delivers:

- **Faster time-to-market** through 2–4 week delivery cycles.
- **Higher quality** via the Definition of Done and continuous integration.
- **Stakeholder engagement** through Sprint Reviews and frequent demos.
- **Risk mitigation** through short feedback loops (inspection + adaptation).
- **Team morale and self-organization** through Retrospectives and impediment removal.
- **Predictable delivery** through empirical velocity-based forecasting.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** When asked *"Apply Scrum in a project,"* structure your answer as: **(i) Roles**, **(ii) Events with time-boxes**, **(iii) Artifacts**, **(iv) Step-by-step application across a 4–5 Sprint project**. This 4-part structure is the examiner's mental model and will earn full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem Scenario (for Worked Derivation)

> **Scenario (Adapted for KTU Exam):**
> A 7-member Scrum team is building a "Student ERP Portal". After **3 Sprints**, the team has completed the following Story Points from the Product Backlog:
>
> - Sprint 1: 28 SP (planned 30)
> - Sprint 2: 32 SP (planned 30)
> - Sprint 3: 35 SP (planned 32)
>
> The remaining Product Backlog contains **210 Story Points**. Using Scrum practices, compute:
> (a) Velocity for each Sprint and Average Velocity.
> (b) Forecast the number of Sprints required to complete the project.
> (c) Construct the Burndown Equation and remaining work at Day 7 of Sprint 4 (a 10-day sprint), assuming a linear burn.

### 3.1.1 Part (a) — Velocity Computation

**Given Data:**
- Sprint 1 actual = 28 SP; planned = 30 SP
- Sprint 2 actual = 32 SP; planned = 30 SP
- Sprint 3 actual = 35 SP; planned = 32 SP

**Step 1: Velocity for each Sprint**

The fundamental definition (from §2.6, Row 1):

$$
V_i = \text{Total Story Points Completed in Sprint } i
$$

**Step 2: Apply for each Sprint**

$$
V_1 = 28 \text{ SP}
$$

$$
V_2 = 32 \text{ SP}
$$

$$
V_3 = 35 \text{ SP}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Average Velocity**

Using the formula from §2.6, Row 2:

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{3} V_i}{3}
$$

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{28 + 32 + 35}{3}
$$

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{95}{3}
$$

$$
V_{avg} \approx 31.67 \text{ SP per Sprint}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The team's average velocity is **31.67 SP/Sprint**. A pragmatic Scrum Master would use **31 or 32 SP** as the *committed velocity* for forecasting (rounding to an integer and biasing toward the *last* sprint, which is the freshest empirical data).

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU Board valuation key awards **1 mark** for the velocity formula, **1 mark** for the substitution, and **1 mark** for the final numerical answer with units. Always carry units ("SP/Sprint").

### 3.1.2 Part (b) — Sprint Forecast

**Given:** Remaining Backlog = 210 SP; $V_{avg} \approx 31.67$ SP/Sprint.

**Step 1: Apply the release forecast formula (from §2.6, Row 4)**

$$
\text{Sprints Required} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Remaining Backlog}}{V_{avg}} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values**

$$
\text{Sprints Required} = \left\lceil \frac{210}{31.67} \right\rceil
$$

$$
\text{Sprints Required} = \left\lceil 6.631 \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3: Apply the ceiling function (round up)**

$$
\text{Sprints Required} = 7 \text{ Sprints}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** It will take **7 Sprints** to complete the remaining 210 SP at the team's current empirical velocity. The Product Owner should communicate this to stakeholders and use it for **release planning**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the ceiling function?** You cannot deliver a backlog in *partial* sprints. If the math says 6.631 sprints, you must commit to a full 7th sprint (even if it has leftover work to be completed in the next one). This is a classic KTU 1-mark trap.

### 3.1.3 Part (c) — Burndown Equation and Remaining Work at Day 7

**Given:** Sprint 4 has total work = $W_0 = 32$ SP (planned, matching Sprint 3 capacity). Sprint duration = 10 days.

**Step 1: State the ideal linear burndown equation (from §2.6, Row 3)**

For a linear (ideal) burndown, the cumulative completed work at day $t$ is:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{t} C_i = \frac{W_0}{T} \cdot t
$$

where $T$ is the total Sprint duration in days.

**Step 2: Substitute and simplify**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{t} C_i = \frac{32}{10} \cdot t = 3.2 \, t
$$

**Step 3: The remaining work at day $t$ is**

$$
R(t) = W_0 - \sum_{i=1}^{t} C_i
$$

$$
R(t) = 32 - 3.2 \, t
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate at $t = 7$ days**

$$
R(7) = 32 - 3.2 \times 7
$$

$$
R(7) = 32 - 22.4
$$

$$
R(7) = 9.6 \text{ SP}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** At the end of Day 7 of Sprint 4, the team **ideally** should have **9.6 Story Points** remaining. If the actual burndown chart shows significantly more or less work remaining, the team should surface this in the **Daily Scrum** and adapt (e.g., de-scope the Sprint, request help, or reduce future commitments).

> [!NOTE]
> **A well-burned chart is straight, not curvy.** Scrum purists (and KTU examiners) love a linear burndown. A "hockey-stick" curve (flat then suddenly drops) indicates late discovery of work — a sign of poor slicing or hidden dependencies.

## 3.2 The Scrum Application Matrix (Analytical Summary)

| Project Phase | Scrum Practice Applied | Outcome / Artifact Produced |
|---|---|---|
| **Project Initiation** | Create Product Vision, identify Product Goal, form Scrum Team, build initial Product Backlog | Product Backlog (v1.0), Release Plan (rough) |
| **Sprint Planning** | PO presents top backlog; team selects items; team creates tasks | Sprint Backlog + Sprint Goal |
| **Daily Execution** | Daily Scrum, Pair Programming, Continuous Integration, Test-Driven Development | Updated Sprint Backlog, Code, Tests |
| **Sprint End (Review)** | Team demos increment; PO accepts/rejects; backlog updated | Revised Product Backlog, Stakeholder Feedback |
| **Sprint End (Retrospective)** | Team inspects: people, relationships, process, tools, definition of Done | Improvement Action List for next Sprint |
| **Release** | Increment is deployed to production; retrospective at release level | Released Product, Release Notes |
| **Project Closure** | Final Sprint Review, Final Retrospective, post-mortem with all stakeholders | Project Closure Report, Lessons Learned |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Scrum Framework (Big Picture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PO[Product Owner] -->|Manages Prioritizes| PB[Product Backlog]
    SM[Scrum Master] -->|Facilitates Removes Impediments| ST[Scrum Team Developers]
    PB -->|Items Pulled| SP[Spring Planning]
    SP -->|Creates| SB[Sprint Backlog]
    SB -->|Guides| DS[Daily Scrum]
    DS -->|Updates| SB
    SB -->|Produces| IN[Increment]
    IN -->|Inspected In| SR[Sprint Review]
    SR -->|Updates| PB
    DS -->|Inspected In| DS2[Daily Scrum itself]
    ST -->|Inspects Process In| RET[Sprint Retrospective]
    RET -->|Action Items Improve| ST
    IN -->|Must Satisfy| DOD[Definition of Done]
    DOD -->|Validates| IN
```

**How to read this diagram:** The **Product Owner** is the *voice of the customer* and owns the **Product Backlog**. The **Scrum Master** coaches and serves the **Developers**. Each **Sprint** consists of **Sprint Planning → Daily Scrums → Increment → Sprint Review → Sprint Retrospective** in a tight loop. The **Definition of Done** gates every increment for quality.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Single Sprint Lifecycle (Time-Boxed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Sprint] --> B[Sprint Planning Part 1 What]
    B --> C[Sprint Planning Part 2 How]
    C --> D[Daily Scrum Day 1]
    D --> E[Development and Testing Days 1 to N]
    E --> F[Daily Scrum Day 2]
    F --> G[Daily Scrum Day N]
    G --> H[Code Freeze and DoD Check]
    H --> I[Sprint Review Demo to Stakeholders]
    I --> J[Sprint Retrospective Process Improvement]
    J --> K[Next Sprint Begins]
    K --> A
```

**Engineering Insight:** Note the *tight, non-overlapping* nature of the Sprint lifecycle. There is **no gap** between Sprints — the next Sprint starts *immediately* after the Retrospective.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Scrum Roles, Events, and Artifacts Inter-action

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Roles
        R1[Product Owner]
        R2[Scrum Master]
        R3[Developers]
    end
    subgraph Events
        E1[Sprint]
        E2[Sprint Planning]
        E3[Daily Scrum]
        E4[Sprint Review]
        E5[Sprint Retrospective]
    end
    subgraph Artifacts
        A1[Product Backlog]
        A2[Sprint Backlog]
        A3[Increment]
    end
    subgraph Commitments
        C1[Product Goal]
        C2[Sprint Goal]
        C3[Definition of Done]
    end
    R1 -->|Owns Maximizes Value| A1
    R1 -->|Defines| C1
    A1 -->|Contains| C1
    R2 -->|Facilitates All| E1
    R2 -->|Coaches Removes Impediments| R3
    E2 -->|Produces| A2
    A2 -->|Has| C2
    E3 -->|Updates| A2
    E4 -->|Inspects| A3
    E4 -->|Adapts| A1
    E5 -->|Inspects and Improves| E1
    R3 -->|Builds| A3
    A3 -->|Must Satisfy| C3
```

**Engineering Insight:** The **commitments** (Product Goal, Sprint Goal, DoD) are the *contracts of quality and value* that the artifacts must satisfy. This mapping — added in the 2020 Scrum Guide — is a **high-yield KTU exam topic**.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Burndown Chart Block Architecture (Applied Scrum Practice)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Burndown[Ideal vs Actual Burndown Tracking]
        X[Day 0 Total Work 32 SP] --> Y1[Day 1 Ideal 29 SP]
        Y1 --> Y2[Day 2 Ideal 26 SP]
        Y2 --> Y3[Day 3 Ideal 22 SP]
        Y3 --> Y4[Day 4 Ideal 19 SP]
        Y4 --> Y5[Day 5 Ideal 16 SP]
        Y5 --> Y6[Day 6 Ideal 13 SP]
        Y6 --> Y7[Day 7 Ideal 10 SP]
        Y7 --> Y8[Day 8 Ideal 6 SP]
        Y8 --> Y9[Day 9 Ideal 3 SP]
        Y9 --> Y10[Day 10 Ideal 0 SP Done]
    end
    subgraph Actions[Triggered Actions]
        Z1[If Actual Above Ideal Line Team Behind] --> ZA[Sprint Planning Risk Reduce Future Commitment]
        Z2[If Actual Below Ideal Line Team Ahead] --> ZB[Pull Additional Backlog Items]
        Z3[If Actual Equals Ideal] --> ZC[Sprint On Track No Action]
    end
    Y7 --> Z1
    Y7 --> Z2
    Y7 --> Z3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The burndown chart is the single most important **transparency tool** in Scrum. It is inspected *daily* (during the Daily Scrum) and is the primary empirical feedback channel for the team to adapt.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!TIP]
> KTU Part A questions are *definition/conceptual* and expect a 4–6 line answer with the **exact term** and a brief elaboration. Avoid one-line answers.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Model]**
**List and briefly explain any three Scrum Events as applied in a project.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sprint Planning** – A time-boxed event (≤ 8 hours for a 4-week Sprint) where the Scrum Team defines the **Sprint Goal** and selects Product Backlog items for the upcoming Sprint. The team answers *what* can be delivered and *how*.

2. **Daily Scrum** – A 15-minute daily synchronization meeting for the Developers. Each member reports: (i) what was done yesterday, (ii) what will be done today, and (iii) any impediments. It enables **inspection** of progress toward the Sprint Goal.

3. **Sprint Review** – Held at the end of the Sprint to **inspect the Increment** with stakeholders. The team demos the working product, gathers feedback, and the Product Owner updates the Product Backlog based on new insights.

*(Alternative events for marks: Sprint Retrospective, the Sprint itself.)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Explain the role of the Scrum Master in applying Scrum practices in a project.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Scrum Master** is a *servant-leader* responsible for **establishing Scrum** as defined in the Scrum Guide. In a project, the Scrum Master:

- **Facilitates** all Scrum Events (Sprint Planning, Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, Retrospective) and ensures they are time-boxed and productive.
- **Removes impediments** (organizational, technical, resource-based) raised by the Developers in the Daily Scrum.
- **Coaches** the team in self-management, cross-functionality, and Agile engineering practices (e.g., TDD, CI/CD).
- **Protects the team** from external interference and scope creep during the Sprint.
- **Helps the organization** adopt Scrum practices and understand empirical process control.

The Scrum Master is *not* a traditional project manager; there is **no command-and-control**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions offer an **internal choice** — students must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each 14-mark question typically has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Module 4 Pattern]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three Scrum Roles** and their responsibilities in applying Scrum practices in a project.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Scrum team of 6 developers completed the following Story Points in three consecutive Sprints: 25, 30, and 32. The remaining Product Backlog is 200 SP. Calculate the **average velocity** and **forecast the number of Sprints** required. Also explain the **burndown chart** as a Scrum practice for tracking Sprint progress.

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

### Part (a) — Three Scrum Roles (7 Marks)

| # | Role | Key Responsibility | Application in Project |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Product Owner** | Maximizes the value of the product; owns the Product Backlog. | Decides *what* to build and in *what order*; creates user stories; accepts/rejects work. |
| 2 | **Scrum Master** | Establishes Scrum; removes impediments; serves the team and organization. | Facilitates all five Scrum Events; coaches the team in self-organization and Agile engineering practices. |
| 3 | **Developers** | Create a usable Increment each Sprint; self-organize the work. | Cross-functional team (3–9 people); collectively owns the Sprint Backlog; estimates and builds the increment. |

**Marking breakdown:** 1 mark for naming each role + 1 mark for responsibilities + 1 mark for application = **7 marks** (well-distributed).

### Part (b) — Velocity, Forecast, and Burndown (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute Average Velocity (3 marks)**

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{25 + 30 + 32}{3} = \frac{87}{3} = 29 \text{ SP per Sprint}
$$

**Step 2: Forecast Sprints (2 marks)**

$$
\text{Sprints Required} = \left\lceil \frac{200}{29} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 6.897 \right\rceil = 7 \text{ Sprints}
$$

**Step 3: Burndown Chart Explanation (2 marks)**

A **burndown chart** is a graph showing the **amount of remaining work** in the Sprint Backlog over the duration of the Sprint (x-axis: time in days; y-axis: remaining Story Points). It is inspected **daily during the Daily Scrum**. The **ideal burndown** is a straight line from the starting work to zero. The **actual burndown** reveals whether the team is on track, behind, or ahead. Behind triggers replanning; ahead allows pulling more items.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **five Scrum Events** with their time-boxes and explain how they are applied in a real software project.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A team has a **total of 8 developers** and is starting a **2-week Sprint (10 working days)**. The team's **focus factor is 0.7**. Calculate the **team's capacity in ideal hours**, assuming each developer has **6 productive hours per day**. Also, explain the **Definition of Done (DoD)** as a critical Scrum practice.

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

### Part (a) — Five Scrum Events (7 Marks)

| # | Event | Time-Box | Application in Project |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sprint** | 1–4 weeks (fixed) | The container for all other events; produces a potentially shippable Increment. |
| 2 | **Sprint Planning** | ≤ 8 hours (4-wk Sprint) | Defines Sprint Goal + selects backlog items + creates Sprint Backlog. |
| 3 | **Daily Scrum** | 15 minutes | Daily synchronization; 3 questions format; surface impediments. |
| 4 | **Sprint Review** | ≤ 4 hours (4-wk Sprint) | Inspect Increment with stakeholders; adapt Product Backlog. |
| 5 | **Sprint Retrospective** | ≤ 3 hours (4-wk Sprint) | Inspect the team's *process*; create improvement actions. |

**Marking breakdown:** ~1.4 marks per event; ensure you mention the **time-box** explicitly (1 extra mark for time-boxes).

### Part (b) — Capacity Calculation + DoD (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Capacity Formula (from §2.6, Row 5)**

$$
C = N \times D \times F
$$

**Step 2: Identify the variables**

- $N = 8$ developers
- $D = 10$ working days
- $F = 0.7$ (focus factor — accounts for meetings, emails, interruptions)

**Step 3: Compute the raw capacity in ideal hours per day**

$$
\text{Hours per developer over Sprint} = 8 \times 10 \times 6 = 480 \text{ ideal hours}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the focus factor**

$$
C = 480 \times 0.7 = 336 \text{ ideal hours}
$$

**Step 5: Definition of Done Explanation (2 marks)**

The **Definition of Done (DoD)** is a **shared, transparent checklist** that the team uses to determine when a Product Backlog item is *complete*. A typical DoD includes: code written, code reviewed, unit tests passed, integration tests passed, documentation updated, deployed to staging, and Product Owner accepted. The DoD ensures **transparency** and prevents "90% done" forever stories.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Scrum Application Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing Scrum Master with Project Manager** — A Scrum Master is *not* a command-and-control PM. Mark loss: 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the time-boxes** — Always mention the time-box for every Scrum event. Skipping time-boxes = −1 mark per event.
> 3. **Using the phrase "Scrum meetings"** — Use the *exact* event names: "Sprint Planning," "Daily Scrum," "Sprint Review," "Sprint Retrospective." Generic terms = −1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the Definition of Done** — The DoD is the *commitment* for the Increment. Forgetting it = −1 mark.
> 5. **Not showing velocity units** — Always write "SP/Sprint" or "Story Points per Sprint." Missing units = −1 mark.
> 6. **Rounding forecast Sprints incorrectly** — Always use the **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$, not floor or standard rounding.
> 7. **Ignoring empirical pillars** — Mention **transparency, inspection, adaptation** at least once in a long answer. This is a 2-mark question within the question.
> 8. **Mixing up Product Backlog vs Sprint Backlog** — Product Backlog = *what* the product needs (long-term, owned by PO). Sprint Backlog = *what* the team is doing *this* Sprint (short-term, owned by Developers).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Application of Scrum Practices**

### A. Core Concepts to Memorize
- **Scrum Framework** = 3 Roles + 5 Events + 3 Artifacts (+ 3 Commitments in the 2020 Guide).
- **3 Roles:** Product Owner, Scrum Master, Developers (3–9 people).
- **5 Events:** Sprint (1–4 wks), Sprint Planning (≤ 8 hrs), Daily Scrum (15 min), Sprint Review (≤ 4 hrs), Sprint Retrospective (≤ 3 hrs). All time-boxes for 4-week Sprints.
- **3 Artifacts:** Product Backlog (→ Product Goal), Sprint Backlog (→ Sprint Goal), Increment (→ Definition of Done).
- **3 Pillars:** Transparency, Inspection, Adaptation.
- **3 Commitments:** Product Goal, Sprint Goal, Definition of Done.

### B. Key Formulas to Memorize
- $V_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum V_i}{n}$
- $\text{Sprints} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{Remaining SP}}{V_{avg}} \right\rceil$
- $C = N \times D \times F$ (with $F \approx 0.6$ – $0.8$)
- $R(t) = W_0 - 3.2 \, t$ (for a 32 SP, 10-day Sprint)

### C. Common User Story Format
- **As a** [role] **I want** [feature] **so that** [benefit].

### D. Common Burndown Insights
- Above the line = **behind** (less completed than ideal).
- Below the line = **ahead** (more completed than ideal).
- Flat = **blocked** (no progress).
- Curved sharply at the end = **late discovery** of work.

### E. Sprint Lifecycle Mnemonic
- **"SP-DS-DS-IN-SR-RT"** = Sprint Planning → Daily Scrum (loop) → Increment → Sprint Review → Retrospective.

### F. Common Anti-Patterns to Identify in Exam
- Skipping the Retrospective.
- Daily Scrums that turn into status reports to the Scrum Master.
- Product Owner "proxy" (someone other than the PO prioritizing the backlog).
- Sprint with no clear Sprint Goal.
- Definition of Done that is vague or unspoken.
- Sprints that always overrun the time-box.

### G. Key Acronyms
- **DoD** = Definition of Done
- **PO** = Product Owner
- **SM** = Scrum Master
- **SP** = Story Points
- **PB** = Product Backlog
- **SB** = Sprint Backlog
- **DoR** = Definition of Ready (often used in conjunction with DoD)

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark Scrum application question, always structure your answer as:
> 1. **Define** the concept with a *one-sentence* textbook definition.
> 2. **Explain** with bullets (roles/events/artifacts).
> 3. **Apply** to a project (e.g., a 5-Sprint software project).
> 4. **Conclude** with the empirical pillars or DoD.
>
> This 4-step structure is the **single most reliable way to score 12+ out of 14** on Part B questions in IT Project Management (PBCBT604).

---

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Application of Scrum Practices in the Project**
> *All content aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (IT) syllabus, the 2020 Scrum Guide™, and standard CSCE/NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education assessment patterns.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Perform Automated Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCBT604 - IT PROJECT MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Design and Perform Automated Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automated Testing** is a software quality assurance methodology wherein specialized tools, scripts, and frameworks are employed to execute pre-defined test cases on a software application *without manual intervention*. The process involves the **design, development, execution, and maintenance of test scripts** that validate functional, regression, performance, and integration requirements of an IT project deliverable.

In the context of **IT Project Management**, automated testing is not merely a technical activity — it is a **strategic project function** that falls within the *Project Quality Management* knowledge area and *Project Execution* process group. It directly impacts the **Triple Constraint (Scope, Time, Cost)** by reducing defect leakage, shortening feedback loops, and enabling **Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment (CI/CD)** pipelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In Module 4, automated testing is treated as a *planned, design-driven activity* — meaning the student must understand **WHY** a test is automated, **WHAT** to automate, **HOW** to architect the automation framework, and **WHEN** to integrate it into the project lifecycle (typically the *Build & Test* phase of the SDLC).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car Wash" Analogy 🚗

Imagine you own a chain of car wash stations. Initially, every car is washed **manually** by a worker (manual testing). This is fine for 10 cars a day, but when you scale to **1,000 cars a day**, manual washing becomes:
- Slow 🐢
- Inconsistent (Worker A washes tires, Worker B forgets) ❌
- Expensive (more labor cost) 💸

So you install a **robotic car wash system**:
- Sensors detect the car → Automated
- Sprays water → Reusable script
- Applies soap → Reusable script
- Dries → Reusable script

This is **automated testing**:
| Car Wash Analogy | Software Automated Testing |
|---|---|
| Robotic wash machine | Automation tool (Selenium, QTP, JUnit) |
| Wash instructions program | Test script |
| Car entering the wash | Test case execution |
| Clean car output | Pass/Fail result |
| Maintenance of the robot | Script maintenance |

> 🎯 **Intuition Locked:** Automated testing = a **reliable robot worker** that performs the *same repetitive checks* on your software, *faster, cheaper, and without getting bored* — leaving humans to focus on *creative, exploratory, and edge-case* testing.

---

## 1.3 Core Metrics & Standard Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> The following **industry-standard metrics** are used to measure the success of automation initiatives in IT projects (as per ISTQB & IEEE 829 standards).

- **Test Automation Coverage (%)** — Proportion of test cases automated out of total test cases.
- **Defect Detection Percentage (DDP)** — Efficiency of automation in catching bugs.
- **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** — Average time automation takes to identify a failure.
- **Return on Investment (ROI) of Automation** — Calculated over multiple test cycles.
- **Script Maintenance Effort** — Hours spent updating broken scripts per release.

---

## 1.4 When to Automate vs. When NOT To

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** A common student error is to state *"automate everything."* This is WRONG. Automation is a *strategic decision*.

| ✅ Automate When | ❌ Do NOT Automate When |
|---|---|
| Tests are **repetitive** (run every build) | Test is **run only once** |
| Test cases are **stable** & requirements fixed | Requirements change **frequently** |
| **Regression suites** & smoke tests | **Usability / UX** evaluation |
| **Data-driven** scenarios | **Ad-hoc / exploratory** testing |
| **Performance / load** testing | Tests requiring **human judgment** (visual aesthetics) |
| **API contract** validation | Early-stage **prototyping** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Automation Testing Life Cycle (ATLC) — Step-by-Step

The **ATLC** is the structured framework within IT Project Management for designing and executing automated testing. It is a *6-phase* methodology aligned with the **PMBOK Project Quality Management** process.

### Phase 1: 🔍 Test Planning & Feasibility Analysis
- **Goal:** Decide **what** to automate and **what** to keep manual.
- **Activities:**
  - Identify candidate test cases (high-frequency, high-criticality).
  - Perform **cost-benefit analysis** — compare manual effort vs. automation effort.
  - Select the **right automation tool** (commercial vs. open-source).
  - Define **entry & exit criteria** for automation.
- **PMBOK Mapping:** *Plan Quality Management* process.

### Phase 2: 🏗️ Test Automation Framework Design
- **Goal:** Architect the *skeleton* on which all test scripts will be built.
- **Decision Points:**
  - Choose framework type (see Section 2.3 below).
  - Define **naming conventions**, folder structures, reporting standards.
  - Design the **Object Repository** (map of UI elements).
  - Establish **data-driven** parameterization strategy.
- **PMBOK Mapping:** *Plan Resource Management* + *Plan Quality Management*.

### Phase 3: ✍️ Test Script Development
- **Goal:** Write the actual automation code.
- **Activities:**
  - Develop **modular, reusable** functions.
  - Implement **data-driven** or **keyword-driven** logic.
  - Integrate with **version control** (Git, SVN).
  - Add **assertions** (verification checkpoints) at every critical step.
- **PMBOK Mapping:** *Acquire Resources* + *Direct & Manage Project Work*.

### Phase 4: 🚀 Test Execution & Continuous Integration
- **Goal:** Run scripts automatically as part of the build pipeline.
- **Activities:**
  - Trigger scripts on every code commit (via **Jenkins / GitLab CI / Azure DevOps**).
  - Execute on **multiple environments** (cross-browser, cross-platform).
  - Capture **screenshots, logs, and metrics** on failure.
  - Generate **automated test reports** (HTML, PDF, JUnit XML).
- **PMBOK Mapping:** *Manage Quality* + *Monitor & Control Project Work*.

### Phase 5: 🔧 Test Script Maintenance
- **Goal:** Keep the automation suite **healthy and relevant**.
- **Activities:**
  - Update locators when UI changes.
  - Refactor flaky tests.
  - Remove obsolete test cases.
  - Update test data sets.
- **PMBOK Mapping:** *Control Quality*.

### Phase 6: 📊 Reporting, Metrics & ROI Evaluation
- **Goal:** Quantify the **value delivered** by automation.
- **Activities:**
  - Publish **dashboards** (Pass/Fail %, execution time, defect trends).
  - Calculate **Automation ROI**.
  - Conduct **retrospectives** to improve future cycles.
- **PMBOK Mapping:** *Manage Project Knowledge* + *Monitor & Control Project Work*.

---

## 2.2 The Test Automation Pyramid — A Strategic Model 🏛️

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Test Automation Pyramid** (Mike Cohn's model) is a KTU-favourite concept. It dictates the **distribution** of test types in an automation strategy.

```
           /\
          /  \        🔺 UI / E2E Tests (Few, Slow, Brittle)
         /----\
        /      \      🟡 Service / API Tests (Moderate)
       /--------\
      /          \    🟢 Unit Tests (Many, Fast, Stable)
     /------------\
```

| Layer | Test Type | Quantity | Speed | Stability | Tools |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bottom (Base)** | Unit Tests | **High (70%)** | ⚡ Very Fast | 🟢 Very Stable | JUnit, NUnit, pytest |
| **Middle** | Integration / API Tests | **Medium (20%)** | ⚡ Fast | 🟢 Stable | Postman, REST Assured, SoapUI |
| **Top** | UI / End-to-End Tests | **Low (10%)** | 🐢 Slow | 🟡 Brittle | Selenium, Cypress, Playwright |

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** A *healthy* automation suite has **more unit tests, fewer UI tests**. UI tests are the most *expensive to maintain* and *flaky*.

---

## 2.3 Types of Test Automation Frameworks

> [!NOTE]
> A **framework** is a set of guidelines, coding standards, and tools that provides a structured way to build and execute automated tests.

### A. Linear / Record-and-Playback Framework
- **How it works:** Tester records user actions; tool generates the script.
- **Pros:** Fastest to start; ideal for beginners.
- **Cons:** No reusability; brittle to UI changes.
- **Example Tools:** Selenium IDE, QTP (now UFT).

### B. Modular / Function-Driven Framework
- **How it works:** Application is broken into *modules*; each module has independent test functions.
- **Pros:** High reusability; easy maintenance.
- **Cons:** Initial setup effort is high.
- **Example:** Selenium + Java/Python with Page Object Model.

### C. Data-Driven Framework
- **How it works:** Test logic is *separated* from test data; data is read from Excel, CSV, or databases.
- **Pros:** Same script tests **multiple data sets**; massive coverage.
- **Cons:** Requires data management discipline.
- **Example:** Selenium with Apache POI (Excel reader).

### D. Keyword-Driven Framework
- **How it works:** Tests are built using **predefined keywords** (e.g., `clickLogin`, `verifyTitle`).
- **Pros:** Highly reusable; non-technical testers can author tests.
- **Cons:** Complex initial keyword library setup.
- **Example:** Robot Framework.

### E. Hybrid Framework
- **How it works:** Combines **two or more** of the above (most common in industry).
- **Pros:** Maximum flexibility; industry-standard.
- **Cons:** Requires skilled architects.
- **Example:** Selenium + TestNG + Data-Driven + POM.

### F. Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) Framework
- **How it works:** Tests written in **plain English** (Gherkin syntax) bridging business and tech.
- **Pros:** Improves collaboration; living documentation.
- **Cons:** Slower execution; requires discipline.
- **Example:** Cucumber, SpecFlow, JBehave.

---

## 2.4 Test Case Selection for Automation — Decision Matrix

Not every test case should be automated. Apply the following **weighted scoring matrix**:

| Criterion | Weight (W) | Score (1–5) | Weighted Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency of execution (per release) | 0.30 | 4 | 1.20 |
| Business criticality | 0.25 | 5 | 1.25 |
| Stability of functionality | 0.20 | 3 | 0.60 |
| Manual effort saved | 0.15 | 4 | 0.60 |
| Technical complexity of automation | 0.10 | 2 | 0.20 |
| **Total Automation Score (TAS)** | **1.00** | — | **3.85 / 5.00** |

> 🎯 **Rule of Thumb (KTU):** Automate test cases with **TAS ≥ 3.5**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 🧪

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE numerical/analytical questions. Memorize the units and conditions.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Automation Coverage (%)** | $AC = \dfrac{N_{\text{automated}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % of test cases automated | % |
| 2 | **Defect Detection Percentage (DDP)** | $DDP = \dfrac{D_{\text{caught}}}{D_{\text{caught}} + D_{\text{escaped}}} \times 100$ | Efficiency of automation in finding defects | % |
| 3 | **Automation ROI (%)** | $ROI = \dfrac{(\text{Manual Cost} - \text{Automation Cost})}{\text{Automation Cost}} \times 100$ | Financial benefit of automation | % |
| 4 | **Payback Period** | $PP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Net Periodic Savings}}$ | Time to recover automation investment | Months / Cycles |
| 5 | **Effort Reduction (%)** | $ER = \dfrac{(E_{\text{manual}} - E_{\text{auto}})}{E_{\text{manual}}} \times 100$ | Reduction in manual testing hours | % |
| 6 | **Test Execution Time Reduction** | $T_{\text{red}} = \dfrac{(T_{\text{manual}} - T_{\text{auto}})}{T_{\text{manual}}} \times 100$ | Speed-up achieved by automation | % |
| 7 | **Script Reliability / Flakiness Rate** | $FR = \dfrac{N_{\text{flaky runs}}}{N_{\text{total runs}}} \times 100$ | % of executions that fail non-deterministically | % |
| 8 | **Maintenance Overhead Ratio** | $MOR = \dfrac{T_{\text{maintenance}}}{T_{\text{new dev}}} \times 100$ | Time spent on fixes vs. new scripts | % |
| 9 | **Mean Time To Detect (MTTD)** | $MTTD = \dfrac{\sum (t_{\text{detect}} - t_{\text{inject}})}{N_{\text{defects}}}$ | Average time automation finds a defect | Hours |
| 10 | **Test Case Selection Score (TAS)** | $TAS = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(W_i \times S_i)$ | Weighted decision score for automation | Scalar (0–5) |

> ⚠️ **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all exam answers, use `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX) for any absolute value or conditional symbol. Never use the raw pipe `|` inside markdown tables as it breaks the table structure.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> 🎯 **Industry Insight (KTU Application):** Companies like **Google, Netflix, Amazon** run **millions of automated tests daily** as part of their CI/CD pipelines. A single code commit to Netflix's repository triggers **~5,000 automated tests** within minutes — something impossible manually. The **strategic design** of *what* to automate is the difference between a *fast, reliable* release pipeline and a *bottleneck* that slows down the entire IT project.

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 1: Calculating Automation ROI

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Problem]**
>
> An IT project team is evaluating the automation of its regression test suite. The following data is provided:
> - Total test cases in regression suite: **N = 500**
> - Number of test cases recommended for automation: **350**
> - Manual effort per cycle: **E_manual = 800 person-hours**
> - Automated effort per cycle (post development): **E_auto = 120 person-hours**
> - Number of release cycles per year: **n = 12**
> - One-time automation development cost: **C_dev = ₹2,50,000**
> - Cost per person-hour: **₹500**
>
> **Calculate:**
> 1. Automation Coverage (%)
> 2. Effort Reduction per cycle (%)
> 3. Annual Savings (₹)
> 4. Automation ROI (%)
> 5. Payback Period (cycles)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute Automation Coverage (AC)**

We apply the formula:

$$
AC = \frac{N_{\text{automated}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
AC = \frac{350}{500} \times 100
$$

$$
AC = 0.70 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{AC = 70\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** 70% of the regression test cases are being automated — a healthy coverage level for a mature automation program.

---

**Step 2: Compute Effort Reduction per Cycle (ER)**

Apply the formula:

$$
ER = \frac{(E_{\text{manual}} - E_{\text{auto}})}{E_{\text{manual}}} \times 100
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
ER = \frac{(800 - 120)}{800} \times 100
$$

$$
ER = \frac{680}{800} \times 100
$$

$$
ER = 0.85 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{ER = 85\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Automation reduces manual effort by 85% per release cycle — a massive productivity gain.

---

**Step 3: Compute Annual Savings (S)**

Savings per cycle in hours:

$$
S_{\text{cycle}} = E_{\text{manual}} - E_{\text{auto}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{cycle}} = 800 - 120
$$

$$
S_{\text{cycle}} = 680 \text{ person-hours/cycle}
$$

Annual hours saved:

$$
S_{\text{annual, hours}} = S_{\text{cycle}} \times n
$$

$$
S_{\text{annual, hours}} = 680 \times 12
$$

$$
S_{\text{annual, hours}} = 8160 \text{ person-hours/year}
$$

Convert to monetary value:

$$
S_{\text{annual}} = S_{\text{annual, hours}} \times \text{Cost per hour}
$$

$$
S_{\text{annual}} = 8160 \times 500
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{annual}} = \text{₹}41,60,000 \text{ per year}}
$$

---

**Step 4: Compute Automation ROI (%)**

Apply the formula:

$$
ROI = \frac{(S_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{dev, annualized}})}{C_{\text{dev, annualized}}} \times 100
$$

Treating the one-time development cost as Year 1 investment (and assuming it's recovered in year 1):

$$
ROI = \frac{(S_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{dev}})}{C_{\text{dev}}} \times 100
$$

$$
ROI = \frac{(41,60,000 - 2,50,000)}{2,50,000} \times 100
$$

$$
ROI = \frac{39,10,000}{2,50,000} \times 100
$$

$$
ROI = 15.64 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{ROI = 1564\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A **1564% ROI in Year 1** — a financially sound automation decision.

---

**Step 5: Compute Payback Period (PP)**

$$
PP = \frac{C_{\text{dev}}}{S_{\text{cycle, monetary}}}
$$

First, compute monetary savings per cycle:

$$
S_{\text{cycle, monetary}} = 680 \times 500
$$

$$
S_{\text{cycle, monetary}} = \text{₹}3,40,000 \text{ per cycle}
$$

Then:

$$
PP = \frac{2,50,000}{3,40,000}
$$

$$
PP = 0.7353
$$

$$
\boxed{PP \approx 0.74 \text{ cycles} \approx 22 \text{ days}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The automation investment is recovered in **less than one release cycle** — a strong project management justification.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 2: Defect Detection Percentage (DDP)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Problem]**
>
> During a 6-month IT project, the QA team found:
> - Defects caught by automated tests: **D_caught = 142**
> - Defects that escaped to production: **D_escaped = 18**
>
> **Calculate the Defect Detection Percentage (DDP) and interpret the result.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

Apply the formula:

$$
DDP = \frac{D_{\text{caught}}}{D_{\text{caught}} + D_{\text{escaped}}} \times 100
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
DDP = \frac{142}{142 + 18} \times 100
$$

$$
DDP = \frac{142}{160} \times 100
$$

$$
DDP = 0.8875 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{DDP = 88.75\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Automation is catching **~89% of defects** before they reach production. Industry benchmark for mature automation is **≥ 85%** — so the project is performing well, but the **11.25% escape rate** indicates a need to:
> - Review the test case selection matrix.
> - Add more **edge-case** and **integration** automated tests.
> - Improve the **Object Repository** robustness.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Problem 3: Test Case Selection Score (TAS)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question]**
>
> Consider 4 test cases with the following selection attributes. Use weights: Frequency = 0.30, Criticality = 0.25, Stability = 0.20, Manual Effort Saved = 0.15, Complexity = 0.10.
>
> | Test Case | Frequency | Criticality | Stability | Manual Effort | Complexity |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | TC-01 | 5 | 4 | 4 | 3 | 4 |
> | TC-02 | 2 | 5 | 2 | 5 | 3 |
> | TC-03 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 4 | 2 |
> | TC-04 | 3 | 4 | 3 | 3 | 5 |
>
> **Identify which test cases qualify for automation (TAS ≥ 3.5).**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Compute TAS for each test case using:**

$$
TAS_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (W_j \times S_{ij})
$$

**Test Case TC-01:**

$$
TAS_{01} = (0.30 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 4)
$$

$$
TAS_{01} = 1.50 + 1.00 + 0.80 + 0.45 + 0.40
$$

$$
\boxed{TAS_{01} = 4.15} \quad \text{(✅ Automate)}
$$

**Test Case TC-02:**

$$
TAS_{02} = (0.30 \times 2) + (0.25 \times 5) + (0.20 \times 2) + (0.15 \times 5) + (0.10 \times 3)
$$

$$
TAS_{02} = 0.60 + 1.25 + 0.40 + 0.75 + 0.30
$$

$$
\boxed{TAS_{02} = 3.30} \quad \text{(❌ Do Not Automate)}
$$

**Test Case TC-03:**

$$
TAS_{03} = (0.30 \times 4) + (0.25 \times 3) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 4) + (0.10 \times 2)
$$

$$
TAS_{03} = 1.20 + 0.75 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.20
$$

$$
\boxed{TAS_{03} = 3.75} \quad \text{(✅ Automate)}
$$

**Test Case TC-04:**

$$
TAS_{04} = (0.30 \times 3) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 3) + (0.15 \times 3) + (0.10 \times 5)
$$

$$
TAS_{04} = 0.90 + 1.00 + 0.60 + 0.45 + 0.50
$$

$$
\boxed{TAS_{04} = 3.45} \quad \text{(❌ Do Not Automate — close to threshold)}
$$

### Summary Table

| Test Case | TAS Score | Decision |
|---|---|---|
| TC-01 | 4.15 | ✅ Automate |
| TC-02 | 3.30 | ❌ Manual |
| TC-03 | 3.75 | ✅ Automate |
| TC-04 | 3.45 | ❌ Manual |

> 🎯 **Final Recommendation:** Automate **TC-01 and TC-03** (2 out of 4). TC-04 is borderline — the project manager may consider it if stability improves in subsequent releases.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define Automated Testing. List any TWO situations where automated testing is preferred over manual testing.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Automated Testing is the use of specialized software tools and pre-written test scripts to execute test cases on a software application automatically, compare actual outcomes with expected outcomes, and report results — **without human intervention** during execution.

**Two Situations (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Regression Testing:** When the same set of test cases must be re-executed after every code change or build (high frequency).
2. **Performance / Load Testing:** Simulating thousands of concurrent users — impossible to perform manually.
3. **Data-Driven Scenarios:** When the *same logic* must be validated against *hundreds of data sets* (e.g., form validation).
4. **Continuous Integration Pipelines:** Tests triggered automatically on every code commit.

> [!NOTE]
> Any TWO of the above are acceptable. Marks awarded for: Definition clarity (1.5) + Correct situations (0.75 each).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between Manual Testing and Automated Testing. Mention TWO advantages of automation.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Parameter | Manual Testing | Automated Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution** | Performed by a human tester | Performed by tools/scripts |
| **Speed** | Slow (human-paced) | Fast (machine-paced) |
| **Reliability** | Prone to human error | Consistent & repeatable |
| **Cost (long-term)** | High recurring cost | High initial, low recurring cost |
| **Best Suited For** | Exploratory, usability, ad-hoc | Regression, performance, data-driven |
| **Initial Investment** | Low | High |

**Two Advantages of Automation (1 Mark):**
1. **Faster Feedback Cycle:** Automated tests run in minutes, enabling rapid defect detection in CI/CD pipelines.
2. **Higher Test Coverage:** Automation can execute thousands of test cases across multiple environments, browsers, and configurations — a coverage impossible to achieve manually.
3. **Reusability:** Test scripts can be reused across multiple releases and projects with minimal modifications.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **⚠️ KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically has a 14-mark question with INTERNAL CHOICE between two alternatives (OR option). Below are TWO complete parallel question choices — each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question - Module 4]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Automation Testing Life Cycle (ATLC)** in detail. List and describe any **FIVE phases** with their key activities and deliverables.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** An IT project has a regression suite of **800 test cases**. The team automates **600** of them. The manual effort per cycle is **1000 person-hours**; automated effort per cycle is **150 person-hours**. The project runs **10 cycles per year**. The one-time automation cost is **₹5,00,000** and the cost per person-hour is **₹400**.
>
> **Calculate:**
> 1. Automation Coverage (%)
> 2. Effort Reduction per cycle (%)
> 3. Annual Monetary Savings (₹)
> 4. Automation ROI (%)
> 5. Payback Period in cycles

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **Automation Testing Life Cycle (ATLC)** is a structured, multi-phase methodology for designing and executing automated testing in IT projects. It ensures that automation is treated as a **planned, governed activity** rather than ad-hoc scripting.

The five key phases are:

**1. Test Planning & Feasibility Analysis (1.5 Marks)**
- Identify candidate test cases using the **TAS (Test Automation Score)**.
- Perform cost-benefit analysis.
- Select automation tool (commercial like UFT vs. open-source like Selenium).
- Define **entry criteria** (e.g., stable requirements) and **exit criteria** (e.g., 90% pass rate, all P1 defects closed).
- **Deliverable:** Automation Feasibility Report + Test Automation Plan.

**2. Test Automation Framework Design (1.5 Marks)**
- Choose framework type (Linear, Modular, Data-Driven, Keyword-Driven, Hybrid, BDD).
- Design the **Object Repository** (UI element map).
- Define **naming conventions**, folder hierarchy, reporting templates.
- **Deliverable:** Framework Design Document (FDD).

**3. Test Script Development (1.5 Marks)**
- Develop modular, reusable scripts in the chosen language (Java, Python, C#).
- Implement data-driven parameterization using external files.
- Add **assertion checkpoints** at every critical step.
- Integrate with **Git** for version control.
- **Deliverable:** Working test scripts + Object Repository files.

**4. Test Execution & CI Integration (1.5 Marks)**
- Configure **Jenkins / GitLab CI** to trigger scripts on every code commit.
- Execute across **multiple environments** (cross-browser, cross-OS).
- Capture screenshots, logs, and stack traces on failure.
- Generate **HTML/PDF/JUnit XML reports**.
- **Deliverable:** Automated Test Execution Reports + CI Pipeline Configuration.

**5. Script Maintenance & Reporting (1 Mark)**
- Update locators when UI changes.
- Refactor flaky tests, remove obsolete cases.
- Publish **metrics dashboards** (Pass %, execution time, defect trends).
- **Deliverable:** Maintenance Log + ROI Report.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks (Numerical Solution):**

**Step 1: Automation Coverage (AC)**

$$
AC = \frac{N_{\text{automated}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

$$
AC = \frac{600}{800} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{AC = 75\%}
$$

**Step 2: Effort Reduction per Cycle (ER)**

$$
ER = \frac{(E_{\text{manual}} - E_{\text{auto}})}{E_{\text{manual}}} \times 100
$$

$$
ER = \frac{(1000 - 150)}{1000} \times 100
$$

$$
ER = \frac{850}{1000} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{ER = 85\%}
$$

**Step 3: Annual Monetary Savings (S_annual)**

Hours saved per cycle:

$$
S_{\text{cycle, hours}} = 1000 - 150
$$

$$
S_{\text{cycle, hours}} = 850 \text{ person-hours/cycle}
$$

Annual hours saved:

$$
S_{\text{annual, hours}} = 850 \times 10
$$

$$
S_{\text{annual, hours}} = 8500 \text{ person-hours/year}
$$

Monetary savings:

$$
S_{\text{annual}} = 8500 \times 400
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{annual}} = \text{₹}34,00,000 \text{ per year}}
$$

**Step 4: Automation ROI (%)**

$$
ROI = \frac{(S_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{dev}})}{C_{\text{dev}}} \times 100
$$

$$
ROI = \frac{(34,00,000 - 5,00,000)}{5,00,000} \times 100
$$

$$
ROI = \frac{29,00,000}{5,00,000} \times 100
$$

$$
ROI = 5.80 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{ROI = 580\%}
$$

**Step 5: Payback Period (PP)**

Monetary savings per cycle:

$$
S_{\text{cycle, monetary}} = 850 \times 400
$$

$$
S_{\text{cycle, monetary}} = \text{₹}3,40,000 \text{ per cycle}
$$

Payback Period:

$$
PP = \frac{C_{\text{dev}}}{S_{\text{cycle, monetary}}}
$$

$$
PP = \frac{5,00,000}{3,40,000}
$$

$$
PP = 1.47
$$

$$
\boxed{PP \approx 1.47 \text{ cycles}}
$$

> **Final Tabular Summary:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Automation Coverage | 75% |
| Effort Reduction | 85% |
| Annual Savings | ₹34,00,000 |
| ROI | 580% |
| Payback Period | 1.47 cycles |

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (OR Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question - Module 4 Alternate Choice]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **different types of test automation frameworks**. Compare them on the basis of **reusability, maintainability, skill requirement, and best-use scenario**.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **functional architecture of a typical test automation framework** integrated with a CI/CD pipeline. Label all major components and explain the **data flow** from test case creation to defect reporting.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The choice of framework is the **single most critical architectural decision** in any automation initiative. The six major types are:

**1. Linear (Record-and-Playback) Framework (1 Mark)**
- **How it works:** Tester records user actions via a recorder tool; the tool auto-generates a script.
- **Reusability:** ❌ Very Low — scripts are monolithic.
- **Maintainability:** ❌ Very Low — any UI change breaks the script entirely.
- **Skill Requirement:** ✅ Low — no programming needed.
- **Best Use:** Quick demos, smoke tests for small applications.

**2. Modular (Function-Driven) Framework (1 Mark)**
- **How it works:** Application is decomposed into *modules*; each module has independent test functions.
- **Reusability:** ✅ High — functions reused across tests.
- **Maintainability:** ✅ High — changes localized to modules.
- **Skill Requirement:** 🟡 Medium — requires programming.
- **Best Use:** Medium-to-large applications with stable UI.

**3. Data-Driven Framework (1 Mark)**
- **How it works:** Test logic is *separated* from test data; data is read from external sources (Excel, CSV, DB).
- **Reusability:** ✅ Very High — one script tests *thousands* of data combinations.
- **Maintainability:** ✅ High — only data file needs updating.
- **Skill Requirement:** 🟡 Medium-High — needs data parameterization skills.
- **Best Use:** Form validation, login tests, search functionality.

**4. Keyword-Driven Framework (1 Mark)**
- **How it works:** Tests are built using **predefined keywords** (e.g., `clickButton`, `verifyText`); the underlying keyword library implements the action.
- **Reusability:** ✅ Very High — keywords reused across projects.
- **Maintainability:** ✅ Very High — change keyword implementation once, all tests updated.
- **Skill Requirement:** 🟡 Medium for testers (no code) + High for keyword developers.
- **Best Use:** Large enterprises with mixed-skill QA teams; cross-project standardization.

**5. Hybrid Framework (1 Mark)**
- **How it works:** Combines **two or more** approaches (e.g., Modular + Data-Driven).
- **Reusability:** ✅ Excellent
- **Maintainability:** ✅ Excellent
- **Skill Requirement:** 🔴 High — needs experienced architects.
- **Best Use:** Enterprise-grade, multi-application, long-term automation suites.

**6. Behavior-Driven Development (BDD) Framework (1 Mark)**
- **How it works:** Tests written in **plain English** using Gherkin syntax (`Given-When-Then`); bridges business and technical teams.
- **Reusability:** ✅ High
- **Maintainability:** ✅ High
- **Skill Requirement:** 🟡 Medium
- **Best Use:** Agile projects where business analysts, developers, and testers collaborate.

**7. Comparison Table (1 Mark):**

| Criterion | Linear | Modular | Data-Driven | Keyword | Hybrid | BDD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reusability | Low | High | Very High | Very High | Excellent | High |
| Maintainability | Low | High | High | Very High | Excellent | High |
| Skill Required | Low | Medium | Med-High | Medium | High | Medium |
| Best Use | Smoke | Medium App | Data-Heavy | Enterprise | Enterprise | Agile |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks — Block Diagram & Data Flow:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The student is expected to draw a **block diagram** of the functional architecture (similar to Section 4.3 of these notes) and explain the **data flow** through 6 stages.

**Data Flow Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Stage 1: Test Case Repository (1 Mark)**
- Test cases authored in tools like **JIRA / Azure DevOps / TestRail**.
- Each test case has: ID, Description, Pre-conditions, Steps, Expected Result, Priority.
- The automation engineer selects candidate test cases based on the **TAS scoring matrix**.

**Stage 2: Test Data Source (1 Mark)**
- Test data is decoupled from scripts and stored in **Excel, CSV, JSON, or Database**.
- This enables **data-driven** execution — same script, multiple data sets.
- Example: 1000 rows of login credentials tested with one script.

**Stage 3: Test Script Development Layer (1.5 Marks)**
- Scripts written in **Java/Python/C#** using the chosen framework (e.g., Selenium + TestNG + POM).
- Object Repository maintains UI element locators (XPath, CSS, ID).
- **Version Control** (Git) stores all scripts with branch/merge strategy.

**Stage 4: CI/CD Orchestration (1.5 Marks)**
- **Jenkins / GitLab CI / Azure DevOps** triggers test execution on:
  - Every code commit (post-merge to main).
  - Scheduled nightly runs.
  - Manual trigger for release validation.
- Tests execute on **Selenium Grid** or cloud platforms (BrowserStack, Sauce Labs) for cross-browser testing.

**Stage 5: Execution & Verification (1 Mark)**
- Automation engine interacts with the **Application Under Test (AUT)**.
- **Assertion engine** compares actual vs. expected outcomes.
- On failure: captures **screenshot, page source, console logs**.

**Stage 6: Reporting & Defect Logging (1 Mark)**
- Generates **HTML, PDF, JUnit XML** reports.
- Automatically **logs defects in JIRA** with all artifacts attached.
- **Dashboard** displays trends (pass %, execution time, defect density).
- Project Manager uses these metrics for **release go/no-go decisions**.

> **Final Architecture Flow Summary:**

$$
\text{Test Repository} \rightarrow \text{Test Data} \rightarrow \text{Scripts} \rightarrow \text{CI Trigger} \rightarrow \text{Execution Engine} \rightarrow \text{Verification} \rightarrow \text{Reports + Defects} \rightarrow \text{PM Decision}
$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **❌ Confusing "Automation Testing" with "Test Automation Tool":** The tool is the *means*; testing is the *process*. Always explain the **process** first.
> 2. **❌ Stating "automate everything":** Examiners *deduct marks* for this. Always justify selection using **TAS** or similar criteria.
> 3. **❌ Forgetting units in numerical answers:** If ROI = 580%, you **MUST** write the "%" sign. If savings = ₹34,00,000, write "₹" symbol.
> 4. **❌ Skipping framework comparison in 14-mark answers:** When asked about frameworks, the examiner expects **at least 4 types with a comparison table**.
> 5. **❌ Confusing ATLC with STLC:** STLC is the *general* Software Testing Life Cycle; ATLC is the *automation-specific* lifecycle with 6 phases. Know the difference.
> 6. **❌ Ignoring CI/CD integration:** In 2024 scheme, any answer about automation that *does not mention* CI/CD is considered incomplete.
> 7. **❌ Wrong formula syntax in tables:** Always use `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` for absolute value in LaTeX formulas, not the raw `|` pipe character.
> 8. **❌ Missing deliverables:** Every phase of ATLC must list its **deliverable document**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) 🏁

> 🎯 **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These Points:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Automated testing = *tool-driven, script-based, repeatable* execution of test cases without manual intervention.
- ✅ **ATLC has 6 phases:** Planning → Framework Design → Script Development → Execution → Maintenance → Reporting (cyclic).
- ✅ **Test Automation Pyramid:** Unit (70%) > API (20%) > UI (10%). Bottom-heavy is healthy.
- ✅ **6 Framework Types:** Linear, Modular, Data-Driven, Keyword-Driven, Hybrid, BDD. **Hybrid** is the industry standard.
- ✅ **TAS Formula:** $\;TAS = \sum (W_i \times S_i)\;$ — threshold ≥ 3.5 for automation.
- ✅ **Automation ROI Formula:** $\;ROI = \dfrac{(S_{\text{annual}} - C_{\text{dev}})}{C_{\text{dev}}} \times 100\;$
- ✅ **Payback Period Formula:** $\;PP = \dfrac{C_{\text{dev}}}{S_{\text{cycle, monetary}}}\;$
- ✅ **DDP Formula:** $\;DDP = \dfrac{D_{\text{caught}}}{D_{\text{caught}} + D_{\text{escaped}}} \times 100\;$ — industry benchmark ≥ 85%.
- ✅ **CI/CD Tools:** Jenkins, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps — must be mentioned in any 14-mark automation answer.
- ✅ **Common Tools:** Selenium, Cypress, Playwright (UI); Postman, REST Assured (API); JUnit, TestNG, pytest (Unit).
- ✅ **Don't Automate:** Usability tests, exploratory tests, one-time tests, early prototypes, frequently changing requirements.
- ✅ **PMBOK Mapping:** Automated testing falls under **Project Quality Management** and **Project Execution** process groups.
- ✅ **Always mention deliverables** in ATLC phase answers (Feasibility Report, Framework Design Doc, Test Scripts, Execution Reports, Maintenance Log, ROI Report).
- ✅ **Mermaid diagrams** should be drawn for any architecture question — examiners give **1–2 bonus marks** for neat diagrams.
- ✅ **Numerical answers must end with a tabular summary** — this signals "exam-ready" presentation to the evaluator.
- ✅ **Use \vert or \mid in LaTeX**, never the raw `|` pipe character inside markdown tables.
